Description: ## Description  Fixes wasp-lang/wasp#3986. Moves the top-level `await setupFn()` + `initializeQueryClient()` side effects out of the generated `routes.tsx` virtual file and into a new dedicated SDK module `operations/bootstrap.ts`, which exposes a single `queryClientPromise` composing user setup with query client creation. Consumers (`WaspApp` via `React.use`, `resources.js` via `await`, and the SPA/SSR entry points) now consume that promise directly, so `routes.tsx` becomes purely declarative (routes only) and the action-at-a-distance pattern is gone. As a simplification, `queryClient.ts` drops the deferred-promise dance (the old `queryClientInitialized` + `resolveQueryClientInitialized` pair) now that the promise is composed at the call site.  ## Type of change  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [x] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  - [x] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in \`examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests\`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in \`waspc/data/Cli/templates\`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in \`examples/\`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated \`examples/tutorials\`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in \`web/docs/\`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [ ] I updated \`waspc/ChangeLog.md\` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in \`web/docs/migration-guides/\`.   - [ ] I **bumped the \`version\`** in \`waspc/waspc.cabal\` to reflect the changes I introduced.

Bumps [dompurify](https://github.com/cure53/DOMPurify) from 3.3.0 to 3.4.0.
Release notes

Sourced from dompurify's releases.

DOMPurify 3.4.0

Most relevant changes:

  • Fixed a problem with FORBID_TAGS not winning over ADD_TAGS, thanks @​kodareef5
  • Fixed several minor problems and typos regarding MathML attributes, thanks @​DavidOliver
  • Fixed ADD_ATTR/ADD_TAGS function leaking into subsequent array-based calls, thanks @​1Jesper1
  • Fixed a missing SAFE_FOR_TEMPLATES scrub in RETURN_DOM path, thanks @​bencalif
  • Fixed a prototype pollution via CUSTOM_ELEMENT_HANDLING, thanks @​trace37labs
  • Fixed an issue with ADD_TAGS function form bypassing FORBID_TAGS, thanks @​eddieran

Description: ## Description  fix `waspc` build script to use `tsx` instead of bare `node` when executing typescript files, enabling successfull build on apple silicon (arm64) without requiring specific node.js version with typescript support.  ## Issue  the `waspc` build script (`waspc/run`) and related typescript build tools use `node` to execute `.ts` files:   e.g. `node ${SCRIPT_DIR}/tools/libs/build.ts`  also, `waspc/tools/libs/build.ts` spawns a child node process using `node`:  `runCmd("node", [join("tools", "get-waspc-version.ts")], { cwd: waspcDirPath })`  This fails on systems where the default `node` cannot handle typescript files without additional loaders, resulting in the following error:  `TypeError [ERR_UNKNOWN_FILE_EXTENSION]: Unknown file extension ".ts"`  ## Changes  replace all `node` instances for `.ts` files with `npx tsx`, which properly loads typescript files.  ## Why  `tsx` requires no config and is available via npx without preinstall and the project already includes `typescript` as a dev dependency in `package.json`; `npx tsx` works across all nodejs versions and platforms.   ## Type of change  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [x ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  - [x ] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa). Description: ## Description  Four CI workflows (`ci-examples-test`, `ci-starters-test`, `ci-wasp-ai-test`, `ci-deploy-test`) were each setting up the full Haskell toolchain and rebuilding `waspc` from source just to get the `wasp-cli` binary. The `ci-waspc-build` workflow already builds the CLI and uploads it as an artifact.  This PR makes those four workflows depend on `waspc-build` and download the pre-built artifact instead, via a new `install-wasp-cli-artifact` composite action. The action auto-detects the runner platform, downloads the matching artifact, extracts it, and puts it on `PATH` with the `waspc_datadir` env var set.  The `prepare-deploy-test` composite action is removed since it is no longer needed.  Trade-off: these workflows now wait for `waspc-build` to finish (~10 min) instead of starting in parallel, but each was spending the same ~10 min rebuilding Haskell anyway.  Result: Net wall-clock time should theoretically be roughly the same, but in the end ends up being faster, because we don't hog the macOS runners as long, so we don't spend so long waiting for them to be picked up in the queue. Total compute is significantly reduced. Re-trying flaky tests is faster because they don't have to recompile the CLI each time, and can re-use it from earlier attempts.  ## Type of change  - [x] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  - [ ] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.

Description: ## Description Fixes: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3875 Our current `validateEnv.ts` plugin directly imports the `clientEnvSchema` from the SDK. In SDK, `clientEnvSchema`'s module imports the user's client env schema (from e.g. `src/env.ts` in `kitchen-sink` in example below). The problem is that the user code can depend on bundler features (notice the extensionless import), and the plugin is resolved before any bundling is done. The fix is to make the plugin use the `ViteDevServer` to bundle the necessary modules for us. When using `vite serve`, we can use the existing `server` and load the module, otherwise for `vite build` we have to start our own `server`. | Before change - crashes | After change - works | | ------------- | ------------- | | | | This PR also change how the validate env plugin works. Now it fails fast, before the client can render in the browser, and the validation message is logged in console. We piggyback on the existing validation mechanism in the SDK's `client/env.ts` file. That means we won't display the env validation error in the browser, but rather we will fail before that. Fails on both `wasp start` (`vite serve`) and `wasp build start` (`vite build`). ## Type of change - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** - [x] **� Bug fix** - [ ] **� New/improved feature** - [ ] **� Breaking change** ## Checklist - [x] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended. - � Tests and apps: - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`. - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed. - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed. - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa). - � Documentation: - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`. - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_ - [x] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change. - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`. - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.

Conclusions: > > * Do more research into other frameworks. Nodbody really does it like us, so no existing example to follow. Whats unavoidable for our approach: - Client env schema validation depends on bundler features. > * Decide what we're trading off and what's necessary. > * If it stays like this, consolidate the approaches (unless it's a perf issue). We can: 1) Resolve the module through an existing Vite server - Works only on `serve` through `configureServer` - GOOD: Very easy and straightforward, import the `client/env.ts` to trigger the validation side-effect. 2) Create additional client env schema validation build artifact which we execute post build - Works only on `build`. - BAD: Executes after artifacts are already created. - BAD: To stop bad deployments we need to cleanup the artifacts. 3) Create a separate Vite server which shares the same module resolution as the user's Vite server. Resolve the module though it. - Works on both `serve` and `build`. - GOOD: Logic is the same as the first approach. - BAD: Requires spinning a separate server. I would discard approach 2) as I would like to warn the users about bad environment variables as soon as possible. So performance wise the ideal solution would be to pick approach 1) for `serve` and approach 3) for `build`. I wanted to test how much large the performance difference during `serve is between 1) and 3). I got the following results: Approach 1) ~ 50ms Approach 3) ~ 75ms Because of that I think that sticking to approach 3) for both `serve` and `build` works the best. However, should users report a noticeable slowdown on `wasp start` we should evaluate this once again. NOTE: `buildStart` hook we use in the solution triggers only once during `wasp start`. Users will have to restart `wasp start` if they change their "`env.ts`" file to pick up the changes. The alternative is to implement HMR action for the plugin.

The Playwright config now supports external URLs via `PLAYWRIGHT_BASE_URL` and `PLAYWRIGHT_SERVER_URL` env vars, and a new `WASP_RUN_MODE=deployed` mode disables the local webServer. Description: ## Description The base of this approach is **module augmentation** combined with **interface declaration merging**. SDK defines empty interfaces in some publicly available module (exported). The user then extends those empty interface, modifying the SDK types. The SDK defines: - `types/index.ts` ```ts export interface Registry {} export interface OperationsRegistry {} export interface CrudOverridesRegistry {} ``` - and exports through `package.json` ```json { "exports": { "./types": "./dist/types/index.js", } } ``` User extends definitions (we generate it for them, but it's part of the user project): - `.wasp/out/types/configuration.mts` (and also `crud.mts` and `operations.mts`) ```ts declare module 'wasp/types' { interface Registry { clientEnvSchema: typeof import('../../../src/env').clientEnvValidationSchema // ... } } ``` - part of project via `tsconfig.json` changes ```json { "include": ["src", ".wasp/out/types"], } ``` Now for the tricky part. For user's module augmentations to work we have to use **conditional types** (`extends` keyword): - `types/index.ts` ```ts // dummy example to understand what we are doing export type UserType = Registry extends { someField: infer TUserType } ? TUserType : DefaultType; // then the generalized form we use export type FromRegistry = K extends keyof Registry ? Registry[K] : Default; ``` And then we can use it in the SDK: - `waspc/data/Generator/templates/sdk/wasp/client/env/schema.ts` ```ts import { FromRegistry } from "../../types"; type UserClientEnvSchema = FromRegistry<"clientEnvSchema", z.ZodObject<{}>>; const userClientEnvSchema: UserClientEnvSchema = {= envValidationSchema.importIdentifier =}; ``` The tricks (problems): 1. The SDK TypeScript compiles by itself (it depends on no other TS project). That means that at the point of compilation, the `Registry` interfaces are always empty, so the conditional types (`FromRegistry`) always default to the default value. The project must be compilable with the default value. 2. TypeScript does not like conditional types. When emitting declaration files, if the type is not explicitly assigned (`: UserClientEnvSchema`, `as UserClientEnvSchema` or `` ), it will be replaced by the inferred value, which is the `DefaultType`. When using user type in SDK, all it's derivatives have to be typed explicitly. E.g.: ```ts export type ClientEnvSchema = z.ZodObject; export const clientEnvSchema: ClientEnvSchema = z.object({ ...userClientEnvSchema.shape, ...waspClientEnvSchema.shape }); export const env: z.infer = ensureEnvSchema( import.meta.env, clientEnvSchema, ); ``` 3. Module declaration and module augmentation use the same syntax. The difference is whether the file they are defined is a script or a module. Modules do module augmentation, while scripts do module declaration. By default files inside `.wasp/out/types` would be considered scripts, since they neither `import` nor `export`. That's why I use the `.mts` file extension to force them to modules. The alternative is to add `exports {}` line. They can't be `.d.ts` files, since declarations are not modules. 4. Type dynamic import syntax (`typeof import("path").field`) is different from runtime dynamic import syntax (`await import ("path").then(m => m.field)`). So we have to add support for it. ### The problems with this PR: This PR is not fully functional. While in the userland everything is fine (no type errors in editor or user project compilation), the SDK won't compile. This is because SDK still depends on copied over user `src` for runtime (`.wasp/out/sdk/wasp/src`), and that copied over `src` does not have user's `tsconfig.json` which means that the generated types are not included in the project (`.wasp/out/types`), nor can we add them since the root is not the same. So the copied over `src` project does not compile (the SDK core itself does because of `DefaultType`). I could try to patch this then revert in runtime bridge PR, but I don't think it's worth the effort. The PR is still split up for sake of readability, and it can still be verified that it works in userland. E.g. in userland we can see that the custom prisma client types are inferred correctly: ## Type of change - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** - [ ] **� Bug fix** - [ ] **� New/improved feature** - [ ] **� Breaking change** ## Checklist - [ ] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended. - � Tests and apps: - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`. - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed. - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed. - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa). - � Documentation: - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`. - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_ - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change. - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`. - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.

Description:
Bumps [axios](https://github.com/axios/axios) from 1.13.3 to 1.15.0.
Release notes

Sourced from axios's releases.

v1.15.0

This release delivers two critical security patches, adds runtime support for Deno and Bun, and includes significant CI hardening, documentation improvements, and routine dependency updates.

⚠️ Important Changes

  • Deprecation: url.parse() usage has been replaced to address Node.js deprecation warnings. If you are on a recent version of Node.js, this resolves console warnings you may have been seeing. (#10625)

� Security Fixes

  • Proxy Handling: Fixed a no_proxy hostname normalisation bypass that could lead to Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF). (#10661)
  • Header Injection: Fixed an unrestricted cloud metadata exfiltration vulnerability via a header injection chain. (#10660)

� New Features

  • Runtime Support: Added compatibility checks and documentation for Deno and Bun environments. (#10652, #10653)

� Maintenance & Chores

  • CI Security: Hardened workflow permissions to least privilege, added the zizmor security scanner, pinned action versions, and gated npm publishing with OIDC and environment protection. (#10618, #10619, #10627, #10637, #10666)
  • Dependencies: Bumped serialize-javascript, handlebars, picomatch, vite, and denoland/setup-deno to latest versions. Added a 7-day Dependabot cooldown period. (#10574, #10572, #10568, #10663, #10664, #10665, #10669, #10670, #10616)
  • Documentation: Unified docs, improved beforeRedirect credential leakage example, clarified withCredentials/withXSRFToken behaviour, HTTP/2 support notes, async/await timeout error handling, header case preservation, and various typo fixes. (#10649, #10624, #7452, #7471, #10654, #10644, #10589)
  • Housekeeping: Removed stale files, regenerated lockfile, and updated sponsor scripts and blocks. (#10584, #10650, #10582, #10640, #10659, #10668)
  • Tests: Added regression coverage for urlencoded Content-Type casing. (#10573)

� New Contributors

We are thrilled to welcome our new contributors. Thank you for helping improve Axios:

  • @​raashish1601 (#10573)
  • @​Kilros0817 (#10625)
  • @​ashstrc (#10624)
  • @​Abhi3975 (#10589)
  • @​theamodhshetty (#7452)

v1.14.0

This release focuses on compatibility fixes, adapter stability improvements, and test/tooling modernisation.

⚠️ Important Changes

  • Breaking Changes: None identified in this release.
  • Action Required: If you rely on env-based proxy behaviour or CJS resolution edge-cases, validate your integration after upgrade (notably proxy-from-env v2 alignment and main entry compatibility fix).

� New Features

  • Runtime Features: No new end-user features were introduced in this release.
  • Test Coverage Expansion: Added broader smoke/module test coverage for CJS and ESM package usage. (#7510)

� Bug Fixes

  • Headers: Trim trailing CRLF in normalised header values. (#7456)
  • HTTP/2: Close detached HTTP/2 sessions on timeout to avoid lingering sessions. (#7457)
  • Fetch Adapter: Cancel ReadableStream created during request-stream capability probing to prevent async resource leaks. (#7515)
  • Proxy Handling: Fixed env proxy behavior with proxy-from-env v2 usage. (#7499)

Description: ## � Installing Claude Code GitHub App  This PR adds a GitHub Actions workflow that enables Claude Code integration in our repository.  ### What is Claude Code?  [Claude Code](https://claude.com/claude-code) is an AI coding agent that can help with: - Bug fixes and improvements   - Documentation updates - Implementing new features - Code reviews and suggestions - Writing tests - And more!  ### How it works  Once this PR is merged, we'll be able to interact with Claude by mentioning @claude in a pull request or issue comment. Once the workflow is triggered, Claude will analyze the comment and surrounding context, and execute on the request in a GitHub action.  ### Important Notes  - **This workflow won't take effect until this PR is merged** - **@claude mentions won't work until after the merge is complete** - The workflow runs automatically whenever Claude is mentioned in PR or issue comments - Claude gets access to the entire PR or issue context including files, diffs, and previous comments  ### Security  - Our Anthropic API key is securely stored as a GitHub Actions secret - Only users with write access to the repository can trigger the workflow - All Claude runs are stored in the GitHub Actions run history - Claude's default tools are limited to reading/writing files and interacting with our repo by creating comments, branches, and commits. - We can add more allowed tools by adding them to the workflow file like:  ``` allowed_tools: Bash(npm install),Bash(npm run build),Bash(npm run lint),Bash(npm run test) ```  There's more information in the [Claude Code action repo](https://github.com/anthropics/claude-code-action).  After merging this PR, let's try mentioning @claude in a comment on any PR to get started!

Description: ## Description  Adds a `Fallback` component that renders `null` and wires it up in two places: 1. **`layout.tsx`** -- used as the fallback content inside `<div id="root">` when `shouldRenderChildren` is false (prerendered/SSR pages before hydration). 2. **`router.tsx`** -- set as `HydrateFallback` on the root route object, so React Router does not render its own default fallback during hydration.  The fallback component is in a `.ts` file (not `.tsx`) to avoid the build emitting a `.jsx` output, which would break the SDK packaging.  Also fixes a missing guide version update in the deployment docs.  ## Type of change  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** - [x] **� Bug fix** - [ ] **� New/improved feature** - [ ] **� Breaking change**  ## Checklist  - [ ] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change.   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.

Description: ### Summary  The `axios` npm package was compromised on March 31, 2026 in a supply chain attack attributed to a North Korean state actor (Sapphire Sleet / UNC1069). Wasp's generated SDK declares `axios: "^1.4.0"` as a dependency, which means any Wasp project running `npm install` without a lockfile (or with a stale lockfile) during the ~3 hour attack window could have resolved to the compromised `axios@1.14.1`.  The malicious versions (1.14.1 and 0.30.4) injected a hidden dependency (`plain-crypto-js`) that deployed a cross-platform RAT via a postinstall hook. The RAT self-deletes after execution, making post-infection detection difficult.  ### Where axios appears in Wasp  The following generated files declare `axios: "^1.4.0"` with a caret range:  - `.wasp/out/sdk/wasp/package.json` - `.wasp/out/web-app/package.json` - `.wasp/build/sdk/wasp/package.json` - `.wasp/build/web-app/package.json`  These are framework-generated — users cannot modify them without the changes being overwritten on the next `wasp build` or `wasp start`.  ### Current workaround  Users can add an override in their root `package.json`:  ```json {   "overrides": {     "axios": "1.14.0"   } } ```  This works but is fragile — users have to know about it, and it could conflict with future Wasp releases that depend on axios features from newer versions.  ### Request  One or more of the following:  1. **Immediate**: Pin axios to `1.14.0` (exact, no caret) in Wasp's generated SDK package manifests to prevent any project from resolving to a compromised version.  2. **Short-term**: Evaluate whether the Wasp SDK's use of axios can be replaced with Node.js native `fetch` (available since Node 18, which Wasp already requires). The SDK appears to use axios primarily for the API client that connects the React frontend to the Express backend. Modern browsers and Node both support fetch natively.  3. **Documentation**: Add a note to Wasp's security docs or changelog advising users to add the override if they haven't updated yet.  ### References  - [[Microsoft Threat Intelligence advisory](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2026/04/01/mitigating-the-axios-npm-supply-chain-compromise/)](https://www.microsoft.com/en-us/security/blog/2026/04/01/mitigating-the-axios-npm-supply-chain-compromise/) - [[Google Threat Intelligence Group attribution](https://cloud.google.com/blog/topics/threat-intelligence/north-korea-threat-actor-targets-axios-npm-package)](https://cloud.google.com/blog/topics/threat-intelligence/north-korea-threat-actor-targets-axios-npm-package) - [[Elastic Security Labs full analysis](https://www.elastic.co/security-labs/axios-one-rat-to-rule-them-all)](https://www.elastic.co/security-labs/axios-one-rat-to-rule-them-all) - [[Socket.dev initial disclosure](https://socket.dev/blog/axios-npm-package-compromised)](https://socket.dev/blog/axios-npm-package-compromised) - [[Huntress incident timeline](https://www.huntress.com/blog/supply-chain-compromise-axios-npm-package)](https://www.huntress.com/blog/supply-chain-compromise-axios-npm-package)  ### Environment  - Wasp version: (check with `wasp version`) - Installed axios version before workaround: 1.13.2 (resolved via `^1.4.0`) - Compromised versions: 1.14.1 and 0.30.4
Discuss Information: Hi, thanks for the report! We're already addressing this issue at multiple levels: - On Axios especifically, we're looking to migrate off it (#2935) and already working on it - In general for the future, we've just merged a common mitigation to not install packages newer than 7 days (#3997)  There's no need for immediate mitigation anymore since action was taken from npm to remove the bad package from the registry.  (Also the workaround you mention is not complete, you'd also need to [set the `wasp.overriddenDeps` field](https://wasp.sh/docs/project/dependencies#overriding-wasps-dependencies-advanced))  I will close this issue since we consider the existing issues already handle the problem. Feel free to comment or reopen if we missed something.

Description:
Bumps [lodash](https://github.com/lodash/lodash) from 4.17.23 to 4.18.1.
Release notes

Sourced from lodash's releases.

4.18.1

Bugs

Fixes a ReferenceError issue in lodash lodash-es lodash-amd and lodash.template when using the template and fromPairs functions from the modular builds. See lodash/lodash#6167

These defects were related to how lodash distributions are built from the main branch using https://github.com/lodash-archive/lodash-cli. When internal dependencies change inside lodash functions, equivalent updates need to be made to a mapping in the lodash-cli. (hey, it was ahead of its time once upon a time!). We know this, but we missed it in the last release. It's the kind of thing that passes in CI, but fails bc the build is not the same thing you tested.

There is no diff on main for this, but you can see the diffs for each of the npm packages on their respective branches:

  • lodash: https://github.com/lodash/lodash/compare/4.18.0-npm...4.18.1-npm
  • lodash-es: https://github.com/lodash/lodash/compare/4.18.0-es...4.18.1-es
  • lodash-amd: https://github.com/lodash/lodash/compare/4.18.0-amd...4.18.1-amd
  • lodash.templatehttps://github.com/lodash/lodash/compare/4.18.0-npm-packages...4.18.1-npm-packages

4.18.0

v4.18.0

Full Changelog: https://github.com/lodash/lodash/compare/4.17.23...4.18.0

Security

_.unset / _.omit: Fixed prototype pollution via constructor/prototype path traversal (GHSA-f23m-r3pf-42rh, fe8d32e). Previously, array-wrapped path segments and primitive roots could bypass the existing guards, allowing deletion of properties from built-in prototypes. Now constructor and prototype are blocked unconditionally as non-terminal path keys, matching baseSet. Calls that previously returned true and deleted the property now return false and leave the target untouched.

_.template: Fixed code injection via imports keys (GHSA-r5fr-rjxr-66jc, CVE-2026-4800, 879aaa9). Fixes an incomplete patch for CVE-2021-23337. The variable option was validated against reForbiddenIdentifierChars but importsKeys was left unguarded, allowing code injection via the same Function() constructor sink. imports keys containing forbidden identifier characters now throw "Invalid imports option passed into _.template".

Docs

  • Add security notice for _.template in threat model and API docs (#6099)
  • Document lower > upper behavior in _.random (#6115)
  • Fix quotes in _.compact jsdoc (#6090)

lodash.* modular packages

Diff

We have also regenerated and published a select number of the lodash.* modular packages.

These modular packages had fallen out of sync significantly from the minor/patch updates to lodash. Specifically, we have brought the following packages up to parity w/ the latest lodash release because they have had CVEs on them in the past:

  • lodash.orderby
  • lodash.tonumber
  • lodash.trim
  • lodash.trimend
  • lodash.sortedindexby
  • lodash.zipobjectdeep
  • lodash.unset
  • lodash.omit
  • lodash.template

Description: ## Description  Adds `min-release-age=7` to `.npmrc` files across the monorepo and starter templates. This leverages the npm 11.10+ config option that prevents installing packages published less than 7 days ago, mitigating supply chain attacks where malicious packages are typically detected and removed within hours of publication.  I added the setting both to our own packages, to protect us while developing, and to the skeleton starter, to protect our users by default.  - Closes #3997 - Depends on #3996  ## Type of change  - [x] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  - [x] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [x] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [x] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.

Description: ## Description  Upgrades TypeScript from 5.9.3 to 6.0.2 across the entire monorepo, addressing all TS 6.0 breaking changes and deprecations. Fixed 4 extensionless imports in the deploy package. - **`baseUrl` deprecated**: Added `"ignoreDeprecations": "6.0"` to web/docs tsconfig (upstream `@docusaurus/tsconfig` issue). - **Peer dep conflict**: Bumped `tsdown` from `^0.20.x` to `^0.21.7` for TS 6 compatibility.  All 910 e2e snapshot tests pass. All TS package type-checks and unit tests pass. Description:
Bumps [lodash](https://github.com/lodash/lodash) from 4.17.21 to 4.18.1.
Release notes

Sourced from lodash's releases.

4.18.1

Bugs

Fixes a ReferenceError issue in lodash lodash-es lodash-amd and lodash.template when using the template and fromPairs functions from the modular builds. See lodash/lodash#6167

These defects were related to how lodash distributions are built from the main branch using https://github.com/lodash-archive/lodash-cli. When internal dependencies change inside lodash functions, equivalent updates need to be made to a mapping in the lodash-cli. (hey, it was ahead of its time once upon a time!). We know this, but we missed it in the last release. It's the kind of thing that passes in CI, but fails bc the build is not the same thing you tested.

There is no diff on main for this, but you can see the diffs for each of the npm packages on their respective branches:

  • lodash: https://github.com/lodash/lodash/compare/4.18.0-npm...4.18.1-npm
  • lodash-es: https://github.com/lodash/lodash/compare/4.18.0-es...4.18.1-es
  • lodash-amd: https://github.com/lodash/lodash/compare/4.18.0-amd...4.18.1-amd
  • lodash.templatehttps://github.com/lodash/lodash/compare/4.18.0-npm-packages...4.18.1-npm-packages

4.18.0

v4.18.0

Full Changelog: https://github.com/lodash/lodash/compare/4.17.23...4.18.0

Security

_.unset / _.omit: Fixed prototype pollution via constructor/prototype path traversal (GHSA-f23m-r3pf-42rh, fe8d32e). Previously, array-wrapped path segments and primitive roots could bypass the existing guards, allowing deletion of properties from built-in prototypes. Now constructor and prototype are blocked unconditionally as non-terminal path keys, matching baseSet. Calls that previously returned true and deleted the property now return false and leave the target untouched.

_.template: Fixed code injection via imports keys (GHSA-r5fr-rjxr-66jc, CVE-2026-4800, 879aaa9). Fixes an incomplete patch for CVE-2021-23337. The variable option was validated against reForbiddenIdentifierChars but importsKeys was left unguarded, allowing code injection via the same Function() constructor sink. imports keys containing forbidden identifier characters now throw "Invalid imports option passed into _.template".

Docs

  • Add security notice for _.template in threat model and API docs (#6099)
  • Document lower > upper behavior in _.random (#6115)
  • Fix quotes in _.compact jsdoc (#6090)

lodash.* modular packages

Diff

We have also regenerated and published a select number of the lodash.* modular packages.

These modular packages had fallen out of sync significantly from the minor/patch updates to lodash. Specifically, we have brought the following packages up to parity w/ the latest lodash release because they have had CVEs on them in the past:

  • lodash.orderby
  • lodash.tonumber
  • lodash.trim
  • lodash.trimend
  • lodash.sortedindexby
  • lodash.zipobjectdeep
  • lodash.unset
  • lodash.omit
  • lodash.template
Commits
  • cb0b9b9 release(patch): bump main to 4.18.1 (#6177)
  • 75535f5 chore: prune stale advisory refs (#6170)
  • 62e91bc docs: remove n_ Node.js < 6 REPL note from README (#6165)
  • 59be2de release(minor): bump to 4.18.0 (#6161)
  • af63457 fix: broken tests for _.template 879aaa9
  • 1073a76 fix: linting issues
  • 879aaa9 fix: validate imports keys in _.template
  • fe8d32e fix: block prototype pollution in baseUnset via constructor/prototype traversal
  • 18ba0a3 refactor(fromPairs): use baseAssignValue for consistent assignment (#6153)
  • b819080 ci: add dist sync validation workflow (#6137)
  • Additional commits viewable in compare view

Description: ## Description  Convert the formatting CI workflow from check-only to auto-fix. Instead of failing the build when formatting issues are found, the workflow now runs the formatters in write/inplace mode, and if changes are detected on a PR, it commits and pushes the fixes automatically.  The three separate jobs (Prettier, Ormolu, cabal-gild) are merged into a single job so the fixes can be collected and committed together.  We only autofix PRs, in order not to create extra commits when pushing directly to `main` or `release`.  GitHub will not re-trigger workflows on pushes made by `github-actions[bot]`, so the formatting commit will not cause an infinite loop or invalidate the already-running CI.   The branch also included intentional formatting issues in `package.json`, `Wasp/Psl/Db.hs`, and `waspc.cabal` to verify the autofix works end-to-end. See [this commit](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/4012/commits/d734db2ac9c9903d855ab2374a43f44e07874024) for an example of how it was autofixed.  ## Type of change  - [x] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty. Confidence Score: 3/5

Safe to merge for same-repo PRs and push events, but will error on any fork PR that has formatting issues — should be fixed before merging to avoid breaking the CI experience for external contributors. The core mechanism (format-in-place → detect changes → commit/push) is sound and well-structured. The main gap is the missing fork-PR guard: because `github.event_name == 'pull_request'` is true for fork PRs too, the push step will fail with a permissions error for any fork contributor whose code has formatting issues. This is a real, reproducible failure that affects an important class of contributors, warranting a score below 4. `.github/workflows/ci-formatting.yaml` — specifically the "Commit and push changes" and "Fail if changes were detected" steps need a fork-PR guard. Confidence Score: 3/5

Merging is risky until `node:24.14.1-alpine3.23` is confirmed to exist on Docker Hub — if it does not, every Wasp app's production Docker build will fail. The Docker image tag concern is a P1 that would silently break all Wasp user deployments if the tag is missing. All other changes are mechanical version bumps that appear correct and consistent. If the Docker tag is verified to exist, confidence rises to 5/5. waspc/data/Generator/templates/Dockerfile — verify that `node:24.14.1-alpine3.23` is a published tag on Docker Hub

Comments Outside Diff (1)

 1. `waspc/data/Generator/templates/Dockerfile`, line 6 ([link](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/697aa8e9ac522332517aa395f3f05fbc4020e509/waspc/data/Generator/templates/Dockerfile#L6)) **`node:24.14.1-alpine3.23` tag may not exist on Docker Hub** The template generates `node:24.14.1-alpine3.23` for every Wasp app's production Dockerfile. Node.js Docker images are published with the Alpine version available at the time of their release — if Node 24.14.1 ships against Alpine 3.21 or 3.22 rather than 3.23, this tag will not exist and `docker pull`/`docker build` will fail for all Wasp apps on every deploy. The e2e golden-file tests do not run `docker build`, so they wouldn't catch this. Worth explicitly verifying the tag exists on Docker Hub before merging.

Description: ## Description  Replaces the Axios in the SDK's `api` module with a [ky](https://github.com/sindresorhus/ky) instance. This gives us a cleaner, more ergonomic HTTP client with built-in JSON handling, method shortcuts, hooks for interceptors, and automatic error throwing -- all built on top of the modern `fetch` instead of the older XHR.  The `api` export from `wasp/client/api` is now a `ky.extend()` instance configured with: - `prefixUrl` for the API base URL (replaces the manual `toAbsoluteUrl` helper) - `beforeRequest` hook for adding the Authorization header - `afterResponse` hook for 401 session invalidation (with the existing race-condition prevention) - Native ky `HTTPError` for non-2xx responses (replaces the custom `WaspHttpError` class) This all mirrors what we had for Axios.  All internal callers are updated to use ky's ergonomic API: `api.post('/path', { json: data }).json(ResponseSchema)`.  - Fixes #2935  ## Type of change  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** - [ ] **� Bug fix** - [ ] **� New/improved feature** - [x] **� Breaking change**  ## Checklist  - [x] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change.   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [x] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [x] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [x] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [x] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.

Description: <!--   Thanks for contributing to Wasp!   Make sure to follow this PR template, so that we can speed up the review process.   It will also help you not forget important steps when making a change.   If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  ## Description  Fixes some leftover stuff me and Filip discussed.  - Renamed `fromVersion` to `versionToPartialVersion`. - `Range` is now a non-empty list instead of a list. - Removed `Comparator` as concept, since it wasn't a proper `Comparator`. Same for `ComparatorSet` (since it had non-comparator items). Now instead we have a `RangeExpression` which can be either a `HyphenRange` or set of `SimpleRangeExpression`s, closely following the grammar (whose our models are based upon).    As such `Comparator.hs` and `ComparatorSet.hs` are merged into `RangeExpression.hs`.   So the current naming is:  `Range` - non empty list of `RangeExpression`   `RangeExpression` - either:   - non empty list of `SimpleRangeExpression`  - `HyphenRange`  `SimpleRangeExpression` - either:  - `Primitive` (>, <, =, >=, <=)  - `Caret Range` (^)  - `Tilde Range` (~)   Ideas for the future: Currently we depend on `VersionBounds` to translate our "range grammar" models into actual version ranges. While this works (we get the same end result) maybe we can changes to be more in-line with `node-semver`. Instead of `VersionBounds` I would make a type class which transforms grammar models into comparators and then changes the versioning logic to work with comparators as base units (the same way `node-semver` does).  So external modules would still work with `Range` (the rich model) but under the hood we would do the `Comparator` operations. We keep the nice API, while hiding the `Comparator` stuff behind. And if users want to use `Comparator`s directly, they would be able to (now they can't).   ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [x] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty.   If you're unsure about any of them, don't hesitate to ask for help. -->  - [ ] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.  <!--   Bumping the version on `waspc/waspc.cabal`:    We still haven't reached 1.0, so the version bumping follows these rules:     - Bug fix:            0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - New feature:        0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - Breaking change:    0.+1.0    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.17.0)    If the version has already been bumped on `main` since the last release, skip this. -->

Description: ## Description  Converts 106 manually duplicated JS/TS tab code blocks across 23 doc files to use the `auto-js` remark plugin, removing ~3,000 lines of duplicate code. The `auto-js` plugin automatically generates JavaScript versions from TypeScript code by stripping type annotations, so maintaining both versions manually is unnecessary.  Three types of conversions were made: - **Single-block tabs** where JS and TS code differed only in type annotations were replaced with a single `auto-js` TS block. - **Multi-block tabs** containing identical wasp/prisma blocks alongside JS/TS code had the identical blocks extracted outside the tabs and the JS/TS code converted to `auto-js`. - Where TS used value imports for type-only symbols (e.g. `import { ServerSetupFn }`), these were updated to `import type` syntax so `auto-js` correctly strips them.  37 tab blocks remain where JS and TS versions intentionally differ (extra type documentation, different code structure, or tutorial-specific notes).  ## Type of change  - Description: ## Description  Remove the `axios` dependency from the SDK and replace it with a native `fetch`-based `api` function. The new `api(input, init?)` function takes the same parameters as the `Request` constructor, prepends the API base URL for relative paths, adds Bearer auth headers, handles 401 session invalidation (with the same race-condition protection), and throws `WaspHttpError` on non-2xx responses — merging the old `handleApiError` logic directly into the function. Description:
Bumps [node-forge](https://github.com/digitalbazaar/forge) from 1.3.1 to 1.4.0.
Changelog

Sourced from node-forge's changelog.

1.4.0 - 2026-03-24

Security

  • HIGH: Denial of Service in BigInteger.modInverse()
    • A Denial of Service (DoS) vulnerability exists due to an infinite loop in the BigInteger.modInverse() function (inherited from the bundled jsbn library). When modInverse() is called with a zero value as input, the internal Extended Euclidean Algorithm enters an unreachable exit condition, causing the process to hang indefinitely and consume 100% CPU.
    • Reported by Kr0emer.
    • CVE ID: CVE-2026-33891
    • GHSA ID: GHSA-5gfm-wpxj-wjgq
  • HIGH: Signature forgery in RSA-PKCS due to ASN.1 extra field.
    • RSASSA PKCS#1 v1.5 signature verification accepts forged signatures for low public exponent keys (e=3). Attackers can forge signatures by stuffing "garbage" bytes within the ASN.1 structure in order to construct a signature that passes verification, enabling Bleichenbacher style forgery. This issue is similar to CVE-2022-24771, but adds bytes in an addition field within the ASN.1 structure, rather than outside of it.
    • Additionally, forge does not validate that signatures include a minimum of 8 bytes of padding as defined by the specification, providing attackers additional space to construct Bleichenbacher forgeries.
    • Reported as part of a U.C. Berkeley security research project by:
      • Austin Chu, Sohee Kim, and Corban Villa.
    • CVE ID: CVE-2026-33894
    • GHSA ID: GHSA-ppp5-5v6c-4jwp
  • HIGH: Signature forgery in Ed25519 due to missing S < L check.
    • Ed25519 signature verification accepts forged non-canonical signatures where the scalar S is not reduced modulo the group order (S >= L). A valid signature and its S + L variant both verify in forge, while Node.js crypto.verify (OpenSSL-backed) rejects the S + L variant, as defined by the specification. This class of signature malleability has been exploited in practice to bypass authentication and authorization logic (see CVE-2026-25793, CVE-2022-35961). Applications relying on signature uniqueness (i.e., dedup by signature bytes, replay tracking, signed-object canonicalization checks) may be bypassed.
    • Reported as part of a U.C. Berkeley security research project by:
      • Austin Chu, Sohee Kim, and Corban Villa.
    • CVE ID: CVE-2026-33895
    • GHSA ID: GHSA-q67f-28xg-22rw
  • HIGH: basicConstraints bypass in certificate chain verification.
    • pki.verifyCertificateChain() does not enforce RFC 5280 basicConstraints requirements when an intermediate certificate lacks both the basicConstraints and keyUsage extensions. This allows any leaf certificate (without these extensions) to act as a CA and sign other certificates, which node-forge will accept as valid.
    • Reported by Doruk Tan Ozturk (@​peaktwilight) - doruk.ch
    • CVE ID: CVE-2026-33896
    • GHSA ID: GHSA-2328-f5f3-gj25

Description: ## Context  We currently only have a single passing mention of Supabase in the docs, at the bottom of the [self-hosted deployment guide](https://wasp.sh/docs/deployment/deployment-methods/self-hosted#database-setup). Given that the Wasp + Supabase combo is popular and we now have a guides section, we should write a dedicated guide.  ## Important security note to include  When using Supabase with Wasp (or any ORM like Prisma/Drizzle), users should be instructed to **turn off the Supabase Data API**. ORMs automatically disable Row Level Security (RLS) whenever migrations are run, which leaves all tables completely open via the Data API. This is a side effect of how Supabase interacts with ORMs rather than a Wasp-specific issue, but it's important to document since users following our guide could unknowingly expose their data.  ## Tasks  - [ ] Write a guide covering how to use Supabase as a database provider with Wasp - [ ] Include a prominent warning about disabling the Data API when using Supabase with an ORM

## Context  In #3855, `initializeQueryClient()` is called as a top-level side effect in `routes.tsx` ([discussion](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3855#discussion_r2958869436)). This was done because `routes.tsx` is the top-most file common to both SSR and client entry points. However, executing setup logic in a routes file is a misplaced responsibility.  ## Current behavior  `initializeQueryClient()` is called in `waspc/data/Generator/templates/sdk/wasp/client/vite/virtual-files/files/routes.tsx` (line 53), as an imperative side effect alongside the user's `setupFn` call:  ```tsx // routes.tsx await setupFn() initializeQueryClient() ```  The function itself lives in `sdk/wasp/client/operations/queryClient.ts` and creates a `QueryClient` instance, resolving a promise that the rest of the framework awaits.  ## Proposed change  Move the initialization to be a top-level `await` inside `queryClient.ts` itself, so the module is self-initializing and `routes.tsx` doesn't need to orchestrate unrelated setup:  ```ts // queryClient.ts — self-initializes on import const queryClient = new QueryClient(queryClientConfig ?? defaultQueryClientConfig) ```  This would remove the action-at-a-distance pattern and keep query client concerns co-located. The routes file should only be responsible for defining route objects.  Alternatively, we could export the Promise, initialize it in a Suspense, and then make it available through a Context.  ### Things to consider - `configureQueryClient()` (public API) must still be callable _before_ the client is created — the initialization order needs to be preserved. - Both SSR and client entry points must still get a properly initialized client.  ## References  - PR: #3855 - Discussion: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3855#discussion_r2958869436

## Description  The `wasp deploy railway launch` command frequently fails due to transient Railway API timeouts (30s GraphQL timeout at `backboard.railway.com`). This affects both CI (~28% failure rate) and end users. The failures are caused by intermittent Railway platform instability, not by bugs in Wasp.  This PR adds automatic retry logic (3 attempts) to the deploy package so that transient errors are retried transparently. Each provider defines its own predicate for classifying which errors are retryable.  Closes #3566  ## Type of change  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** - [x] **� Bug fix** - [ ] **� New/improved feature** - [ ] **� Breaking change**  ## Checklist  - [x] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [x] I added **unit tests** for my change.   - [x] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.

_commit: 576a70d_ |

Greptile Summary

This PR adds a generic `retryOnTransientError` utility and a Railway-specific error predicate (`isRailwayTransientError`) to automatically retry `wasp deploy railway` commands when they encounter transient Railway API failures (timeouts, connection errors, rate limiting, 5xx upload errors). The retry logic (3 attempts by default) is applied to the `setup` and `deploy` steps both in the standalone commands and inside the `launch` command, without double-wrapping. **Key changes:** - `src/common/retry.ts`: New generic retry loop with configurable `maxAttempts`, `isRetryable` predicate, and `retryDescription` for user-facing messages. - `src/providers/railway/transientErrors.ts`: Railway-specific predicate that classifies `ProcessOutput` errors from the `zx` library by inspecting `stderr` for known transient patterns. - `launch.ts` / `index.ts`: Retry wrapping applied to each individual step; the `launch` command's action is not additionally wrapped since `launchFn` handles retries internally. - Tests cover all success and failure paths for both the retry utility and the error predicate. **Notable concern:** - There is no delay between retry attempts. For rate-limiting errors in particular, immediately re-issuing the same request after receiving a "ratelimited" response is unlikely to succeed and could worsen the situation. A small fixed or exponential back-off (e.g. 2–5 s per attempt) would make the retry loop significantly more robust.

Confidence Score: 4/5

Safe to merge; the retry logic is correct and well-tested, with only a missing inter-attempt delay as a non-blocking improvement. The implementation is clean and correctly scoped — non-ProcessOutput errors are never retried, transient patterns are well-defined and tested, and there is no accidental double-retry in the launch flow. The sole meaningful gap is the absence of any sleep between attempts, which reduces effectiveness against rate limiting. This does not break the happy path and the PR is still a net improvement over the current behaviour (no retries at all). retry.ts — missing inter-attempt delay; all other files are clean.

Important Files Changed

| Filename | Overview | |----------|----------| | waspc/data/packages/deploy/src/common/retry.ts | New generic retry utility; logic is correct but has no delay between attempts, which reduces effectiveness against rate limiting. | | waspc/data/packages/deploy/src/providers/railway/transientErrors.ts | New Railway-specific error predicate; correctly restricts retries to ProcessOutput errors and well-defined transient patterns. | | waspc/data/packages/deploy/src/providers/railway/commands/launch/launch.ts | Wraps setup and deploy independently with retry; launch command action in index.ts is not double-wrapped, so retry counts are correct. | | waspc/data/packages/deploy/src/providers/railway/index.ts | Standalone setup and deploy commands correctly wrapped with retry at the action level; launch command delegates to launchFn which handles its own retries. | | waspc/data/packages/deploy/test/common/retry.test.ts | Thorough unit tests covering success, transient retry, non-retryable throw, exhausted attempts, custom maxAttempts, and bad input. | | waspc/data/packages/deploy/test/providers/railway/transientErrors.test.ts | Good coverage of all transient and permanent error patterns, including non-ProcessOutput inputs. |

Description: ## Description  When deploying with `wasp deploy fly launch <app-name>` where the app name contains `-client` or `-server` (e.g. `test-client-srr-ks`), the reported URL after deployment was wrong.  `inferClientBasename` and `inferServerBasename` used `String.replace("-client", "")` which strips the **first** occurrence instead of the suffix. For an app named `test-client-srr-ks`, the Fly client app `test-client-srr-ks-client` would have the wrong `-client` stripped, producing basename `test-srr-ks-client` instead of `test-client-srr-ks`, leading to the incorrect URL `https://test-srr-ks-client-client.fly.dev`.  Fixed by using a regex with `$` anchor so only the trailing suffix is stripped.  ## Type of change  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** - [x] **� Bug fix** - [ ] **� New/improved feature** - [ ] **� Breaking change**  ## Checklist  - [ ] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [x] I added **unit tests** for my change.   - [x] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [x] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.

Description: ## Description  Adds an optional `prerender` boolean field to the Route declaration type (`false` by default). When a user sets `prerender: true` on a route, that route's path is collected and passed to the Vite SSR plugin as a prerendering path (populating the previously empty `ssrPaths` array).  - Creates prerendering test pages in `kitchen-sink`, checking that the page is correctly prerendered and no hydration mismatches occur. - Adds prerendering of `/` to kitchen-sink, waspleau, and ask-the-documents (the ones that don't have an auth-required main page). - Change the lazy routes to use `React.lazy` instead of React Router's native lazy handling. [It has a bug](https://github.com/remix-run/react-router/issues/14955) that caused React to throw away the prerendering. - We tell the client pages if they were rendered as a fallback or not (so there are no hydration mismatches). This is done by adding a Wasp-controlled way of passing data from the prerendering to the rendering stage, similar to React Router's way. - Simplified the SSR entry by just deferring to the Layout component - Fixed a hydration mismatch in our WebSocket support  ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [x] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty. Cloudflare client deployment guide was also updated to workers from pages, as pages are deprecated.  Since cloudflare's default SPA mode is not good for us (it does not support pre-rendered pages, assumes everything is an SPA), we have to write the routing ourselves. Confidence Score: 4/5

Core code changes are correct and consistent; one targeted fix remains in the Cloudflare Workers documentation/workflow before it is fully reliable for all users. The fundamental fix (renaming the fallback to 200.html and updating all deployment targets) is solid. The Railway auto-deploy path is verified by the PR author on real infrastructure. The only concrete issue is the Cloudflare Workers GitHub Actions example missing the worker.js recreation step after npx vite build — this would cause CI-based Cloudflare deployments to fail. Description: ## Description  Fixes `sh: 1: wasp-config: Permission denied` error that consistently broke example e2e tests in CI.  ### Root Cause  `wasp-config` was invoked via `npx wasp-config`, which requires the bin entry point (`dist/src/run.js`) to have executable permissions. Two things conspired to strip those permissions:  1. **`cabal install` copies data files with 0644** regardless of source permissions, so the installed `run.js` is never executable. 2. **npm only chmod's bin files when it first creates the `.bin/` symlink**, not on subsequent installs. When `ts-setup` re-copies `.wasp/wasp-config/` but the `node_modules/.bin/wasp-config` symlink still exists, npm skips the chmod.  ### Fix  Invoke wasp-config via `node .wasp/wasp-config/dist/src/run.js` instead of `npx wasp-config`. This sidesteps the executable permission requirement entirely - `node` doesn't need the file to be executable.  A new function `getInstallablePackageScriptInProject` in `NodePackageFFI` resolves the path to an installable package's main script relative to the project root.  ## Type of change  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** - [x] **� Bug fix** - [ ] **� New/improved feature** - [ ] **� Breaking change**  ## Checklist  - [x] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change.   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog:   - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.

## Description  Simplifies the auth pills in the docs by replacing per-auth-method background colors with a cleaner design using `currentColor` for both text and border. This follows the design direction suggested by @infomiho in #2775:  - Pills use `currentColor` for text and border color, adapting naturally to light/dark mode - Removed all per-method CSS color variables (no more needing distinct colors for each auth method) - Reduced padding and font size for a less dominant appearance - Moved styles from inline to a CSS class for cleaner code  The result is a simpler, more maintainable pill component that works consistently across themes without requiring custom color combinations. Description: ## Description  Visual walkthrough of the full refactor: https://auth-refactor-wasp.static.miho.dev/  This is step 1 of a 4-PR stack that consolidates scattered auth provider definitions into a single registry.  **PR Stack:** #3951 (this) -> #3952 -> #3953 -> #3954  This PR introduces the foundation and proves the pattern on one real generator:  - **`Auth.Provider`**: `OAuthProviderSpec` record type + `allOAuthProviders` registry. Description: ## Description  The deploy package's `tsconfig.json` was missing an `include` field, so `tsc` tried to compile all `.ts` files (test files, `vitest.config.ts`). Since `rootDir` is `"src"` but test files live outside `src/`, this caused `TS6059` errors in CI.  This was not caught by CI because: - `packages-test` only runs vitest (which uses its own transpiler), not `tsc` - `waspc-build` runs `./run build` which does run `tsc` for each package, but the for-loop in `WASP_PACKAGES_COMPILE` silently swallowed subshell failures (deploy is first in the glob, so its failure was overwritten by later packages succeeding)  Fixes: 1. Add `"include": ["src"]` to the deploy tsconfig (matching other packages like `prisma`, `studio`, `ts-inspect`) 2. Add `|| exit 1` to the `WASP_PACKAGES_COMPILE` loop so `./run build` fails fast if any package build fails  ## Type of change  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** - [x] **� Bug fix** - [ ] **� New/improved feature** - [ ] **� Breaking change**  ## Checklist  - [x] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change.   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.

Description: ## Description  Now that we're about to start touching how we generate the web-app (#3891 or #3855), we should have visibility into how the output of Vite is changing.  Thus, this PR extends the wasp-build e2e snapshot test to run `npx vite build` on the generated project and capture the build output as snapshots. This allows us to detect unwanted changes to the Vite build output (bundled code, CSS, HTML structure) in future releases.  We wrap the default Vite config with a `mergeConfig` that modifies some build settings to be easier to analyze and compare: no minification, no hash-based filenames, and a plugin that externalizes node_modules imports so the snapshots focus on app code changes.  ## Type of change  - [x] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  - [ ] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.

Description: ## Description  - Related #3927   > [!IMPORTANT] > For an overview of what is Mise, how it works and how it (doesn't) change our local environments, I made this small write-up: https://gist.github.com/cprecioso/a5cdae82d31dfe3561c7db1c49df594c  Introduce [mise](https://mise.jdx.dev/) as the version manager for formatting tools (ormolu and cabal-gild), replacing the previous approach of building them from source via `dev-tool.project` and using third-party GitHub Actions. This gives a single source of truth for tool versions (`mise.toml`), faster CI runs (pre-built binaries instead of Cabal builds), and a simpler local developer experience (run through `mise` instead of `install_dev_tool`/`dev_tool_path`).  Changes: - Add `mise.toml` and `mise.lock` at the repo root pinning ormolu and cabal-gild versions. - Update `waspc/run` to invoke tools via `mise x` instead of building them from `dev-tool.project`. - Remove ormolu and cabal-gild version constraints from `dev-tool.project`. - Replace `haskell-actions/run-ormolu` and `tfausak/cabal-gild-setup-action` in CI with `jdx/mise-action`. _commit: ade7b29_ | > Would love if it was explained how some packages got version restrictions? Basically, we had this situation: ```cabal lib my-lib: ... random-dep ^>= 4 -- this _is not_ used here test-suite my-lib-tests: ... my-lib, random-dep -- this _is_ used here -- we don't set a version because we depend on `my-lib` so we inherit theirs ``` So when I removed the dep from `my-lib`, I had to move their version specifier to that same dep in `my-lib-tests`, in order to not lose any version constraint.

Description: ## Description  Replaces the two-step TypeScript compiler + manual asset copy process with tsdown, a modern bundler-less build tool powered by Rolldown. This resolves #3121 by eliminating the copy-assets.js workaround.  ## Key Changes  - **Removed:** `copy-assets.js` script and its npm postbuild hook - **Added:** `tsdown.config.ts` with asset externalization via custom Rolldown plugin - **Added:** `tsconfig.build.json` for tsdown-specific TypeScript configuration - **Updated:** Package.json exports to use `.js`/`.d.ts` extensions (tsdown defaults to `.mjs`) - **Updated:** Virtual-files imports to use `new URL()` constructs for static asset resolution  CSS, SVG, and image imports are now externalized and copied to `dist/` via build hooks, allowing Vite to process them at runtime. Description: ## Description  Closes #3512  - Instead of maintaining a hardcoded enum of allowed models that needs updating every time OpenAI releases new ones, Wasp AI now accepts any model name string and lets the OpenAI API validate it. The interactive CLI still offers curated GPT-5 defaults for users who don't want to look up model names.  - The CLI model selection was changed from a selection to free text input. Users can press Enter to accept the defaults (`gpt-5` for planning, `gpt-5-mini` for coding) or type any model name.  - The creativity level feature in Mage mapped to OpenAI's temperature parameter, but GPT-5 reasoning models reject custom temperature values. Rather than adding model-specific workarounds, we removed the feature since it no longer provides meaningful control over output quality with current models. Historical creativity level data is preserved and visible in admin stats and user pages.  - Mage now explicitly defines its models and pricing in a single config file (`src/config/aiModels.ts`), which is used by both the server (to pass model names to `wasp-cli`) and the client (to estimate generation cost).  ## Type of change  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** - [ ] **� Bug fix** - [x] **� New/improved feature** - [ ] **� Breaking change**  ## Checklist  - [x] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change.   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [x] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog:   - [x] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.
