Description: ## Description  Add two new command-line flags to wasp-app-runner for automated testing and CI workflows:  - **`--run <cmd>`**: Waits for both client (port 3000) and server (port 3001) to be ready, then executes the given command with `$client_url` and `$server_url` environment variables set. - **`--exit`**: Exits the process after both servers are ready (and after the `--run` command completes, if provided).  This allows us to do commands such as  ```sh $ run-wasp-app build --run 'npx lighthouse $client_url' --exit ```  ## Type of change  - [x] **� New/improved feature** - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** - [ ] **� Bug fix** - [ ] **� Breaking change**  ## Checklist  - [ ] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change.   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog:   - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.

Description: ## Goal  Integrate the SSR machinery proven in the spike (#3881) into the Wasp compiler, without any per-page opt-in yet.  ## How  The prerendering solution from #3881 (PR #3855) can be wired into the Wasp build pipeline with no behavior change by not passing any specific page paths. This means:  - The build pipeline runs the prerender step, but only generates the fallback/shell page (`_fallback.html`) - No pages are individually prerendered yet — that comes in #3882 - The production server is tested to confirm it correctly routes to the fallback page  ## Why this step matters  This is a low-risk integration point: it validates the end-to-end pipeline (compiler → build → prerender → serve) without requiring any user-facing config changes. If the shell prerendering works correctly, per-page opt-in (#3882) can be layered on top.
Discuss Information: Done in https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3855

_commit: 3627597_ | Can see how it looks like in the CI

Description:
- Fixes #3892 ## Description Converts the react-router route generation from eager imports to lazy-loaded routes using react-router's `lazy` property. Each page component now becomes its own code-split chunk, loaded on-demand as users navigate instead of being bundled upfront. This is controlled by a new `lazy: ` field on the `route` declaration. It is optional and `true` by default. I documented this in a new advanced routing page in the docs, and added some advanced features from RR we already support, so it's not so empty. --- The result is reducing the initial load time of Wasp applications, especially those with many pages.
Example from OpenSaaS.sh (this is not an ideal app as it doesn't have many pages, so the savings are modest)
Before After
Lighthouse
Network tab - 8 / 37 requests - 551 kB / 2,713 kB transferred - 1,514 kB / 3,722 kB resources - Finish: 439 ms - DOMContentLoaded: 284 ms - Load: 288 ms - 12 / 41 requests - 271 kB / 2,491 kB transferred - 856 kB / 3,077 kB resources - Finish: 159 ms - DOMContentLoaded: 64 ms - Load: 67 ms
_(In the `X / Y` data points, `X` is everything except images, and `Y` is everything including images.)_
Example from community member app (56 pages)

Lighthouse

Description: - Resolves https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3625  ## Description  We've found differences between the in-tree Wasp version and the npm-packaged one (https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3870). But we already test the in-tree wasp binaries in the build workflows. Running these same tests on the npm-packaged binaries would be wasteful, and would make a big part of our CI wait on one of our longest jobs instead of parallelizing.  Instead, I opted for a simpler solution where npm-package tests get tested in two ways: - (Already existed) Installing the npm package and calling `wasp version` -> Checks that the binary is included and works. - (New) Compares that the list of data files of installed npm packages are the same as they should be according to the `waspc.cabal` file.  With these two tests, we can keep testing with the in-tree Wasp, because we're just ensuring that the binary works and that it will find the same files in the same places.  ## Changes  Adds a step in the Wasp build workflow that outputs a sorted list of all data files with per-file SHA-256 hashes by running `cabal sdist --list-only` and computing a hash for each file. This list is passed to the npm package test workflow, which verifies that each platform-specific npm package's `./data` folder contains exactly the expected files with matching content hashes.  This ensures consistency between what Cabal considers part of the source distribution and what actually ends up in the npm packages, catching any mismatches in file inclusion early.  ## Type of change  - [x] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** - [ ] **� New/improved feature** - [ ] **� Breaking change**  ## Checklist  - [ ] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change.   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog:   - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.

_commit: ad99dd6_ | ## Lazy Types with Declaration Merging ### How we fetch user types Wasp uses TypeScript module augmentation to bring user-defined types into the SDK. Empty registry interfaces are declared in `wasp/types`, and generated `declare module` blocks merge user types into them at build time: ```typescript // SDK: empty registry export interface CrudOverridesRegistry {} // Generated augmentation (from user's code): declare module 'wasp/types' { interface CrudOverridesRegistry { 'tasks': { GetAll: typeof import('./tasks').getAllOverride } } } ``` Conditional types resolve the override or fall back to a default: ```typescript type GetAllQueryResolved = FromCrudOverridesRegistry<'tasks', 'GetAll', DefaultGetAllQuery> // → user's override if augmented, DefaultGetAllQuery otherwise ``` ### Why types must stay lazy The SDK is compiled with `tsc` to produce `.d.ts` declaration files, which consumer code (user's app) then imports. TypeScript's declaration emitter handles inferred vs annotated types differently: **With explicit annotation** — the emitted `.d.ts` preserves the type alias: ```typescript // Source const _getAllQuery: QueryFor = createQuery(...) // Emitted .d.ts declare const _getAllQuery: QueryFor; ``` The consumer resolves `GetAllQueryResolved` against the augmented `CrudOverridesRegistry`, picking up the user's override. **Without annotation** — the emitter expands the type into its full structural form: ```typescript // Source const _getAllQuery = createQuery(...) // Emitted .d.ts (conditionals already resolved to defaults) declare const _getAllQuery: { (args: {}): Promise<{ id: number; description: string; userId: number }[]>; queryCacheKey: string[]; }; ``` The declaration emitter asks the type checker for the fully inferred type of the variable. Since the variable is unannotated, the checker evaluates the conditional using only the types visible during SDK compilation. The override is lost — the consumer sees only the default Prisma types. Note: source type-checking (`tsc --noEmit`) works correctly in both cases — the issue is specifically in declaration emit. The SDK builds first, then the consumer imports the emitted `.d.ts` files. This two-phase compilation is why the timing of type resolution matters. ### Two approaches to lazy types **Generic function defaults** (TanStack Router's approach): ```typescript function useLoaderData() { ... } ``` Generics must remain abstract in emitted declarations, so the emitter preserves them rather than resolving them. Each call site resolves the default against its own augmentation context. This pattern works naturally for APIs that resolve types at function call sites, but not for stored values. **Explicit type annotations** (Wasp's approach): Wasp exposes value-based APIs — `env.MY_VAR`, `tasks.get.query`, `dbClient` — where types must be stored in variables. Explicit annotations force the declaration emitter to preserve the type alias references rather than expanding them: ```typescript const _getQuery: QueryFor = createQuery(...) export const env: ClientEnv = _env export const dbClient: UserPrismaClient = setupFn() ``` Even when the annotation appears redundant (e.g., `createQuery` returns `QueryFor`), removing it causes the declaration emitter to expand the type, breaking override resolution for consumers. Since all annotations live in generated template code, this cost is invisible to users. | Mostly copied to other two PRs: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/4049 https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/4067

Description: ## Description  The skeleton template's `.gitignore` file was being stripped by npm during `npm publish`, preventing new Wasp projects from getting the `.gitignore` file. This happened because npm treats `.gitignore` files as packaging directives rather than inert content.  The fix follows the existing pattern used elsewhere in the codebase for dotfiles (e.g., `dockerignore`, `server/gitignore`, `server/npmrc`): rename the file to drop the leading dot (`gitignore`), then restore it during project creation.  ## Type of change  - [x] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue -->  ## Checklist  - [x] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [x] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced. Description: <!--   Thanks for contributing to Wasp!   Make sure to follow this PR template, so that we can speed up the review process.   It will also help you not forget important steps when making a change.   If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  ## Description  This PR fixes #3430 by implementing workspace filtering for Railway projects when users provide the `--workspace` option to the `wasp deploy railway launch` command.  Previously, when listing Railway projects, the code ignored the workspace parameter and returned all projects across all workspaces that the user had access to. This made it impossible to accurately check if a Railway project exists in the selected workspace, which is needed for #2926.  The implementation adds the optional `workspace` parameter throughout the Railway project querying chain: - `getRailwayProjects` now accepts and uses the workspace parameter when calling Railway CLI's `list` command - `getRailwayProjectById` and `getRailwayProjectByName` now accept and forward the workspace parameter - `getRailwayProjectStatus` now accepts and propagates the workspace parameter - The workspace is properly passed from the command options down to the project listing functions  This allows for more precise filtering when a workspace is specified, while maintaining backward compatibility when no workspace is provided.  ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [x] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty. Description: <!--   Thanks for contributing to Wasp!   Make sure to follow this PR template, so that we can speed up the review process. If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  ## Description  This PR refactors network HTTP logic into a centralized `Wasp.Util.Network.HTTP` module as requested in issue #3661. Previously, timeout and HTTP request logic was duplicated across multiple files. This change extracts common patterns into reusable utilities to establish a single source of truth for network operations.  **Key changes:**  - Extended `Wasp.Util.Network.HTTP` module with three new utility functions:    - `withTimeout` - Generic timeout wrapper that converts IO actions with timeouts to Either results   - `setRequestTimeout` - Standardized HTTP request timeout setter (takes seconds, handles microseconds internally)   - `httpLBS` - Simplified wrapper for basic HTTP requests returning lazy ByteString  - Refactored files to use the centralized utilities:   - `Wasp.Cli.Command.News.Fetching` - Now uses `withTimeout` instead of manual timeout logic   - `Wasp.Cli.Archive` - Now uses `httpLBS` instead of `Network.HTTP.Conduit.simpleHttp`   - `Wasp.AI.OpenAI.ChatGPT` - Now uses `setRequestTimeout` instead of manual timeout configuration  **Benefits:**  - Eliminates code duplication - Provides consistent timeout handling across the codebase - Makes future network-related changes easier to maintain - Improves code readability by abstracting low-level details  ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [x] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty. ## Description  Closes #3832.  The Wasp compiler now validates that `vite.config.ts` (or `vite.config.js`) exists in the user's project directory during compilation. Without this file, the project would fail at runtime with a confusing error instead of at compile time with a clear message.  **What it does:** - Missing vite config file → **compile error** - Vite config missing the `wasp/client/vite` plugin import → **compile error**  Both errors link to https://wasp.sh/docs/project/custom-vite-config#required-configuration.  The validation is integrated into `readExternalConfigs` alongside the existing package.json and tsconfig validations.  ## Type of change  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** - [ ] **� Bug fix** - [x] **� New/improved feature** - [ ] **� Breaking change**  ## Checklist  - [x] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change.   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed.   - [x] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `waspc/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog:   - [x] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.

We'll exchange it for proper templates once we implement https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3767 so I don't consider it a feature (i.e., no tests, no docs, no nothing).  If this doesn't happen, we have a contingency plan: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3864  Why not just start by implementing https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3767? Description: ## Description  - By default `actions/upload-artifact` skips leading-dot files, so we weren't embedding the `.gitignore` file in the build artifact. Now we do. - npm will detect `.gitignore` files and use them as ignore files for the packaging process, skipping the `.gitignore` file itself. Instead, we ask npm to explicitly not ignore it with `.npmignore`  - Fixes #3850  ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [x] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty.   If you're unsure about any of them, don't hesitate to ask for help. -->  - [x] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [x] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.  <!--   Bumping the version on `waspc/waspc.cabal`:    We still haven't reached 1.0, so the version bumping follows these rules:     - Bug fix:            0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - New feature:        0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - Breaking change:    0.+1.0    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.17.0)    If the version has already been bumped on `main` since the last release, skip this. -->

Description: ## Description  Refactors `wasp-app-runner` to eliminate all `process.exit()` calls, replacing them with structured error handling that ensures Docker containers and child processes are properly cleaned up before exit.  **Motivation:** `process.exit()` bypasses normal cleanup paths, which can leave Docker containers and child processes running after the runner exits. By switching to thrown errors and `Disposable`/`using` patterns, we get deterministic cleanup through normal control flow.  **Key changes:**  - **`CLIError` + `logger.fatal()`**: A new `fatal()` method on the logger throws a `CLIError` (typed as `never`), which propagates up to `main()` where it sets `process.exitCode = 1` instead of calling `process.exit()`.  - **`AbortController`-based shutdown** (`shutdown.ts`): A centralized `shutdownSignal` replaces the old `ChildProcessManager` class. SIGINT/SIGTERM abort the controller, which propagates to all child processes.  - **`Process` class using `execa`**: Replaces the old `spawnWithLog`/`spawnAndCollectOutput` free functions with a `Process` class that supports `.print()`, `.collect()`, `.wait()`, and `.kill()`. Uses `detached` mode + process-group kill for reliable cleanup (handles Wasp not always forwarding signals to its children). We're using `execa` because it has facilities for easily doing what we want to do (process line-by-line, automatically kill children once the script is done, and such).  - **`startServer()` helper + `Disposable` pattern**: A new `Server` type wraps a `Process` and implements `Disposable`, so `using` declarations automatically kill containers/processes when their scope exits. Used for Postgres, SMTP, and Wasp dev/build servers.  - **`waitUntilHttp()` readiness checks**: `waspStart` and `waspBuildStart` now use `startServer()` with HTTP readiness polling on ports 3000/3001, so the runner knows when the app is actually ready.  ## Type of change  - [x] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  - [x] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. <!-- Internal refactor of process management; tested manually. -->   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. <!-- N/A -->   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.

_commit: ce5fa26_ | # Some extra review comments from Claude These are take it or leave it kind of comments, feel free to ignore them if nonsensical. ## Code Review: `cprecioso/remove-process-exit` ### Issues **1. `process.ts`: `env` doesn't inherit `process.env`** `process.ts` (new) - The `Process` constructor passes `options.env` directly to execa. Unlike the old `spawnWithLog` which did `{ ...process.env, ...extraEnv }`, execa's `$()` with an explicit `env` replaces `process.env` entirely. This means `waspMigrateDb`, `waspStart`, etc. will lose `PATH` and everything else from the environment. Either merge with `process.env` in the constructor, or use execa's `extendEnv: true` option (which is the default for `execa()` but should be verified for `$()`). ```ts this.#proc = $(options.cmd, options.args, { cwd: options.cwd, env: options.env, extendEnv: true, // explicit is better here // ... }); ``` **2. `process.ts`: `print()` has fire-and-forget promises** `process.ts` (new, `print()` method) - `iterable.forEach(...)` returns a promise that is never awaited or caught. If the iteration throws, it becomes an unhandled rejection. ```ts print(): this { iterable.forEach(this.stdoutLines, (line) => this.#logger.info(line)); iterable.forEach(this.stderrLines, (line) => this.#logger.error(line)); return this; } ``` Consider adding `.catch()` handlers or storing the promises to await later. **3. `process.ts`: `collect()` and `print()` both consume streams** If someone calls `.print()` then `.collect()`, the streams will have been consumed by `print()` already. There's nothing preventing this misuse. Consider making them mutually exclusive or documenting the constraint. **4. `shutdown.ts`: `shutdownPromise` is exported but never used** `shutdown.ts:11` - `shutdownPromise` is defined and exported but no file in this PR imports it. Part of #3519  ## Goal Generate a POC that shows that Wasp can be fully type and value wired, thus breaking the cyclic dependency between SDK code and user code.  ## Context This combines the findings from the individual POCs (#3819, #3820, #3821) into a complete working solution. The SDK must: 1. **Compile independently** — no imports of user code at the TypeScript level 2. **Have full type safety** — user-defined types (entities, env schemas, prisma extensions) propagate through the SDK's public API 3. **Wire user code at runtime** — user implementations are connected to the SDK (without mutable global state ideally)  ### Key items to validate: - SDK `tsc` compilation succeeds with no user code present - User project `tsc` compilation succeeds with full types - Client runtime works (Vite dev server, production build) - Server runtime works (Rollup bundle, Node.js execution) - Test that all types propagate properly, e.g. Prisma extensions (`$extends`) type propagation through `PrismaClient` conditional type
Discuss Information: This branch does manage to break the full cycle with a mix of techniques (random experiments I did while slowly transitioning the whole SDK to not depend on user code). https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3828  All in all, POC that it works exists in the branch.

Description: ## Summary  Bumps the minimum axios version floor from `^1.4.0` to `^1.7.9`.  Closes #2935  ## Motivation  - Addresses GHSA-wf5p-g6vw-rhxx (SSRF vulnerability fixed in axios 1.7.4) - The `^1.4.0` floor allowed installing versions with known security issues  ## Changes  - **`waspc/src/Wasp/Generator/DepVersions.hs`**: `SV.Version 1 4 0` → `SV.Version 1 7 9` - **4 e2e golden snapshots**: Updated `"axios": "^1.4.0"` → `"axios": "^1.7.9"`  No template/API changes needed — axios API is stable across 1.x.  ## Note  There's discussion in #2935 about potentially migrating from axios to `fetch` or a fetch-based library (also related: #3288 re: streaming support). This PR is a conservative version bump that fixes the security issue. A full axios→fetch migration would be a separate, larger effort.

I will close all of these PRs, but if you're up to see it through and pass it through review, feel free to reopen one (only one of them!), and we can work together on it; and if it's a good experience we can see about the other ones. You can also always drop by [our Discord](https://discord.gg/rzdnErX) and ask for help or guidance.

Description: <!--   Thanks for contributing to Wasp!   Make sure to follow this PR template, so that we can speed up the review process.   It will also help you not forget important steps when making a change.   If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  ## Description  This PR introduces validation for 3 options we don't want users to change in their `vite.config.ts`:  **1. These 2 options I'm sure we should force:** - `base`   - user can change this via `client.baseDir` Wasp file option (this changes both Vite options and React Router options so we need to control this option)  - `build.outDir`   - our `deploy` package expects the value to be `.wasp/out/web-app/build` so users can't change it  **2. These options might be okay for user to change:** - `envPrefix`   - if they change the `envPrefix` to something else, our docs become irrelevant e.g. everywhere where we say "define REACT_APP_API_URL as x" becomes incorrect.    - Users can change it, but then they are on their own.    - Since this is related to security (setting the prefix ensures only prefixed options are included in the browser build) I'm more inclined to forbid changing it.   Closes #3635   ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [x] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty. Description:
Bumps [playwright](https://github.com/microsoft/playwright) to 1.58.2 and updates ancestor dependency [@playwright/test](https://github.com/microsoft/playwright). These dependencies need to be updated together. Updates `playwright` from 1.51.1 to 1.58.2
Release notes

Sourced from playwright's releases.

v1.58.2

Highlights

#39121 fix(trace viewer): make paths via stdin work #39129 fix: do not force swiftshader on chromium mac

Browser Versions

  • Chromium 145.0.7632.6
  • Mozilla Firefox 146.0.1
  • WebKit 26.0

v1.58.1

Highlights

#39036 fix(msedge): fix local network permissions #39037 chore: update cft download location #38995 chore(webkit): disable frame sessions on fronzen builds

Browser Versions

  • Chromium 145.0.7632.6
  • Mozilla Firefox 146.0.1
  • WebKit 26.0

v1.58.0

� Playwright CLI+SKILLs �

We are adding a new token-efficient CLI mode of operation to Playwright with the skills located at playwright-cli. This brings the long-awaited official SKILL-focused CLI mode to our story and makes it more coding agent-friendly.

It is the first snapshot with the essential command set (which is already larger than the original MCP!), but we expect it to grow rapidly. Unlike the token use, that one we expect to go down since snapshots are no longer forced into the LLM!

Timeline

If you're using merged reports, the HTML report Speedboard tab now shows the Timeline:

UI Mode and Trace Viewer Improvements

  • New 'system' theme option follows your OS dark/light mode preference
  • Search functionality (Cmd/Ctrl+F) is now available in code editors
  • Network details panel has been reorganized for better usability
  • JSON responses are now automatically formatted for readability

Thanks to @​cpAdm for contributing these improvements!

Miscellaneous

browserType.connectOverCDP() now accepts an isLocal option. When set to true, it tells Playwright that it runs on the same host as the CDP server, enabling file system optimizations.

Breaking Changes ⚠️

  • Removed _react and _vue selectors. See locators guide for alternatives.

Description:
Bumps [webpack](https://github.com/webpack/webpack) from 5.99.5 to 5.105.0.
Release notes

Sourced from webpack's releases.

v5.105.0

Minor Changes

  • Allow resolving worker module by export condition name when using new Worker() (by @​hai-x in #20353)

  • Detect conditional imports to avoid compile-time linking errors for non-existent exports. (by @​hai-x in #20320)

  • Added the tsconfig option for the resolver options (replacement for tsconfig-paths-webpack-plugin). Can be false (disabled), true (use the default tsconfig.json file to search for it), a string path to tsconfig.json, or an object with configFile and references options. (by @​alexander-akait in #20400)

  • Support import.defer() for context modules. (by @​ahabhgk in #20399)

  • Added support for array values ​​to the devtool option. (by @​hai-x in #20191)

  • Improve rendering node built-in modules for ECMA module output. (by @​hai-x in #20255)

  • Unknown import.meta properties are now determined at runtime instead of being statically analyzed at compile time. (by @​xiaoxiaojx in #20312)

Patch Changes

  • Fixed ESM default export handling for .mjs files in Module Federation (by @​y-okt in #20189)

  • Optimized import.meta.env handling in destructuring assignments by using cached stringified environment definitions. (by @​xiaoxiaojx in #20313)

  • Respect the stats.errorStack option in stats output. (by @​samarthsinh2660 in #20258)

  • Fixed a bug where declaring a module variable in module scope would conflict with the default moduleArgument. (by @​xiaoxiaojx in #20265)

  • Fix VirtualUrlPlugin to set resourceData.context for proper module resolution. Previously, when context was not set, it would fallback to the virtual scheme path (e.g., virtual:routes), which is not a valid filesystem path, causing subsequent resolve operations to fail. (by @​xiaoxiaojx in #20390)

  • Fixed Worker self-import handling to support various URL patterns (e.g., import.meta.url, new URL(import.meta.url), new URL(import.meta.url, import.meta.url), new URL("./index.js", import.meta.url)). Workers that resolve to the same module are now properly deduplicated, regardless of the URL syntax used. (by @​xiaoxiaojx in #20381)

  • Reuse the same async entrypoint for the same Worker URL within a module to avoid circular dependency warnings when multiple Workers reference the same resource. (by @​xiaoxiaojx in #20345)

  • Fixed a bug where a self-referencing dependency would have an unused export name when imported inside a web worker. (by @​samarthsinh2660 in #20251)

  • Fix missing export generation when concatenated modules in different chunks share the same runtime in module library bundles. (by @​hai-x in #20346)

  • Fixed import.meta.env.xxx behavior: when accessing a non-existent property, it now returns empty object instead of full object at runtime. (by @​xiaoxiaojx in #20289)

  • Improved parsing error reporting by adding a link to the loader documentation. (by @​gaurav10gg in #20244)

  • Fix typescript types. (by @​alexander-akait in #20305)

  • Add declaration for unused harmony import specifier. (by @​hai-x in #20286)

  • Fix compressibility of modules while retaining portability. (by @​dmichon-msft in #20287)

  • Optimize source map generation: only include ignoreList property when it has content, avoiding empty arrays in source maps. (by @​xiaoxiaojx in #20319)

  • Preserve star exports for dependencies in ECMA module output. (by @​hai-x in #20293)

Description: ## Problem Currently, when a snapshot test process errors and exists early, that error propagates and also interrupts the main process (`cabal test e2e-tests`), but other snapshot test shell command processes continue working orphapned. So the workflow is: In the failing test: 1. wasp fails → shell exits → `callCommand` throws exception (it internally checks exit code) 2. Exception propagates up to `mapConcurrently` In sibling tests: 1. `mapConcurrently` throws `AsyncCancelled` to them 2. `callCommand` is blocked in `waitForProcess` internally 3. `waitForProcess` is interrupted by `AsyncCancelled` 4. No cleanup 5. `AsyncCancelled` propagates, threads die 6. Shell + wasp processes are still running (nobody sent them a signal) Then: 1. `mapConcurrently` propagates the original exception from the failing test 2. It reaches `main` 3. Haskell process exits 4. Shell + wasp processes are now orphans (re-parented to PID 1) 5. They keep running and writing to the terminal ## Solution So what we want to do is for all processes started by a snapshot test process to die together with it. My solution is snapshot test process run the commands as separate process groups, that cleanup on interruptions: ```hs -- | Interruptible version of callCommand that terminates the entire process tree on async exception. -- Uses process groups so that when a thread is cancelled (e.g., when another concurrent test fails), -- all child processes are also terminated rather than continuing to run. callCommandInProcessGroup :: String -> IO () callCommandInProcessGroup cmd = bracket (createProcess (shell cmd) {create_group = True}) (\(_, _, _, ph) -> interruptProcessGroupOf ph) ( \(_, _, _, ph) -> do exitCode <- waitForProcess ph case exitCode of ExitSuccess -> return () ExitFailure code -> fail $ "Command failed with exit code " ++ show code ++ ": " ++ cmd ) ``` So the workflow is: In the failing test: 1. wasp fails → shell exits → `waitForProcess` returns `ExitFailure` 2. We throw via `fail` - "Command failed..." 3. `bracket` cleanup runs (but process already exited, so it's a no-op) 4. Exception propagates up to `mapConcurrently` In sibling tests: 1. `mapConcurrently` throws `AsyncCancelled` to them 2. `waitForProcess` is interrupted 3. `bracket` cleanup runs → `interruptProcessGroupOf` kills the running processes 4. `bracket` re-throws `AsyncCancelled` 5. Threads die Then: 1. `mapConcurrently` propagates the original exception from the failing test 2. It reaches `main` 3. Haskell process exits ## Type of change - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** - [x] **� Bug fix** - [ ] **� New/improved feature** - [ ] **� Breaking change** ## Checklist - [ ] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended. - � Tests and apps: - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`. - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed. - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed. - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa). - � Documentation: - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`. - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_ - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change. - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`. - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.

Description: <!--   Thanks for contributing to Wasp!   Make sure to follow this PR template, so that we can speed up the review process.   It will also help you not forget important steps when making a change.   If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  ## Description  Copying of SDK assets (CSS, HTML files) happens on each SDK build which happens on each code change. This causes Vite to do a full-reload because now we are copying the virtual `index.html` in the SDK.  ### Solution  If the source file is newer or the destination file doesn't exist - copy the source file.  ### Context from Discord  Right now on _each_ user file change: 1. Wasp compiler regenerates files     - Generator is "smart" by using checksums 2. Runs ⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨`tsc`⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩ to rebuild the SDK     - smart with ⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨`incremental`⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩ compilation 3. Copies extra files for the SDK ⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨`dist`⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩ dir     - just copies files each time  **This causes Vite dev server to detect these changes and reload the client app changes. The side-effect is sometimes full page reload which wipes the client state.**  :one: We prevented the ⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨`tsc`⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩ from doing too many changes by implementing ⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨`incremental`⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩ compilation: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/1931  :two: Since we introduced asset copying (for CSS, HTML files which ⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨⁨`tsc`⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩⁩ doesn't handle) in the SDK, this started happening again more aggressively.   ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [x] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty.   If you're unsure about any of them, don't hesitate to ask for help. -->  - [ ] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [x] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.  <!--   Bumping the version on `waspc/waspc.cabal`:    We still haven't reached 1.0, so the version bumping follows these rules:     - Bug fix:            0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - New feature:        0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - Breaking change:    0.+1.0    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.17.0)    If the version has already been bumped on `main` since the last release, skip this. -->

Description: <!--   Thanks for contributing to Wasp!   Make sure to follow this PR template, so that we can speed up the review process.   It will also help you not forget important steps when making a change.   If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  ## Description  Fixes https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3699  TLDR: The issue is that we don't support optional minor and patch version. We can't just assume that missing values are "0", because some operators (~) have special behavior around missing values.  So the `Version` has to support not defining minor/patch values.  Approaches I've thought about:  ### Just add `Maybe` approach (also the one I implemented)  ```hs data Version = Version   { major :: !Natural,     minor :: !(Maybe Natural),     patch :: !(Maybe Natural)   }   deriving (TH.Lift) ``` Requires overriding default `Eq` and `Ord` implementation: ```hs -- Also similar for 'Ord' instance Eq Version where   Version maj1 mnr1 ptc1 == Version maj2 mnr2 ptc2 =     maj1 == maj2       && fromMaybe 0 mnr1 == fromMaybe 0 mnr2       && fromMaybe 0 ptc1 == fromMaybe 0 ptc2 ``` Other than that there are no real changes to the old code, except the new syntax for `Version`, which is also the biggest downside: ```hs prismaVersion :: SV.Version prismaVersion = SV.Version 5 (Just 19) (Just 1) ``` To help with that we can use pattern synonyms: ```hs {-# LANGUAGE PatternSynonyms #-}  -- | Pattern synonym for the common case where all three version components are present. pattern FullVersion :: Natural -> Natural -> Natural -> Version pattern FullVersion maj mnr ptc = Version maj (Just mnr) (Just ptc) ``` Which is then used as: ```hs prismaVersion :: SV.Version prismaVersion = SV.FullVersion 5 19 1 ``` ### Three constructors approach  ```hs data Version   = MajorOnly !Natural   | MajorMinor !Natural !Natural   | MajorMinorPatch !Natural !Natural !Natural   deriving (TH.Lift) ``` Seemed like it would bring too much complexity.  ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty.   If you're unsure about any of them, don't hesitate to ask for help. -->  - [ ] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.  <!--   Bumping the version on `waspc/waspc.cabal`:    We still haven't reached 1.0, so the version bumping follows these rules:     - Bug fix:            0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - New feature:        0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - Breaking change:    0.+1.0    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.17.0)    If the version has already been bumped on `main` since the last release, skip this. -->

Description: Version: 0.21.0  As of 0.21.0, `wasp news` is pretty basic:   - We publish the news by editing a static JSON file published on https://news.wasp.sh.   - The [news repo's CI](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp-news) ensures we don't push malformed data.   - The CLI fetches the static JSON file and either:     - Shows all entries in that static file when the user runs `wasp news`.     - Shows unseen important entries every now and then during `wasp start`. The CLI decides what to show based purely on time and what the user already saw.  In other words, all users get the same news.  But we could do much more. For example, the CLI could communicate the user's Wasp version and we could tell them only about news they care about (e.g., security vulenarbilities for their specific version).   We should brainstorm about what to do, perhaps check other projects like that thing from Hashicorp's (can't find it now and can't remember the name, @Martinsos will know).

Description: - Fixes https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3431  ## Description  Add a `--custom-server-url` CLI option to the `wasp deploy` command for both Fly.io and Railway providers. This allows users to explicitly specify the server URL that the client connects to, replacing the workaround of using the `REACT_APP_API_URL` environment variable.  Users can now use: ```bash wasp deploy fly deploy --custom-server-url https://my-server.com wasp deploy railway deploy myproject --custom-server-url https://my-server.com ```  The implementation follows a clean architecture where each provider's deploy function resolves the URL (custom option takes priority, otherwise defaults are used), and passes it to the shared `buildClient` function.  ## Type of change  - [x] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  - [ ] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change.   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog:   - [x] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [x] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.

Description: - Part of #3120:   1. #3655 (this one)     - Why: Since we're going to allow overriding with configs at the `package.json` level, we'll need to bring dependency conflicts checking to the PackageJsonValidator.   2. #3656     - Why: Now that we check for conflicts before going into the generator proper, we can remove that code from it.   3. #3643     - The meat of the change  ---  ## Description   This PR improves the package.json dependency validator to detect version conflicts between user-specified dependencies and all Wasp-managed dependencies.  Previously, the validator only checked user dependencies against a small explicit list of required/optional packages. Now it validates against all dependencies used across the entire Wasp ecosystem: - User requirements (required runtime deps, dev deps, optional deps) - WebApp generator dependencies - Server generator dependencies  This prevents subtle version conflicts where a user might install a package version that conflicts with what Wasp internally uses, which could cause hard-to-debug runtime issues.  Key changes: - Added `toPair` helper to `Dependency` module - Created new `Common.hs` module with shared dependency specifications and `getAllWaspDependencies` function - Refactored validator to check optional deps against the full dependency map - Threaded `AppSpec` through the validator chain to access generator dependency info  ## Type of change  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [x] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty. Description: <!--   Thanks for contributing to Wasp!   Make sure to follow this PR template, so that we can speed up the review process.   It will also help you not forget important steps when making a change.   If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  ## Description  ### Context  **Our release process:** When we're doing a release, we create both RCs and final releases. Regardless of the kind of release we're doing, a GH Release is created as draft and then we manually mark it as Pre-Release or Release.  **npm tags:** in the npm registry, you can give aliases ("tags") to a specific package version e.g. `rc` tag → `0.21.0-rc1` version, or `nightly` → `0.0.0-dev+8a3c4d`. These tags can be updated to point to another version, making it a bit like "channels" for you to publish your package to.     - `npm i wasp@0.21.0` → installs the specific version    - `npm i wasp@rc` → installs the version that `rc` tag points to at that moment    - `npm i wasp` → when no version or tag is defined, npm by default installs the `latest` tag      - by default, `npm publish` also modifies the `latest` tag to point to your newly-published version, so the general case of just publishing regular versions is seamless  ### Problem  The automation for publishing when we create a tag is not giving the `npm publish` command a tag, so even RCs are being released to the `latest` tag. Instead, we should make it behave a bit more like our current GH releases workflow, where the release is not widely made available, but requires a manual action to set as a release version.  ### Solution  We make it so that the automation will publish everything to the `rc` channel. This one doesn't have any special meaning, just the fact that it won't present itself as `latest` (i.e. default). Then, when we have tested the release and want to make it widely available, we run an `npm` command to set it as `latest`, same as with the GH releases.  ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [x] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty. Description: ## Description  Built on top of https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3505 Solves https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3517  Implements infrastructure for multiple tsconfig project SDK. The new structure is: ``` └── sdk/wasp               # `root` project     ├── tsconfig.json           ├── core               # `core` project     │   └── tsconfig.json       ├── user-core          # `user-core` project     │   └── tsconfig.json       ├── src                # part of `user-core` project     └── dist               # notice that `dist` follows the source folder structure         ├── core         ├── src         └── user-core ``` The `root` project hold scripts and references all other projects.  It is the starting point of the SDK.  The `core` project will hold all code that does not depend on user's code (`src`) in any way.  The `user-core` project is sdk code that does depend on the user's code. We can discuss how to actually name this project, changing the name is easy.  Even though the we the structure suggest that we have 4 separate projects (`root`, `core`, `user-core`, and `src`), `src` is actually part of the `user-core` project (for now).   This PR features the following changes: - Setting up the multiple tsconfig projects setup (sdk/wasp/tsconfig, sdk/wasp/core/tsconfig.json, sdk/wasp/user-core/tsconfig.json). - Refactored `strong-path` infrastructure for multiple tsconfig project:   - `SdkGenerator/Common.hs` - Shared utilities across all SDK projects   - `SdkGenerator/Root/Common.hs` - Helpers for the root tsconfig project   - `SdkGenerator/Core/Common.hs` - Helpers for the core tsconfig project   - `SdkGenerator/UserCore/Common.hs` - Helpers for the user-core tsconfig project   NOTE: I plan to move files used by the specific tsconfig projects into their respective folders in future PRs. E.g. `HttpError.ts` generator will be in `SdkGenerator/Core` folder.   Edit: starting PR here https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3653    - Removed old server/client path helpers (they were used inconsistently, and now with multiple projects its more tedious to use them). - Refactoring all `SdkGenerator/**/*.hs` files to use the new infrastructure. - Moving most of `sdk/wasp` files to `sdk/wasp/user-core`, except for `HttpError.ts` which was moved to `sdk/wasp/core`.   - This move automatically "formatted imports" on my IDE, so some files have extensions "optimized". I reverted all pure sorting changes to reduce the amount of changes.  ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty.   If you're unsure about any of them, don't hesitate to ask for help. -->  - [ ] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.  <!--   Bumping the version on `waspc/waspc.cabal`:    We still haven't reached 1.0, so the version bumping follows these rules:     - Bug fix:            0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - New feature:        0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - Breaking change:    0.+1.0    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.17.0)    If the version has already been bumped on `main` since the last release, skip this. -->

Description: ## Description  Built on top of https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3505 Solves https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3517  Implements infrastructure for multiple tsconfig project SDK. The new structure is: ``` └── sdk/wasp               # `root` project     ├── tsconfig.json           ├── core               # `core` project     │   └── tsconfig.json       ├── user-core          # `user-core` project     │   └── tsconfig.json       ├── src                # part of `user-core` project     └── dist               # notice that `dist` follows the source folder structure         ├── core         ├── src         └── user-core ``` The `root` project hold scripts and references all other projects.  It is the starting point of the SDK.  The `core` project will hold all code that does not depend on user's code (`src`) in any way.  The `user-core` project is sdk code that does depend on the user's code. We can discuss how to actually name this project, changing the name is easy.  Even though the we the structure suggest that we have 4 separate projects (`root`, `core`, `user-core`, and `src`), `src` is actually part of the `user-core` project (for now).   This PR features the following changes: - Setting up the multiple tsconfig projects setup (sdk/wasp/tsconfig, sdk/wasp/core/tsconfig.json, sdk/wasp/user-core/tsconfig.json). - Refactored `strong-path` infrastructure for multiple tsconfig project:   - `SdkGenerator/Common.hs` - Shared utilities across all SDK projects   - `SdkGenerator/Root/Common.hs` - Helpers for the root tsconfig project   - `SdkGenerator/Core/Common.hs` - Helpers for the core tsconfig project   - `SdkGenerator/UserCore/Common.hs` - Helpers for the user-core tsconfig project   NOTE: I plan to move files used by the specific tsconfig projects into their respective folders in future PRs. E.g. `HttpError.ts` generator will be in `SdkGenerator/Core` folder.   Edit: starting PR here https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3607/changes    - Removed old server/client path helpers (they were used inconsistently, and now with multiple projects its more tedious to use them). - Refactoring all `SdkGenerator/**/*.hs` files to use the new infrastructure. - Moving most of `sdk/wasp` files to `sdk/wasp/user-core`, except for `HttpError.ts` which was moved to `sdk/wasp/core`.   - This move automatically "formatted imports" on my IDE, so some files have extensions "optimized". I reverted all pure sorting changes to reduce the amount of changes.  Rarely, I clean up some code, but when I do I leave additional comment with my reasoning.   ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty.   If you're unsure about any of them, don't hesitate to ask for help. -->  - [ ] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.  <!--   Bumping the version on `waspc/waspc.cabal`:    We still haven't reached 1.0, so the version bumping follows these rules:     - Bug fix:            0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - New feature:        0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - Breaking change:    0.+1.0    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.17.0)    If the version has already been bumped on `main` since the last release, skip this. -->

Description: <!--   Thanks for contributing to Wasp!   Make sure to follow this PR template, so that we can speed up the review process.   It will also help you not forget important steps when making a change.   If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  ## Description  This is a preparation step before we moving Vite user-land. The idea is to minimise the logic in `web-app` and move it to the SDK so it can be easily used from the user-land.   In this PR, we moved most of the routing logic to the SDK, all of the React components and the Vite plugins.  We wrapped all the client providers (websockets, React Query) into a `WaspApp` component which we mount on start. We have to use `getWaspApp` and `getRouter` to be able to pass in the routes from `web-app` so we don't have to import user code from the SDK. Description: <!--   Thanks for contributing to Wasp!   Make sure to follow this PR template, so that we can speed up the review process.   It will also help you not forget important steps when making a change.   If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  ## Description  - Part of https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/2631 - On top of https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3595 - On top of https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3596  This PR adds the "Guides" section to our website.   ![Screen Recording 2026-01-09 at 11 18 47](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/feda380b-2f40-44b5-ad98-53a678eae404)   Some notes [from the RFC](https://www.notion.so/wasp-lang/RFC-Adding-3rd-party-how-tos-to-the-docs-2e318a74854c805caef2fd9dcfe10ffd?source=copy_link):  > - Create a “Guides” section in our website, conceptually separate from “Docs” >     - Maintains the mental separation, lets us set different maintenance expectation levels for this section vs. the docs section. > - Letting it participate in the versioning of docs >     - This means that when we do a release, the current state of guides gets archived in `versioned_docs`. The version selector changes between these archives. >     - e.g. if we modify the deployment strategy from Wasp 0.20 to Wasp 0.21, the guides should also change whether you’re in one or the other. >     - This feels like additional maintenance burden but I think with the “last checked” dates and everything, we are allowing ourselves to play more loosely with it so it doesn’t create this burden. >     - Implementation note: Docusaurus doesn’t let us show the “Guides” menu item only on some versions, so older versions that didn’t have a Guides section will just show a “Guides not available” document.  ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [x] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty.   If you're unsure about any of them, don't hesitate to ask for help. -->  - [ ] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [x] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.  <!--   Bumping the version on `waspc/waspc.cabal`:    We still haven't reached 1.0, so the version bumping follows these rules:     - Bug fix:            0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - New feature:        0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - Breaking change:    0.+1.0    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.17.0)    If the version has already been bumped on `main` since the last release, skip this. -->

Description: <!--   Thanks for contributing to Wasp!   Make sure to follow this PR template, so that we can speed up the review process.   It will also help you not forget important steps when making a change.   If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  - Part of #2631   ## Description  Adds a comment system with [Giscus](https://giscus.app/).  Giscus acts as "just" a nicely embeddable frontend for GitHub Discussions. When a user first comments on a page, Giscus creates a discussion for that page, and through GitHub's API posts the comment to the discussion, from the user. Later comments are posted to that same discussion, as well as replies to top-level items. This forum style is nicely suited for people creating threads; and allows us to just use GitHub's moderation tools for comments.  Details: - Pages have to explicitly opt into the comment system through setting `comments: true` in their frontmatter. - It uses the `Doc` context from the Docusaurus Docs plugin, so it only works in the `web/docs` subolder (not the landing page nor the blog).  Example (not in this PR but in a future one): - Enabling the comments in the document: 	https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/bac814871916a02402e078b01f5abc83e883602c/web/docs/guides/configuration/cors-multiple-domains.md?plain=1#L3 - Live comments on the page (scroll to bottom): 	https://push-mqztwroqlrkz.wasp-docs-on-main.pages.dev/docs/guides/configuration/cors-multiple-domains - The underlying GitHub Discussion: 	https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp.sh-comments/discussions/3  ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [x] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty. Description: ## Bug Description  After upgrading from Wasp 0.18 to 0.20, Docker deployment fails with native module and CLI path issues on Alpine Linux.  ## Reproduction Steps  1. **Setup**: GitHub Actions workflow using `ubuntu-latest` (glibc) 2. **Build**: Run `wasp build` (generates `.wasp/build` directory) 3. **Docker**: Build using generated Dockerfile (Alpine 3.20 base image) 4. **Result**: Build fails with errors  ## Errors  ### Error 1: argon2 native module ``` Error: Failed to load native binding Cannot find module './argon2.linux-x64-musl.node' Cannot find module '@node-rs/argon2-linux-x64-musl' ```  ### Error 2: Prisma CLI not found ``` > npm run db-migrate-prod sh: prisma: not found ```  ## Environment  - **Wasp 0.18**: ✅ Works perfectly (no custom Dockerfile needed) - **Wasp 0.20**: ❌ Fails (requires custom Dockerfile workaround) - **CI**: GitHub Actions (`ubuntu-latest`, glibc) - **Docker Base**: `node:22.12.0-alpine3.20` (musl libc) - **Deployment**: GitHub Actions → Docker build → GitHub Container Registry  ## Root Cause Analysis  ### Issue 1: Cross-platform native module (@node-rs/argon2)  The generated Dockerfile copies the entire `.wasp/out/sdk` directory from GitHub Actions build:  ```dockerfile COPY sdk .wasp/out/sdk ```  This SDK includes `node_modules` with **glibc** version of `@node-rs/argon2` (installed on GitHub Actions runner). When the Docker container runs on Alpine (musl), it cannot load the glibc native binaries.  **Why this worked in Wasp 0.18**: Unknown, possibly different Dockerfile generation logic or different dependency handling.  ### Issue 2: Prisma CLI PATH  The generated Dockerfile structure: ```dockerfile WORKDIR /app COPY --from=server-builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules  # Prisma is here WORKDIR /app/.wasp/build/server ENTRYPOINT ["npm", "run", "start-production"]  # Calls 'prisma migrate deploy' ```  The `db-migrate-prod` script calls `prisma` directly, but the binary is in `/app/node_modules/.bin/prisma`, not in PATH from the current WORKDIR.  ## Workaround  Create `app/Dockerfile` with the following content (Wasp automatically appends this to the generated Dockerfile):  ```dockerfile # Fix @node-rs/argon2 for Alpine Linux (musl libc) # When npm install runs on macOS/Linux (glibc), it only downloads glibc binaries. # Alpine Linux needs linux-x64-musl binaries which must be installed explicitly. RUN npm install @node-rs/argon2-linux-x64-musl@1.8.3 --prefix /app --save=false --ignore-scripts  # Fix Prisma CLI path # Prisma is in /app/node_modules, add it to PATH so db-migrate-prod can find it ENV PATH="/app/node_modules/.bin:${PATH}" ```  ## Expected Behavior  Wasp 0.20 should work like 0.18 - **no custom Dockerfile should be needed** for standard Docker deployment scenarios.  ## Impact  This is a **regression bug** that breaks existing deployment workflows when upgrading from 0.18 to 0.20.  ## Additional Context  - Wasp 0.20.1 (released 2025-12-31) does not fix this issue - No similar issues found in GitHub Issues for v0.20.x - This affects any deployment using:   - Cross-platform builds (e.g., macOS/Linux CI → Alpine Docker)   - GitHub Actions → Docker workflow
Discuss Information: Hi @bkidy, we had this same issue reported previously [on Discord](https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1273962730566717471), and the user solved it by running `wasp clean`, removing their `package-lock.json`, and regenerating everything. Can you check if that fixes your issue? | Hi @bkidy I will close this issue now for inactivity. That's not the end of it though! We can reopen if you come back to us with more information. Otherwise, I hope your issue has been solved. �

Description: <!--   Thanks for contributing to Wasp!   Make sure to follow this PR template, so that we can speed up the review process.   It will also help you not forget important steps when making a change.   If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  ## Description  Updates the `basic` starter template to the latest best practices from React and the React Typescript Handbook.  - Types function components directly, without helper types from `@types/react` [(link)](https://react-typescript-cheatsheet.netlify.app/docs/basic/getting-started/function_components/) - Types `children` as `ReactNode` [(link)](https://react-typescript-cheatsheet.netlify.app/docs/basic/getting-started/basic_type_example#useful-react-prop-type-examples) - Uses `ComponentProps<"tag-name">` to get html component props, including their refs [(link)](https://react-typescript-cheatsheet.netlify.app/docs/react-types/componentprops/) - Uses `import { bla } from "react"` instead of `import * as React` or `import React` [(link)](https://legacy.reactjs.org/blog/2020/09/22/introducing-the-new-jsx-transform.html#:~:text=The%20default%20imports%20will%20keep%20working%20in%20React%2017%2C%20but%20in%20the%20longer%20term%20we%20encourage%20moving%20away%20from%20them.) - Stores element refs in `useState` instead of `useRef` where possible, which is preferred and improves syncing between the React and DOM worlds [(link)](https://react.dev/learn/referencing-values-with-refs#differences-between-refs-and-state) - Moves stuff out of effects and into event callbacks where possible [(link)](https://react.dev/learn/you-might-not-need-an-effect) - Removes `forwardRef` [(link)](https://react.dev/reference/react/forwardRef) - And more context-specific changes that I will explain in the comments  I didn't use more modern features like [Actions](https://react.dev/reference/react/useTransition#examples) or [Suspense](https://react.dev/reference/react/Suspense) as these are generally worse understood for beginners and there's not as much mindshare for them.  ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  -
