If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  - Resolves #2483  ## Description  This PR does two main things:  - Removes all special handling of Tailwind in the compiler - Updates our support to TW v4+ (docs, starters, examples)  Both changes are as expected, no weird things are needed, and everything is largely automatic as part of following the upgrade guide:  - We outright remove every Tailwind-specific handling from AppSpec and Generator - Tailwind is upgraded to v4   - Changed from PostCSS installation to Vite installation, as recommended by their guide      > "If you're using Vite, we recommend migrating from the PostCSS plugin to our new dedicated Vite plugin for improved performance and the best developer experience" - CSS component libraries that build upon Tailwind are also upgraded (in example apps, Hero UI and Flowbite).  The only caveat that differentiates a Wasp TW app from a regular TW app is that we need to add a `source` parameter to the Tailwind import:  ```diff  // src/Main.css -@import "tailwindcss"; +@import "tailwindcss" source("."); ```  This is done because Vite is called from `web-app` so we need to tell TW explicitly to find the source files in the user's project.  ---  This is a big PR, and we can't do the changes in it separately as they are mutually dependent on each other. However, the types of changes are quite scoped depending on the filetype so it is easy to dismiss large chunks of this PR:  - Changes to classes in JS/TS files are all the result of running the TW upgrade CLI and the formatter with the updated TW plugin. (Exceptions are marked with an explanatory comment) - Changes to `package.json` are version upgrades - In every example/starter app:   - Removed the postcss and tw config   - Put the tw config into the main CSS file   - Added the tw plugin in the vite config - In the compiler, I remove any specific Tailwind support, and change the `starter` code so that it doesn't write a TW config file, but writes a Vite config with the TW plugin.  I checked that every app and starter works well and hasn't changed styles.  ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [x] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [x] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty.   If you're unsure about any of them, don't hesitate to ask for help. -->  - [x] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [x] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [x] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [x] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [x] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [x] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [x] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.  <!--   Bumping the version on `waspc/waspc.cabal`:    We still haven't reached 1.0, so the version bumping follows these rules:     - Bug fix:            0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - New feature:        0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - Breaking change:    0.+1.0    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.17.0)    If the version has already been bumped on `main` since the last release, skip this. -->

Description: <!--   Thanks for contributing to Wasp!   Make sure to follow this PR template, so that we can speed up the review process.   It will also help you not forget important steps when making a change.   If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  ## Description  - Resolves #3498   From the description of #3498:  > Right now wasp-app-runner does (pseudo code): >  > ```js > parallel([ >   series([ clientBuild, clientStart ]), >   series([ serverBuild, serverStart ]) > ]) > ``` > This leads to the client usually being ready much sooner than the server, and the Vite message with the URLs being scrolled out by the server build logs. >  > I think it'd be much better to do: > ```js > series([ >   parallel([ clientBuild, serverBuild ]), >   parallel([ clientStart, serverStart ]) > ]) > ``` >  > So both sides start more or less at the same time.  In the end what this PR does is, more or less:  ```js await Promise.all([clientBuild(), serverBuild()])  const longRunningClient = clientStart()  // we need to wait for this first because client must strictly be up before server await httpReady(longRunningClient)  const longRunningServer = serverStart()  await Promise.race([longRunningClient, longRunningServer]) ```   <details> <summary>Checklist not applicable, click here to show anyway</summary>   ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty. Description: <!--   Thanks for contributing to Wasp!   Make sure to follow this PR template, so that we can speed up the review process.   It will also help you not forget important steps when making a change.   If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  ## Description  Built on top of https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3505 Solves https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3517  Implements infrastructure for multiple tsconfig project SDK. The new structure is: ``` └── sdk/wasp               # `root` project     ├── tsconfig.json           ├── core               # `core` project     │   └── tsconfig.json       ├── user-core          # `user-core` project     │   └── tsconfig.json       ├── src                # part of `user-core` project     └── dist               # notice that `dist` follows the source folder structure         ├── core         ├── src         └── user-core ``` The `root` project hold scripts and references all other projects.  It is the starting point of the SDK.  The `core` project will hold all code that does not depend on user's code (`src`) in any way.  The `user-core` project is sdk code that does depend on the user's code. We can discuss how to actually name this project, changing the name is easy.  Even though the we the structure suggest that we have 4 separate projects (`root`, `core`, `user-core`, and `src`), `src` is actually part of the `user-core` project (for now).   This PR feature the following changes: - Setting up the multiple tsconfig projects setup (`sdk/wasp/tsconfig`, `sdk/wasp/core/tsconfig.json`, `sdk/wasp/user-core/tsconfig.json`). - `strong-path` code for the new SDK tsconfig infrastructure in `SdkGenerator/Common.hs` - Refactoring all `SdkGenerator/**/*.hs` files to use the new infrastructure. - Moving most of `sdk/wasp` files to `sdk/wasp/user-core`, except for `HttpError.ts` which was moved to `sdk/wasp/core`.   - This move automatically "formatted imports" on my IDE, so some files have extensions "optimized". I reverted all pure sorting changes to reduce the amount of changes.  I highly recommend reading `SdkGenerator/Common.hs` changes, before looking at all other `SdkGenerator/` files. As the changes will make more sense.  Rarely, I clean up some code, but when I do I leave additional comment with my reasoning.   ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty. Description: <!--   Thanks for contributing to Wasp!   Make sure to follow this PR template, so that we can speed up the review process.   It will also help you not forget important steps when making a change.   If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->   - Related to #1769 - On top of #3539 - Resolves #3163  ## Description   - Removes the `.wasp/out` and `.wasp/build` split. - Updates references that pointed to `.wasp/build`. - `wasp build` explicitly built `.wasp/out/sdk`. Removed it as it's now unneeded. - Updates the `Command` `Requirable`s, since they were checking for `.wasp/build` existence as a proxy for knowing if the `wasp start` or `wasp build` had been done. Now they read the [.waspinfo file](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3539) to be certain of which build has been performed. - Removes the requirement to have the `.wasp/build/*` workspace. 	- It is now forbidden to have it, since we don't want npm to be picking up leftovers and fudging the resolution. - Makes build and dev generated packages have the same name, avoiding double resolutions.  > [!NOTE] > This PR does not attempt to solve the differences between our `wasp build` and `wasp start` processes (e.g. the extra copied files like the user `tsconfig` or `.env` files). However, we ensure one build process does not contaminate the other by [doing a cleanbuild if needed](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3539).  ### Motivation  Context: #1769  Our generated code is working on an assumption that does not hold true: - **Assumption:** production output is independent of development output (we build to different folders) - **Breaks:** `.wasp/build` depends on `.wasp/out/sdk`  We are doing work to maintain this separation when it was not needed, trying to make as if our process is not modal. Some complications we have are: [copying user code to our generated output](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/1769#issuecomment-1952938100); or [having to add each npm workspace twice to the user's package-lock](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3504).  We're better off accepting that these two builds aren't independent and make them mutually exclusive, so we can build them in the same place, avoiding workarounds.   ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [x] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [x] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty.   If you're unsure about any of them, don't hesitate to ask for help. -->  - [x] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [x] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [x] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [x] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [x] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [x] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [x] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.  <!--   Bumping the version on `waspc/waspc.cabal`:    We still haven't reached 1.0, so the version bumping follows these rules:     - Bug fix:            0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - New feature:        0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - Breaking change:    0.+1.0    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.17.0)    If the version has already been bumped on `main` since the last release, skip this. -->

If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  ## Description  - Part of #3540 - On top of #3538  Adds `BuildType` information to our existing `.wasp/{out,build}/.waspinfo` file and makes it machine-readable.  Checks it in `wasp compile` and removes the full directory output if it was built with a different wasp version or build type.  ### Motivation  I'm preparing to "unsplit" `.wasp/out` and `.wasp/build` in #3540. Given that there's variance in not only the contents of the files, but in which files are created (like whether an `.env` file is copied or not), it is safer to do a clean build of the directory when changing from `wasp start` to `wasp build` (this was already happening), but also from `wasp build` back to `wasp start`.  ## All the PRs  - #3540 - #3539 - #3538   <details> <summary>Checklist not applicable, click here to show anyway</summary>     ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty. Description: <!--   Thanks for contributing to Wasp!   Make sure to follow this PR template, so that we can speed up the review process.   It will also help you not forget important steps when making a change.   If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  ## Description  - Part of #3540  Creates a new `data BuildType = Development | Production`, replacing the `AppSpec.isBuild :: Bool` property, with a more explicit name that reflects its usage.  I created shorthand functions `isDevelopment`/`isProduction` and used them around the codebase.  ### Motivation  The `AppSpec.isBuild` property has a very overloaded name. We meant it as a "is production" property, but it would be easy to confuse with the general process of _building_ (what we call _compiling_ in our codebase).  Moreover, passing the value to functions lacks a lot of context. e.g. if we had to do `makeWaspInfo True`, the `True` could mean a bunch of different things.  ## All the PRs  - #3541 - #3540 - #3539 - #3538  <details> <summary>Checklist not applicable, click here to show anyway</summary>     ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty. Description:
- Resolves https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3444 - Needs https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3524 ## Description Publishes npm packages when tagging releases. Adds an option to the npm package build workflow to override the version (e.g. when publishing 0.20.0-rc1, we want to ignore the 0.20.0 in waspc.cabal, and publish it as an rc. Publishing gets done through the [Trusted Publishers](https://docs.npmjs.com/trusted-publishers) system, where GitHub (through the `id-token: write` permissions) can sign a request to npm proving which repo and workflow its calling from; and on the npm side you can set it up to trust that specific repo and workflows.
Screenshot
I already set it up, so we can publish without setting up any token.

Description: ## Description  The client build completes much faster than the server build, causing the Vite URLs to be scrolled away by server build logs. This changes the execution order from `parallel([series([clientBuild, clientStart]), series([serverBuild, serverStart])])` to `series([parallel([clientBuild, serverBuild]), parallel([clientStart, serverStart])])`.  **Changes:**  - Refactored `client.ts` and `server.ts` to expose separate `build*` and `start*` functions - Updated `build/index.ts` to use `Promise.all()` for parallel builds, then start both apps - Added proper error handling with safe property access for promise rejections - Added infinite promise to keep main process alive while child processes run  Both applications now start at roughly the same time, preventing the Vite URLs from being buried in server build output.  ## Type of change  - [x] **� Bug fix**  ## Checklist  - [ ] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change.   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed.  - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.  <!-- START COPILOT ORIGINAL PROMPT -->    <details>  <summary>Original prompt</summary>  >  > ---- >  > *This section details on the original issue you should resolve* >  > <issue_title>`wasp-app-runner build`: Wait until both builds are done before running them</issue_title> > <issue_description>Right now wasp-app-runner does (pseudo code): >  > ```js > parallel([ >   series([ clientBuild, clientStart ]), >   series([ serverBuild, serverStart ]) > ]) > ``` > This leads to the client usually being ready much sooner than the server, and the Vite message with the URLs being scrolled out by the server build logs. >  > I think it'd be much better to do: > ```js > series([ >   parallel([ clientBuild, serverBuild ]), >   parallel([ clientStart, serverStart ]) > ]) > ``` >  > So both sides start more or less at the same time.</issue_description> >  > ## Comments on the Issue (you are @copilot in this section) >  > <comments> > </comments> >    </details>    <!-- START COPILOT CODING AGENT SUFFIX -->  - _commit: d4ece09_

Description:
Bumps [zx](https://github.com/google/zx) from 8.8.0 to 8.8.5.
Release notes

Sourced from zx's releases.

8.8.5 — Temporary Reservoir

This release fixes the issue, when zx flushes external node_modules on linking #1348 #1349 #1355

Also globby@15.0.0 arrives here.

8.8.4 — Flange Coupling

It's time. This release updates zx internals to make the ps API and related methods ProcessPromise.kill(), kill() work on Windows systems without wmic. #1344 webpod/ps#15

  1. WMIC will be missing in Windows 11 25H2 (kernel >= 26000)
  2. The windows-latest label in GitHub Actions will migrate from Windows Server 2022 to Windows Server 2025 beginning September 2, 2025 and finishing by September 30, 2025.

https://github.blog/changelog/2025-07-31-github-actions-new-apis-and-windows-latest-migration-notice/#windows-latest-image-label-migration

8.8.3 — Sealing Gasket

Continues #1339 to prevent injections via Proxy input or custom toString() manipulations.

8.8.2 — Leaking Valve

Fixes potential cmd injection via kill() method for Windows platform. #1337 #1339. Affects the versions range 8.7.1...8.8.1.

8.8.1 — Turbo Flush

We keep improving the projects internal infra to bring more stability, safety and performance for artifacts.

Featfixes

  • Applied flags filtration for CLI-driven deps install #1308
  • Added kill() event logging #1312
  • Set SIGTERM as kill() fallback signal #1313
  • Allowed stdio() arg be an array #1311
const p = $({halt: true})`cmd` p.stdio([stream, 'ignore', 'pipe'])

Enhancements

  • Added check for zx@lite pkg contents #1317 #1316
  • Simplified ProcessPromise[asyncIterator] inners #1307
  • Updated deps: chalk 5.6.0, fs-extra 11.3.1, yaml 2.8.1 #1309 #1323 #1326
  • Added TS@next to the test matrix #1310
  • Optimized internal shell setters #1314
  • Refactored build-publish pipelines and scripts #1319 #1320 #1321 #1322 #1324 #1325 #1327
Commits
  • 1ca9270 chore: pr template formatting (#1359)
  • 52b01a1 chore: update pr template (#1358)
  • 70ecf23 chore: update issue templates (#1357)
  • 8b08fc0 chore: bump version to 8.8.5 (#1356)
  • 6045c34 chore: update globby to v15 (#1354)
  • a4d1bc2 fix: checks node_modules ref on linking (#1355)
  • 9ef6d3c fix(cli): prevent external node_modules deletion with --prefer-local (#1349)
  • cc2a4f7 ci: update zizmor to v1.15.1 (#1353)
  • b47a5f0 ci: refactor smoke tests naming (#1352)
  • 1fb8c9f ci: update gh actions (#1351)
  • Additional commits viewable in compare view

Description: <!--   Thanks for contributing to Wasp!   Make sure to follow this PR template, so that we can speed up the review process.   It will also help you not forget important steps when making a change.   If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  ## Description  Use Wasp compiler version as the lib version. This is great for shipping the libs to our users - the libs are of the same version as the compiler.  This is a bit annoying for us when developing the libs since we need manually bust the `npm` cache for the libs to test the updated libs code. That's why we implement the `./run libs:bust-cache` command.  Related to https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/2936  ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [x] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty.   If you're unsure about any of them, don't hesitate to ask for help. -->  - [x] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.  <!--   Bumping the version on `waspc/waspc.cabal`:    We still haven't reached 1.0, so the version bumping follows these rules:     - Bug fix:            0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - New feature:        0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - Breaking change:    0.+1.0    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.17.0)    If the version has already been bumped on `main` since the last release, skip this. -->

_commit: 802583b_ | @sodic had a great point, we added hashes because we had an issue _while we are developing Wasp_ which we solved for us first and then we thought about the end user. If we were to design the versioning scheme with users in mind, I'd pick to version the libs the same way as the Wasp version. Then this leaves us with cache busting as dev problem, which we can solve for ourselves with a development only script. I liked the hashing approach because it worked, but the fact is that there is a runtime penalty for each generation. I liked the `manifest.json` approach because it was compile time, but it was maybe too heavy for what was in the end a development only issue for us, Wasp compiler developers. | I see your point but I'm still not convinced. The libs are not a user facing feature, they are for ease of development for us. If we were to design the API without the current constraints, I think I'd not version them at all, as you say in the previous state of the code, the version is whatever is in the repo at the same time. I also think it's useful trying to see what an unconstrained version of this might look like, but we have to remember that no such unconstrained version will ever exist. It's a general direction not a design goal. And we need to remember that this specific tool is supposed to help _our_ ease of development. And neither solution affect users in any tangible way. I'm voting for practicality and systems that help us, rather than make us perform more steps on our regular development flow. | My take can be summarized with: > In most cases, the product's observable behaviors shouldn't change on account of our development workflows. The user should always come first. Replying to @cprecioso: > The libs are not a user facing feature, they are for ease of development for us. Hm, I would argue that they are. Not that users use and work with libs directly, but libs do change Wasp's observable behavior. And every observable behavior, no matter how innocuous, can bubble out into unpredictable scenarios we'll have to debug. > If we were to design the API without the current constraints, I think I'd not version them at all, as you say in the previous state of the code, the version is whatever is in the repo at the same time. I forgot the details but yes, I think we should design the API without adapting it to our workflow. If that means "no reason to have versions," then no versions :) > but we have to remember that no such unconstrained version will ever exist. Why not? It isn't possible? I forgot the details. > I'm voting for practicality and systems that help us, rather than make us perform more steps on our regular development flow. Me too, but consider this. Besides polluting observable behavior, the hashes approach carries a complexity cost. We must: - Document and remember how this system works. - Reason about it every time we want to make a change. - Know that a part of our Wasp doesn't serve a purpose within the product. Anyone new coming to this code will have a more difficult time putting all the pieces together when analyzing what's broken, or what to refactor/change. Not only because the system is more complex, but also because it's untypical for DX optimizations to make their way into the product. We can add comments, sure. But it all adds to the complexity and rigidness. To sum up, there are three costs: - Runtime (probably negligible) - Polluting observable behavior on behalf of DX - Complexity (which implies rigidness and fragility, more things to go wrong) Finally, a hot take: how about not dealing with the cache at all for now? We can address it if and when it becomes a problem. Perhaps it never will because libs are meant to be a standalone thing. To summarize, I vote for: - Designing the API with the user in mind. If the user doesn't need something, we don't have it. - Don't worry about cache busting until it becomes a problem. @infomiho Where do you stand on this? I won't block the change regardless of what we decide so you can make the call as far as I'm concerned. You'll better anticipate future cache problems. @cprecioso's concerns are valid as well, so no option is too bad IMO. The only thing I'd like to avoid is the manifest approach - it's even more complex than using hashes and causes different behavior in dev compared to production. | > but we have to remember that no such unconstrained version will ever exist. @sodic sorry, I was overloading the word "version" there. Changed for a better word: > I also think it's useful trying to see what an unconstrained _solution_ for this might look like, but we have to remember that no such unconstrained _solution_ will ever exist. I was just musing on the general truism that you basically never have a 100% perfect solution for a problem, that there are always some constraints why solutions have to be modifyd or dirty'd. i.e.: nothing interesting for this specific discussion � | @cprecioso @sodic I'm gathering input from you two and trying to see what I prefer and then implement it. So my latest stance is reflected in the latest implementation (this PR). I accepted hashing as a _obvious and practical_ solution to a painful problem in development. The extra arguments from FIlip about us doing complex work that doesn't benefit the user (or that the users won't even feel the caching problem) pushed me to find a simple enough dev workflow that now in the face of it, I want to adopt it vs. the hashing solution. The hashing solution has the element of always needing to compute the hash in runtime. I disliked that but that was the price I was willing to pay since the alternatives at the time seemed even worse. Lib versioned as Wasp version -> users don't have caching issues and it's the simplest solution. For us, we run a script when we need to test the latest iteration of the lib. > Don't worry about cache busting until it becomes a problem. It is a problem for us in dev, now - every time you go to test the lib to see how it integrates, you need to bust the cache. I'd go for _this_ solution _now_ and iterate on it as we work on libs. | That's fine, I think my point is understood and I trust your judgement, happy to defer to your solution then. | @sodic @cprecioso ready for another look | I'm not sure if `[absdir|/|]` is portable, the CI is throwing an error, I'll research what can be done: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/actions/runs/20819859556/job/59805172601?pr=3501 | @infomiho you might have to do `[absdirP|/|]` | @sodic ready for another look, moved the lib building scripts to JS in a separate PR: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3648

Description: <!--   Thanks for contributing to Wasp!   Make sure to follow this PR template, so that we can speed up the review process.   It will also help you not forget important steps when making a change.   If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  ## Description  - Prompted by https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3483#pullrequestreview-3572325998  Moves npm packages from `waspc/packages/*` to `waspc/data/packages/*`, removes the scripts that copied them from the former to the latter, and updates scripts and documentation that pointed to the old location.  ### Motivation  We embed npm packages as part of the CLI functionality (wasp deploy, tsconfig, studio, etc). These lived in `waspc/packages`, and we had a script to copy them to `waspc/data/packages`, where they were actually picked up by the build.  The reason to have this two-stage system was to enable us to remove node_modules when we copied to the data dir, so that we wouldn't ship that folder to users (the CLI does an `npm install` on these packages on first run).  This solution proved to be ineffective, as we were bundling them anyway. I realized that and updated the packaging script to read and follow the Cabal data file globs, which already filtered out the node_modules. (#3483)  This means that the initial copy step from `waspc/packages` to `waspc/data/packages` is now redundant, so we can eliminate it and simplify the process.  ### Why were we bundling `node_modules` into the binary even though we filtered them at the copy step?  In the build process, we are nonetheless calling `npm install` on the packages in their new location, recreating the `node_modules` folder. The packaging script would then just blindly copy them into the final tarball.  ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->   ## Checklist   **Checklist not applicable** I did test the final built artifacts in a Wasp project and run every command that depends on an embedded package, successfully.  <details> <summary>Show it anyway</summary>  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty.   If you're unsure about any of them, don't hesitate to ask for help. -->  - [x] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.  <!--   Bumping the version on `waspc/waspc.cabal`:    We still haven't reached 1.0, so the version bumping follows these rules:     - Bug fix:            0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - New feature:        0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - Breaking change:    0.+1.0    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.17.0)    If the version has already been bumped on `main` since the last release, skip this. -->    </details>

Description: ## Description  During #2482, @cprecioso noticed that our example apps don't work on older versions of Node we claim to support. This PR explores what to do about it. Discussion welcome.  The main question is: Which tests do we run on which versions of Node? After we decide that, we can deal with how and where we specify this: - I first choose a repostiroy variable for now - @cprecioso then suggested using `.nvmrc` which was a much better idea (comment below)  ### Final solution We now run all tests and builds with Wasp's lowest supported vresion of Node. Unfortunatley, not all places could read the version from `.nvmrc`, so there is some duplication. More info on this in the comments.  For consistency, I used `waspc/.nvmrc` as a source-of-truth for the Node version wherever I could, not just where it matters to the user. Description: We want a channel to tell users about important updates in wasp (e.g., new versions, security updates). We want to do this with a new CLI command: `wasp news`.  Here's how our prototype currently works: - Calling `wasp news` explicitly prints out all news of all levels. - `wasp start` commands prints all news once per day until the user runs explicitly runs `wasp news` to acknowledge them.  What's left to decide: - How many priorities/levels do we want and what's their semantics (i.e., when to show and repeat what). - Decide when to show news (just `wasp start`, all commands, etc.). - Decide how we want to mark news as "read:"   - When the user runs `wasp news` as we currently do.   - Interactively - we block and wait until they acknowledge them. This might be annoying in the CI. - Decide whether and how we want to mark unseen news.  What's left to implement: - Implement the new/changed features we decide to have. - Polish the code and remove all the hacks. - Replace the news server with a static file.
Discuss Information: Decisions @Martinsos and I made: - Wasp news have 3 levels: **Critical** (e.g., security vulnerabilities), **Important** (e.g., Wasp versions), and **Info** (not sure yet but maybe launch weeks). - Users can see all news with the `wasp news` command. - Running `wasp news` marks all shown news as "seen." - We draw the news from a static json file on our website. - We show some news during `wasp start` and we check for new news once per day:   - **Critical** news (e.g., security vulenrabilities) block `wasp start` and ask users for explicit confirmation before the command continues. When the user interactively confirms they've read the news, they are considered "seen" and no longer show up on `wasp start`. Since we're only showing news on `wasp start`, this won't cause problems in the CI (plus, we'll add an env var for disabling `wasp news`).   - **Important** news (e.g., new Wasp versions) are shown on `wasp start` but don't block execution. We fetch and show them once per day until the user explicitly runs `wasp news` to mark them as seen. - **Info** news are only shown when users run `wasp news`, never on `wasp start`. | > Important news (e.g., new Wasp versions) are shown on wasp start but don't block execution. We fetch and show them once per day until the user explicitly runs wasp news to mark them as seen.  I think this might be too aggressive, I'd maybe show them up to 3 times or something like that. Or just once.   EDIT: Okay, it's once per day. But still, what if there are 4, 5 important news that the user never cleared. Do news have an expiry date?

Description: ## Description This is a libs cache busting experiment: - When we ship libs to users, the libs can have the same version as the compiler (e.g. 0.19.2). Compiler version 0.19.2 will always use lib version 0.19.2. Users don't need cache busting since once the lib is shipped with the compiler, that's it. Once they update the compiler, the lib has the new version and `npm` will install the new version normally. - When we (Wasp developers) work on Wasp, we want the cache busting to work on each change to the lib. We don't want to make a change in the lib and not see those changes because `npm` cached it because the version stayed e.g. 0.20.0. We can manually change the version in lib `pacakge.json` on each change, but that's annoying. One thing we can do is to generate a random version on each lib build while in dev so `npm` always uses the latest code. The issue with random versions is that this means random tarball names and Wasp compiler won't know the name of the file in its `data` dir that it needs to copy to the user's dir. To enable using random versions, I introduced a `manifest.json` file that contains the lib -> tarball name mapping: ```json { "@wasp.sh/lib-auth": "wasp.sh-lib-auth-0.0.0-dev-638e73b3c7071af4.tgz" } ``` Our compiler loads the `manifest.json` to know what is the tarball name for each lib. This gives the ability to have: - a random tarball version while developing Wasp (`wasp.sh-lib-auth-0.0.0-dev-638e73b3c7071af4.tgz`) - and a fixed tarball name with the Wasp compiler version (`wasp.sh-lib-auth-0.19.2.tgz`). I made it with Template Haskell so the `manifest.json` is loaded in compile time. ## Type of change - [x] **� Just code/docs improvement** - [ ] **� Bug fix** - [ ] **� New/improved feature** - [ ] **� Breaking change** ## Checklist - [x] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended. - � Tests and apps: - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`. - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed. - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed. - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa). - � Documentation: - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`. - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_ - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change. - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`. - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.
Discuss Information: > Document how to bust caches during development.  It's quite annoying - you have to delete the `package-lock.json` each time you want to test changes. I suspect we'll quickly get tired of this.  Check what we'd have to do here: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3501/changes#diff-0ee39dc1b0edc3e3323e2f964524eba4fb605a9068c818f070400f3e145901aaR37-R48  We'd keep modifying the `package-lock.json` if we nuke it or run a complex command to refresh the cache.  | Closing this in favor of: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3501 since I found an _okay_ way to bust the cache and added a new `./run libs:bust-cache` command.

Discuss Information: The comment says: ```hs -- Server auth deps must be installed in the SDK because "@lucia-auth/adapter-prisma" -- lists prisma/client as a dependency. -- Installing it inside .wasp/out/server/node_modules would also -- install prisma/client in the same folder, which would cause our -- runtime to load the wrong (uninitialized prisma/client). ```  After I moved the deps from SDK to server, the same problem happened but in a different place.  The old problem was that we had the `server` package and the SDK package which would both install `@prisma/client`. We got rid of the `server` install by moving its auth deps to the SDK.  The new problem is similar, the `server` is now part of the npm workspace, so its deps get installed in the root project. But we still have the SDK which is outside of the npm workspace and we still have two instances of `@prisma/client`.  I believe these deps should be moved to the server deps after the SDK becomes a part of the workspaces. This issue won't happen then. Check out #3233

Description: ## Description ### What are we adding? Before this PR, `waspc/e2e-tests` only had snapshot tests. They function in a way where we want to keep their artifacts (snapshots) as part of the git-tracked files. This PR adds **regular CLI tests** that validate command execution without persisting any artifacts. Before going to deep into the solution be aware that we are aiming to refactor the whole `waspc/e2e-tests` by moving them away from the current unix shell commands approach. Instead we will write all of our logic in pure Haskell. This is to make them cross-platform compatible. That is why the tests which would require complex unix shell command implementations were only partially implemented. As the unix shell commands logic will be dropped, they are not worth the time investment. More on cross platform compatible e2e tests here: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3404 In short, we want a net-positive gain from these tests, not complete coverage. Standard good code quality rules still apply. The CLI commands table, which the implementation was based on can be found here: https://www.notion.so/wasp-lang/CLI-Testing-24518a74854c8051b0fef88533592504?source=copy_link In there we mention which commands we are testing, which we are not, and why. Note, as we now have additional variant of tests besides the snapshot tests, I decided to refactor the structure so that the snapshot tests logic is moved under the `waspc/e2e-tests/SnapshotTest` folder. Changes related to moving snapshot tests folder are in a separate PR: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3468 **New Directory structure:** ``` e2e-tests/ ├── SnapshotTest/ # Snapshot-based tests (compare golden vs current output) │ ├── snapshots/ # Git-tracked golden files │ └── *.hs # Snapshot test modules ├── Test/ # Regular tests (validate exit codes, no persisted artifacts) │ ├── test-outputs/ # Temporary test outputs (gitignored) │ └── *.hs # Test modules ├── SnapshotTest.hs # Snapshot test framework ├── Test.hs # Regular test framework └── Main.hs # Entry point ``` ### How new tests work Tests consist of a name (which creates a same-named directory under `Test/test-outputs/`) and test cases. Each test case is a unix shell command executed **sequentially**. This is different from snapshot tests which only ever have a single unix shell command. This approach makes it much easier to build tests (since we can split them into steps), and also allows validating individual test cases separately. Validity is determined by checking exit status. Sequentiality is enforced by executing the unix shell commands while creating `Hspec`'s `Spec`. The captured exit status is translated into SUCCESS/FAIL after running all of the tests. This is because I couldn't force Tasty to run them in sequence even while using the [`sequential` function](https://hackage-content.haskell.org/package/hspec-2.11.14/docs/Test-Hspec.html#v:sequential). ~~Blocked by https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3480 which enables us to use the `wasp db reset` command non-interactively. This PR assumes the above PR is merged.~~ Fixes: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/1963 ## Type of change - [x] **� Just code/docs improvement** - [ ] **� Bug fix** - [ ] **� New/improved feature** - [ ] **� Breaking change**

Description:
Bumps [node-forge](https://github.com/digitalbazaar/forge) from 1.3.1 to 1.3.2.
Changelog

Sourced from node-forge's changelog.

1.3.2 - 2025-11-25

Security

  • HIGH: ASN.1 Validator Desynchronization
    • An Interpretation Conflict (CWE-436) vulnerability in node-forge versions 1.3.1 and below enables remote, unauthenticated attackers to craft ASN.1 structures to desynchronize schema validations, yielding a semantic divergence that may bypass downstream cryptographic verifications and security decisions.
    • Reported by Hunter Wodzenski.
    • CVE ID: CVE-2025-12816
    • GHSA ID: GHSA-5gfm-wpxj-wjgq
  • HIGH: ASN.1 Unbounded Recursion
    • An Uncontrolled Recursion (CWE-674) vulnerability in node-forge versions 1.3.1 and below enables remote, unauthenticated attackers to craft deep ASN.1 structures that trigger unbounded recursive parsing. This leads to a Denial-of-Service (DoS) via stack exhaustion when parsing untrusted DER inputs.
    • Reported by Hunter Wodzenski.
    • CVE ID: CVE-2025-66031
    • GHSA ID: GHSA-554w-wpv2-vw27
  • MODERATE: ASN.1 OID Integer Truncation
    • An Integer Overflow (CWE-190) vulnerability in node-forge versions 1.3.1 and below enables remote, unauthenticated attackers to craft ASN.1 structures containing OIDs with oversized arcs. These arcs may be decoded as smaller, trusted OIDs due to 32-bit bitwise truncation, enabling the bypass of downstream OID-based security decisions.
    • Reported by Hunter Wodzenski.
    • CVE ID: CVE-2025-66030
    • GHSA ID: GHSA-65ch-62r8-g69g

Fixed

  • [asn1] Fix for vulnerability identified by CVE-2025-12816 PKCS#12 MAC verification bypass due to missing macData enforcement and improper asn1.validate routine.
  • [asn1] Add fromDer() max recursion depth check.
    • Add a asn1.maxDepth global configurable maximum depth of 256.
    • Add a asn1.fromDer() per-call maxDepth option.
    • NOTE: The default maximum is assumed to be higher than needed for valid data. If this assumption is false then this could be a breaking change. Please file an issue if there are use cases that need a higher maximum.
    • NOTE: The per-call maxDepth parameter has not been exposed up through all of the API stack due to the complexities involved. Please file an issue if there are use cases that require this instead of changing the default maximum.
  • [asn1] Improve OID handling.
    • Error on parsed OID values larger than 2**32 - 1.
    • Error on DER OID values larger than 2**53 - 1 .

  • 2598244 Update changelog.

Description: ## Description  `wasp build start --help` (and other commands using `parseArguments`) incorrectly displayed "Parsing arguments failed" and exited with code 1 instead of 0.  **Root cause**: `parseArguments` didn't distinguish between help requests and actual parsing errors. When optparse-applicative returns a `ParserFailure`, it includes an exit code—`ExitSuccess` for help/version, `ExitFailure` for errors—but the code only checked if it was a failure.  **Solution**: Modified `parserResultToEither` to inspect the exit code. For `ExitSuccess`, call `handleParseResult` to print help and exit with 0. For `ExitFailure`, return error as before.  **Changes**: - `Wasp.Cli.Util.Parser.parseArguments` now returns `m (Either String a)` to support IO for printing help - Updated call sites in Description: Our `.env.vault` files don’t contain production env var values. Each app should have a development vault and a production vault.  We found this out the hard way during 7/18 (when someone deleted all Fly apps (EDIT: it was us hehe, Filip is making a joke, I am writing this edit because it otherwise sounds like somebody came in and deleted them and we don't know who). Getting our apps back up was difficult because we had to go hunting for production secrets all around the place: dig out Google secrets from our Google cloud, call Martin for Github secrets, etc.  The solution is creating a production `.env.vault` file. Along with https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3026, this will solve most of our deployment env var problems.  I did some reserach and don't think this approach has security issues. If it does, we should at least keep production env vars in our password manager.

Description: ## Description  Fixes #2953.  Some decisions I made, feel free to challenge:  - We're only testing whether `wasp new:ai` exits successfully and generates the project. We're not looking at the project:    - This was decided in the issue. Testing much more than this isn't worth it. We just want to catch big bugs like [this one](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/2951) before the release. - Wasp AI test is its own thing:   - It's not part of Snapshot tests because we don't care about the generated context. Adapting snapshot tests to work with this would be both costly and incorrect, as they wouldn't be testing snapshots.   - It's not a part of starters tests for a similar reason. Starter tests are headless test verifying the apps work correctly. In this case, we only care about the exit code of `wasp new:ai`.   - We don't yet have a platform for running these kinds of tests (other than CI). I believe @FranjoMindek is working on one but the PR is not yet ready. - Testing steps are only listed in CI. There is no local `./run something` way to run this test. Reasons:   - The local test would be cumbersome to set up (where to put the generated project, how to clean it up).   - The "test" is simple enough for us to run manually.   - We don't plan to often run the test locally.   - Even if we're running the test locally, we'll probably want to run all the steps manually anyway.   ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [x] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty. Description:
Bumps [zx](https://github.com/google/zx) from 8.3.2 to 8.8.5.
Release notes

Sourced from zx's releases.

8.8.5 — Temporary Reservoir

This release fixes the issue, when zx flushes external node_modules on linking #1348 #1349 #1355

Also globby@15.0.0 arrives here.

8.8.4 — Flange Coupling

It's time. This release updates zx internals to make the ps API and related methods ProcessPromise.kill(), kill() work on Windows systems without wmic. #1344 webpod/ps#15

  1. WMIC will be missing in Windows 11 25H2 (kernel >= 26000)
  2. The windows-latest label in GitHub Actions will migrate from Windows Server 2022 to Windows Server 2025 beginning September 2, 2025 and finishing by September 30, 2025.

https://github.blog/changelog/2025-07-31-github-actions-new-apis-and-windows-latest-migration-notice/#windows-latest-image-label-migration

8.8.3 — Sealing Gasket

Continues #1339 to prevent injections via Proxy input or custom toString() manipulations.

8.8.2 — Leaking Valve

Fixes potential cmd injection via kill() method for Windows platform. #1337 #1339. Affects the versions range 8.7.1...8.8.1.

8.8.1 — Turbo Flush

We keep improving the projects internal infra to bring more stability, safety and performance for artifacts.

Featfixes

  • Applied flags filtration for CLI-driven deps install #1308
  • Added kill() event logging #1312
  • Set SIGTERM as kill() fallback signal #1313
  • Allowed stdio() arg be an array #1311
const p = $({halt: true})`cmd` p.stdio([stream, 'ignore', 'pipe'])

Enhancements

  • Added check for zx@lite pkg contents #1317 #1316
  • Simplified ProcessPromise[asyncIterator] inners #1307
  • Updated deps: chalk 5.6.0, fs-extra 11.3.1, yaml 2.8.1 #1309 #1323 #1326
  • Added TS@next to the test matrix #1310
  • Optimized internal shell setters #1314
  • Refactored build-publish pipelines and scripts #1319 #1320 #1321 #1322 #1324 #1325 #1327

8.8.0 — Pressure Tested

This release enhances the coherence between the ProcessPromise and the Streams API, eliminating the need for certain script-level workarounds.

✨ New Features

unpipe() — Selectively stop piping

You can now call .unpipe() to stop data transfer from a source to a destination without closing any of the pair. #1302

Latest commit: 2c85b90

Preview URL: https://300f2db6.wasp-docs-on-main.pages.dev
Branch Preview URL: https://martin-win-unstyled-term-out.wasp-docs-on-main.pages.dev
[View logs](https://dash.cloudflare.com/?to=/75c37602a6b75c790961219411cb1b92/pages/view/wasp-docs-on-main/300f2db6-32ae-4c5c-8202-7f1ee9d9c88f) | Ok, so question is: do we want to turn it off on Windows for real? What we know is that on Win server 22, in powershell, ansii codes work. In cmd, they don't. I haven't tried it yet on Win 11. So it might be we want to leave it on by default, or maybe we do some smart checking for support. But we still could offer the option to not color the output via env flag even if by default we go with it. Btw is there a standardized name for such env flag? Also look into that. Potentially useful info: https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/powershell/module/Microsoft.PowerShell.Core/about/about_ansi_terminals?view=powershell-5.1 . Also, might we worth checking what others re doing. Here is a quick output from asking Claude Code to analyze `bun` codebase (gotta check how/if it is correct, this is direct from AI): ``` 1. Bun Explicitly Enables ANSI on Windows Bun proactively enables Virtual Terminal Processing on Windows at startup by calling SetConsoleMode() with the ENABLE_VIRTUAL_TERMINAL_PROCESSING flag: // src/output.zig:172-218 _ = c.SetConsoleMode(stdout, w.ENABLE_PROCESSED_OUTPUT | std.os.windows.ENABLE_VIRTUAL_TERMINAL_PROCESSING | w.ENABLE_WRAP_AT_EOL_OUTPUT | mode ); This enables ANSI escape sequences on Windows 10+ (including Windows Server 2016+). 2. No Differentiation Between cmd.exe and PowerShell Critical finding: Bun does NOT differentiate between cmd.exe and PowerShell. Both are treated identically: - Uses uv_guess_handle(fd) on Windows which returns UV_TTY for any console - Applies the same console mode flags to both shells - No shell-specific detection code exists 3. Windows Build-Based Color Detection Bun detects color support based on Windows build numbers, not shell type: // src/js/internal/tty.ts:56-172 if (process.platform === "win32") { const { release } = require("node:os"); OSRelease = release().split("."); if (+OSRelease[0] >= 10) { const build = +OSRelease[2]; if (build >= 14931) return COLORS_16m; // TrueColor (16M colors) if (build >= 10586) return COLORS_256; // 256 colors } return COLORS_16; // 16 colors for older Windows } Windows Server 2022 has build 20348+, which means it should support full TrueColor (24-bit) in both cmd.exe and PowerShell. 4. ANSI Support Detection Method To detect ANSI support, Bun checks: // src/js/internal/util/colors.ts:16-22 shouldColorize(stream) { if (process.env.FORCE_COLOR !== undefined) { return getColorDepth(process.env) > 2; } return stream?.isTTY && (stream.getColorDepth?.() > 2 : true); } 5. Environment Variable Support Bun respects these environment variables (in priority order): - FORCE_COLOR - Force colors (empty/"1"/"true" = 16, "2" = 256, "3" = TrueColor) - NODE_DISABLE_COLORS - Disable colors - NO_COLOR - Disable colors - TERM=dumb - Disable colors ``` | @FranjoMindek and @cprecioso linked https://clig.dev/#output , and this is the most relevant part: > Disable color if your program is not in a terminal or the user requested it. These things should disable colors: > > - stdout or stderr is not an interactive terminal (a TTY). It’s best to individually check—if you’re piping stdout to another program, it’s still useful to get colors on stderr. > - The NO_COLOR environment variable is set and it is not empty (regardless of its value). > - The TERM environment variable has the value dumb. > - The user passes the option --no-color. > - You may also want to add a MYAPP_NO_COLOR environment variable in case users want to disable color specifically for your program. > > Further reading: [no-color.org](https://no-color.org/)

_commit: 7e8e109_ | ## GEO/SEO Improvement Suggestions Here are priority improvements for AI citation readiness and search visibility, ranked by impact: 1. **Add `keywords` and `description` to frontmatter** — Missing entirely. Should have 10+ long-tail keywords (e.g., `"npm workspaces tutorial"`, `"how npm workspaces work"`, `"monorepo node_modules"`, `"javascript monorepo dependencies"`) and a 1-2 sentence meta description optimized for target keywords. 2. **Add a `## TL;DR` section at the top** — The style guide requires this for technical posts. AI engines prioritize extractable summaries — 3-4 bullet points summarizing key takeaways would be the single highest-impact addition. 3. **Add a `## FAQ` section at the bottom** — 3-5 common questions (e.g., "What are npm workspaces?", "How do workspaces resolve dependencies?", "When should I use workspaces?") would directly match AI query patterns and enable `FAQPage` schema. 4. **Make the title keyword-rich** — "Workspaces under the hood" is vague for search. Something like "How npm Workspaces Work Under the Hood: Dependencies, Symlinks, and Node.js Resolution" would rank better and match AI query patterns. 5. **Add a bold definitional sentence early in the "Enter workspaces" section** — A crisp, extractable definition like: "**npm workspaces** are a feature that lets you manage multiple JavaScript packages in a single project, sharing dependencies and enabling cross-package imports." AI engines extract bold definitional sentences. 6. **Include at least one original data point from Wasp's experience** — e.g., install time savings, dependency deduplication numbers. Original data is the #1 thing AI engines cite. 7. **Add `last_update` date to frontmatter** — Freshness signals account for ~10% of RAG retrieval weight.

Description:
Bumps [glob](https://github.com/isaacs/node-glob) to 11.1.0 and updates ancestor dependency . These dependencies need to be updated together. Updates `glob` from 11.0.2 to 11.1.0
Changelog

Sourced from glob's changelog.

changeglob

12

  • Remove the unsafe --shell option. The --shell option is now ONLY supported on known shells where the behavior can be implemented safely.

11.1

GHSA-5j98-mcp5-4vw2

  • Add the --shell option for the command line, with a warning that this is unsafe. (It will be removed in v12.)
  • Add the --cmd-arg/-g as a way to safely add positional arguments to the command provided to the CLI tool.
  • Detect commands with space or quote characters on known shells, and pass positional arguments to them safely, avoiding shell:true execution.

11.0

  • Drop support for node before v20

10.4

  • Add includeChildMatches: false option
  • Export the Ignore class

10.3

  • Add --default -p flag to provide a default pattern
  • exclude symbolic links to directories when follow and nodir are both set

10.2

  • Add glob cli

10.1

  • Return '.' instead of the empty string '' when the current working directory is returned as a match.
  • Add posix: true option to return / delimited paths, even on Windows.

10.0.0

  • No default exports, only named exports

Description:
Bumps [js-yaml](https://github.com/nodeca/js-yaml) from 3.14.1 to 3.14.2.
Changelog

Sourced from js-yaml's changelog.

[3.14.2] - 2025-11-15

Security

  • Backported v4.1.1 fix to v3

[4.1.1] - 2025-11-12

Security

  • Fix prototype pollution issue in yaml merge (<<) operator.

[4.1.0] - 2021-04-15

Added

  • Types are now exported as yaml.types.XXX.
  • Every type now has options property with original arguments kept as they were (see yaml.types.int.options as an example).

Changed

  • Schema.extend() now keeps old type order in case of conflicts (e.g. Schema.extend([ a, b, c ]).extend([ b, a, d ]) is now ordered as abcd instead of cbad).

[4.0.0] - 2021-01-03

Changed

  • Check migration guide to see details for all breaking changes.
  • Breaking: "unsafe" tags !!js/function, !!js/regexp, !!js/undefined are moved to js-yaml-js-types package.
  • Breaking: removed safe* functions. Use load, loadAll, dump instead which are all now safe by default.
  • yaml.DEFAULT_SAFE_SCHEMA and yaml.DEFAULT_FULL_SCHEMA are removed, use yaml.DEFAULT_SCHEMA instead.
  • yaml.Schema.create(schema, tags) is removed, use schema.extend(tags) instead.
  • !!binary now always mapped to Uint8Array on load.
  • Reduced nesting of /lib folder.
  • Parse numbers according to YAML 1.2 instead of YAML 1.1 (01234 is now decimal, 0o1234 is octal, 1:23 is parsed as string instead of base60).
  • dump() no longer quotes :, [, ], (, ) except when necessary, #470, #557.
  • Line and column in exceptions are now formatted as (X:Y) instead of at line X, column Y (also present in compact format), #332.
  • Code snippet created in exceptions now contains multiple lines with line numbers.
  • dump() now serializes undefined as null in collections and removes keys with undefined in mappings, #571.
  • dump() with skipInvalid=true now serializes invalid items in collections as null.
  • Custom tags starting with ! are now dumped as !tag instead of !<!tag>, #576.
  • Custom tags starting with tag:yaml.org,2002: are now shorthanded using !!, #258.

Added

  • Added .mjs (es modules) support.
  • Added quotingType and forceQuotes options for dumper to configure string literal style, #290, #529.
  • Added styles: { '!!null': 'empty' } option for dumper (serializes { foo: null } as "foo: "), #570.

... (truncated)

Commits
  • 9963d36 3.14.2 released
  • 10d3c8e dist rebuild
  • 5278870 fix prototype pollution in merge (<<) (#731)
  • See full diff in compare view

Description: ## Description  Wasp 0.18.0 changed Tailwind config files from CJS to ESM. The exact version `3.2.7` in the docs doesn't support ESM configs, causing Tailwind to fail.   Updated the install command from `npm install -D tailwindcss@3.2.7` to `npm install -D tailwindcss@3` to install the latest v3 version, which supports ESM. This aligns with how starter templates already specify Tailwind (e.g., `^3.4.17`).  **Files changed:** - `web/docs/project/css-frameworks.md` (latest docs) - `web/versioned_docs/version-0.18.0/project/css-frameworks.md` (0.18.0 docs) - `web/docs/migration-guides/migrate-from-0-17-to-0-18.md` (latest migration guide) - `web/versioned_docs/version-0.18.0/migration-guides/migrate-from-0-17-to-0-18.md` (0.18.0 migration guide) - `waspc/src/Wasp/AI/GenerateNewProject/InitialFiles.hs` (AI generator) - `waspc/src/Wasp/Generator/SdkGenerator/DepVersions.hs` (SDK generator) - `waspc/ChangeLog.md` (added documentation and improvement entries)  **Migration guide updates:** Added instruction under the "Rename Tailwind Configuration Files" section to update Tailwind CSS to the latest v3 for compatibility with ESM configuration.  **AI generator update:** Updated the default Tailwind version from `^3.2.7` to `^3.4.17` to ensure AI-generated projects use a version compatible with ESM configs.  **SDK generator update:** Updated the Tailwind version from `3.2.7` to `3.4.17` in `DepVersions.hs` to ensure the SDK generator uses a version compatible with ESM configs.  **ChangeLog updates:** - Added documentation entry about updating the install command in docs - Added improvement entry about raising the internal minimal requirement from `^3.2.7` to `^3.4.17`  ## Type of change  - [x] **� Just code/docs improvement** - [ ] **� Bug fix** - [ ] **� New/improved feature** - [ ] **� Breaking change**  ## Checklist  - [x] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests and apps:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change.   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** in `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** in `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **example apps** in `examples/`, as needed.     - [ ] _(if you updated `examples/tutorials`)_ I updated the tutorial in the docs (and vice versa).  - � Documentation:    - [x] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [x] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [x] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** in `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.  - Closes #3377  <!-- START COPILOT CODING AGENT TIPS --> ---  � You can make Copilot smarter by setting up custom instructions, customizing its development environment and configuring Model Context Protocol (MCP) servers. Learn more [Copilot coding agent tips](https://gh.io/copilot-coding-agent-tips) in the docs.

Description: <!--   Thanks for contributing to Wasp!   Make sure to follow this PR template, so that we can speed up the review process.   It will also help you not forget important steps when making a change.   If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  ## Description  The automation was incorrectly labelling our own PRs as "External". This is because apparently we're just marked as `CONTRIBUTOR`s [(logs)](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/actions/runs/19425641131/job/55588775189#step:2:69).  "Contributor" means "anyone who has commited in the past" [according to the docs](https://docs.github.com/en/graphql/reference/enums#commentauthorassociation), which is suboptimal (even if someone has committed in the past they might still be external). So we shouldn't use that to check if the PR is from a team member.  And given we need the `pull_request_target` event, which can lend itself to secret exfiltration, I don't want to use a privileged token to check the member association.  Therefore, I think the best course of action is to remove this action altogether.  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty. <!--   Thanks for contributing to Wasp!   Make sure to follow this PR template, so that we can speed up the review process.   It will also help you not forget important steps when making a change.   If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  ## Description  Fixes #3327   ### What was the cause?   The `BlogLayout` component was used for both list of blog posts and single blog post. The layout used the `row` class **always** which had `-16px` horizontal margin but used `container` class **only on single blog post page**. The `container` class and `row` classes are meant to be used **together** because the `container`'s `16px` padding neutralizes the negative `16px` margin of the `row`.  The list page didn't want to use the container so the hero section can be 100% width, so I've now made it explicit that there are two layouts: `BlogPostsLayout` and `SingleBlogPostLayout`.  ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [x] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty. @sodic I can see that the links are wrong, sorry! I did the PR descriptions late at night and my mind was fried apparently. I fixed the links here and I will take the opportunity to re-check all the links between PRs. **With regards to this PR:** - The correct "on top of" is https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3344 - The correct "as part of" is https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3338 In https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3338 I am adding a command to be able to generate a template from a folder (the current design is `wasp new:custom -f ~/dev/open-saas` but that is subject to change). I will definitely need a _directory argument parser_ for it, that's why I'm adding it here. I am adding this on top of https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3344, where I extracted the _file argument parser_ from its previous place (the _env var argument parser_) so I can colocate both the file path and the dir path argument parsers. | ## Deploying wasp-docs-on-main with    Cloudflare Pages

Description: <!--   Thanks for contributing to Wasp!   Make sure to follow this PR template, so that we can speed up the review process.   It will also help you not forget important steps when making a change.   If you don't know how to fill any of the sections below, it's okay to leave   them blank and ask for help. -->  ## Description  The logic in ProjectDescription is not easy to follow. While adding the functionality for #3338, I refactored it to make it simpler. I extracted the refactor to this PR.  In the previous version, we were calling down to slightly different implementations of similar functions that each dealt with different variables being missing, and the functions were calling down to each other, which made the logic more difficult to follow.  It also made extension difficult (like the one I need to do in #3338), because the functions needed very specific combinations of missing/present information.  In the new version, the missing information is handled at the top level, and all the extra functions consolidated to a single one that only deals with present information. That way we remove the multiple layers of indirection, and it's very easy to see where we get or ask for each piece of information. This structure also makes it easy for me to extend and share logic in #3338.  ## Connections  - Working towards https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3073 - Part of https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3338 -- I split the refactors into their own PRs for ease of review: 	- https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3340 	- https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3341 	- https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3342 	- https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3343 	- https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3344 	- https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3345 	- https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3346  ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [x] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty. ## Description The action was not erroring out in the case of it not finding the a successful workflow run. The empty string was then passed to `gh run download` which then just picks up the first run it finds instead of erroring out (this seems to be the intended behaviour). Now we explicitly check and error out. Example run: https://github.com/wasp-lang/open-saas/actions/runs/19230001646/job/54966078262?pr=540#step:6:36 ## Type of change - [x] **� Just code/docs improvement** - [ ] **� Bug fix** - [ ] **� New/improved feature** - [ ] **� Breaking change** ## Checklist ### Not applicable - [ ] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended. - � Tests: - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** at `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`. - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** at `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed. - � Documentation: - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`. - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_ - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change. - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** at `web/docs/migration-guides/`. - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.

We need to use `pull_request_target`, as intended by GitHub: https://securitylab.github.com/resources/github-actions-preventing-pwn-requests/#:~:text=The%20reason%20to%20introduce%20the%20pull_request_target%20trigger.  However, this event is dangerous _when paired with a code checkout_ (explained in the link above), so I explicitly add a CAUTION message. Description: ## Description  As discussed in #3318, we need a small equivalent to the `run` script that works, at least, for building Wasp on Windows. It is a temporary script before having a cross-platform script to handle this task (see #3151). This PR adds a `run.ps1` script that is compatible to PSVersion 5.1, which is the default version on Window 10/11.   Example: ``` ./run USAGE   run <command>  COMMANDS   build             Builds the Haskell project.   build:all         Builds the Haskell project + all sub-projects (i.e. TS packages).   wasp-cli <args>   Runs the dev version of wasp executable while forwarding arguments.                     Builds the project (hs) first if needed. Doesn't require you to be in the waspc project to run it. ```  ## Type of change  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [ ] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [x] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  - [ ] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** at `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** at `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** at `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.
