Description: ## Description  Fixes #3321  It does two things: - pins the Postgres version used by `wasp start db` to version 18 - uses the correct path for the Docker volume mount for Postgres 18  ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [x] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty.   If you're unsure about any of them, don't hesitate to ask for help. -->  - [x] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests:    - [ ] ~~I added **unit tests** for my change.~~ This is a hotfix and there were no existing unit tests to update.   - [ ] ~~_(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed.~~ This was a configuration change, not a regression.   - [ ] ~~_(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** at `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.~~   - [ ] ~~_(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** at `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.~~  - � Documentation:    - [ ] ~~_(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.~~  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [x] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] ~~_(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** at `web/docs/migration-guides/`.~~   - [x] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.  <!--   Bumping the version on `waspc/waspc.cabal`:    We still haven't reached 1.0, so the version bumping follows these rules:     - Bug fix:            0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - New feature:        0.X.+1    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.16.4)     - Breaking change:    0.+1.0    (e.g. 0.16.3 bumps to 0.17.0)    If the version has already been bumped on `main` since the last release, skip this. -->

### Reproduction  Wasp version: **0.18.1** (latest at the time of writing)  Reproduction steps: ```bash # Force Docker to fetch PG 18 docker image rm postgres:latest  # Create a new app cd /tmp wasp new saas-app -t saas cd saas-app/app wasp db start ```  The error we get is: ``` wasp db start ✨ Starting a PostgreSQL dev database (based on your Wasp config) ✨  Additional info:  ℹ Connection URL, in case you might want to connect with external tools:      postgresql://postgresWaspDevUser:postgresWaspDevPass@localhost:5432/OpenSaaS-299c73562d  ℹ Database data is persisted in a docker volume with the following name (useful to know if you will want to delete it at some point):      wasp-dev-db-OpenSaaS-299c73562d  ... Unable to find image 'postgres:latest' locally latest: Pulling from library/postgres Digest: sha256:41fc5342eefba6cc2ccda736aaf034bbbb7c3df0fdb81516eba1ba33f360162c Status: Downloaded newer image for postgres:latest Error: in 18+, these Docker images are configured to store database data in a        format which is compatible with "pg_ctlcluster" (specifically, using        major-version-specific directory names).  This better reflects how        PostgreSQL itself works, and how upgrades are to be performed.         See also https://github.com/docker-library/postgres/pull/1259         Counter to that, there appears to be PostgreSQL data in:          /var/lib/postgresql/data (unused mount/volume)         This is usually the result of upgrading the Docker image without        upgrading the underlying database using "pg_upgrade" (which requires both        versions).         The suggested container configuration for 18+ is to place a single mount        at /var/lib/postgresql which will then place PostgreSQL data in a        subdirectory, allowing usage of "pg_upgrade --link" without mount point        boundary issues.         See https://github.com/docker-library/postgres/issues/37 for a (long)        discussion around this process, and suggestions for how to do so. wasp-bin: callCommand: docker run --name wasp-dev-db-OpenSaaS-299c73562d --rm --publish 5432:5432 -v wasp-dev-db-OpenSaaS-299c73562d:/var/lib/postgresql/data --env POSTGRES_PASSWORD=postgresWaspDevPass --env POSTGRES_USER=postgresWaspDevUser --env POSTGRES_DB=OpenSaaS-299c73562d postgres (exit 1): failed ```  ### What's going on  From Postgres 18, Docker images now require us to use ` /var/lib/postgresql` as the mounting dir for the volume and not ` /var/lib/postgresql/data` as we did before.   Erasing the existing volumes used by the development database didn't help.   ### Solution  We need to change the container mounting location to ` /var/lib/postgresql`.   Running `docker run --name wasp-dev-db-OpenSaaS-299c73562d --rm --publish 5432:5432 -v wasp-dev-db-OpenSaaS-299c73562d:/var/lib/postgresql --env POSTGRES_PASSWORD=postgresWaspDevPass --env POSTGRES_USER=postgresWaspDevUser --env POSTGRES_DB=OpenSaaS-299c73562d postgres` worked for me locally.  ## Workarounds for users  This affects all Wasp versions since all Wasp version use `postgres:latest` for the development DB. The fix will be implement in the future Wasp 0.18.2 but for older versions of Wasp this issue will persist. So, we are offering a couple of workarounds:  ### Tag `postgres:latest` as `postgres:17`  - Run `docker tag postgres:17 postgres:latest` which will make Docker use PG 17 instead of PG 18 - If you get a error about an existing database, you probably just need to delete the dev DB volume with `docker volume rm wasp-dev-db-<specific-to-your-app>` (you'll find the volume name in the error message)  ### Use Docker directly for your dev needs   You can run the Docker command yourself with a specific Postgres version: ``` docker run --name wasp-dev-db-custom --rm --publish 5432:5432 -v wasp-dev-db-custom:/var/lib/postgresql/data --env POSTGRES_PASSWORD=postgresWaspDevPass --env POSTGRES_USER=postgresWaspDevUser --env POSTGRES_DB=custom postgres:17 ``` And you'll need to set the `DATABASE_URL` in the `.env.server`: ``` postgresql://postgresWaspDevUser:postgresWaspDevPass@localhost:5432/custom ```  I've used `custom` here as the app name - you can change this.
Discuss Information: Fixed in Wasp `0.18.2` in [#3323](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3323)

Description: ## Description This PR fixes the npx path issue in #3148. It is the error from running Wasp on Window. ``` cabal run wasp-cli -- new hello cabal run wasp-cli -- start ```  ``` Internal Wasp error (bug in the compiler):   Failed while calling prisma format via wasp's prisma ts package: Error: spawn npx ENOENT       at ChildProcess._handle.onexit (node:internal/child_process:285:19)       at onErrorNT (node:internal/child_process:483:16)       at process.processTicksAndRejections (node:internal/process/task_queues:90:21) {     errno: -4058,     code: 'ENOENT',     syscall: 'spawn npx',     path: 'npx',     spawnargs: [       'prisma',       'format',       '--schema',       'C:\\Users\\....\\AppData\\Local\\Temp\\prisma--26004-j4zlwOhxaaNz-.schema'     ]   }    CallStack (from HasCallStack):     error, called at src\Wasp\Psl\Format.hs:35:21 in waspc-0.18.1-inplace:Wasp.Psl.Format ``` It is because the Node's spawn could not find the localtion of `npx`, which is `npx.ps1` on Window.  One way to patch this new error is adding the option [shell: true](https://nodejs.org/api/child_process.html#child_processspawncommand-args-options) to Node's spawn in https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/0b1cf6199f84816b8539f09a4af6118ac81de909/waspc/packages/prisma/src/format.ts#L25-L28  ## Related PR This PR also contains the commits from https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3318. Please merge #3318 before this PR.  ## Type of change  <!-- Select just one with [x] -->  - [ ] **� Just code/docs improvement** <!-- no functional change --> - [x] **� Bug fix** <!-- non-breaking change which fixes an issue --> - [ ] **� New/improved feature** <!-- non-breaking change which adds functionality --> - [ ] **� Breaking change** <!-- fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected -->  ## Checklist  <!--   Check all the applicable boxes with [x], and leave the rest empty.   If you're unsure about any of them, don't hesitate to ask for help. -->  - [ ] I tested my change in a Wasp app to verify that it works as intended.  - � Tests:    - [ ] I added **unit tests** for my change. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you fixed a bug)_ I added a **regression test** for the bug I fixed. <!-- If not, explain why. -->   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I added/updated **e2e tests** at `examples/kitchen-sink/e2e-tests`.   - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I updated the **starter templates** at `waspc/data/Cli/templates`, as needed.  - � Documentation:    - [ ] _(if you added/updated a feature)_ I **added/updated the documentation** in `web/docs/`.  - � Changelog: _(if change is more than just code/docs improvement)_   - [ ] I updated `waspc/ChangeLog.md` with a **user-friendly** description of the change.   - [ ] _(if you did a breaking change)_ I added a step to the current **migration guide** at `web/docs/migration-guides/`.   - [ ] I **bumped the `version`** in `waspc/waspc.cabal` to reflect the changes I introduced.

Description: **Describe the bug** When running wasp db start I get an error message due to using the wrong volume path in postgres 18.0 (currently the postgres:latest docker image version), Previously to 18.0 it used "/var/lib/postgresql/data" now uses "var/lib/postgresql". **To Reproduce** 1. Clear current postgres:lastest in docker, to do a clean pull (e.g a new user) 2. Go to project folder and run "wasp db start" 3. Runs using old path but uses latest image with new path 4. See error **Expected behavior** If postgres version is 18, use the new volume location. This shouldn't be a big change but I'm unsure about retrocompatibility for older builds. **Screenshots** **Desktop (please complete the following information):** - OS: Linux - Browser On terminal **Additional context** For now running the command manually with the new volume dir works as a simple workaround, so wouldn't consider this urgent
Discuss Information: @0xHolyTech , this is caused by the new `latest` image for postgress, which seems to be incompatible with the older one (due to different place where it stores data, as you correctly identified).  Check out here for issue where we plan to pin this down so it doesn't happen so suddenly: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/1606 . We will be implementing this soon.  As for right now, I think the easiest solution for you is to delete the old volume -> that should be enough I believe! Or, as you said yourself, you can use custom command instead.  I will be closing this one for now, but feel free to write more here and we will respond, and we can even reopen if needed. | Ok, for now I've been pulling postgres:17.6 and tagging it as latest to prevent docker from pulling the 18.0 version, this has been a better workaround then what I mentioned before in case anyone else comes across the issue for the time being. | > Ok, for now I've been pulling postgres:17.6 and tagging it as latest to prevent docker from pulling the 18.0 version, this has been a better workaround then what I mentioned before in case anyone else comes across the issue for the time being.  Oh that is a good one, makes sense! | This error breaks any  new person from trying WASP db templates.. SO the installed is BROKEN when you choose option #3 | @mediashock why do you think it is for every new person? From what we understood, it doesn't happen for new users, but for existing users that used `wasp start db` previously.  By option 3, you mean choosing open-saas in `wasp new`? aha I guess for other starters (1 and 2) it works because those use SQLite. | @mediashock @0xHolyTech thank you for raising awareness about this issue for us! We misdiagnosed it when we first spotted it. We are tracking it here: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3321 and I'm working on a hotfix for the version **0.18.X** which we'll release today/tomorrow.  | Fixed in Wasp `0.18.2` in [#3323](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3323)

They are not aided by the UI, and I foresee the following scenarios: > > * Beginner contributors might ignore the instructions, fill out everything, or get overwhelmed, leaving in a lot of noise > * More advanced contributors will probably do it right, but they'd have no problems filling out a regular checklist as well > * For us, it just creates a lot of noise, and it is somewhat annoying to remove sections from a Markdown file. > * Plus, if a small change turns into a breaking change, it's a hassle to bring back the previously-removed checklist. > * In general, I prefer to err on the side of easier/more useful for us, than easier for external contributors, since we're going to be seeing and using it a lot more often. > * I like your first option more ([New PR template #3255 (comment)](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3255#issuecomment-3467978786)). > * Regarding blank checkboxes: yep, it's a struggle to know if they have ignored or explicitly left blank the checkbox. I don't think we should require strikethrough since it's somewhat obscure, and annoying to apply in checklists. (GitLab has a specific marker for this `[~]` instead of `[x]`, but it's not available in GitHub) > * I like the idea of how you expressed the version number bumps, but this "selector" is very confusing/noisy to parse at first glance. I don't know how I'd improve it though. I might recommend to go back to the regular patch/minor/major notation because of this. Ok, let's avoid split list then! I like it becase I have to think less when I am filling it in, but I get the bad sides of it. And you are right about PR possibly changing, that is a PITA then, yeah good point. Yeah strikethrough is hard to apply hm. `[~]` would be awesome! Too bad it doesn't work in github. Ok yeah the "selector" part certianly confused people, Miho even though he needs to fill it in it seems, so better to go without it. How about we just have emojis? wrench, ladybug, rocket, explosion? Those are pretty standard? | Btw from my side, I think we are close -> what we have now combined with additional feedback and all, I think it should bring us there. So I am all good for now, waiting for the next iteration of what you amke out of it @cprecioso , and then I will review again. | @Martinsos ready for re-review

Updates `@vitejs/plugin-react-swc` from 3.3.2 to 3.11.0
Release notes

Sourced from @​vitejs/plugin-react-swc's releases.

plugin-react-swc@3.11.0

Add HMR support for compound components (#518)

HMR now works for compound components like this:

const Root = () => <div>Accordion Root</div> const Item = () => <div>Accordion Item</div> 
export const Accordion = { Root, Item }

Return Plugin[] instead of PluginOption[] (#537)

The return type has changed from react(): PluginOption[] to more specialized type react(): Plugin[]. This allows for type-safe manipulation of plugins, for example:

// previously this causes type errors react()   .map(p => ({ ...p, applyToEnvironment: e => e.name === 'client' }))

plugin-react-swc@3.10.2

Suggest @vitejs/plugin-react-oxc if rolldown-vite is detected #491

Emit a log which recommends @vitejs/plugin-react-oxc when rolldown-vite is detected to improve performance and use Oxc under the hood. The warning can be disabled by setting disableOxcRecommendation: false in the plugin options.

Use optimizeDeps.rollupOptions instead of optimizeDeps.esbuildOptions for rolldown-vite #489

This suppresses the warning about optimizeDeps.esbuildOptions being deprecated in rolldown-vite.

Add Vite 7-beta to peerDependencies range #497

React plugins are compatible with Vite 7, this removes the warning when testing the beta.

plugin-react-swc@3.10.1

Add explicit semicolon in preambleCode #485

This fixes an edge case when using HTML minifiers that strips line breaks aggressively.

plugin-react-swc@3.10.0

Add filter for rolldown-vite #470

Added filter so that it is more performant when running this plugin with rolldown-powered version of Vite.

Skip HMR preamble in Vitest browser mode #478

This was causing annoying Sourcemap for "/@react-refresh" points to missing source files and is unnecessary in test mode.

Skip HMR for JSX files with hooks #480

... (truncated)

Description: Problem Summary    New users who complete email verification cannot log in due to an iframe sandbox error. The error message is:   Blocked form submission to '' because the form's frame is sandboxed and the 'allow-forms' permission is not set.    Environment    - Wasp Version: ^0.17.0   - Browser: Multiple browsers (Chrome, Firefox, Safari)   - Auth Method: Email + Password with verification   - Issue Scope: Only affects new users after email verification; existing users can log in normally    Reproduction Steps    1. Register a new user with email/password authentication   2. Complete email verification by clicking the verification link   3. Attempt to log in with verified credentials   4. Login form fails with iframe sandbox error in browser console   5. User remains unable to log in    Expected Behavior    After email verification, users should be able to log in normally without any iframe-related errors.    Actual Behavior    - Login fails with iframe sandbox permission error   - Form submission targets empty URL ('')   - Error suggests form is being submitted within a sandboxed iframe without allow-forms permission   - Workaround: Using incognito/private browsing mode allows successful login    Configuration Details    main.wasp auth config:   auth: {     userEntity: User,     methods: {       email: {         fromField: {           name: "App Name",           email: "admin@example.com"         },         emailVerification: {           clientRoute: EmailVerificationRoute,           getEmailContentFn: import { getVerificationEmailContent } from "@src/auth/emails",         },         userSignupFields: import { getEmailUserFields } from "@src/auth/userSignupFields",       }     },     onAfterSignup: import { onAfterSignup } from "@src/auth/hooks",     onAuthFailedRedirectTo: "/login",     onAuthSucceededRedirectTo: "/explore",   }    EmailVerificationPage component:   import { VerifyEmailForm } from 'wasp/client/auth';   import { appearance } from '../appearance';    export function EmailVerificationPage() {     return (       <AuthPageLayout>         <VerifyEmailForm appearance={appearance} />         {/* ... */}       </AuthPageLayout>     );   }    Browser Console Details    - Error occurs specifically during form submission   - Form action attribute appears to be empty ('')   - Error indicates missing allow-forms permission on iframe sandbox   - No custom iframe code in our application    Impact    - Critical: New users cannot complete registration flow   - Workaround exists: Private/incognito browsing allows login   - User Experience: Confusing for users as verification appears successful but login fails    Additional Context    - The issue suggests VerifyEmailForm or its authentication flow may be creating an iframe with insufficient sandbox permissions   - Only affects the post-verification login process, not the verification step itself   - Existing users who registered before this issue can log in normally    Questions for Maintainers    1. Does VerifyEmailForm internally use iframes for any authentication flows?   2. Are there known issues with form submission handling in the email verification process?   3. Could this be related to recent changes in browser security policies affecting sandboxed iframes?    Would appreciate any guidance on resolving this issue or potential workarounds while maintaining the email verification flow.
Discuss Information: Hi @bkidy, in which environment are you testing your application?  Judging by the error message, it seems you have your app inside an `iframe`, i.e. your Wasp app thinks it is embedded in another web page, but we don't use `iframe`s anywhere in Wasp. This could happen when your Wasp app is embedded into app, or an online editor for example.   Another option could be that your own code is using `iframe`s -- this might happen if an AI didn't 100% understand how to make things happen in this kind of app.  Can you check these two things and come back to me? I'll help you debug | This scenario occurs after a user clicks on the registration link in their email. However, I tested different email providers and found that only some of them encountered this issue. Additionally, I did not make many modifications to WASP's default registration and login components. I hope this information helps with diagnosing the problem. | Which email providers? | https://www.linshiyouxiang.net/ | Mmmm can you share with me the name of the mailbox that was created for you so I can check? | At the time, I was testing with this temporary email service provider, and I found that as soon as the new user clicked the verification link from the temporary email, they were unable to log in after completing the email verification. The browser displayed an iframe sandbox error with the specific error message. However, the issue could be resolved by opening the browser in private mode or restarting the browser. Therefore, it is suspected that there might be some impact during the link redirection from the temporary email. | I understand that. I'm asking if you can share the specific temporary mailbox address that you used so I can inspect it myself.  My suspicion is that this specific website is somehow using `iframe`s for links inside emails. If this is the case, we can't really do anything, it's a mistake on that email provider's part. | I actually had the same suspicion as you, and if that's the case I'm not worried about that.Thank you for taking so much time to help me resolve this issue. Wasp has really brought us a lot of convenience.

Description: ## This is an issue for the PR  https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3020  **Describe the bug**  Cannot build Wasp studio on Windows due to the Unix commands in package.json  **To Reproduce** Steps to reproduce the behavior:  - Install [NodeJS](https://nodejs.org/en/download) - Clone commit bba407d - Change directory to ./waspc/packages/studio/ - Install dependencies ```bash npm ci ``` - Run build ```bash npm run build ``` - The console will show the error: ```bash > wasp-studio-server@1.0.0 build > npm run build:client && rm -rf dist && tsc && cp -r ./public ./dist/public  > wasp-studio-server@1.0.0 build:client > npm --prefix ./client install && npm --prefix ./client run copy  > wasp-studio-client@0.0.0 copy > npm run build && cp -r ./dist/* ../public  > wasp-studio-client@0.0.0 build > tsc && vite build  vite v4.5.3 building for production... Browserslist: caniuse-lite is outdated. Please run:   npx update-browserslist-db@latest   Why you should do it regularly: https://github.com/browserslist/update-db#readme ✓ 1057 modules transformed. dist/index.html                     0.40 kB │ gzip:   0.27 kB dist/assets/index-17ce6ed4.css    157.34 kB │ gzip:  18.98 kB dist/assets/index-62a9d21a.js     496.83 kB │ gzip: 162.59 kB dist/assets/Flow-b5112d3d.js    1,373.26 kB │ gzip: 421.00 kB  (!) Some chunks are larger than 500 kBs after minification. Consider: - Using dynamic import() to code-split the application - Use build.rollupOptions.output.manualChunks to improve chunking: https://rollupjs.org/configuration-options/#output-manualchunks - Adjust chunk size limit for this warning via build.chunkSizeWarningLimit. ✓ built in 13.36s  'cp' is not recognized as an internal or external command, operable program or batch file. ```  **Problem**  `cp -r` and `rm -rf` are not valid commands on Windows  **Environment:**  - OS: Windows 11 (Build 10.0.26100) - Node v22.14.0 - NPM 11.4.2 - Powershell 5.1.26100.4768 - CMD Version 10.0.26100.4770  PS. I've already fixed this issue
Discuss Information: looks like part of this is just unix shell commands not being compatible on windows, but that final heap crash message is a bit more interesting.  we’ve seen similar memory failures when chunk sizes in the build process get semantically overloaded — meaning, even if a single file isn’t huge, its structure or dynamic imports create large logical bundles that exceed the engine’s tolerance. sometimes this isn’t caught early because the bundler doesn’t report semantic weight, just raw file size.  you might already have patched the surface issues, but if you see this kind of heap failure again, it could point to an underlying collapse in build logic or module chunking. we’ve handled a few of these before and had to implement semantic-aware chunk guards to keep things stable. happy to share if useful. | Hi @onestardao   Indeed, I've fixed the surface issues around the unix shell commands and there are still some problem regarding the Wasp studio. If you can reproduce this problem on Linux, I suggest opening a new issue for Wasp studio. I know it's still in experimental development, so your patch would be really useful. | thanks for the thoughtful follow-up — sounds like you’re already in that rare state where folks debug not what’s missing, but why something gets misrouted inside the stack.  if helpful, we documented this failure mode in Problem Map 1.0 – No.4: Improper Semantic Boundary, which weirdly shows up even in non-AI toolchains when internal chunk logic silently fails upstream.  it’s here if you want to dig: https://github.com/onestardao/WFGY/blob/main/ProblemMap/README.md (also links to 2.0, which is way faster to spot the actual pain points.) | @onestardao I don't get it. Could you explain how this issue relate to Problem Map 1.0 - Np. 4: Improper?  | sure happy to explain!  Problem Map 1.0 – No.4 (“Improper Semantic Boundary”) refers to a situation where chunked logic (whether in build steps, module bundling, or async resolution) silently collapses because the system *thinks* it preserved boundaries, but the actual logical units (like large functions, dynamic imports, or unscoped shared states) end up misaligned.  In your case, the heap crash could be a downstream symptom — the shell patch fixes the syntax-level failure, but if you later see unexpected memory churn or chunk reuse issues (especially on Linux), the root cause might be improper semantic separation in build-time logic.  We also documented a different angle in Problem Map 2.0 (semantic map version), which focuses more on when resolution trees miswire due to flattening or overload:  [WFGY Problem Map 2.0 ](https://github.com/onestardao/WFGY/blob/main/ProblemMap/rag-architecture-and-recovery.md)  Both maps aim to catch the same class of failures, just from different points of view — feel free to dig in or ping me if you want a test patch!

Description: What often happens: 1. A user reports a bug and gives us a project to reproduce it. 2. We reproduce the bug and fix it. 4. We keep working on Wasp and break the same behavior again.  It would be nice if we had an easy way to write and maintain tests to ensure this doesn't happen, a way to automatically perform regression testing for all our bugs.  Some open source repos have a directoriy called `bugs` with many subdirectories named after github issues, each with a test that verifies the code doesn't have the bug described in that issue.  @Martinsos's formulation: > A way to easily write/maintain tests that set up a specific wasp app for themselves and check its behaviour [...]  > an easy way to define a test where I pick some kind of starter template for Wasp, define some changes to that code unique to this test (likely as a diff or patch or what is it called), and then also define some expected behaviour (be it wasp CLI behaviour, or something we do via playwright), and I have a test. > > So kind of an ability to define test-specific Wasp apps with minimal code + tests to run on them?  @cprecioso's idea: > My 2c is that I'd take one main action: invest in having a easy-to-author test harness -- it is a bother to create a full wasp app to test only one aspect of our code. The boilerplate should be abstracted away. (Incidentally I think @miho's work on #2814 can be applied here) Once we have that, we have to use it and create a lot of tests, and no new feature or bug without a regression test
Discuss Information: An example of what we should cover with this: - https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3253#issuecomment-3406301125 - https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3339#issuecomment-3515915040  Implementing tests for that behavior didn't make sense at the time: - Fringe feature. - Complicated to test it.  After we standardize this kind of stuff, we should look into it again.

Description: ## Motivation  Currently. Wasp doesn't run natively on Windows OS, which is a pain point for many developers (#2484, #2890). While the program can be used through  WSL, the experience can be unpredictable and has known performance issues (#2717). This PR aims to address a specific issue that prevents Wasp from running alias commands on Windows (like npm.ps1, npm.cmd, npx.ps1, npx.cmd), bringing us one step closer to native Windows support.  ## Description  - This PR fixed #48 by changing `System.Process proc` to `System.Process shell`. - This change is a minimal and easy way (compared to https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/658) to allow Haskell's `System.Process` to correctly identify and execute alias commands on Windows, particularly `npm` and `npx` which is often a PowerShell script (`npm.ps1`/`npx.ps1`) rather than a simple executable. - It has been tested on Windows 11. Further testing on both Linux and MacOS is needed to ensure no regressions.  ## Problem  While running Wasp on Windows, the `wasp start` command fails because it cannot find `npm` despite `npm` and `node` being present in the user's path.   ```bash ? --- [Error] Node/NPM requirement not met: --------------------------------------  Running `npm --version` failed.   `npm --version` command not found! Make sure you have `npm` installed and in your PATH. ```  This is because Haskell's `System.Process proc` struggles to execute PowerShell script (`npm.ps1`) or Cmd script (`npm.cmd`), depending on current shell.  ## Previous PR  The previous PR (https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/658) solved this problem by separate the logic for executing `npm` on Windows. It works well, but I have no idea why it has never been merged into the main code.   ## Solution  The solution is to switch from `System.Process proc` to `System.Process shell` [ref](https://hackage-content.haskell.org/package/process-1.6.26.1/docs/System-Process.html). This change allows the Haskell process to utilize the system's shell (no matter which shell is), which is better way to handle the alias commands on Windows' PowerShell and Cmd.  ## Security Considerations  However, the downside of this solution is `System.Process shell` might be use for executing malicious codes if user-supplied input is passed to the shell. we must ensure that the `shell` function is only used with a limited and predefined set of commands (like node, npm, npx), and any user provided arguments are properly filtered.  ### Steps to compile in Windows 11  - Install [ghcup](https://www.haskell.org/ghcup/) - Change directory to `waspc` - Set ghc to version 8.10.7   ```ps   ghcup tui   ``` - Build `wasp-cli`   ```ps   cabal build all   ```  ### Test suits `run test` See commend below.  ## Unrelated issues on native Windows   - #3024  - #3028  - #3042 - #3043   ### Select what type of change this PR introduces:  [x] **Bug fix** (non-breaking change which fixes an issue).  ### Update Waspc ChangeLog and version if needed  [x] I updated [`ChangeLog.md`](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/main/waspc/ChangeLog.md) with description of the change this PR introduces.

Description: Motivated by this Discord thread: https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1396259947305631824/1398223811031208017  We allow users to override the Wasp `Dockerfile` with their custom commands. They can override the whole thing and it's quite an advanced concept which expects from users to know well what they are doing.  But... users often start with taking the whole _existing `Dockerfile`_ and  copy pasting it to `Dockerfile` in their project root. That's great and it's making sure they don't miss any of our important commands. The trouble is then we **update the Wasp `Dockerfile`** and users have no way of knowing if they need new extra line or if they need to delete it.  For example, here's the diff between `Dockerfile` 0.16.0 and `Dockerfile` 0.17.0: ```diff diff --git a/waspc/data/Generator/templates/Dockerfile b/waspc/data/Generator/templates/Dockerfile index f4e271e05..67712b296 100644 --- a/waspc/data/Generator/templates/Dockerfile +++ b/waspc/data/Generator/templates/Dockerfile @@ -33,6 +33,7 @@ WORKDIR /app  COPY src ./src  COPY package.json .  COPY package-lock.json . +COPY tsconfig*.json .  COPY server .wasp/build/server  COPY sdk .wasp/out/sdk  # Install npm packages, resulting in node_modules/. @@ -50,14 +51,11 @@ RUN cd .wasp/build/server && npm run bundle  FROM base AS server-production  # In case they want to use python3 in their app.  RUN apk add --no-cache python3 -ENV NODE_ENV production +ENV NODE_ENV=production  WORKDIR /app  # Copying the top level 'node_modules' because it contains the Prisma packages  # necessary for migrating the database.  COPY --from=server-builder /app/node_modules ./node_modules -# Copying the SDK because the server bundle doesn't bundle the SDK -# and references the 'wasp' package. -COPY --from=server-builder /app/.wasp/out/sdk .wasp/out/sdk  # Copying 'server/node_modules' because we require dotenv package to  # load environment variables  # TODO: replace dotenv with native Node.js environment variable loading ```  It would be great if somehow communicated these changes to the user: 1. Maybe we make it a part of our migration guides? 2. Maybe we introduce some sort of validation logic to see if certain lines exist and give users a warning. This might be easier to do after/if we do https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/1459
Discuss Information: Maybe we can just have them do a `FROM wasp-app` and we inject our layers there? | One idea I had for this kind of stuff, where you take the hardcoded file from Wasp and then use it as your own, was to use the checksums to detect the change in the file.  Specifically, I had that idea for when/if we implement the ability to override any of the Wasp templates with your own (https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/1245). E.g. you override the server/index.ts with your own. But what if it changes in the future and you don't know? Well, if we recorded the checksum of the old original file, we can compare it with the new one, and notify user if those are not the same.  So imagine in the Dockerfile having a comment on top being like `# BASED_ON_WASP_DOCKERFILE <checksum>`. We can then check if that checksum matches the current checksum of default Wasp dockerfile and warn them if it doesn't! We can even do the diff if we want or they can do it on their own.  This is cool because: 1. It is easy to implement (and maintain). 2. They can easily opt out of it (just delete the comment) if they don't want this behaviour. 3. It is explicit. 4. They just need to update the checksum in the comment with the new one to say that they are good with the changes. 5. This mechanism might be useful in multiple places in Wasp.

Description: If you try to sign in via Wasp Auth and email is already taken, we won't tell you that, for security reasons (we don't want anybody to be able to learn which accounts exist), instead we will tell you to go check your email for verification link, but email will never arrive.  Similar with login, we won't tell you if email is non-existant, but that a combo of email and pass is wrong.  While this is good security-wise, it is not great UX wise, if you for example forgot which email you are using in the app, of if you forgot that you already have an account.  We should look into ways to possibly improve UX without loosing on the security side. Maybe we could send emails in the background to the existing account to provide more info (hey you used wrong pass). Maybe we can leave it to the Wasp app to customize the error message, provide more info if they want so. We should also look into what other popular apps are doing, maybe we are going overboard here?
Discuss Information: @Martinsos can i pick up the fix for this ?  I will fix it | Hello! @Martinsos  I improves UX for existing account signup attempts by sending helpful emails and updating frontend messages, while maintaining security by not leaking account existence information. Template file linter errors are expected and don't affect generated code.

Description: ### High-level overview This PR: 1. Introduces the `waspc/libs` directory 	- This directory contains the source code of libraries 2. Introduces the `data/libs` directory 	- This directory contains the built libraries 	- The built libraries will be copied to `.wasp/out` alongside the SDK directory so the SDK can import the libs 4. Introduces `auth` library in the `libs` directory (named `@wasp.sh/libs-auth`) 	- This library contains some auth code that was extracted from the Wasp SDK 	- It demonstrates how we can build the source code with [`tsdown` ](https://tsdown.dev/) 	- It demonstrates how to pack the library using `npm pack` and copy it into the `data` dir 	- It demonstrates how to define deps e.g. `oslo` 	- It demonstrates how to have multiple entry points e.g. `./sdk` and `./sdk/browser` to be able to separate server and browser exports 5. Introduces the concept of a `WaspLib` which is how we refer to the libraries in Haskell 	- We define the libs by name and this is enough to know where to find the packaged libs (`.tgz` files) and where to copy them 	- We also generate the dependencies needed for SDK from the `WaspLib` (e.g. `file:../../lib-1.0.0.tgz`) 	- We defined the `version` to be fixed to `0.0.0` since it will be replaced with the lib checksum when copied 6. Uses the `auth` library in the SDK and the server 	- It uses some `jwt`, `cookies` and `password` helpers, these helpers depend on external deps e.g. `oslo` and `@node-rs/argon2` (native deps) 	- It defined some React helpers in the `./sdk/browser` entry to show we can split the code  > [!NOTE]  > **Note for reviewers:** there are three sections:  > 1. the `auth` library > 2. the way the `auth` library is used in the SDK > 3. and the machinery to package the libs and copy them to the data dir.  > > I'd suggest reviewing the `auth` library last since it's just a JS project, nothing new to see there.  ### How to test it locally  1. Run `./tools/install_packages_to_data_dir.sh` 2. Start the `waspc/examples/todoApp` with the `cabal run` or your alias of choice e.g. `wrun` 3. Try using Auth UI to verify the browser code works 4. Try to login with email to verify password hashing works, reset a password to verify JWTs still work, try OAuth to verify cookies still work  I've tested it in development and production build modes and it works as expected ✅   ### Known issues in development  ~~You have to delete the `node_modules` and the `@wasp.sh/libs-auth` entry from `package-lock.json` to install the new development lib version that you built locally.~~  EDIT: fixed by using a checksum of the library's `.tgz` checksum as the version which prevents `npm` from using a stale version. Also, I've excluded the libs from being optimized (and then becoming stale) by Vite in development mode.   ### Left to do  - [x] We need to update the snapshot tests runner not to care about the `.tgz` files. I'll do this in a separate PR to keep it easier to review this one. https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/3078 - [x] Update development workflow in the `waspc/README.md` and in `run` script  Closes #2936  Closes #3069

I haven't explored `npm`'s behaviour but it sounds like it does the same thing as `yarn` (which I have used extensively). `yarn` will not unpack and reinstall the package if the package specifier has not changed. In this case the package specifier is the file path. The version is only important here in as much as it is a part of the file path. IMO, "generating a random version" sounds too hacky, no need to introduce randomness. Copying the file into `.wasp/out/` with a hash suffix can be enough. We ensure that tarballs with different contents will have a different path, thus a different package specifier, thus will get reinstalled, regardless of the version of the package inside. | @cprecioso > Copying the file into .wasp/out/ with a hash suffix can be enough. We ensure that tarballs with different contents will have a different path, thus a different package specifier, thus will get reinstalled, regardless of the version of the package inside. I understand the logic, the filename will change and npm will need to reinstall the new tarball. This would mean our Haskell code will need to compute the hash and use it in both the SDK dep specification and in the destination filename. I guess that's not that complex. I like it. Did I understand this correctly? | @infomiho yep! | @cprecioso I've implemented your suggestion in this commit https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/2989/commits/083fecbb510bf7c4e939c4aaf1a7125bf92024be The trouble is that I think `npm` still uses the `version` field from the `package.json` and doesn't want to update the code it uses _even though the tarball path changed_. **One thing did happen** - I no longer have to delete both `package-lock.json` and `node_modules` - only `node_modules` to get `npm` to pick up the new code. The tarballs are now installed with a checksum of the file. I've tried doing: - `some-name-XXXX-0.0.0.tgz`(part of name) - `some-name-XXXX.tgz` (version) None of the above produce the desired effect of cache busting. Description: ### Description  closes #2933   ### Select what type of change this PR introduces:  1. [ ] **Just code/docs improvement** (no functional change). 2. [x] **Bug fix** (non-breaking change which fixes an issue). 3. [ ] **New feature** (non-breaking change which adds functionality). 4. [ ] **Breaking change** (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected).  ### Description  Many users deploy a Wasp app, hit the client, and see CORS error in the browser console even though the real cause is a missing env-var or other 5xx on the server. This happens because the response that bubbles up from a crashed server has no Access-Control-Allow-Origin header, so the browser rewrites the failure as a CORS issue and hides the actual JSON body.  I maybe wrong on the issue but the only problem regarding the CORS which i found out in our codebase was it,
Discuss Information: Hey @0xTaneja thank you for the contribution.   I think this might be going too loose with security because we are effectively disabling CORS for all endpoints for all of our users. We want to be explicit in which domains we allow to connect to our server. Setting the value to `*` allows any client to connect to our server (which is [usually fine](https://advancedweb.hu/is-access-control-allow-origin-star-insecure/) but we don't want that _by default_). Users can do this themselves by modifying the global middleware.  I'd like us to more explicitly solve #2933 by doing something like this: - we already have `/` that returns 200 and an empty body - we can add `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` for only that endpoint -  we can ping the server at `/` when there is Network error on the client, so we can output something in the browser e.g. `Hey, it seems like the client can't connect to the server - the server is probably not running or the client is trying to connect to the wrong URL. If the URL looks correct XXX, check the server logs to debug.` 	- I'd be as precise as possible here and try to output it only Network errors if possible.  This is a rough idea, I'm not 100% about this, but I'm trying to give you an idea how we'd like the DX to look like.  | yep i agree with you @infomiho i have added `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` globally but i can make it to that particular endpoint. pinging could be an option and also one more thing maybe we can do is validate things like if required env's to make the app run are missing, i know we are doing this on haskell side but maybe if we can do this on server side as well then we can throw the error !! another thing i suggest is /health route as well just to check things are good for db and env's stuff, and in deploy documentation maybe tell users to check /health route when they get error !! | > yep i agree with you @infomiho i have added `Access-Control-Allow-Origin: *` globally but i can make it to that particular endpoint. pinging could be an option and also one more thing maybe we can do is validate things like if required env's to make the app run are missing, i know we are doing this on haskell side but maybe if we can do this on server side as well then we can throw the error !! another thing i suggest is /health route as well just to check things are good for db and env's stuff, and in deploy documentation maybe tell users to check /health route when they get error !!  Validating required envs -> we are actually doing this on the server side now also since recently if I am correct, check out https://wasp.sh/docs/project/env-vars#custom-env-var-validations -> we will run those checks on server start, and if a deployed server is missing env vars, it will fail with useful error message! Right @infomiho ? That said hm, I guess that error message will be logged up there into the logs on the hosting provider, and you might still be getting CORS errors from the client perspective. So that helps if you go there to see what the issue is, but you still need to konw that CORS error might not be CORS error.  @infomiho pinging idea makes general sense to me -> by checking `/` or `/health` or sometihng we can check if server is running or not, so if it is not, we can make it clear that is the real error, not CORS. Although we have to then do that check on CORS errors hm, sounds a bit weird to be performing such extra checks in production, but if it works ... .  I wonder if there is some other way to go around all this? So first of all, why do we get CORS errors if server crashed / there is some 500? @0xTaneja said the following:  > This happens because the response that bubbles up from a crashed server has no Access-Control-Allow-Origin header, so the browser rewrites the failure as a CORS issue and hides the actual JSON body.  If that is correct, would the solution be adding Access-Control-Allow-Origin header onto errors, so that they properly get through? We still wouldn't be doing it for all respnses from our server, so no global `*`, but only for these error response so they get properly through? Am I talking nonsense or is this doable?  Additionally: - We don't really have a standard way to do a health check. I know we discussed it multikple times but never ended up on a solution. I think we concluded users can add it on their own, but maybe indeed we should have something of our own hm. Still not sure, I can see people wanting to remove in case we add it. - Could we provide people with better default CORS story? We know the URL of the client anyway, we could set CORS to that as a deafult setting? But ok this is story for another issue (I added it to another CORS issue right now). | Hi, I will close this based on inactivity, feel free to reopen or send a new one �

Description: ### Terminology  - **User dir:** The directory containing the `main.wasp`, the one the user mainly interacts with - **Generated code:** All the code the wasp compiler writes out `<user dir>/.wasp` - **Generated npm projects:** The folders containing `package.json`s in the _generated code_ (i.e. `<user dir>/.wasp/{out,build}/{server,web-app}`).  # Problem  Right now, most of the dependencies on our _generated npm projects_ use `^` ranges, so these dependencies get updated automatically. Moreover, a `wasp clean` during development (not uncommon) will regenerate the locks, and so upgrade deps too. This has caused problems recently (#2865, #2958, #2932, https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/commit/8ff048174ff2c95c3cd0792df01a9ac0c28dec51, etc), and we're moving to pinning the offending dependencies.  # Idea  Maybe we can ship the `package-lock.json` files for our _generated npm projects_. That means, generating (somehow) the `<user dir>/.wasp/{out,build}/{server,web-app}/package-lock.json`. Arguably, Wasp is delivered as a complete package, and users do not need to worry about the dependency versions.   ### Prior art  Next.js moved to bundle almost their entire depency tree (effectively pinning their versions) for faster installs and control over dependency versions.  ### Pros - Pinning our entire dependency tree with known-good versions we've tested with, no surprise changes - Shorter `npm install` (done on every `wasp start`), as the dep tree is pre-resolved  ### Cons - No automatic bug fixes or security patches. We'd have to release a new Wasp version to adopt them. (Stop-gap workaround: We can always ask users to go into the `package.json`s and update the versions manually) - Users usually ask in our channels for the latest version of our deps (even if not relevant)  ### Other solutions - Have all our deps defined with exact versions (e.g. `1.9.1` instead of `^1.9.1`) -> transitive dependencies still are mostly defined by ranges (#2958 was caused by a transitive dep). - Bundle our deps with our code -> Much bigger `wasp` install size, much shorter compile time, needs architectural changes
Discuss Information: ### Q: What happens when we migrate generated code to `npm` workspaces? (#1838)  In this case, all the `package-lock.json` files would disappear, and only have the top-level (user directory) one.  We could still write out this top-level package lock on `wasp init` (and create a command such as `wasp deps --regenerate` or whatever) with our known-good versions. Package managers do not update package versions unless necessary or explicitly requested. New deps added by the user would get resolved on top of the already-existing dep tree, so it would try to maintain pinning as much as possible.  Moreover, this would provide a good path for user overriding. We can ask the user to manually set an `override` for a dependency version in the main `package.json`, both appeasing users who want to upgrade dependencies, and making it clear that it is not the dep we tested with. | This is related to #559 , correct? | > This is related to [#559](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/559) , correct?  More with #2830

Description: This PR upgrades the Haskell toolchain from GHC 8.10.7 (released in 2021) to GHC 9.0.2, bringing the codebase up to date with more modern Haskell compiler features and improved performance.  ## Changes Made  ### Core Configuration Updates - **`waspc/cabal.project`**:   - Updated `with-compiler: ghc-8.10.7` → `with-compiler: ghc-9.0.2`   - Updated `index-state` from `2023-03-09` to `2025-01-01` to access newer package versions   - Added `allow-newer` directives for compatibility with newer dependency versions   - Added constraint `stm-hamt == 1.2.1` for stable build  - **`waspc/waspc.cabal`**:   - Relaxed `template-haskell` constraint from `^>= 2.16.0` to `>= 2.16.0` for GHC 9.0.2 compatibility   - Relaxed `hashable` constraint from `^>= 1.3.5.0` to `>= 1.3.5.0` for dependency resolution   - Relaxed `strong-path` constraint from `^>= 1.1.4` to `>= 1.1.4` for flexibility  ### Dependency Resolution The upgrade required careful dependency resolution to handle version conflicts between: - `template-haskell` (GHC 9.0.2 ships with 2.17.0.0) - `hashable` (newer versions required by `stm-containers`) - `strong-path` (needed compatibility with newer `hashable`) - `stm-hamt` (required specific version for GHC 9.0.2 compatibility)  ## Benefits - **Performance**: GHC 9.0.2 includes significant performance improvements in compilation and runtime - **Language Features**: Access to newer Haskell language extensions and improvements - **Security**: Updated compiler with security fixes from the past 3 years - **Ecosystem**: Better compatibility with modern Haskell packages - **Maintenance**: Easier to maintain with a more recent toolchain  ## Testing - All dependencies build successfully with GHC 9.0.2 - The upgrade maintains backward compatibility with existing code - Build system works correctly with the new compiler version  ## Next Steps This upgrade provides a solid foundation for: 1. Further upgrades to GHC 9.6.7 (recommended) or 9.12.2 (latest) 2. CI/CD system updates to use the new GHC version 3. Potential adoption of newer Haskell language features  ## Breaking Changes None - this is a toolchain upgrade that maintains full backward compatibility with existing Wasp applications.  <!-- START COPILOT CODING AGENT TIPS --> ---  � Share your feedback on Copilot coding agent for the chance to win a $200 gift card! Description: Resolves #2919   **Context:** - Caches can be shared **from a base branch to its parent branch**. For example, a PR can pick up the cache for `main`. They can't be shared the opposite direction, nor between any other branches. [(docs)](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/dependency-caching-reference#restrictions-for-accessing-a-cache) - Caches are evicted if either: (a) haven't been accessed in 7 days, or (b) the repo goes over the 10GB limit for caches (LRU evicted first). [(docs)](https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/dependency-caching-reference#usage-limits-and-eviction-policy)  **Therefore:** - We want to ensure the cache for our `main` branch is never stale and never at risk of being evicted. An evicted `main` cache means un-cached runs for all the PRs. - Caches for branches that have been merged are **immediately** no longer useful, so we can remove them. - This lowers the risk of evicting other actually important caches (such as `main`'s)  **This PR:** Adds two new workflows:  1. Runs every 6 days (so we don't go over the 7 days limit) and makes sure that our `main` caches are warm, by running `waspc-ci` and `waspc-build` 2. Every time a PR is merged, goes through the caches and evicts the ones linked to no-longer-existing branches.

(https://github.com/advisories/GHSA-wf5p-g6vw-rhxx)  The vulnerability seems to be moot for our own usecase, as the origins we request to (our server) is trusted. | We might want to migrate off `axios` (based on `XmlHttpRequest`) and to `fetch`-based libraries (personally I like [`ky`](https://ghub.io/ky)) or `fetch` directly.  `XmlHttpRequest` has not been the best practice for a long time, and `axios` has [a big bundle size](https://packagephobia.com/result?p=axios) because it tries to bungle it into a usable API. `fetch` is isomorphic, modern, and has good defaults and ergonomics. And it is included into the runtime by default. | @cprecioso consdier adding this to relevant epic! There is "set relationship" on the right, you can use that to make it a subtask if you wish. | >We might want to migrate off axios (based on XmlHttpRequest) and to fetch-based libraries (personally I like [ky](https://ghub.io/ky)) or fetch directly.  I'm +1 for `fetch`. I've used it directly before without any issues, everything that `axios` does can be done with it.  Haven't used any `fetch`-like libraries, so I won't comment on those. | Related issue https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3288

Description: Related to https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/2930#issuecomment-3049285401  Some of our users have version `5.2.2` in their `package-lock.json` and some have version `5.8.2`. The version of Typescript which we use in our headless tests is probably not the same as what our users have. This means that our tests are useless - they don't catch regression that might happen with a different Typescript version.  We should consider using a stricter range `~5.1.0` vs. `^5.1.0` or downright pin the version to `5.1.0` (I'm using this version as an example here) because [doesn't follow semver exactly](https://github.com/microsoft/TypeScript/issues/14116).  @cprecioso would like to be able to use any version he wants as a user. I agree with that sentiment, but I'm not sure how to pull that off, that's something @sodic thought about more.
Discuss Information: We decided to go with a tilde (~) range, but that it was harder to support than expected.  Here's an issue: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/2941#issue-3219057067 | In the meantime, we set TypeScript to a fixed version (`5.8.2`) in #2940.

Description: Resolves #2798  `@prisma/client`, as installed from `npm` is just a placeholder empty client. When calling `prisma generate`, that placeholder gets overwritten with the actual client.  The problem is the placeholder is CJS and the actual built client is ESM. Together with [our workaround](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/a771c50e5cadc589d7fbd54b3fed0c0ec38545b4/waspc/data/Generator/templates/react-app/vite.config.ts#L45-L53) for correct Vite resolution of Prisma in our old version; Vite gets confused.  I made the Prisma importing conditional on there being actual entities in our AppSpec, so we will never import an empty client.  We can remove the workarounds when we: - #2504  - #2867

Description: - https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/584 - https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/491 - https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/263
Discuss Information: Would multi-tenancy fit in here? - https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/2129 | > Would multi-tenancy fit in here? >  > * [Implement richer support for multi tenancy #2129](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/2129)  I would say they are related, but not directly connected.  Multi-tenancy also means different thigns to people: to some it means having a concept of "organization" in the app, and people belonging to it and similar, and for others it means actually having multiple deployments of the app per client.  You can have multi-tenancy without having RBAC support from Wasp but rolling your own, and you could use Wasp's RBAC without having multi-tenancy directlyh supported by Wasp, so in that way they are not directly connected.  That said, it might be interesting to look at one while building another, for possible "cooperation" opportunities. | Writing to vote for multi-tenancy and RBAC!  I think that will be a huge step in helping developers keep tight permission controls if it is controlled through wasp. | > Writing to vote for multi-tenancy and RBAC! I think that will be a huge step in helping developers keep tight permission controls if it is controlled through wasp.  Thanks! When you say multi-tenancy, what does it mean for you? I descvribe right above that it means different stuff to different people. | As inspiration:   Laravel allows writing Policies. Policies are collection of verbs related to a resource type. For example, for the resource post there could be the verbs create, publish, edit, add-to-list, delete, etc. These verbs are expressed as functions that can take any number of parameters (for example and most importantly, the current user; but could also be the list that it is currently being added to). Inside the policy, functions are free-form and can call the DB and run whatever checks they want, as long as they ultimately return a boolean. Finally, you can call these policies through `can()` function or `@can` template directive: `can(“edit”, $myPost)`. The rest of the parameters can usually be injected from the DI framework and don’t need to be passed specifically (but you can do it).  This system makes it very easy to spam checks wherever they might be needed, and to author the policies as they don’t require a strict structure, they’re just functions. Being free-form, you can also implement RBAC or other authz schemes on top of it as well. | # RBAC  First step could be offering a simple (static roles) but useful RBAC solution baked into Wasp. Read on for more details.  We had quite some more interested from users into RBAC, stating it would be quite important for them!  You could implement it manually right now: define some roles on the user, throw some checks around on FE and BE. But people don't like implementing such important logic manually if avoidable (they told us so), to the level they might choose another framework if it does offer this functionality out of the box. So maybe we really should offer it out of the box ourselves! Many frameworks do.  We could easily have a solution that covers 80% of cases, while leaving space for going custom if you are in those 20%.  One approach could be very "Wasp-style", and that is offering declarative way to define it from Wasp Spec (`main.wasp`). So in `authRequired` for routes, you would be able to pass the list of roles. That takes care of frontend. Then, for backend, we could have similar thing for the operations, also in main.wasp. And then, we could also add this at the Prisma level actually: we could restrict access to certain fields or whole models based on the role. This one I actually quite like as an idea -> It is easy to make a mistake in the backend logic (I speak from my own experience here, especially on bigger codebases with multiple team members, and well hah with AI I bet) and fetch data that you shoudln't be able to return to that user. But if there is an extra layer that says that specific field should never be shown to specific role (or opposite or whatever), then you really have some confidence in that. It is "security by doubling", but in practice I think quite effective, I would love to have that. This one is the trickiest to pull off, but maybe it is easier than it sounds. https://github.com/zenstackhq/zenstack is doing this btw, maybe we oculd even use them, or implement our own simple version of it. We are talking about static roles btw, defined at compile/dev time, but that is what 80% of people need (at last based on the feedback we got, that is what they were looking for).  Note: If we have FSMs ready, this could be interesting case study for an FSM. Maybe it won't work if FSMs are not powerful enough yet and if this is too integrated with the rest of the framework, but still, would be an interesting study.

Description: We try our best to write as secure code as possible, but the idea here is that we could benefit from doing a dedicated "security sweep" of our codebase for potential security issues or weak points. Document any we find and decide on a timeline for handling them. Especially Auth.

# Must  - Do research and figure out the best way to proceed with them. Would it make any sense to switch to some external library (grpc, orpc, telefunc, ts-rest…)? Remember we will want to support other languages in the future also.     - This would potentially save us the trouble of serialization and worrying about typing everything         - but keep in mind we would like to support polyglot - Runtime validation for input and output of Operations (Zod) ([issue](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/2682)).     - Automatic types but much more secure!     - Let’s use Standard Schema, not Zod directly? - Improve the design     - Drop the `: Foo<...>` way of defining operations and do it through a function.     - Decide what to do about arguments (switch them to positional or keep as they are). - Make the API for directly calling operations fully usable ([one issue](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/2066))     - Seamless code-level function calling (like RSC, telefunc) - Decide on what to do with our serialization - do we keep it in runtime or embrace the compiler ([details](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/2027)) - Upgrade how our “automatic query invalidation via entities” currently works (we have a GH issue for this)  # Should  - Easy/stable way of calling operations from *outside* the Wasp client     - for testing, but also for usage from external services - https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/2396
Discuss Information: ## Some possibly related issues:  - https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/2396 - https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/2361 - https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/2304 - https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/2244 - https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/2050 - https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/2017 - https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/1367 | Idea by Zeke:  would love to see some stuff defined in the files that they're implemented in. kinda like SvelteKit remote functions. Stuff like:  ```ts // GetPost.route.tsx export const route = {   path: '/posts'   authRequired: true } export default function GetPost() {   return /* component */ } ```  ```ts // getPost.action.ts import {action} from 'wasp' export const getPost = action({ /* standard schema validation?! */ }, async (payload, db) => {   return db.posts.findAll() }, {   authRequired: true,   entities: ['post'] }) ```  that way you don't need to rebuild to update types, they'd be inferred naturally, then when built it would update the implementation to the RPC | https://kubb.dev/ -> Looking very well put together, should help with inspiring us when we rework the RPC, payload validation etc (@infomiho says).

Description: ### Problem  If user signed up but they haven't verified their email yet, and then they try to log in, they get 401 and generic "invalid credentials" message.  We went with 401 and no more info because it protects the info about who is among the existing users, otherwise somebody could easily learn who signed up so far.  But it is a bit blah when you are the actual user and indeed you haven't verified the email and you don't know that is so but think that you provided wrong credentials!  ### Possible solution  I wonder if we can somehow get both. Or if we should give up on the security aspect (not fond of that though).  What do other popular web apps do? We should check that out. I know we did it in the past when making the initial decision, but I forgot about the result. Once we do, we should also put this into the docs, because this is not he first time we got this question + forgot myself what we learned then. We should explain in the docs how our flow goes in this case + explain the reasoning behind it.  We might also want to offer people to give up on it and go for their custom message/response?  Btw one way to satisfy all is telling users something like "Email doesn't exist or is not verified". But maybe that is ugly to read, too much?  Also, do they get an email right now when this happens, telling them they are not verified? That would be helpful at least, so they don't get info in the web app screen, but hey get it in their email at least. Still not perfect DX though, feels like a half-solution.  Here is relevant Discord chat: https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/686873245428875297/1385493013383352371 .

Description: ### Description  Modifies how environment variables are parsed to avoid JSON parsing errors.  Currently, variable parsing calls `show value`, but the `show` function in Haskell automatically adds quotes around strings and escapes internal quotes. So when you have: ```env PG_BOSS_NEW_OPTIONS={"teamConcurrency":3} ```  it gets converted to: ```env PG_BOSS_NEW_OPTIONS="{\"teamConcurrency\":3}" ```  The value that gets passed to `JSON.parse()` includes the outer quotes and escaped inner quotes, which is why it fails.  Fixes #2846  ### Select what type of change this PR introduces:  1. [ ] **Just code/docs improvement** (no functional change). 2. [x] **Bug fix** (non-breaking change which fixes an issue). 3. [ ] **New feature** (non-breaking change which adds functionality). 4. [ ] I updated [`ChangeLog.md`](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/main/waspc/ChangeLog.md) with description of the change this PR introduces. 2. Description: We had a quite tricky situation when react-query released a broken release on their side, because Wasp pulled in that broken release for everybody (since it used `^` for react-query version) and Wasp CLI wouldn't work, older versions also. User's had no simple way of going around this. We also had similar thing in the past when another popular package had a bug in their patch release, meaning that using `~` is also not a solution -> packages can always make a mistake, even in the smallest updates.  Relevant Discord convo: https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1378216620203180127  This is probably  most relevant: What makes this tricky is that on one hand we want people to get the newest versions of packages Wasp uses, like react-query, but on the other hand, there is always a chance of one of these packages making a mistake like this, even if they are just releasing a patch. But on the other hand you want patches in case they are fixing a security issue. Solution is actually not to pin them down completely, but to give you some freedom on choosing the version yourself in the rare case when something goes wrong like this. But this requires some smart design on our side. We will be thinking about this soon.  Options: - we pin down the versions exactly. Sucks because you can't get a security patch if there is one. Maybe it is critical, what then? Yeah, not good. - we use `~`. But then we can get the same issue still. - actually, it doesn't matter what we use exactly, I think we can go with `^` or `~`, probably `~`, the real solution is giving people the flexibility to specify a version they want, in case something goes wrong, like it did with react-query. Yeah, that is the thing we need, without that it doesn't make sense. Give people control to choose the version if they need to. We can still check it is in the range we care for (e.g. >= 4.29.0 for react-query).
Discuss Information: I threw this one back to triage because I think we should assess it again, it might be good to tackle it sooner than later.  @infomiho how does this relate to recent issues we had with Rollup and Typescript versions? Is it the same (class of) problem? | ### Recent troubles   Typescript -> yes and no, it was a case of us specifying `^5.1.0` which allowed users to have any version from `5.1.0` to `5.8.3`. But one of our changes required **at least `5.4.0`**. Pinning the version ensured that there is no discrepancy between the Typescript version in our automated tests (which used latest version) vs. version which the user used. Rollup -> this was a different class of error where Rollup in the client app is a dependency of our dependency (Vite specified Rollup with a range). We sorted this by say "make sure Vite uses this specific Rollup version, and not a range".  ### Potential solution  I think I might have a solution to our "problem" which would allow users to be more strict if we faced an issue with a dep like `@tanstack/react-query`. Check this comment: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/1859#issuecomment-2273422879  If we compared what users defined _semantically_ users could have the freedom to be within a range we allow. e.g. we say we require `^4` for `@tanstack/react-query` and if we check the version semantically (not as a string) users could pin the version to a specific version temporarily until Tanstack fixes it. | One more thing to add: I'm of opinion if we consider Wasp to be the "complete package" and you are relying on us to provide you a stable mix of technologies, us pinning versions should be acceptable because that's the only way we can guarantee things. Of course, we'd have to be on top of our update game then - always bumping versions with each release (should be easy with minor version bumps �) | > One more thing to add: I'm of opinion if we consider Wasp to be the "complete package" and you are relying on us to provide you a stable mix of technologies, us pinning versions should be acceptable because that's the only way we can guarantee things. Of course, we'd have to be on top of our update game then - always bumping versions with each release (should be easy with minor version bumps �)  I also agree with that, but the problem is that it is always possible that it is discovered some version of package P has a security vulnerability and needs to be updated from a.b.c to a.b.d (so patch version), so we need to allow for that. And you could say ok sure, we will just specify deps with `~a.b.c`, but then what if the situation is opposite, if a.b.c was fine but a.b.d is problematic (which happened multiple times), then they need to be able to go back to a.b.c . We could say we will pin them down completely, so `=a.b.c` and then we will release new versions of Wasp if upgrade is needed, but that too dependent on us -> it introduces a lot of urgent work for us if stuff like that happens, and they have to wait for us to make a release for multiple Wasp versions that we are currently supporting.  So it seems to me that we must give them an option to specify an exact version themselves, if they want, as I wrote in the comment above. We can check it matches our expectations though, as you said above, and we should, and those expectations can be as tight as we like, probably `~`. So > If we compared what users defined _semantically_ users could have the freedom to be within a range we allow. e.g. we say we require `^4` for `@tanstack/react-query` and if we check the version semantically (not as a string) users could pin the version to a specific version temporarily until Tanstack fixes it.  -> yup, this. | I had an idea last week that can be a solution for this, complete with pros/cons and workarounds: #2965  | Closing since this is now possible in https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3120

Description: **Describe the bug** When running wasp build (or tsc --build && vite build), the following TypeScript error occurs:  `src/index.tsx:25:28 - error TS2322: Type 'import("/home/ubuntu/projects/smartzapp/app/node_modules/@tanstack/query-core/build/lib/queryClient").QueryClient' is not assignable to type 'import("/home/ubuntu/projects/smartzapp/app/.wasp/build/web-app/node_modules/@tanstack/query-core/build/lib/queryClient").QueryClient'.   Types have separate declarations of a private property 'queryCache'.  25       <QueryClientProvider client={queryClient}>                               ~~~~~~ `  This appears to be caused by the project resolving two different @tanstack/query-core versions: one in app/node_modules/ and another in .wasp/build/web-app/node_modules/, causing the QueryClient types to become incompatible due to separate instances of the package.  Ubuntu 24.04.2 LTS wasp version 0.16.4 npm: '10.8.2', node: '20.19.0',  Note: I'm self-hosting the app with this tutorial: https://gist.github.com/infomiho/80f3f50346566e39db56c5e57fefa1fe
Discuss Information: Related: #1838 | @gioramies Were you able to get around this ? | @ankuratudemy are you hitting this also?  | @gioramies @ankuratudemy Hmm, I can't reproduce this issue. It's possible you guys somehow ended up in an corrupted project configuration.  What happens if you delete `package-lock.json` and run `wasp clean`? Does that fix the problem? | @sodic  It's quite possible some config was corrupt I am in process of upgrading wasp from 0.11 to 0.16 , and above error popped up when i updated to 0.14.2 (I am upgrading 0.11 -> 0.12--> 0.13 --> 0.14--> following documentation and not skipping version just to be clear)  I tried wasp clean, deleting package-lock.json and .wasp folder as well, but build kept failing.   Ultimately I added override in package.json :  "overrides": {     "@tanstack/query-core": "^4.39.1"   },  and this let me build and deploy app without errors.   Since then I have updated to 0.15.      | I'm not sure what caused the error. I upgraded from 0.16.4 to 0.16.5, and the error disappeared. I suspect there was a version mismatch or incompatibility between different ^4.3x.x versions of @tanstack/query-core used across multiple folders. Locking the version might help avoid similar issues in the future. | It was probably the combo of all that + the fact that react-query released a broken release right at that moment.  Yup, Wasp allowing more flexibility on our side regarding versions of react-query and similar should help! Tracking that in another issue.
