Description: Currently, it takes a bit of configuration to get all tooling around wasp working. Especially around Haskell whose tooling is less supported by text editors.  We should provide config files for often used text edits which would solve above problems. e.g. `.vscode` for VSC  Even better if they could include recommendations for all extensions needed to work with wasp normally. e.g. `.vscode\extensions.json` for VSC  This would solve issues around: 1. Which formatters to use (e.g. `ormolu` for Haskell, which is not default) 2. Setting up formatters (e.g. pointing to wasp's local `ormolu` binary) 3. Setting up right Haskell environment (e.g. use `GHCup`) 4. Wasp packages     - e.g. wasp extension for VSC has `wasp.server.executable` which allows it to grab AppSpec from local development version of binary, which is necessary to test any AppSpec changes with wasp extension for VSC  TODO: any more issues solved by this? TODO: which text editors to support? should each be it's own issue with this being an parent issue?
Discuss Information: Related to #2736 (we can also add prettier formatting) | Formatters and environment are different problems though. I can't see us running GHCup inside a VS Code configuration right? (If it's even possible?) | More about pointing haskell to right binaries, since user might have multiple versions installed, and might not have our version in path as default.  e.g. `.vscode`  ```jsonc {   "haskell.manageHLS": "GHCup",   "haskell.toolchain": {     "hls": "1.8.0.0"   }, } ```  Users can always override these settings, if needed. | > Users can always override these settings, if needed.  No, they can't though. If I modify my User preferences, the workspace ones will take precedence. If I modify the Workspace preferences, this will show up as a modified file in git. | That's what I meant, this configuration files should follow how we specify to set up the environment in our `waspc` readme. Any deviation from that and you should not expect it to be set up for you.  If they are deviating I don't see it a problem that they have it as an modified file in git.  | > If they are deviating I don't see it a problem that they have it as an modified file in git.  I don't agree � I think each environment's setup (especially if only related to a single IDE) is a local workstation concern and not one that should be stored in the repo (moreover if it's going to override my User preferences).  For reference I have my haskell setup through [mise](https://mise.jdx.dev/) and not through GHCup. Git would keep bothering me to stash the config file or whatever. | That is a good point. How about limiting the scope to what is actually done on workspace level, rather than tampering with global configuration? e.g. we always store `ormolu` binary we want to use in repo, so having that might be worthwhile? | My preference would be to not add any settings for the environment at all (e.g. `ormolu`), and just add those related to workflow (e.g. auto-formatting). If we can define the versions without setting paths for them, I'm also up for it. | I haven't read everything, I would just be careful that we don't spend too much time here on somethign that at the end won't work for everybody.  On one hand, having clear instructions in README on what to install is maybe the best -> they can follow that easily and all is good.  It will depend on the specifics of the implementation here, I am just saying, I wouldn't push this too far -> if we can get something very practical very easily, great, if not, manual instructions are probably good enough. | I think we can close this.  I don't think there is a single perfect solution for this, and I don't think we want to enforce a certain approach to users. Also going down this path means we should then support as many popular editors as possible, which is both a maintenance problem and requires research.   @Martinsos @cprecioso if you agree we can close this.  | agree | Also agree!

Description: GHC was complaining:  ``` src/Wasp/Project/Analyze.hs:19:1: warning: [-Wunused-imports]     The qualified import of ‘Wasp.AppSpec.ConfigFile’ is redundant       except perhaps to import instances from ‘Wasp.AppSpec.ConfigFile’     To import instances alone, use: import Wasp.AppSpec.ConfigFile()    | 19 | import qualified Wasp.AppSpec.ConfigFile as CF    | ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ ```
Discuss Information: I've also seen this warning:  ``` Defined but not used: ‘debouncerRunningEvents’ ```  Referring to:  https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/830d12cb570039587891c67a0fc7237d5aae73d6/waspc/waspls/src/Wasp/LSP/Debouncer.hs#L85-L95  I tried removing the `debouncerRunningEvents` definition, but it complains that `newtype`s must have at least one field. I don't know enough Haskell yet to know what to do, if someone could help out that'd be great. | > I've also seen this warning: >  > ``` > Defined but not used: ‘debouncerRunningEvents’ > ``` >  > Referring to: >  > https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/830d12cb570039587891c67a0fc7237d5aae73d6/waspc/waspls/src/Wasp/LSP/Debouncer.hs#L85-L95 >  > I tried removing the `debouncerRunningEvents` definition, but it complains that `newtype`s must have at least one field. I don't know enough Haskell yet to know what to do, if someone could help out that'd be great.  I am guessing that `Debouncer` is used somewhere in the code, or you could just delete the whole type. But if `debouncerRunningEvents` is not used, that means `Debouncer` is constructed with positional arguments, not named ones. So somewhere we have `Debouncer foo`, and not `Debouncer { debouncerRunningEvents = x }`. So one soluition could be to rewrite the first form to the second one. That is anyway recommended with records, to not use positional arguments when constructing them. Although I am guessing this one has just one field so it was kinda ok. If not that, then the solution would be to supress the error with some kind of comment. But I would go for the rewrite.

Description: ### Description   Context: [RFC](https://www.notion.so/wasp-lang/RFC-Building-binaries-with-wide-libc-support-1f818a74854c809e93f8eddfd2d618b8?pvs=4)  This PR extracts the building and packaging logic from `wasp-ci.yaml` to its own `waspc-build.yaml`.  It tries to simplify the steps as much as possible. The intention is that the `steps` are easily readable and understandable in a single screenful.  Most complexity lays in how we define the environments we run in, as we declare there the OS-specific dependencies we need to install.  Through this abstraction, we can define in a single place _all_ our build targets, keeping compatibility workarounds to a minimum:  - `wasp-linux-x86_64`: built on a Ubuntu 20.04 Docker image so we have a low `glibc` requirement - `wasp-linux-x86_64-static`: **(NEW)** built on an Alpine image and with [the `--enable-static-build` cabal flag](https://cabal.readthedocs.io/en/stable/cabal-project-description-file.html#cfg-flag---enable-executable-static), so musl gets embedded into the binary and we're completely independent of the user's distro - `wasp-darwin-x86_64`: no changes, built on the GitHub macOS runner - `wasp-darwin-amd64`: **(NEW)** we use the new GitHub macOS ARM runners to build an Apple Silicon binary - `wasp-darwin-universal`: **(NEW)** I do an extra step at the end of picking up both macOS binaries and creating a Universal binary out of both of them.  All binaries are uploaded as artifacts of the workflow run, which means they are available to later steps. They are also accessible to us through [the workflow's summary page](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/actions/runs/15134136572?pr=2768).  > [!IMPORTANT] > This workflow is not active yet. We can run it manually [through GitHub's interface](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/actions/workflows/waspc-build.yaml) when it comes to the default branch. I first sent the approach so we can discuss it by itself. The automation is complete and available at #2773.  ### FAQs  <details> <summary>Why do we need to manually specify so many dependencies when we weren't doing it before?</summary>  Before, we were building on the GitHub runners, which come with a lot of dependencies already installed (even GHC!). Now we're building inside Docker images, and they are usually bare-bones, so they are missing many basic dependencies (e.g. Ubuntu is missing `curl` and Alpine is missing `bash`).  </details>  <details> <summary>Why build inside of Docker?</summary>  So we don't depend on GitHub Action runners anymore. They keep updating them, changing the available software, and deprecating old runners. If we want to keep building on an old Glibc, we need pin the environment through Docker.  </details>  <details> <summary>Why do we build an ARM image for macOS and not Linux?</summary>  GHC 8.10.7 has support for ARM on macOS but not on Linux. Updating to a newer version (#1446) will enable us to do ARM binaries for Linux.  </details>  <details> <summary>Why are we now able to build executables for macOS on ARM?</summary>  New images from GitHub became available.  </details>  <details> <summary>Why have two binaries for Linux and three for macOS?</summary>  These are implemented in order for us to continue building in different environments and testing before release. We don't need to publish all of them.  I'd suggest to publish the Linux static and the macOS universal only. If we agree on that we can remove the others.  </details>  <details> <summary>Can we statically link the macOS binaries?</summary>  No. The syscall interface is an internal API in macOS, so we must dynamically link to `libSystem.dylib`, which is the public API. Fortunately, libSystem is much lighter than libc, and quite stable between releases, so it is not a big problem there.  </details>  <details> <summary>Why extract this into a new workflow?</summary>  `wasp-ci` is already quite heavy to read through, and full of workarounds. Having the build there would have been more messy than helpful. We can still call to this build from other workflows, so we don't lose functionality.  </details>  ---  - Related to #2773  - Related to #2703 - Related to #2003 - Related to #2283

Discuss Information: Wow cool PR @Scorpil ! How was it contributing, the whole experience? Anything particularly tricky in this PR that we should pay attention to? Any parts that you are not sure if they are done the right way / could benefit from more input? We will check it thoroughly in any case, but this kind of input helps us also.  @infomiho is the master of Auth, so I added him as a reviewer! | Hey @Martinsos, thanks. I think contributing documentation is pretty good and got me a good start. Also build scripts work pretty much out of the box, which is nice (in my experience, a lot of open source projects struggle with this).  Codebase is straightforward enough once you get a hang of how generator/templates relate to client/server/sdk. I have prior knowledge of Haskell, but even without it the code is simple enough to contribute feature like this.  For this particular feature I benefited a lot from using other OAuth providers (mainly discord) as a reference of what needs to be added.  As for the less than optimal parts: - Because of the complex setup, I didn't manage to configure my IDE to resolve imports correctly, which made development inconvenient. Would appreciate some docs around that. - minor setback was that llvm 13 is disabled in brew since the beginning of 2025, but a workaround described [in this SO thread](https://stackoverflow.com/questions/73586208/can-you-install-disabled-homebrew-packages) worked well - there _might_ be an opportunity to de-duplicate some of the OAuth provider-related code, now that we support more OAuth providers. But not 100% sure how easy it is seeing how everyone seems to implement OAuth in their own way... Maybe a little duplication actually servers a purpose tbf.  Apart from studio change I mention in a separate comment, rest of the code was manually tested and should work well. Where in doubt I implemented behaviors similar to discord (e.g. after registration email status is unverified).  One thing that I'm not completely sure about is whether we should expose `configFn` for slack. On one side, it's good for consistency, since every other provider has it. On the other side: slack doesn't allow requesting additional scopes during sign in (https://api.slack.com/authentication/sign-in-with-slack#request) so it's not useful. I opted in for consistency here.  ---  @infomiho looking forward to your review. | @Scorpil thanks for the details response, that all sounds reasonable and good! Glad to hear you found the `run` script worked well, I just recently updated the waspc/README.md to leverage it exclusively + udpated it a bit, so it was a success then :). All right, let's wait for @infomiho then! | > Great stuff @Scorpil, when can you start on the next PR? �  Next time I hit a roadblock in my project �  All fixed up now, please take another look @infomiho  | I've reworked the docs a bit in this commit: [`4e54a5a` (#2764)](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/2764/commits/4e54a5a8902a64eea20178422b67978c3cc7987c)  This is good to go � After the CI passes I'll merge it.  | Thanks @infomiho.  Wait a minute with merging please - I think I misread this part in Slack docs:  > You request the OpenID scopes—[openid](https://api.slack.com/scopes/openid), [email](https://api.slack.com/scopes/email), and [profile](https://api.slack.com/scopes/profile). (With legacy Sign in with Slack, you requested legacy identity.* scopes.)  I assumed all three are required but I _think_ email and profile are actually optional. I'm testing it out and will push changes shortly if necessary. | Tested & confirmed @infomiho. Changed to only have `openid` by default (no need to force people into PII processing �), left `openid`, `email` and `profile` as overrides in examples and docs since that's probably the most common use-case. All done from my side now.

Description: Longtime users who regularly update Wasp now have a large chunk of their disk space taken by our binaries (especially thanks to #2718).  For example, let's say that a user's been using Wasp since 0.11. and updating regularly. By the current version of Wasp (v0.16.3), the total size of their `.local/share/wasp-lang` is **8.5 GB.**  Fixing #2718 mitigates the problem, but should we still offer the users a chance to delete old binaries on updates? We don't support anything except the last version or two, so there's no point in keeping older versions installed locally.  This could be as simple as twaking the installer to say something like: > You have many older versions of wasps installed locally taking up a lot of space, run `wasp uninstall && curl -sSL https://get.wasp.sh/installer.sh | sh -s` to only keep the latest one.
Discuss Information: Sorry, this issue was closed by a crazy integration, undid it and fixed the configuration | Ok, Inspector Gadget.  At least I kept my Discord messages this time :D

Description: Issue detected in Wasp 0.16.3.  Wasp `.tar.gz` files are much larger than we intended them to be: - **Intended size:** ~15 MB compressed, 92 MB uncompressed - **Actual size we've been shipping:** ~110 Mb compressed, ~500 MB uncompressed  ## Why?  The difference comes from our build accidentally including several `node_modules` directories, specifically: - `data/packages/studio/client/node_modules` - Since [v0.11.5-studio1](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/releases/tag/v0.11.5-studio-1).     This directory got in because the script `install_packages_to_data_dir` [deletes only the top-level `node_modules` directory](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/b411bc2f5ebb1eb4be7d2f0c0b23d7b61c9b0596/waspc/tools/install_packages_to_data_dir.sh#L19), while the `studio` package also has a `node_modules` directory under the `client` subdir.  - `data/packages/prisma/node_modules` - Since [v0.15.0](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/releases/tag/v0.15.0).    This directory got in because:    1. Our CI runs e2e tests before packaging the binary.   2. The generator runs `prismaFormat` which uses  the `prisma` package.   3. Using the `prisma` package installs its dependencies and creates the `node_modules` folder.   4. Nothing in `make_binary_package` deletes the created `node_moduels` folder so it ends up in the binary.   ## What should we do? We certainly shouldn't change how we write the code to satisfy the build process (e.g., not use `prismaFormat` or something).  Possible solutions: 1. Tweak the `install_packages_to_data_dir` script to recursively delete all `node_modules` - fixes only the subdir problem. 2. Don't run e2e tests in the CI job used for building the package - fixes only the accidental `node_modules` creation problem. 3. Tweak the `make_binary_package` script to delete or exclude `node_modules` - fixes both problems, but our `data/packages` directory no longer corresponds to what ends up in the binary (might not be a problem)  4. Ignore `node_modules` in `waspc.cabal` (if possible)  - same as solution 3, but is at least declarative :) 5. Reorganize our scripts so that running `make_binary_package` in no way depends on the current state of the project. Perhaps we should even reorganize the entire procedure around this packages (e.g., never copy them, always keep them in the data dir, and exclude what we don't need from the final build).   I believe we should do 5 (which possibly includes 1, 3,  4)  1 is probably the best short term fix, but it's incomplete and a little dirty. I don't like 1+2 because we'd be solving the problem in the wrong place. Our build should be "pure" and functional on its own, not only under specific CI constraints or broader execution context).
Discuss Information: @sodic while taking a look at [Stack](https://docs.haskellstack.org/en/stable/) I saw [Hpack](https://github.com/sol/hpack) and think that the glob syntax for data files might better suited for this than Cabal's. I made a first stab at it, maybe useful: #2759 (disclaimer: I am not well-versed in Haskell ecossystem so maybe this causes more problems than it solves, I leave it up to you) | Another possibility:  Our **current** approach now is basically:  1. Build: 	1. Install dependencies and build each package 	2. Remove the `node_modules` folder 	3. Copy to `data/packages` 2. We ship the full package folders in the `data` 3. Runtime: 	1. Install dependencies for the package (inside the data dir) 	2. Run the script  ---  Maybe we can use `npm pack` instead of copying the folders around. [`npm pack`](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v8/commands/npm-pack) is the npm command that produces the tarballs that later get updated to the registry. It doesn't include the `node_modules`, and in fact only includes the files declared in [`package.json#files`](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v11/configuring-npm/package-json#files). We could probably only have our `dist` folders there.  Later, `npm install` can just be pointed to the tarball, and will install it as if it was any other package from the registry. This is not an esoteric use-case, it is [documented](https://docs.npmjs.com/cli/v8/commands/npm-install#:~:text=a%20gzipped%20tarball%20containing) and supported.  1. Build: 	1. Install dependencies and build each package 	2. **`npm pack -o wasp-deploy.tgz`** (e.g. for the deploy package) 	3. Copy **the tarball** to `data/packages` 2. We ship the the tarballs in the `data` 3. Runtime: 	1. `npm i data/wasp-deploy.tgz` (open question: _where do we install the package to? maybe in `.wasp/build`?_) 	2. Run the script  ---  As a further improvement, this is very similar to how we would install a published package if we ever want to move the supporting packages to be published in npm (like e.g. `https://npmjs.com/package/@wasp/deploy`)

Description: ## Why is GLIBC problematic?  Quick background for anyone unfamiliar: - GLIBC is the "GNU C Library", a core part of the Linux operating system. It's a bunch of wrappers around Linux kernel system calls (so it's essential for all programs). - Wasp's executable depends on GLIBC. - GLIBC is a *dynamically linked* library, meaning that it's not packaged into the executable during build. Instead, the executable expects to find it on the host system and "import" it dynamically. - Problems occur when you run a program on a system with a GLIBC version that's older than the one used when compiling (because then your program relies on features that don't exist on the host system).  So, what sometimes happens with Wasp: 1. We compile `wasp` on a new Linux system with a recent GLIBC version (e.g., Ubuntu 22.04 with GLIBC 2.34). 1. Users try to run `wasp` on an older Linux system with an older GLIBC version (e.g., Ubuntu 20.04 with GLIBC 2.31). 1. Wasp fails because it can't find the newer GLIBC functions it relies on.   We usually fix the problem by telling our CI to use the lowest possible version for building Linux executables. This maximizes compatibility (explained [here](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/10cba640cdb51b1130db396c2b1b449c1ee43aff/.github/workflows/waspc-ci.yaml#L44)).  ## The current problem  Wasp will probably always break for some portion of users: - GitHub only allows us to build on Ubuntu versions still within their LTS period (the oldest being 22.04 at the time of writing). - Users are possibly still using older versions despite the LTS expiry (20.04 at the time of writing).  So there's always a gap between what Github allows us to build on and what some users are still running. This gap is largest immediately after each LTS expiry. Here's what happens when users try to run Wasp 0.16.3 on Ubuntu 20: ``` filip@ubuntu-20$ wasp version /home/filip/.local/share/wasp-lang/0.16.3/wasp-bin: /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6: version `GLIBC_2.34' not found (required by /home/filip/.local/share/wasp-lang/0.16.3/wasp-bin) /home/filip/.local/share/wasp-lang/0.16.3/wasp-bin: /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6: version `GLIBC_2.32' not found (required by /home/filip/.local/share/wasp-lang/0.16.3/wasp-bin) /home/filip/.local/share/wasp-lang/0.16.3/wasp-bin: /lib/x86_64-linux-gnu/libc.so.6: version `GLIBC_2.33' not found (required by /home/filip/.local/share/wasp-lang/0.16.3/wasp-bin) ```  The main question is: > Does the problem affect a significant portion of our users and is it something we want to fix?  @Martinsos What do you think?  If so, I see two possible fixes: - Figuring out how to build Wasp with an even older, LTS-expired version. - Exploring how to link GLIBC statically  (see [here](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/650#issuecomment-1180488040)).  ## Motivation and references This issue pops up every once in a while: - https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/650 - https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/867 (thanks @EmmanuelTheCoder!) - https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/2703
Discuss Information: @sodic that is a good analysis! I am not sure at the moment what is the right move, it feels like we should investigate more. Maybe look into what others are doing.  There was this comment on that older issue: > If I were you, I would just do what the rest of the world does and build on a distribution like Alpine that uses musl. This will greatly simplify things.  That potentially sounds like an interesting direction, if it makes sense for our use case. | musl sounds like the best direction possibly, but let's see if it really affect people. | Looks like it does: https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/686873245428875297/1367162171812745237 | I've manually built the latest Wasp version with Ubuntu 20, so Wasp 0.16.3 no longer has this problem.  If you have Wasp 0.16.3 and are still experiencing the issue, you must delete the old binary and pull the new one:  ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/c2461262-4015-4dd0-ba6d-ce1d1d135ff0)  That should fix it :) | We're now building binaries with a. low glibc requirement, and b. static musl included; as part of our release process.  #2804 will teach the installer to fetch them.

Description: ### Environment  - **Wasp Version(s) Tried:** 0.16.3 (latest), 0.16.2 - **Node Version:** (please fill in your node version, e.g., 20.x) - **OS:** macOS  ---  ### Problem Summary  I am unable to get Wasp to generate the SDK modules for my actions and queries. Specifically, the modules under `@wasp/actions/...` are never generated, resulting in persistent TypeScript errors in my frontend code:  ``` error TS2307: Cannot find module '@wasp/actions/setPhotographerAvailability' or its corresponding type declarations. error TS2307: Cannot find module '@wasp/actions/createOrUpdatePhotographerProfile' or its corresponding type declarations. ```  ---  ### What I Have Tried  #### 1. **File Structure and Imports**  - My backend operations are defined in `src/photographer/operations.ts` (and also tried as `operations.js`). - All actions/queries are exported as named exports. - My `main.wasp` imports actions/queries using the documented `@src/photographer/operations.js` (and previously `.ts`) syntax:  ```wasp action setPhotographerAvailability {   fn: import { setPhotographerAvailability } from "@src/photographer/operations.js" } action createOrUpdatePhotographerProfile {   fn: import { createOrUpdatePhotographerProfile } from "@src/photographer/operations.js" } # ...and similar for all queries ```  #### 2. **Cleaning and Rebuilding**  - Ran `wasp clean`, deleted `.wasp/out`, `.wasp/cache`, and `node_modules`. - Reinstalled all dependencies. - Ran `wasp db migrate-dev` and `wasp start`.  #### 3. **Tried Both .ts and .js Extensions**  - Tried both TypeScript (`operations.ts`) and plain JavaScript (`operations.js`) for the operations file. - Updated all imports in `main.wasp` accordingly.  #### 4. **Upgraded and Downgraded Wasp**  - Upgraded to the latest Wasp (`0.16.3`). - Downgraded to `0.16.2` (which then failed with a dependency conflict: `@types/node, ^20.0.0` vs `^18.0.0`).  #### 5. **Checked tsconfig.json and Project Structure**  - Confirmed that `tsconfig.json` includes all of `src/`. - No TypeScript errors in the backend code itself; only the missing SDK modules.  #### 6. **Full Error Output**  Each build fails at the SDK generation step with:  ``` [  Wasp  ] src/photographer/usePhotographerAvailability.ts(4,41): error TS2307: Cannot find module '@wasp/actions/setPhotographerAvailability' or its corresponding type declarations. [  Wasp  ] src/photographer/usePhotographerProfile.ts(4,47): error TS2307: Cannot find module '@wasp/actions/createOrUpdatePhotographerProfile' or its corresponding type declarations.  ❌ --- [Error] Your wasp project failed to compile: -------------------------------  - SDK build failed with exit code: 2 ```  ---  ### Additional Info  - The rest of the app compiles and runs as expected if I comment out the frontend imports of these actions/queries. - I have followed all Wasp documentation and best practices for actions/queries. - The project is a real estate listing app with a focus on photographer onboarding and availability management.  ---  ### Steps to Reproduce  1. Create actions/queries in `src/photographer/operations.ts` (or `.js`). 2. Import them in `main.wasp` using the documented `@src/photographer/operations.js` path. 3. Run `wasp clean`, delete caches, reinstall, and rebuild. 4. Observe that the SDK modules under `@wasp/actions/...` are never generated.  ---  ### What Should Happen  - Wasp should generate the SDK modules for all actions and queries as documented, and the frontend should be able to import them without TypeScript errors.  ---  ### What Actually Happens  - The SDK modules are never generated, resulting in persistent TypeScript errors in the frontend.  ---  ### Screenshots/Code  (You can attach screenshots of the error output and your `main.wasp`/`operations.js` file if helpful.)  ---  ### Request  - Please advise on any possible workaround, or let me know if this is a known issue or regression. - If a patch or new release is required, please provide an ETA or workaround.  Thank you!
Discuss Information: `@wasp/...` imports don't exist (they existed a 1 year+ ago, but we changed the import paths)  If you are using LLMs to generate code, we kindly ask you to please go through the Wasp's tutorial first to get the hang of Wasp basics: https://wasp.sh/docs/tutorial/create | Closing this one for now since this is probably correct soluiton, but if there is something else we can reopen it!

Description: Wasp CLI demands a certain shape of both the `package.json` (packages and package versions) and `tsconfig.json` files (certain options being present and set to certain values). Users get errors similar to: ``` ❌ --- [Error] Your wasp project failed to compile: -------------------------------  - The "compilerOptions.module" field is missing in TS config, you must set it to: "esnext". ``` User have to then manually update the configs to be correct. It would be a nice DX improvement if Wasp CLI could update the config to have correct values. For the example error above, it would modify the `module` field to `"esnext"`.   Notes: - `package.json` is just JSON, so parsing it isn't a problem and writing it should be fine - `tsconfig.json` is JSON with comments (we actually use `node` to parse it) and writing it with new values might prove difficult
Discuss Information: Personally, this feels very intrusive. I'd rather not let some framework/tool change my code on a whim.  Generated files are okay and expected to be changed by frameworks/tools. Init scripts adding their respective base settings is also okay (e.g. `sentry` initializing itself). Especially when they ask you of changes they are going to do and you need to confirm.  This is a case of "your settings don't match my needs, I'm just gonna overwrite it". Feels a bit too much. | It is good that we explore this idea, but after a bit of thinking I am also more on the "too aggresive" side. We inform them about the issue, and they can easily fix it. If it is not so easy to fix and we want to help, we can, in the warning/error message, provide extra helpful info on how to do it. But modifying stuff for them is both work on our end that is tricky to implement and then maintain, and also can feel intrusive and possibly mess with their workflows.  I have a recent example from dotenv tool modifying gitignore to add entries that ignore env files. It is really annoying and makes me wnat to drop it. I don't like how it does it, I have a better way of doing it, and I just wish I could turn it off but I can't. They removed it now in the new version of it.

Description: We had a user using SSL certificates for his database connection and while it worked fine with Prisma, they had some issues with PgBoss. [Discord Thread](https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1360545905362866226/1360545905362866226)  > Pg boss expects base64 encoded cert data in the variables ssl.ca, ssl.cert and ssl.key in the json structure that must be set in the environment variable `PG_BOSS_NEW_OPTIONS`, (including the connectionString set to DATABASE_URL without the prisma specific ssl arguments). This is not secure or preferable from a workflow perspective. What would be preferable is to be able to provide paths to the cert files and settings to other configurable parameters of pgBoss, just like prisma expects.  User fixed the PgBoss related issue by editing Wasp framework files (`pgBoss.ts`): ```ts  let pgBossNewOptions = {     connectionString: config.databaseUrl.split('?').at(0),     ssl: (env.PG_ROOTCERT && env.PG_CLIENTCERT && env.PG_CLIENTKEY) ? {         rejectUnauthorized: true,         ca: fs.readFileSync(env.PG_ROOTCERT),         cert: fs.readFileSync(env.PG_CLIENTCERT),         key: fs.readFileSync(env.PG_CLIENTKEY)     } : false,     max: 20, // number of allowed simultaneous connections     application_name: 'wasp-pgboss' };  ```  The user describes their ideal workflow next:  > Ideally we would setup db connection properties generally via environment variables and let wasp handle `DATABASE_URL` internally (if needed). It could be backwards compatible by not defining `DATABASE_URL` and that would tell wasp to look for other environment vars.  > As one way of making this less obscure, because I just now realized some of the DATABASE_URL arguments are not picked up by prisma, but some are which is obviously very confusing.  > For example, the prisma-specific-argument `sslaccept` is picked up in dev, but blocks prisma from finding prisma-specific-arguments `sslcert` and `sslidentity` in production.  > `pgboss` on the other hand, want a root certificate (if not defined by node or system) a client certificate and a client key to be provided to it [(see more)](https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1360545905362866226/1360560301547847811).  So making that smoother from the wasp interface would be nice.
The process would be: when there is a bug, write test for it. If it is hard to write test, that means we don’t have the setup for those tests or it is not good → so open an issue for that.

Description: *   **Wasp Version:** `^0.16.2`   ---  ## Problem Description  I have a Wasp application with a custom API endpoint `askDocumentsApi` defined for the route `POST /api/ask`. This API is intended to be called from a Chrome extension using a custom JWT authentication scheme (JWT + `X-Device-ID` header).  In `main.wasp`, the `askDocumentsApi` is defined **without** the `auth: true` flag, explicitly intending to bypass Wasp's built-in session/user authentication for this specific route and handle authentication manually within the function using the custom JWT.  ```tsx // main.wasp excerpt (Initial state causing the issue) api askDocumentsApi {   fn: import { askDocumentsApi } from "@src/apis.js",   entities: [Document, Section, User], // User entity is required for manual verification logic below   httpRoute: (POST, "/api/ask")   // auth: true <-- REMOVED / COMMENTED OUT }  apiNamespace apiNamespace {   middlewareConfigFn: import { apiMiddleware } from "@src/apis.js",   path: "/api" } ```  The `apiNamespace` applies a simple CORS middleware (`apiMiddleware` in `src/apis.js`) to all routes under `/api`, configured with `origin: '*'` and allowing `Authorization` and `X-Device-ID` headers.  ```javascript // src/apis.js - apiMiddleware excerpt (Initial state) export const apiMiddleware = (middlewareConfig) => {   middlewareConfig.set('cors', cors({     origin: '*',     methods: ['GET', 'POST', 'PUT', 'DELETE'],     // --- ADD CUSTOM HEADER TO CORS ---     allowedHeaders: ['Content-Type', 'Authorization', 'X-Device-ID', 'X-Auth-Token'] // Added X-Auth-Token for workaround     // --- END ADD ---   }));   return middlewareConfig; }; ```  The Chrome extension successfully: 1.  Authenticates with Google using `chrome.identity.launchWebAuthFlow`. 2.  Sends the Google ID token and a unique `deviceId` to another Wasp API endpoint (`/api/generate-jwt`). 3.  Receives a custom JWT from `/api/generate-jwt`. 4.  Attempts to call `POST /api/ask` including the following headers:     *   `Authorization: Bearer <received_jwt>`     *   `X-Device-ID: <unique_device_id>`     *   `Content-Type: application/json`  ---  ## Expected Behavior  The `POST /api/ask` request should reach the `askDocumentsApi` function defined in `src/apis.js`. Since `auth: true` is not specified for this API in `main.wasp`, Wasp's internal auth checks should be bypassed, allowing the function's code to execute (which includes custom JWT verification via `verifyJwt`).  ---  ## Actual Behavior  The `POST /api/ask` request consistently receives a `401 Unauthorized` response from the Wasp server **when the `Authorization` header is present**.  Crucially, when this 401 occurs, logging added within the `askDocumentsApi` function (even when simplified) **does not appear** in the Wasp terminal output. Furthermore, logs added inside the `apiMiddleware` function (which is applied via the `apiNamespace`) **also do not appear** for the `/api/ask` request. The only output in the Wasp terminal related to this request is the final `POST /api/ask 401 Unauthorized ...` log line.  ---  ## Debugging Steps Taken  1.  Confirmed `auth: true` is removed from `askDocumentsApi` definition in `main.wasp`. 2.  Confirmed `apiMiddleware` correctly configures CORS to allow necessary headers and origin. 3.  Added extensive `console.log` statements (via `logServer` helper) at the very beginning of the `askDocumentsApi` function -> **Logs do not appear when 401 occurs.** 4.  Simplified `askDocumentsApi` function to its bare minimum (log entry + return 200 OK) -> **Still 401 when `Authorization` header present, logs do not appear.** 5.  Added `console.log` statements at the beginning of the `apiMiddleware` function -> **Logs do not appear for the `/api/ask` request when 401 occurs.** 6.  Confirmed the `/api/generate-jwt` endpoint works and the extension sends the correct `Authorization` and `X-Device-ID` headers to `/api/ask`. 7.  **Confirmed that removing the `Authorization` header from the request allows the request to reach the simplified `askDocumentsApi` function and return 200 OK.** This pinpointed the `Authorization` header as the trigger for the premature 401.  ---  ## Hypothesis  The presence of the `Authorization: Bearer ...` header seems to be triggering an early rejection (401) within Wasp's request processing pipeline, possibly by a global middleware or the core routing/auth mechanism, *before* the route-specific configuration (`auth: false`) or even the namespace middleware (`apiMiddleware`) is fully respected or executed. It behaves as if Wasp's internal auth check is still implicitly active whenever an `Authorization` header is present, regardless of the `auth: false` setting (or lack thereof) on the API definition.  ---  ## Workaround Implemented  To bypass the premature 401 triggered by the `Authorization` header, the following workaround was implemented:  1.  **Use a Custom Header:** Changed the Chrome extension to send the JWT in a custom header `X-Auth-Token` instead of `Authorization`. 2.  **Update CORS:** Modified the `apiMiddleware` in `src/apis.js` to allow the new `X-Auth-Token` header. 3.  **Adapt Token Extraction:** Updated the `askDocumentsApi` function in `src/apis.js` to read the token from `req.headers['x-auth-token']`.  *(Note: A secondary issue related to missing `User` in the API's `entities` list was also identified and fixed, but is separate from the core `Authorization` header problem being reported here.)*  ### Code Changes for Workaround (Focusing on Header Change)  *   **`chrome-extension/popup.js` (inside `askDocuments` function):**     ```javascript     // ...             const token = await getJwtToken();             const deviceId = await getDeviceId();              if (!token || !deviceId) {                 logDebug('Missing token or deviceId for askDocuments call.');                 displayLoginError('Not logged in. Please log in first.');                 hideLoading();                 return { error: 'Authentication required' };             }              const apiUrl = `${API_BASE_URL}/api/ask`;              // --- USE CUSTOM HEADER ---             const headers = {                 'Content-Type': 'application/json',                 'Accept': 'application/json',                 'X-Auth-Token': token, // Custom header                 'X-Device-ID': deviceId,             };             // --- END CUSTOM HEADER ---              const requestOptions = {                 method: 'POST',                 headers: headers,                 body: JSON.stringify({ query }),                 mode: 'cors',                 cache: 'no-cache'             };     // ...     ```  *   **`src/apis.js` (`apiMiddleware`):**     ```javascript     export const apiMiddleware = (middlewareConfig) => {       // ... logs ...       middlewareConfig.set('cors', cors({         origin: '*',         methods: ['GET', 'POST', 'PUT', 'DELETE'],         // --- ADD CUSTOM HEADER TO CORS ---         allowedHeaders: ['Content-Type', 'Authorization', 'X-Device-ID', 'X-Auth-Token']         // --- END ADD ---       }));       // ... logs ...       return middlewareConfig;     };     ```  *   **`src/apis.js` (`askDocumentsApi` - excerpt):**     ```javascript     export const askDocumentsApi = async (req, res, context) => {       // ... logs ...       try {         // ... logs ...          // --- EXTRACT TOKEN FROM CUSTOM HEADER ---         const token = req.headers['x-auth-token']; // Use lowercase header name         const deviceId = req.headers['x-device-id'];         // --- END TOKEN EXTRACTION ---          if (!token) { // Check for custom token             logServer('[askDocumentsApi] ERROR: Missing X-Auth-Token header.');             return res.status(401).json({ error: "Authentication required: Missing token" });         }         if (!deviceId) {             logServer('[askDocumentsApi] ERROR: Missing X-Device-ID header.');             return res.status(401).json({ error: "Authentication required: Missing device ID" });         }          // ... rest of the try block (calling verifyJwt, etc.) ...        } catch (jwtError) {         // ... error handling ...       }       // ... rest of the function ...     };     ```  *(Note: The `main.wasp` definition for `askDocumentsApi` requires `User` in its `entities` list for the manual `verifyJwt` logic to access the database, but this is unrelated to the `Authorization` header issue.)*  ---  ## Conclusion  With the workaround (using `X-Auth-Token`), the `POST /api/ask` request successfully bypasses the premature 401, reaches the `askDocumentsApi` function, reads the token from the custom header, and proceeds with the intended manual authentication logic.  However, the need for this workaround suggests an underlying issue or unexpected behavior in Wasp's handling of the standard `Authorization` header on API routes explicitly configured without Wasp's built-in authentication (`auth: false` or default). It seems Wasp attempts internal processing based on the header's presence, contrary to the expectation set by `auth: false`.
Discuss Information: The default is `auth: true` you should put explicitly `auth: false` to prevent Wasp from checking the `Authorization` header.  Here's the auth middleware: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/main/waspc/data/Generator/templates/sdk/wasp/core/auth.ts  Here's the logic for setting the default value as true: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/main/waspc/src/Wasp/Generator/SdkGenerator/ServerApiG.hs  Let me know if you got it to work with this info. | ```auth: false``` solved the issue, thank you Miho! ![boi](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/6d43e572-2ec7-43b8-af96-6c6e04a9b798)

Description: Wasp allows users to mix client and server code (we even recommend it in some cases).  What happens if the same file contains: 1. An innocuous utility function called `foo` (used on the client). 2. A "private" function (containing some secret) called `bar` (used on the server).  And the user imports `foo` into a client source file.  Does Wasp accidentally leak `bar` to the client? Even if it doesn't, that's probably thanks to tree-shaking. Assuming this is the case, we should put in a conscious effort to prevent such leakage. Tree-shaking is primarily a performance optimization and we shouldn't rely on it for security purposes.  Researching code elimination in Next.js might be helpful here.  ### A deeper question Even when everything works properly (i.e., the server-side code is consciously eliminated), users can still accidentally leak their data (e.g., by misorganizing their code and including secrets in client chunks). This is a problem in all projects, but Wasp's relaxed way of mixing client and server code increases the chances of it happening.
Discuss Information: @cprecioso please check this   Might be related to #1864  | Possibly related also: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/1741 .

Description: ### Description  Fixes #2266  When SendGrid throws an error, we will try to see if it has a `response.body.errors` property [(recommended by them)](https://github.com/sendgrid/sendgrid-nodejs/blob/main/docs/use-cases/success-failure-errors.md), and surface those errors through an `AggregateError`.  New console output:  ``` [ Server ] POST /auth/email/signup 200 63.315 ms - 16 [ Server!] Failed to send email AggregateError: SendGrid error: Unauthorized [ Server!]     at <anonymous> { [ Server!]   [errors]: [ [ Server!]     { [ Server!]       message: 'The provided authorization grant is invalid, expired, or revoked', [ Server!]       field: null, [ Server!]       help: null [ Server!]   code: 403, [ Server!]   response: { [ Server!]     headers: { [ Server!]       server: 'nginx', [ Server!]       date: 'Mon, 02 Sep 2024 10:41:16 GMT', [ Server!]       'content-type': 'application/json', [ Server!]       'content-length': '281', [ Server!]       connection: 'keep-alive', [ Server!]       'access-control-allow-origin': 'https://sendgrid.api-docs.io', [ Server!] [ Server!]       'x-no-cors-reason': 'https://sendgrid.com/docs/Classroom/Basics/API/cors.html', [ Server!]       'strict-transport-security': 'max-age=600; includeSubDomains' [ Server!]     }, [ details: 'Forbidden', [ Server!]   type: 'MailgunAPIError' [ Server!] } ```  ### Select what type of change this PR introduces: 1. It would make sense that users can access it! We had [one user](https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1347645809952424017/1347645809952424017) who set up a global middleware that enriches `req` with unique UUID that they then use in the rest of the code when logging, to connect different logs to the same request. This makes sense, but they couldn't access that UUID from the Operation, because `req` is not exposed!  Might it make sense to just add `req` to `context` (`context.req`)?
Discuss Information: I believe this mixes levels of abstractions and I wouldn't do it in our current system (e.g., the users could then override our RPC pipeline and cause all kinds of errors).  That said, you could make the argument that "if you're using `req`, you know what you're doing," so it might not be so bad.  Ideally, I'd offer operations in several levels of abstraction: 1. Don't deal with the network, just deal with the data (what we have now) 2. Deal with the network (explicit `req` and `res`)  But then again, the second group would miss out on superjson parsing, so I guess adding `req` to `context` is probably the best course of action for now. | +1 -> we had user who wanted to obtain IP from the `req` and use it in Operation, but can't get to it. | Hey guys. Almost one year from the initial post and I'd like to say I'd really love to have access to the request object so I can leverage the headers cloudflare gives me for free. TBH the Context object is almost useless for me, barely using it  for user validations. I have thin actions/queries and delegate the business logic and data access to other layers (Service / Repository pattern) so using prisma client at the end for db access and not the models added in the context object.   this is a typical action for me:  ```TS type StartEventActionInput = {   eventId: string; };  export const startEvent: StartEvent<   StartEventActionInput,   HostEventWithRelations > = async ({ eventId }, context) => {   assertAuthenticated(context.user);   const hostId = context.user.id;    const event = await eventsService.startEvent({ hostId, eventId });    await CacheInvalidation.invalidateEvent(eventId);    return event; }; ``` But in many places I need to identify users location, ip, language, etc and to solve that I had to add a fingerprinting mechanism to identify the user with as many information as possible using an API endpoint (the only place I can have access to the req) and return an identifying UUID to the caller and later use that as param on every action/query  so I can circumvent the lack of request object and properly identify where the user is from. Maybe in the action/query definition in main.wasp the developer can decide the signature of the action, something like:  ``` action startEvent {   fn: import { startEvent } from "@src/server/actions/events/index",   entities: [User, Event, Game]   withReqRes: true // default false, but allowing the action implementation to expect the request and/or response  } ```  | @cea2k thanks for bringing this up, it's one of our ideals to allow you to "peel the onion" of our abstractions and use things directly e.g. using `req` when using our RPC abstractions.   We'll talk about this in the next planning meeting to see what kind of solution we can work on _now_ or in the _near future_ and I'll report back.   For now, the escape hatch you described is something that works, albeit it's quite annoying. Ideally, we'd either allow you to defined the operations with lower level primitives e.g.  1. Define a custom API endpoints _but wrap it with `createAction` helper_ 2. Which you can use on the client with full-stack type-safety as if you defined it using the `action` declaration  Another approach is to expose extra info in the `context` like you suggested, adding`req` to `context`. | @infomiho thanks for your response, appreciated!. Just another idea that might seems simpler for you to allow. I tried this before trying to hack into the context, but didn't work as the Context is generated on the fly at compile time so is not possible to override it. I added this middleware:   ```TS // In app/src/server/setup/index.ts export const middlewareConfigFn: MiddlewareConfigFn = middlewareConfig => {   middlewareConfig.set('requestContext', extractRequestContext);    return middlewareConfig; };  // then in app/src/server/setup/requestContext.ts ... export interface RequestContext {   ipAddress: string;   country?: string;   city?: string;   timezone?: string; }  /**  * Extract request context from headers and body  * Works with both Cloudflare (production) and localhost (development)  */ export function extractRequestContext(   req: Request,   res: Response,   next: NextFunction ) {   const isProduction = process.env.NODE_ENV === 'production';    let ipAddress = isProduction     ? (req.headers['cf-connecting-ip'] as string) ||       (req.headers['x-forwarded-for'] as string)?.split(',')[0] ||       req.ip ||       'unknown'     : req.ip || '127.0.0.1';    let country = isProduction     ? (req.headers['cf-ipcountry'] as string) || undefined     : 'CL';   let city = isProduction     ? (req.headers['cf-ipcity'] as string) || undefined     : 'Santiago';   let timezone = isProduction     ? (req.headers['cf-timezone'] as string) || undefined     : 'America/Santiago';    // Attach to request   (req as any).context = {     ipAddress,     country,     city,     timezone,   } as RequestContext;    next(); } ``` | @cea2k Yeah, that will add things to `req` but we still have the issue of getting the `req` object in an operation context. Of course, we could just add `req` to operations, but I had some ideas about "context providers". This is a feature where you would defined what extra fields from the Express layer you want to bring to the operation `context` by defining a function. This is very much in line with other hooks have and I think it could work well for your use case as well.   This is something we'll work on for sure, as we are approach to Wasp 1.0 (sooner than later, don't wory :)). Thank you for your input on this, this means a lot for our API design.

Description: Currently in waspc project README we state the following: """ :warning: If you are on Mac and get "Couldn't figure out LLVM version!" error message while building, make sure you have LLVM installed and that it is correctly exposed via env vars (PATH, LDFLAGS, CPPFLAGS). The easiest way to do it is by just running `brew install llvm@13`, this should install LLVM and also set up env vars. """  This is a problem because llvm@13 has been disabled (and soon to be deprecated) by brew: ``` ➜  waspc git:(main) brew install llvm@13 Error: llvm@13 has been disabled because it is a versioned formula! It was disabled on 2024-12-31. ➜  waspc git:(main) ``` The status of each version can be found on: https://formulae.brew.sh/formula/llvm   The problem is also that the cabal project can't compile with higher versions of llvm (tried myself), and the same is stated in the error message: ``` <no location info>: error:     Warning: Couldn't figure out LLVM version!              Make sure you have installed LLVM between [9 and 13) ```  ## Workaround  This can be fixed by a workaround inspired by: https://stackoverflow.com/questions/73586208/can-you-install-disabled-homebrew-packages/78631773#78631773  EDIT: a workaround without tapping homebrew/core is described here: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/2533#issuecomment-2729430553  Tap homebrew-core: ```brew tap homebrew/core --force```  Edit the formula with: ```brew edit llvm@13```  Remove the line: ```disable! date: <DATE>, because: <REASON>```  And install it with: ```HOMEBREW_NO_INSTALL_FROM_API=1 brew install llvm@13```  Though this requires tapping homebrew/core which is ~1GB in size and a bit of work.  How do we want to proceed with the update for the docs?
Discuss Information: The docs from the GHC version we're using (GHC 8.10.7 release notes), say:  >  This release of GHC supports LLVM versions up to LLVM 12.   And Homebrew has not (yet) disabled `llvm@12` (it will in July '25), but apparently it has no security attestations so you do have to run it like `HOMEBREW_NO_VERIFY_ATTESTATIONS=1 brew install llvm@12`.  ---  EDIT: Couldn't build Wasp with LLVM 12, will keep trying.  Another option to modify the formula is to just download [the formula file](https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-core/blob/master/Formula/l/llvm%4013.rb), edit it, and do `brew install ./llvm@13.rb`, no need to tap and clone the repo. | Will be solved by #1446

Prisma has a Decimal type which SuperJSON can't handle, we need to implement custom handling for it.   > We should probably implement a custom serializer for the Prisma.Decimal type: https://github.com/flightcontrolhq/superjson?tab=readme-ov-file#decimaljs--prismadecimal  Source: https://ptb.discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1337972417171165296/1338461339898740757
Discuss Information: Hm, this isn't a bug. It's probably an enhancement (assuming we do it).  That said, I'm not sure we should do it. Perhaps we should approach it from a different angle (e.g., better support for custom serialization).  `Decimal` is an some arbitrary database type (probably a `class` of some sort). JavaScript doesn't support it natively, and Superjson is a serialization layer for native JavaScript types.  I understand the convenice of directly returning the result of a Prisma operation from a Query**, but supporting its serialization seems out of place (imagine `Decimal` sitting in [this table](https://github.com/flightcontrolhq/superjson?tab=readme-ov-file#examples-3)). Why stop at `Decimal`, we should then support all custom Prisma types. And even then, why stop at Prisma? We should support all possible custom types anyone can come up with. We have to draw the line somewhere, and I feel Superjson drew it in the right place.  We could add support for generic clases though (Superjson supports kinda supports it: https://github.com/flightcontrolhq/superjson/issues/34), but I think there was a type-safety issue with that.  Another relevant issue: #2027  @infomiho I've changed some labels, I think these are more appropriate.  ** Btw, I know we do this a lot in our examples, but directly returning Prisma results isn't the best of practices. It makes it really easy to accidentally leak suff to the client. | > And even then, why stop at Prisma? We should support all possible custom types anyone can come up with.  I have to counter this by saying: we chose to build a batteries included framework with Prisma at its core - so it's not going too far if we want to support serializing Prisma results in our queries. Users will hit this - we should have a solution for them, either automatically (serialisation upgrade) or manually (with some helpers that transform the problematic data types).  I wanted to mention https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/2027 originally, but I felt like these are separate issues (one is about upgrading what we already have, the other will require research to see if it will be possible to build it and if we want to build it). | Users running into this: https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1349702247889768510/1349702247889768510 | i came here for just this issue.  i'm writing an app that handles money, which is a very common scenario.  i have a db field as shown below.  i was all ready to go, only to find out it doesn't serialize.  this is just too common a need imo to not support natively.  if you are going to support prisma, you're going to need to support scenarios like this, or, update your prisma page to explicitly say which field types you will and won't support.  and again imo, the latter won't go over well.  a framework like wasp shouldn't place such limitations upon its users.  not trying to be critical - just the opposite, i want wasp to succeed!  i'm writing an app that will go to production in the next couple weeks, so i'm invested for sure :).  in the meanwhile, is there some manually way you know of to make this work?    maybe another longer-term way you could look at this is by actually submitting a fix to superjson itself?  i found this issue that is still open...  https://github.com/flightcontrolhq/superjson/issues/152    ```   amount Decimal @db.Money ``` | Hey @johnwest80, thanks for the feedback (and the patience). Your comment somehow slipped through without us noticing.  Anyway, we're definitely adding it in (it's all but decided). In the meanitime, here's what you can do: - Try [this fix](https://github.com/flightcontrolhq/superjson?tab=readme-ov-file#recipes) recommended by the SuperJson team. You can place the code in Wasp's server setup function. It will require some type assertions in the operation. - You can always serialize and deserialize the `DECIMAL` data yourself (as you would if Wasp isn't doing the serialization for you). - Finally, unless your app is an exchange or something that requires large values with large precision, you probably don't need to store money as a decimal type. In fact, it's maybe better if you don't. You could use integers and represent cents (or the tenths of a cent if you want more precision). Even if you go with the more precise option (i.e., the number `1000` represents a single dollar, `10` represents a single cent, etc.). Singed 64-bit integers allow you to store balances within a range of 18 quadrillion dollars (9.2e18), which should probalby be enough :) | Merged, will be released with the next version cc @johnwest80

Description: ### Description  1. Version Switching - `wasp version <version>` installs/switches to a new Wasp version. - `--force` flag enables a hard install from the shell script. - The most recent release is allowed to sit in the shell wrapper, delegating commands to other wasp binaries depending on the user selection.  2. Updating - `wasp` update fetches and installs the latest Wasp version. - `--force flag` does a hard install from the shell script.  3. Dev builds - Massive improvement to the dev experience. ./run install has been overhauled, it fits by default in the new versioning system. - With the install script, you can run your builds right off the bat on wasp, but protecting your other versions. - The install script will detect if your build is going to overwrite something, and give you the chance to add a suffix to it (i.e. 0.16.0-dev). Upon successfully building, it automatically puts your new build as the activated wasp version. - This way, you don't have to worry about your builds, just give them a quick suffix. Great for switching between a massive amount of branches without having to redownload release ones. If you wish, you can overwrite the binary when building - `./run install --force` will hard install your build as the source of truth for the shell wrapper.  ### Select what type of change this PR introduces: 1. [ ] **Just code/docs improvement** (no functional change). 2. [ ] **Bug fix** (non-breaking change which fixes an issue). 3. [x] **New feature** (non-breaking change which adds functionality). 4. [ ] **Breaking change** (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected).  ### Update Waspc ChangeLog and version if needed If you did a bug fix, new feature, or breaking change, that affects waspc, make sure you satisfy the following: 1. [ ] I updated [ChangeLog.md](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/main/waspc/ChangeLog.md) with description of the change this PR introduces. 2. [ ] I bumped waspc version in [waspc.cabal](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/main/waspc/waspc.cabal) to reflect changes I introduced, with regards to the version of the latest wasp release, if the bump was needed.  ### Update example apps if needed If you did code changes and added a new feature or modified an existing feature, make sure you satisfy the following: 1. [ ] I updated `waspc/examples/todoApp` as needed (updated modified feature or added new feature) and manually checked it works correctly. 2. [ ] I updated `waspc/headless-test/examples/todoApp` and its e2e tests as needed (updated modified feature and its tests or added new feature and new tests for it).
Discuss Information: Hi @sktripamer , thanks for this big contribution! Sorry for taking so long to review it -> we are heavily in planning mode lately so it wasn't so easy for me to find enough time to properly review such a big PR. I finally took a look at it today, and while it looks impressive regarding what it tries to do, I have to admit I am finding it quite hard to review it properly due to its size -> there is a lot of stuff happening at the same time.  I think the best course of action from here would be to possibly split this PR into smaller PRs, where each PR has reduced scope, so we can go step by step and review each one properly. Ideally each one of them would be introducing a somewhat smaller change, that is easier to comprehend and review. Also, more explanations / comments might help -> you already provided quite a big description for this PR, but still, it doesn't feel enough -> I don't get from it why certain decisions were made, what was the thinking behind it. There is also nothing to guide me throuth the new design of code, which to me at the moment is not obvious enough for me to grasp it without additional context.  I am curious, was this PR developed in big part with the help of AI? If not my apologies, but it does give that vibe. If that is so, that makes this harder because I can't rely on you thinking through every single details of the PR, and that means we have to do it as reviewers. | Hi, I'm closing this PR due to the author not responding. Feel free to create a new one or reopen this one and fix it if you're still interested in seeing this merged!

Description: **Describe the bug**  I am currently trying to deploy an app built with Open-Saas on Netlify and AWS EC2 instance with Nginx. Everything was working in the developer environment, but when I try logging in via Google it breaks. The Google Auth screen shows up normally and gives a response that results in several redirects, but after signing in it eventually redirects to https://myapp.netlify.app/oauth/callback, where it is met with a 404 (the same redirection returns a 304 in local developing environment). I have posted the screenshots from Google Dev console. At the time of failure, the url showing up in chrome is https://myapp.netlify.app/oauth/callback#{long_token}  **To Reproduce** Use the [Open Saas Template](https://opensaas.sh/) to deploy a project frontend on Netlify and Docker container on an AWS EC2 instance with Nginx using Google Auth. The Http requests will break due to Netlify's security policy (Https requests will also be banned if you use a self-generated certificate), so add a custom domain name and use openssl to generate a trusted certificate and host a redirect on CloudFront.  Signing in via Google Auth will result in an error. In partilcular, it will result in a Netlify's "This page does not exist" error.  **Expected behavior** Signing in via Google Auth results in a website error page / redirects to the example ai app.  **Screenshots** Here is the error page I got: ![Screenshot 2024-10-18 at 03 10 05](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/1c33a825-e4ca-4523-b012-05a1f5e9ec35)  Here is the expected error page (Ideally it should sign me in and go to the app but even if Google Auth fails we should see this error instead of the previous one): ![Screenshot 2024-10-18 at 03 12 41](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ddcaec22-fdea-4d2a-8aca-44785c2cd874)  Here are the two redirect requests being met with different responses: ![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/ddbff43d-2452-40c2-b81b-1dd221baf1dd) ![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/4dc7c78a-7e0e-49a4-8072-8ab3a26f2ddf)   **Desktop (please complete the following information):**  - OS: Mac  - Browser: Chrome  - Version Version: 129.0.6668.91 (Official Build) (arm64)  **Additional context** I went though the docs and previous questions but was not able to find much helpful information. I imagine it has something to do with the netlify.toml file but am not sure what to change. Please let me know if there are any other information I can provide. Any suggestion would be much appreciated!
Discuss Information: Additionally, when I try to directly enter https://myapp.netlify.app/oauth/callback, it gives the Netlify “page not exist” error page. When I enter http://localhost:3000/oauth/callback in the local dev environment, however, it brings me to the ai demo app page (if I signed in) or to a error page still with the web app’s nav bar (if I did not sign in)  This leads me to hypothesize that the front end code might just be built incorrectly. Does this auth/callback URL correspond to a webhook or a file? (I am new to this so sorry in advance for the dumb question) Any suggestions on how to debug it? | Hmm tired to run the built version for front end locally and http://localhost:3000/oauth/callback also brings me to the app or the error page. Seems like there is something wrong with either the router or building the oauth callback page. This bug is quite mysterious so far, not sure what could have gone wrong. Any advice is appreciated! | I asked the question on Discord and got a solution:  I was originally building the app on push not through the Netlify CLI's terminal command, but through linking Netlify with the Git Repo and using a hacky deploy command through their web interface on each Git push:  ``` curl -sSL https://get.wasp-lang.dev/installer.sh | sh -s -- -v 0.15.0 && /opt/buildhome/.local/bin/wasp build && cd .wasp/build/web-app && npm install && REACT_APP_API_URL=https://server.com/ npm run build ```  For some reason, this does NOT build the `OAuthCallbackPage` correctly and results in the issue.  The issue is fixed when I switched to using the Netlify CLI's terminal command in Github Actions to deploy. Here is my Github Action file for deploying frontend, people may find this useful:   <details> <summary>Github Action</summary>  ``` name: Deploy Client to Netlify  on:   push:     branches:       - main  # Deploy on every push to the main branch  jobs:   deploy:     runs-on: ubuntu-latest      steps:       - name: Checkout Code         uses: actions/checkout@v2        - name: Setup Node.js         id: setup-node         uses: actions/setup-node@v4         with:           node-version: '20'        - name: Docker setup         uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3        - name: Install Wasp         run: curl -sSL https://get.wasp-lang.dev/installer.sh | sh -s -- -v 0.15.0 # Change to your WASP version        - name: Wasp Build         run: cd ./app && wasp build        - name: Install dependencies and build Vite project         run: |           cd ./app/.wasp/build/web-app           npm install           REACT_APP_API_URL=${{ secrets.WASP_SERVER_URL }} npm run build        - name: Deploy to Netlify         run: |           cd ./app/.wasp/build/web-app           npm install -g netlify-cli           netlify deploy --prod --dir=build --auth=$NETLIFY_AUTH_TOKEN --site=$NETLIFY_SITE_ID      env:       NETLIFY_AUTH_TOKEN: ${{ secrets.NETLIFY_AUTH_TOKEN }}       NETLIFY_SITE_ID: ${{ secrets.NETLIFY_SITE_ID }} ``` </details> | The original issue is closed but I think it would make sense in the Docs to explicitly mention to NOT deploy through the Netlify auto deploy on push option on the Netlify website, which seems very tempting since it is a lot less work than typing up a Github Action yml file. This is generally the default option for most people building site there).  I originally encountered this issue yesterday at 3pm (that's when I asked in Discord) and the issue is solved at 3am today. Now when I look back, a small update in the Docs would really save me a lot of time. Especially because I originally thought it was a backend network issue in the AWS EC2 instance (there's no documentation on deploying via AWS) and completely overengineered my nginx setting �  If possible, I would like to update the docs myself - I think this project is really cool and am excited to contribute to it! However, I'm not sure if this issue only applies to projects built upon the Open Saas template, or whether it applies to all Wasp projects in general. If I am to update the docs, should I update the docs for the Open Saas project or just for Wasp?  | I answered in more details here: https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1296568113692938272/1296926685735817308 ! Super short: Problem is that for wasp to work on Netlify, since Wasp is SPA, we need Netlify to redirect requests to index.html in correct way. Wasp comes withi netlify.toml for that, but one needs to make sure to use that file or set the setting another way. Related issue: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/1982  .

Updates `socket.io` from 4.7.2 to 4.8.0
Release notes

Sourced from socket.io's releases.

socket.io-client@4.8.0

Features

Custom transport implementations

The transports option now accepts an array of transport implementations:

import { io } from "socket.io-client"; import { XHR, WebSocket } from "engine.io-client"; 
const socket = io({ transports: [XHR, WebSocket] });

Here is the list of provided implementations:

Transport Description
Fetch HTTP long-polling based on the built-in fetch() method.
NodeXHR HTTP long-polling based on the XMLHttpRequest object provided by the xmlhttprequest-ssl package.
XHR HTTP long-polling based on the built-in XMLHttpRequest object.
NodeWebSocket WebSocket transport based on the WebSocket object provided by the ws package.
WebSocket WebSocket transport based on the built-in WebSocket object.
WebTransport WebTransport transport based on the built-in WebTransport object.

Usage:

Transport browser Node.js Deno Bun
Fetch :white_check_mark: :white_check_mark: (1) :white_check_mark: :white_check_mark:
NodeXHR :white_check_mark: :white_check_mark: :white_check_mark:
XHR :white_check_mark:
NodeWebSocket :white_check_mark: :white_check_mark: :white_check_mark:
WebSocket :white_check_mark: :white_check_mark: (2) :white_check_mark: :white_check_mark:
WebTransport :white_check_mark: :white_check_mark:

(1) since v18.0.0 (2) since v21.0.0

Added in f4d898e and b11763b.

Test each low-level transports

When setting the tryAllTransports option to true, if the first transport (usually, HTTP long-polling) fails, then the other transports will be tested too:

import { io } from "socket.io-client"; </tr></table>

Description: Depending on user requests, we could support the TypedSQL Prisma feature released in Prisma 5.19.0  It enables users to write custom SQL queries and get the type safety for the results when using them in their app.   https://www.prisma.io/docs/orm/prisma-client/using-raw-sql/typedsql#getting-started-with-typedsql
Discuss Information: Ended up here searching for a way to implement a self-referential recursive query using Prisma in Wasp. Prisma doesn't natively support them, but they can be achieved through the use of Prisma's TypedSQL feature.   But it seems while Wasp does allow for Prisma Preview Features generally, it doesn't allow for the TypedSQL feature?   Is it because under the hood Wasp doesn't add the ``--sql`` flag to the ``prisma generate`` command? | > Is it because under the hood Wasp doesn't add the --sql flag to the prisma generate command?  Yes, I'm guessing that's the case. Here's the code that calls `prisma generate`: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/499e84b906ad2c2ba07bd8138c8e70b80f670946/waspc/src/Wasp/Generator/DbGenerator/Jobs.hs#L159  I'll tag @infomiho. He's the expert.  Should we maybe support the `--sql` flag by default? Are there any the downsides. @the-notable Feel free to jump in, you likely know more than I do :)

Description: Looking at the stack trace in this [Discord thread](https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1280117478285905921/1280117478285905921) it's obvious we are not outputting the full error which is crucial for successful debugging. Also, we could maybe recognise common sets of errors and give extra info to the user like "it looks like you received a 403 error, did you set up the sending email properly?" and similar.
Discuss Information: One more user hit it: https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1280117478285905921/1291512858718240861 . | I'm copying the message from the original Discord thread for easier searching: ``` [ Server!] Failed to send email ResponseError: Forbidden [ Server!]     at /home/kaloyan/projects/podcast-FE/app/node_modules/@sendgrid/client/src/classes/client.js:146:29 [ Server!]     at process.processTicksAndRejections (node:internal/process/task_queues:95:5) { [ Server!]   code: 403, [ Server!]   response: { [ Server!]     headers: { [ Server!]       server: 'nginx', [ Server!]       date: 'Mon, 02 Sep 2024 10:41:16 GMT', [ Server!]       'content-type': 'application/json', [ Server!]       'content-length': '281', [ Server!]       connection: 'keep-alive', [ Server!]       'access-control-allow-origin': 'https://sendgrid.api-docs.io', [ Server!]       'access-control-allow-methods': 'POST', [ Server!]       'access-control-allow-headers': 'Authorization, Content-Type, On-behalf-of, x-sg-elas-acl', [ Server!]       'access-control-max-age': '600', [ Server!]       'x-no-cors-reason': 'https://sendgrid.com/docs/Classroom/Basics/API/cors.html', [ Server!]       'strict-transport-security': 'max-age=600; includeSubDomains' [ Server!]     }, [ Server!]     body: { errors: [Array] } [ Server!]   } [ Server!] } ``` | I ran into the same problem by updating the email inside the auth block.  It might be helpful to mention this step in the documentation to avoid confusion for others.

Release notes

Sourced from webpack's releases.

v5.94.0

Bug Fixes

  • Added runtime condition for harmony reexport checked
  • Handle properly data/http/https protocols in source maps
  • Make bigint optimistic when browserslist not found
  • Move @​types/eslint-scope to dev deps
  • Related in asset stats is now always an array when no related found
  • Handle ASI for export declarations
  • Mangle destruction incorrect with export named default properly
  • Fixed unexpected asi generation with sequence expression
  • Fixed a lot of types

New Features

  • Added new external type "module-import"
  • Support webpackIgnore for new URL() construction
  • [CSS] @import pathinfo support

Security

  • Fixed DOM clobbering in auto public path

Bug Fixes

  • Generate correct relative path to runtime chunks
  • Makes DefinePlugin quieter under default log level
  • Fixed mangle destructuring default in namespace import
  • Fixed consumption of eager shared modules for module federation
  • Strip slash for pretty regexp
  • Calculate correct contenthash for CSS generator options

New Features

  • Added the binary generator option for asset modules to explicitly keep source maps produced by loaders
  • Added the modern-module library value for tree shakable output
  • Added the overrideStrict option to override strict or non-strict mode for javascript modules

v5.92.1

Bug Fixes

  • Doesn't crash with an error when the css experiment is enabled and contenthash is used

Bug Fixes

  • Consider runtime for pure expression dependency update hash
  • Return value in the subtractRuntime function for runtime logic

Description: Motivated by this convo on Discord: https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1277607531363172496/1277645496743231550 .   > we wanted to make sure that wasp was not just a beginner friendly "vendor" lockin which seems to NOT be the case. Building on this it would be beneficial for this information to be transparent to help developers who DO want to fast track their development but also do have scaling concerns. This is to make sure that wasp does not appear just as a quick way to MVP but as a quick way to bootstrap enterprise quality webapps   Motivated by this I took a look at our landing page and I wonder how clear is it at all that Wasp is not a one-shot code generator but a proper framework.  For example that diagram we have, if you look at it quickly, looks like it is code generator, not a framework.  ![image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/220b8e6e-aa29-41a1-be73-197f8223aae0)   Seems to me like one would have to read the landing page quite carefully to get the right notion!  We should probably make it clear what Wasp is + modify that diagram or text around it to make it very clear that Wasp is not a code generator. Maybe the diagram should go somewhere lower, it is really talking about the impleemntation details that are not that super important to somebody just learning about Wasp, while they don't still get what it is.
Discuss Information: > I think adding additional details for the “Production / Scaling” part of the docs would indeed be the best choice since you can pretty much explain what I was told here, that basically people have nothing to fear because at the end of the day they can use the same tools they could use directly from the JS ecosystem for scaling or tackling similar concerns (even if you don’t have internal solutions for that at this point). >  > In general, in these docs I would want to be reassured through that production section that Im “buying” into a framework(lang?) that takes care (or at least does not stop me from doing it) of security, scalability, maintainability since Im already sold on ease of use, speed and efficiency. > Actually that would solve a major issue of choosing to develop (backend) in the JS ecosystem in general not just wasp
