Description: Right now in our managed dev db we use docker image `postgres:latest`, which means at some point, version of postgres changes, and your local dev database might now work with it.  At that moment you have to delete the old volume to get it to work again.  We might want to pin down the version of postgres we use and then update that when we release new versions of Wasp. We need to remember to update it though!  Or, we might somehow remember which version of postgres is used for that project for dev db and then keep using that image.  Or we should allow users to pick their own image (and we just pre-set it for them, the value for it?).  Or maybe it is fine as it is, once it happens it shouldn't be that hard for users to start with new dev db.  Related Discord convo: https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/943092165964349500/1186266204344696912 .
Discuss Information: User hit this: https://ptb.discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1318540556435193888/1318540556435193888 | Possibly another user hitting this: https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1415330929316200488/1418961834714730607 . | Latest conclusion is that we will likely want to pin down the version of docker image, and we will update it every so and so with new versions of Wasp. This way upgrade won't happen suddently, out of the blue, but when you ugrade Wasp version, which is something you can control, so it is a better option. | Btw interesting workaround that user used: they locally, on their machine, tagged a specific postgres docker image with "latest". That way they didn't have to change anything on the Wasp side and can still use `wasp start db`, but have control over which version of postgres is it. Of course, this is not great if you need actual "latest" for something else and now it is mapped to older version.  This does open up an interesting solution though: we could say that wasp pulls in a docker image with certain tag, e.g. "wasp-db", and if not then "latest", giving you control. But it would have to be project specific though, and that gets complicated now, yeah. Probably not the solution to consider on the long term.

Description: Currently directly deploying Wasp frontend on GH pages doesn't work because of how client side routing is done, GH pages explain the issue here: https://create-react-app.dev/docs/deployment/#notes-on-client-side-routing . They also explain different solutions: switching to different kind of routing, or doing this https://github.com/rafgraph/spa-github-pages#usage-instructions .  What we could do: 1. Switch from using HTML5 history API to routing with hashes. But I am not sure if we can also guarantee users of Waps will stick with it, so it sounds problematic. 2. Do the suggested trick with to teach GitHub Pages to handle 404s by redirecting to your index.html page with a custom redirect parameter. If they are deploying to GH pages, we could make sure to generate client files so that they are already ready for this. 3. Add documentation about this, so people are aware of the issue and can handle it on their own.  Relevant GH convo: https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1170037967574802513/1170037967574802513 .
Discuss Information: I ran into a similar issue when trying to deploy a Wasp project front-end (modified from OpenSaaS, version 0.17.0) on GitHub Pages via GitHub Actions. I tried several solutions and spend probably way more time than I should fixing the issue. I would like to document my attempts here. Since I am documenting failed attempts as well, this will be a long comment.   If you are unfortunately suffering from similar issues and just needs a solution, I would recommend looking at the **Epilogue** section at the very end before reading the rest of this comment.  Do note I followed the migration guide to update my project to the newest version from version 0.15.0, which might be why some of the seemingly promising attempts did not work.  ### Issue Description  The issue itself is pretty simple: - I was visiting `https://user.github.io/project-name/` or the new homepage. - The router knows nothing of the route `/project-name`. - Router is mad and throws error. As a result:  The page shows: ``` There was an error rendering this page. Check the browser console for more information. ``` The console shows: ``` ErrorResponseImpl {status: 404, statusText: 'Not Found', internal: true, data: 'Error: No route matches URL "/project-name/"', error: Error: No route matches URL "/project-name/"     at getInternalRouterError (http://loc…} ```  ### Attempt 1: Update `package.json` (Failed)  I added the following field in `package.json`: ```diff {   "name": "opensaas",   "type": "module", + "homepage": "https://user.github.io/project-name/",   ... ```  However, that did not fix the error. After doing some research, it seems that the homepage updates the asset path when the application wants a file. Here is an example: ```html <!-- Without homepage --> <script src="/assets/index-abc123.js"></script>  <!-- With homepage: "https://user.github.io/project-name/" --> <script src="/project-name/assets/index-abc123.js"></script> ```  Unfortunately, that has nothing to do with the router - it still has no clue why we are requesting a non existant page `/project-name` and therefore refuse to load the page, throwing an error instead.  ### Attempt 2: Configure `base` in `npm run build` (Failed)  I updated the build command in GitHub Actions: ```diff       - name: Install deps & build Vite project         working-directory: app/.wasp/build/web-app         env:           REACT_APP_API_URL: ${{ secrets.ENV_WASP_SERVER_URL }}         run: |           npm ci || npm install -          REACT_APP_API_URL=${{ secrets.ENV_WASP_SERVER_URL }} npm run build +          REACT_APP_API_URL=${{ secrets.ENV_WASP_SERVER_URL }} npm run build -- --base=/project-name/ ```  Also did not work, presumably due to the same reasons as **Attempt 1**.  ### Attempt 3: Follow [spa-github-pages](https://github.com/rafgraph/spa-github-pages#usage-instructions) Tutorial (Partial Success)  I added the following step in GitHub Actions: ```       - name: Create SPA routing script         working-directory: app/.wasp/build/web-app/build         run: |           cat > spa-routing.js << 'EOF'           // GitHub Pages SPA routing fix           (function(l) {             if (l.pathname.startsWith("/project-name")) {               var newPath = l.pathname.replace("/project-name", "") || "/";               window.history.replaceState(null, null, newPath + l.search + l.hash);             }           }(window.location))           EOF            # Inject the script into index.html and 404.html           sed -i '/<head>/a <script src="/project-name/spa-routing.js"></script>' index.html 404.html ```  For details, see [spa-github-pages](https://github.com/rafgraph/spa-github-pages#usage-instructions), basically this updates the subpath right before the router processes it, so the router does not see the `/project-name` in the subpath.  Combined with **Attempt 1** or **Attempt 2**, the page `https://user.github.io/project-name/` loads normally, but the url bar shows `https://user.github.io`, which means users can not refresh the page. Do note I only tested this solution with Chrome - if you are using other browsers you may see different behavior.  Additionally, all redirection fails since `href='/feature'` jumps to `https://user.github.io/feature` instead of `https://user.github.io/project-name/feature`. I tried to update `404.html` in accordance to the tutorial but it failed due to unknown reasons.   I tried to update all the redirection paths manually (e.g. `href='/feature'` becomes `href='project-name/feature'`) and it seems to work, although the `/project-name` segment is always missing in the url bar, so neither the back button nor the refresh button works normally.  I am building the site for an institution, which has strict criterias on the url format of the site. As a result, this was not a feasible solution for my particular use case.  ### Attempt 4: Update `main.wasp` (Failed)  I added the following field in `main.wasp`: ```diff   client: {     rootComponent: import App from "@src/client/App", +    baseDir: "/project-name",   }, ```  This did not work - still same error as very beginning. Now this is confusing since the feature should be supported according to [Wasp version 0.17.0 documentation](https://wasp.sh/docs/0.17.0/project/client-config#basedir-string). According to the documentation, this setting should do two things: 1. Set Vite's `base` option (similar to **Attempt 1** or **Attempt 2**) 2. Set React Router's `basename`  My current belief is that the setting somehow glitched (maybe due to the update process from 0.15.0 to 0.17.0) and only completed task 1 and not task 2.  ### Attempt 5: Update `main.wasp` AND Update Generated `router.tsx` (Partial Success)  This immediately follows **Attempt 4**. I directly peeked into the generated router at `app/.wasp/out/web-app/src/router.tsx`.  I saw a fresh router with no base configured:  ```jsx const browserRouter = createBrowserRouter([   {     path: '/',     element: <App />,     ErrorBoundary: DefaultRootErrorBoundary,     children: [...waspDefinedRoutes, ...userDefinedRoutes],   }, ]); ```  This is the issue! I the manually overwrote the router into something like:  ```jsx const browserRouter = createBrowserRouter([{   path: '/',  // Root path   element: <App />,   ErrorBoundary: DefaultRootErrorBoundary,   children: [     // Relative path for waspDefinedRoutes but with no leading slash     // '/oauth/callback' becomes 'oauth/callback'     {       path: 'oauth/callback',         Component: OAuthCallbackPage,     },     ...userDefinedRoutes   ], }], {    basename: '/project-name'  // Basename handles the prefix }) ```  And this finally fixed the problem! For implementation, I created a Python file and added a step in GitHub Actions:  <details> <summary>Click to expand Python script & GitHub Actions</summary> </br>  New GitHub Action Step (This should be after running `wasp build` to compile the Wasp application into React application and before running `npm run build` to compile the React application into servable html / css / js files).  ```yaml       - name: Fix Wasp router for GitHub Pages         working-directory: app/.wasp/build/web-app         run: |           python3 ../../../../scripts/fix-router.py src/router.tsx ```  Python script:  ```python #!/usr/bin/env python3 """ Router configuration fix script for GitHub Pages deployment. Place this file in your repository (e.g., scripts/fix-router.py) and call it from GitHub Actions. """  import sys import os from pathlib import Path  def main():     if len(sys.argv) != 2:         print("Usage: python3 fix-router.py <router-file-path>")         sys.exit(1)          router_file = Path(sys.argv[1])          if not router_file.exists():         print(f"❌ Router file not found at {router_file}")         sys.exit(1)          print(f"Found router file: {router_file}")          # Paste your ORIGINAL string here between the triple quotes     original_string = """const browserRouter = createBrowserRouter([{   path: '/',   element: <App />,   ErrorBoundary: DefaultRootErrorBoundary,   children: [     ...waspDefinedRoutes,     ...userDefinedRoutes,   ], }])"""      # Paste your REPLACEMENT string here between the triple quotes     replacement_string = """const browserRouter = createBrowserRouter([{   path: '/',  // Root path   element: <App />,   ErrorBoundary: DefaultRootErrorBoundary,   children: [     {       path: 'oauth/callback',  // Relative path (no leading /)       Component: OAuthCallbackPage,     },     ...userDefinedRoutes  // These should also be relative   ], }], {    basename: '/project-name'  // Basename handles the prefix })"""          try:         # Read the file         content = router_file.read_text(encoding='utf-8')                  # Check if original string exists         if original_string not in content:             print("⚠️  Original string pattern not found in file")             print("File might already be modified or pattern changed")             sys.exit(1)                  # Perform the replacement         new_content = content.replace(original_string, replacement_string)                  # Write back to file         router_file.write_text(new_content, encoding='utf-8')                  print("Router configuration replacement completed")                  # Verify the replacement worked by checking for the basename         if 'basename: \'/project-name\'' in new_content:             print("✅ Router configuration updated successfully")         else:             print("❌ Replacement may have failed - basename not found in output")             sys.exit(1)                  except Exception as e:         print(f"❌ Error processing file: {e}")         sys.exit(1)  if __name__ == "__main__":     main() ``` </details>  This solves the problem partially.  ### Tying Up Loose Ends  Weirdly, contrary to what the documentation / Discord / GPT has been telling me, setting `WASP_WEB_CLIENT_URL` to `https://user.github.io/project-name` actually did NOT work and results in a cors error! I had to update it to just `https://user.github.io` for the page to load at all. I imagine this might be due to the updates and there's probably a low level bug hidden somewhere, but at this point I am too tired to do a deep dive into different versions of Wasp source code.  Additionally, some of the hard coded links are still non-functional and I had to update them manually similar to **Attempt 3** (e.g. `href='/feature'` becomes `href='project-name/feature'`). Luckily my web app only has ~3 pages and there were only a few links. If anyone is running a much bigger application they might need to find a more scalable solution.  Finally, we were using Google Auth. Wasp's Google Auth configuration was *really* not happy with our `baseDir` change and very stubbornly redirects back to `oauth/callback` instead of `project-name/oauth/callback` (To be more specific, the login path successfully redirects to `https://accounts.google.com/v3/signin`, but then after signin completes Google redirects us to `https://user.github.io/oauth/callback`).  Apparently setting `WASP_WEB_CLIENT_URL` to include `/project-name` is the official solution, but then the page won't load! As a result, I spent quite a while (again) diving into the source code and slapping on another similar monkey patch Python file to overwrite the function that builds the Redirect Url for Google Auth, this time updating the GitHub action for backend deployment.  <details> <summary>Click to expand Python script & GitHub Actions</summary> </br>  New GitHub Action Step (This should be after running `wasp build` to compile the Wasp application into Node backend and before packaging the backend into a Docker container.  ```yaml       - name: Fix Oauth for GitHub Pages         working-directory: app/.wasp/build/server         run: |           python3 ../../../../scripts/fix-oauth.py src/auth/providers/oauth/user.ts ```  Python script:  ```python #!/usr/bin/env python3 """ Oauth configuration fix script for GitHub Pages deployment. Place this file in your repository (e.g., scripts/fix-oauth.py) and call it from GitHub Actions. """  import sys import os from pathlib import Path  def main():     if len(sys.argv) != 2:         print("Usage: python3 fix-oauth.py <router-file-path>")         sys.exit(1)          router_file = Path(sys.argv[1])          if not router_file.exists():         print(f"❌ Oauth file not found at {router_file}")         sys.exit(1)          print(f"Found router file: {router_file}")          # Paste your ORIGINAL string here between the triple quotes     original_string = """export async function finishOAuthFlowAndGetRedirectUri({   provider,   providerProfile,   providerUserId,   userSignupFields,   req,   oauth }: {   provider: ProviderConfig;   providerProfile: unknown;   providerUserId: string;   userSignupFields: UserSignupFields | undefined;   req: ExpressRequest;   oauth: OAuthData; }): Promise<URL> {   const providerId = createProviderId(provider.id, providerUserId);    const authId = await getAuthIdFromProviderDetails({     providerId,     providerProfile,     userSignupFields,     req,     oauth,   });    const oneTimeCode = await tokenStore.createToken(authId)    return getRedirectUriForOneTimeCode(oneTimeCode) }"""      # Paste your REPLACEMENT string here between the triple quotes     replacement_string = """export async function finishOAuthFlowAndGetRedirectUri({   provider,   providerProfile,   providerUserId,   userSignupFields,   req,   oauth }: {   provider: ProviderConfig;   providerProfile: unknown;   providerUserId: string;   userSignupFields: UserSignupFields | undefined;   req: ExpressRequest;   oauth: OAuthData; }): Promise<URL> {   const providerId = createProviderId(provider.id, providerUserId);    const authId = await getAuthIdFromProviderDetails({     providerId,     providerProfile,     userSignupFields,     req,     oauth,   });    const oneTimeCode = await tokenStore.createToken(authId)    const redirectUri = getRedirectUriForOneTimeCode(oneTimeCode)      console.log('Original OAuth redirect URI:', redirectUri.toString())      // Fix GitHub Pages subpath for OAuth callback   if (redirectUri.hostname === 'user.github.io' && redirectUri.pathname === '/oauth/callback') {     redirectUri.pathname = '/project-name/oauth/callback'   }      console.log('Modified OAuth redirect URI:', redirectUri.toString())      return redirectUri }"""          try:         # Read the file         content = router_file.read_text(encoding='utf-8')                  # Check if original string exists         if original_string not in content:             print("⚠️  Original string pattern not found in file")             print("File might already be modified or pattern changed")             sys.exit(1)                  # Perform the replacement         new_content = content.replace(original_string, replacement_string)                  # Write back to file         router_file.write_text(new_content, encoding='utf-8')                  print("Oauth configuration replacement completed")                  # Verify the replacement worked by checking for the basename         if replacement_string in new_content:             print("✅ Oauth configuration updated successfully")         else:             print("❌ Replacement may have failed - new string not found in output")             sys.exit(1)                  except Exception as e:         print(f"❌ Error processing file: {e}")         sys.exit(1)  if __name__ == "__main__":     main() ``` </details>  And finally the application works normally.  ### Epilogue  Overall, my final solution sits on top of a large pile of rather unsustainable money patches (especially the exact string matching Python scripts). I am only doing this to meet the requirements for a website review, after which I will eventually move the site to a normal subdomain managed by the organization, so this was acceptable.  If any indie-devs are thinking of hosting web apps on GitHub pages with default subpath, I would strongly advise against it. It would be much easier to just buy your own domain or host the site on one of the better supported options. | @Bojun-Feng thank you very much for a detailed report once again - I just got around to checking this out.   The missing `basename` is definitely a bug on our side. Since it is a rarely used feature that we didn't cover with our automated tests - we didn't notice the issue until now.  I've noted it here: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3253 | Getting CORS to work was not clear, I agree. We ask you to set `WASP_WEB_CLIENT_URL` to the exact URL with the subdir for the e.g. email verification links to work (`https://user.github.io/project-name`) and then use that same value for the allowed CORS domains - which is wrong, subdirs don't constitute a separate domain - so we should drop the subdir from our CORS config before using it (`https://user.github.io`).  I've noted it here: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3254 | I haven't been able to reproduce the issues with OAuth redirects: 1. Deployed the client to `https://wasp-lang.github.io/github-pages-test-wasp/` 2. Deployed the server to `https://gh-pages-test-server.fly.dev/` 3. Set the `WASP_WEB_CLIENT_URL=https://wasp-lang.github.io/github-pages-test-wasp`  **Applied 2 fixes as patches:** <details> <summary>router.tsx basename fix</summary>  ```diff --- .wasp/build/web-app/src/router.tsx	2025-10-13 12:14:44.761625314 +0200 +++ ideal__router.tsx	2025-10-13 12:27:23.536979919 +0200 @@ -42,6 +42,8 @@      ...waspDefinedRoutes,      ...userDefinedRoutes,    ], -}]) +}], { +  basename: "/github-pages-test-wasp" +})    export const router = <RouterProvider router={browserRouter} /> ``` </details> <details> <summary>CORS subdir fix</summary>  ```diff --- .wasp/build/server/src/middleware/globalMiddleware.ts	2025-10-13 13:21:11.121365281 +0200 +++ ideal__globalMiddleware.ts	2025-10-13 13:31:09.325208909 +0200 @@ -10,11 +10,31 @@    const _waspGlobalMiddlewareConfigFn = (mc: MiddlewareConfig) => mc   +// Helper function to remove subdirectory from a URL origin +function transformOrigin(origin: string): string { +  try { +    const url = new URL(origin) +    // Keep only protocol, hostname, and port (remove pathname) +    return `${url.protocol}//${url.host}` +  } catch { +    // If it's not a valid URL, return as-is +    return origin +  } +} + +// Helper function to transform allowedCORSOrigins +function transformCORSOrigins(origins: string | string[]): string | string[] { +  if (Array.isArray(origins)) { +    return origins.map(transformOrigin) +  } +  return transformOrigin(origins) +} +  // This is the set of middleware Wasp supplies by default.  // NOTE: Remember to update the docs of these change.  const defaultGlobalMiddlewareConfig: MiddlewareConfig = new Map([    ['helmet', helmet()], -  ['cors', cors({ origin: config.allowedCORSOrigins })], +  ['cors', cors({ origin: transformCORSOrigins(config.allowedCORSOrigins) })],    ['logger', logger('dev')],    ['express.json', express.json()],    ['express.urlencoded', express.urlencoded()], ``` </details>   **And also, enabled the Github Pages client to work as an SPA:** <details> <summary>Github SPA code in index.html</summary>  ```diff --- .wasp/build/web-app/index.html	2025-10-13 14:13:18.010212000 +0200 +++ ideal__index.html	2025-10-13 14:13:29.228811604 +0200 @@ -11,6 +11,21 @@          <link rel='icon' href='/favicon.ico' />            <title>github-pages-test-wasp</title> + +        <!-- Single Page Apps for GitHub Pages --> +        <!-- https://github.com/rafgraph/spa-github-pages --> +        <script> +          (function(l) { +            if (l.search[1] === '/' ) { +              var decoded = l.search.slice(1).split('&').map(function(s) { +                return s.replace(/~and~/g, '&') +              }).join('?'); +              window.history.replaceState(null, null, +                  l.pathname.slice(0, -1) + decoded + l.hash +              ); +            } +          }(window.location)) +        </script>      </head>        <body> ``` </details>  <details> <summary>404.html in the public dir</summary>  ```html <!DOCTYPE html> <html>   <head>     <meta charset="utf-8">     <title>Redirecting...</title>     <script>       // Single Page Apps for GitHub Pages       // https://github.com/rafgraph/spa-github-pages       // This script takes the current url and converts the path and query       // string into just a query string, and then redirects the browser       // to the new url with only a query string and hash fragment,       // e.g. https://www.foo.tld/one/two?a=b&c=d#qwe, becomes       // https://www.foo.tld/?/one/two&a=b~and~c=d#qwe        var pathSegmentsToKeep = 1; // Keep /github-pages-test-wasp        var l = window.location;       l.replace(         l.protocol + '//' + l.hostname + (l.port ? ':' + l.port : '') +         l.pathname.split('/').slice(0, 1 + pathSegmentsToKeep).join('/') + '/?/' +         l.pathname.slice(1).split('/').slice(pathSegmentsToKeep).join('/').replace(/&/g, '~and~') +         (l.search ? '&' + l.search.slice(1).replace(/&/g, '~and~') : '') +         l.hash       );     </script>   </head>   <body>   </body> </html>  ``` </details>  **Extra note** The fact that the Github Pages require hacks in both `index.html` and an another `404.html` to work with SPAs leads me to believe that Github Pages are not _the best place_ for hosting SPA apps. I'd recommend using something like Cloudflare Pages or Netlify.

Description: #1544 bumped the version of Prisma from 4.12 to 4.16.  This change introduced a regression in our full-stack type safety DX. The functionality is still there, but type names are far less ergonomic.  We should do our best to find a way to again show meaningful type names again, and also address other potential changes between Prisma 4.12 and 4.16.  ### Before ![image](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/assets/22752307/897e7ced-c5e1-43de-9e96-caa3244ad091)  ### After ![image](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/assets/22752307/00768df0-0cfe-4d6d-affa-3bbb74341c84)
Discuss Information: This will most likely be solved by upgrading to Prisma 5.X according to this fix: https://github.com/prisma/prisma/pull/20319  | One extra thing to consider when we solve this: Prisma started branding their model types so our branded `_Entity` types are no longer needed.  | Also related: generating variations of Prisma entity types: https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1246062324792557638/1246062324792557638

Description: Closes #1435  We had a regression with the way we handle **DB server** being available but the **DB** not existing.  1. **Before** the DB check was introduced     - we wouldn't check **DB server** connectivity     - Prisma would create the **DB** if missing 2. **After** the DB check was introduced     - we check for **DB server** connectivity by trying to execute a dummy SQL command on the **DB**     - Wasp fails with an error "DB server is not running" 3. After the fix     - we check for **DB server** connectivity by trying to execute a dummy SQL command on the **DB**     - if the error is "DB doesn't exist" -> we tolerate it, _since we know Prisma will create the DB if missing_     - in all other cases -> Wasp fails with an error "DB server is not running"

### Description  Enhanced/improved the Security Report Guidelines as well as the Security Contact Information via updating the SECURITY.md file. Making it more clear/comprehensible for the users. **Closes:** #1547   ### Select what type of change this PR introduces:  1. [x] **Just code/docs improvement** (no functional change).  2. [ ] **Bug fix** (non-breaking change which fixes an issue). 3. [ ] **New feature** (non-breaking change which adds functionality). 4. [ ] **Breaking change** (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected).  ### Update Waspc ChangeLog and version if needed If you did a bug fix, new feature, or breaking change, that affects waspc, make sure you satisfy the following: 1. [ ] I updated [ChangeLog.md](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/main/waspc/ChangeLog.md) with description of the change this PR introduces. 2. [ ] I bumped waspc version in [waspc.cabal](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/main/waspc/waspc.cabal) to reflect changes I introduced, with regards to the version of the latest wasp release, if the bump was needed.
Discuss Information: @Martinsos @sodic  Please review my PR and if according to the guidelines please do merge it :) | @Martinsos @sodic  Have you reviewed my PR? Are there any changes to be made? | Closes Issue: #1547  | @tvermaashutosh thanks for the effort here! Unfortunately, this is not the change we can accept at this moment. This updated security doc make quite a bit of claims and promises that we haven't had the opportunity yet to think through and/or decide to stick with them; for some of them we also currently don't have the resources. Wasp is still in Beta, so I would rather we keep our security processes quite simple at the moment, and we can evolve them as time goes. Once we come to 1.0, this will be worth revising and taking to the next level, but for now I would rather keep it quite simple and agile.

Description: `wasp deply fly launch` is great if everything is going well, but if something goes wrong (and it often does on Fly lately), then it is really hard for a user to figure out what to do now. Where should they continue from? What is already set up?  I would suggest we do two things: 1. Make our script a bit more robust to errors from the Fly side: if error happens, it could try to repeat the command, in case Fly is just being flaky. 2. We could make it clearer to user how to continue after the error.  For (2), one idea that we could do is that before we start executing commands, we could make a plan first. That means that our `wasp deploy fly launch` command would first print the plan, which is a list of commands it plans to run, and in which order, with some explanations for the user. Then, if the whole things breaks at some point, user can in theory pick up on their own from that point and run the rest of the commands from the plan.  Alternatively, Wasp CLI could itself be smart enough to recognize where the whole process broke and continue from there -> either by learning from Fly which resources are already allocated and skipping those, or by maybe even having it written locally on the disk.  Third option is to roll-back: if `wasp deploy fly launch` fails, then we would remove all the resources that were allocated during it so far, so that the whole command becomes transactional, and the next time we run it we start with a clean slate, if error occurred previously. That said, this sounds tricky and what if deleting resources also fails? So I am not so sure about this approach.
Discuss Information: For me, for example, it died while trying to attach db to the server. Connection timed out. Who knows why -> maybe it took longer for db to initialize? That is an operation we might benefit from retrying.  ``` Error: Get "http://fdaa:2:6dba:a7b:2cc3:401d:954a:2:5500/commands/databases/wasp_thoughts_server": connect tcp [fdaa:2:6dba:a7b:2cc3:401d:954a:2]:5500: operation timed out ```  However, the result of that was that I had no idea what to do next.  I did manage to figure it, but it was not easy: I had to manually run `fly ...` command to attach that db (I could find it in stdout of failed command, so that was a plus), and then I ran `wasp deploy fly deploy` to finally do the deployment -> that was a bit harder to figure out.  It would be great if I knew exactly which steps worked out and at which step it failed, and I was told that I needed to somehow attach the db myself, and then I need to continue with `wasp deploy fly deploy`. Or, that I can delete everything and try from scratch. Something like that.  EDIT: I ended up deleting all the machines Fly created and basically started from the scratch. Although that was also tricky because it didn't want to do it right on, because our CLI said we already have toml files on the disk. | Another error I got on `wasp deploy fly deploy` is: ``` https://api.machines.dev/v1/apps/wasp-thoughts-server/machines/e286512dc75038/wait?instance_id=01HCJ5JYN2Y1B280H6YYZQ28MV&state=started&timeout=60 ``` | I'm also running into this issue. Specifically, the CLI timed out while waiting for the DB to boot up, so I re-ran the command. After which it seems to have misidentified the server name (`my-wasp-app-morning-feather-500-server` when it was actually named something else, likely due to a naming conflict).  | @tconroy thanks for sharing! That is probably related to https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/1588 . Once we fix that + do some improvements here, I think experience should improve.

Description: **Description** So basically, what happens, is that when we run `wasp db migrate-dev`, and our app is in a directory which has white-space in its path, database migration fails because it breaks on that white-space.  **To Reproduce** Steps to reproduce the behaviour: 1. Following the setup guide from the readme.md , *todoApp* project under run example app.  2. Run `cabal run wasp-cli db migrate-dev` output of the above command: ```─$ cabal run wasp-cli db migrate-dev Up to date  � --- Compiling wasp project... --------------------------------------------------                                                                                                                                                                                          � --- Setting up database... -----------------------------------------------------                                                                                                                                                                                          [Db!]     Error: P1001 [Db!]      [Db!]     Can't reach database server at `localhost`:`5432` [Db!]      [Db!]     Please make sure your database server is running at `localhost`:`5432`. [Db!]      ✅ --- Database successfully set up. ----------------------------------------------                                                                                                                                                                                          ✅ --- Your wasp project has successfully compiled. -------------------------------                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                                ❌ --- [Error] Can not connect to database: ---------------------------------------                                                                                                                                                                                         The database needs to be running in order to execute this command. You can easily start a managed dev database with `wasp start db`. ```  3. Run `cabal run wasp-cli start db` output of the above command: ```└─$ cabal run wasp-cli start db       Up to date ✨ Starting a PostgreSQL dev database (based on your Wasp config) ✨                                                                                                                                       Additional info:                                                                                                                       ℹ Connection URL, in case you might want to connect with external tools:                                                                  postgresql://postgresWaspDevUser:postgresWaspDevPass@localhost:5432/todoApp-29a2c8c08a                                            ℹ Database data is persisted in a docker volume with the following name (useful to know if you will want to delete it at some point):                                                                                                                                          wasp-dev-db-todoApp-29a2c8c08a                                                                                                                                                                                                                                         ...  PostgreSQL Database directory appears to contain a database; Skipping initialization  2023-10-09 18:33:27.246 UTC [1] LOG:  starting PostgreSQL 16.0 (Debian 16.0-1.pgdg120+1) on x86_64-pc-linux-gnu, compiled by gcc (Debian 12.2.0-14) 12.2.0, 64-bit 2023-10-09 18:33:27.257 UTC [1] LOG:  listening on IPv4 address "0.0.0.0", port 5432 2023-10-09 18:33:27.257 UTC [1] LOG:  listening on IPv6 address "::", port 5432 2023-10-09 18:33:27.316 UTC [1] LOG:  listening on Unix socket "/var/run/postgresql/.s.PGSQL.5432" 2023-10-09 18:33:27.380 UTC [28] LOG:  database system was shut down at 2023-10-08 17:45:39 UTC 2023-10-09 18:33:27.642 UTC [1] LOG:  database system is ready to accept connections   ```  Keeping this terminal running. 4. Open another terminal to same example app path and Check working directory  ```  └─$ pwd /media/naruto/New Volume/Open Source Contribution/wasp/waspc/examples/todoApp  ```  5.  Run again `cabal run wasp-cli db migrate-dev`  It will give an error with incorrect file path ```  └─$ cabal run wasp-cli db migrate-dev Up to date  � --- Compiling wasp project... --------------------------------------------------   � --- Setting up database... -----------------------------------------------------   [Db]      [-] Removed tables [Db]        - User [Db]        - SocialLogin [Db]        - Task [Db]       [Db]      [*] Changed the `SocialLogin` table [Db]        [-] Removed foreign key on columns (userId) [Db]       [Db]      [*] Changed the `Task` table [Db]        [-] Removed foreign key on columns (userId) ✅ --- Database successfully set up. ----------------------------------------------                                                                                                                                                                                          ✅ --- Your wasp project has successfully compiled. -------------------------------                                                                                                                                                                                          � --- Starting database migration... ---------------------------------------------                                                                                                                                                                                         zsh:1: no such file or directory: /media/naruto/New                                                                                                                                        ❌ --- [Error] Migrate dev failed: ------------------------------------------------                                                                                                                                                                                         Migrate (dev) failed with exit code: 127  ```  **Expected behaviour** CLI should consider the full path of the project directory to run the command So ask you can see the project path is  "*/media/naruto/New Volume/Open Source Contribution/wasp/waspc/examples/todoApp*" But it is considering only till the first white space  ```zsh:1: no such file or directory:  /media/naruto/New```     **Desktop (please complete the following information):**  - Description: Wasp no longer knows how to initialize (create) the database for a project.  Before: 1. Set the database URL 2. Run `wasp db migrate-dev` or `wasp start` 3. Wasp create the database and start successfully.  Now: 1. Set the database URL 2. Run `wasp db migrate-dev` or `wasp start` 3. Wasp tells you that the database doesn't exist and does not create it.  ![image](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/assets/22752307/1b166216-fc28-4209-8eab-833171e271b3)  This is a recent regression. @Martinsos  knows (or he had a feeling) which feature caused it (something Craig implemented IIRC).
Discuss Information: Oh wow, didn't know about this @sodic ! Yeah might likely be caused by that. @craigmc08 added checks, at the start of database commands, that check if database is running -> if it is not, they inform us with an informative error (before we would end up getting some quite confusing errors). I believe in the background we use `prisma db execute` command to test the connection -> but this command should work even if no prisma migrations are in the database, so I am a bit surprised that this would be an issue. Still, it is probably reason to start looking at these changes as the initial starting point.  Here is the commit: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/commit/802e0c5e5d64d81f9775cd919650046e1e165be4 . | This happens because of an internal Prisma behaviour we relied on before.  If you take a look at this piece of their code: https://github.com/prisma/prisma/blob/main/packages/migrate/src/utils/ensureDatabaseExists.ts#L126  It does the check if DB is running in a different way from us.  If there is an error connecting to the **DB server**, they see two cases:   1. maybe the DB doesn't exist in **DB server**      - if this was the case -> **create the DB**      - continue   2. or the connection really failed      - fails the command   Our **new** check for DB connection relies on the DB existing in the **DB server** while our old logic relied on Prisma creating the DB for us and this enabled us to handle the missing DB case more gracefully.  | Now the question remains:  1. Do we adopt the Prisma behaviour in our Haskell based check?     - This would mean we _somehow_ create the DB if it's missing 3. Can we fall back to using Prisma and their logic yet again?     - Using a Prisma command to check DB connection that would create the DB as a side-effect? 5. Do now require that the DB existing in **DB server** which mean we accept this regression as the new behaviour | @infomiho I vote for option 2. I find it annoying having to connect to the Docker container and manually create a database (Ok, I could be a normal person and use some kind of a GUI client, but still).  If we're currently aiming for out-of-the-box ease-of-use, and the second option is trivial to implement, I think it makes sense to go back to that. What do you think? | Yeah I am also voting for Option 2. I dont' feel like I have enough info to say more though. I don't understand the regression that was made (what exactly did we use before, and what exactly do we use now?). I also don't understand how Option 1 or Option 2 or Option 3 will be implemented, and what is their impact -> what are the pros/cons?  EDIT: I see now this is nicely described in the PR!

Description: Right now, Wasp requires LTS node version, to ensure stability (we had issues where the latest node version would introduce bugs / changes that would cause issues).  However, we had a couple of people already be surprised and even disturbed by this requirement, expecting it to be more relaxed.  So, we might want to relax it indeed! We do then risk npm/node potentially introducing some issues, but maybe it is a good tradeoff if that means adoption will be easier for people.  In that case, we could relax it so that all node versions >= LTS are ok. We might take this step further and say that odd versions are not ok with Wasp, since `node` maintainers use those as unstable versions, but we also might just let people care about that themselves.
Discuss Information: Related to #1407  | I think it would be good option to have Node versions above LTS as compatible, especially as we're at even non-LTS version (20.5.1). I would personally just let users know in CLI warning that they're using non-LTS version and it can potentially have issues.   That way people who are trying out Wasp don't have to care too much about versions and switching just yet and it makes the initial process more streamlined. | I'd just make this a warning if we really want to (i.e. this is an odd version > LTS it might not work or something like that)  Since the problem I think wasp has right now is that 20 will be LTS in a month and none of the projects outside of wasp can test if they work with 20 so I'd def make it so you can run whatever you want to prevent this in the future (might be a depend miss match or something, users upgrade to wasp 0.12 and it breaks since it's gonna use the the new LTS)  Also agree with @martinovicdev that new (>LTS) odd version should produce warnings (ideally odd <LTS would produce an error since you shouldn't be using that unless you really know why), but testing next gen node should be an option  PS: node odd versions are feature versions that are not supposed to be used in prod (unless you know what you're doing) and won't get an LTS | @martinovicdev @Zeko369 those are good arguments, we should do so! Ok, so let's make it simple: we will allow any version >= than the latest LTS, or the LTS before it, if the new LTS has been out only shortly (depends on how long it takes us to update the deps). We open ourselves a bit to possible node/npm bugs, but we will handle that if happens. As for warnings -> we could have those, we could you warn you if you are using an odd version of node, but we can skip that for now, to keep it simple.

Description:
## Motivation We are looking to enable users to add additional fields to signup process when they are using Auth UI + Wasp Auth. |![Screenshot 2023-08-18 at 13 37 50](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/assets/2223680/43445d20-eb09-4b50-8013-402c09223e1b)| |:-:| |Our regular Signup form| For example, if we have a User model that looks like this: ```prisma entity User {=psl id Int @id @default(autoincrement()) username String @unique password String address String? psl=} ``` and want to ask user for their `address` in the Signup process, how could it look like? ## Parts of the problem There are three pieces of the problem: 1. UI components 2. Validating extra fields on backend 3. Including the fields when creating a new User ### UI components If the user is using Auth UI then the Auth UI components for Signup need to be extensible. ### Backend validation Frontend validation is not enough, it needs to also happen on the backend. So, when the requests comes, the fields need to be checked e.g. `address` is present and has at least 5 characters. ### Save the fileds When we are creating a new user, we are explicit about which fields we include (for security reasons) from the body of the request. Therefore, any extra fields, need to be specified explicitly as well. ## Proposed solution In this PR, we can see the above mentioned parts solved with a new field `auth.signupCustomizatiion` and an extension point in the `` component. Let's see how it can be done for the `address` field from the example above. ### **main.wasp** We defined a new dict called `signupCustomization` with one field called `additionalFields: ExtImport` ```diff title="main.wasp" {5} app crudTesting { // ... auth: { userEntity: User, methods: { usernameAndPassword: {}, }, onAuthFailedRedirectTo: "/login", + signup: { + additionalFields: import { fields } from "@server/auth.js", + }, }, } ``` ### **auth.ts** - We export an object with additional field names as keys and their definition as the value. - The definition has two fields: `get` and `validate`, as shown below ```ts import { defineAdditionalSignupFields } from '@wasp/auth/index.js' export const fields = defineAdditionalSignupFields({ // This object is type-safe and complains about defining keys that are not part // of the User model. // We also exclude the "username" and "password" fields since // they are already set by Wasp in the signup process address: (data) => { if (typeof data.address !== 'string') { throw new Error('Address is required.') } if (data.address.length < 10) { throw new Error('Address must be at least 10 characters long.') } return data.address }, }) ``` ### **SignupPage.tsx** Users can extend the UI in two ways: - providing an object describing the field (easier, but limited) - providing a render fn that render any UI (harder, but fully flexibile) Users extend the UI via the `additionalFields` prop. They can pass in: - an array of objects - an array of both objects and render fns - just a single render fn (instead of the array) **Declaring an array of only objects** ```tsx import { SignupForm } from "@wasp/auth/forms/Signup"; export const SignupPage = () => { return (

Signup

); }; ``` Each entry can look like this: ```ts { name: string; label: string; type: "input" | "textarea"; validations?: RegisterOptions // `react-hook-form` specific options for the `register` function } ``` **Adding render functions** They can add render functions inside of the `additionalFields` array. They are provided with the `react-hook-form` functions that help them to pass data from their UI to the server. ```tsx import { SignupForm } from "@wasp/auth/forms/Signup"; import { FormError, FormInput, FormItemGroup, FormLabel, } from "@wasp/auth/forms/internal/Form"; export const SignupPage = () => { return (

Signup

( <> Phone number {errors.phone && {errors.phone.message}} ) ]} />
); }; ``` They can render **whatever** they need to, not just fields: ```tsx import { SignupForm } from "@wasp/auth/forms/Signup"; export const SignupPage = () => { return (

Signup

Extra info below
, ]} />
); }; ``` **Just using the render fn** Or simply, they can provide a single render fn that renders any UI they need. ```tsx import { SignupForm } from "@wasp/auth/forms/Signup"; import { FormItemGroup, } from "@wasp/auth/forms/internal/Form"; export const SignupPage = () => { return (

Signup

( <> What ever UI they need )} />
); }; ``` | ![Screenshot 2023-08-18 at 13 24 44](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/assets/2223680/dcb1b0c7-894a-46fd-ab5e-7d6e7e7d3d0e) | |:-:| |Frontend validation| |![Screenshot 2023-08-18 at 13 25 22](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/assets/2223680/c0b7709b-46ce-44f3-81f4-e08a0291c0f9)| |:-:| |Backend validation| |![Screenshot 2023-08-18 at 13 25 47](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/assets/2223680/15a184eb-5694-4f80-904c-eaf56998e207)| |:-:| |Saved in the DB| ## Left to do - [x] Agree on the API - [x] Docs - [x] Changelog

It would give you mulitple advantages (not only better and more advance error/typing, but also validation on the server)  > It's a recommendation in the docs. Until we include `zod` ourselves by default, users have to install it. After we include it by default (for example for action input/output validation) I would make it the "official" way to validate fields in the docs.  I'd maybe add it here, but if not then I'll be eagerly waiting for zod actions/queries  > >  SQL Injection in your code  > I think using Prisma kinda solves this. We are not operating at the raw SQL level, so we should be good.  You can still return a raw object to create/update/delete relations i.e. if you return `todo: (data) => data.todo` but it should be fine since you're probably not gonna be doing that  > > Don't get me wrong, I love RHF but it's quite far from a "user friendly option"  > I thought having two APIs: one simpler and one full blown complex one should solve the issue for novice users + advanced users. Since we are using RHF under the hood, how do you imagine providing `value` and `setValue` would look like? And let's not forget the errors as well.  Controller api provides a more "regular" input api, while register requires you to use a "uncontrolled" input (which is better I know) but it's harder for custom components / newer devs   https://www.react-hook-form.com/api/usecontroller/controller ```tsx <Controller   control={control}   name="test"   render={({     field: { onChange, onBlur, value, name, ref },     fieldState: { invalid, isTouched, isDirty, error },     formState,   }) => (     <Checkbox       onBlur={onBlur} // notify when input is touched       onChange={onChange} // send value to hook form       checked={value}       inputRef={ref}     />   )} /> ```  > > but I think you should destructure/delete password from the data object, you won't want users possibly storing it unencrypted in the DB and later shooting themselves in the food  > That's a solid suggestion, I think it makes sense. I'll remove the password before passing in the data object �   �  | @Zeko369   1️⃣  I've stripped away the password from the data the users get in their `additionalFields` getter functions �   2️⃣  I'd like to keep the full `react-hook-form` available in the custom rendering functions for two reasons: 1. It's intended for more advanced use cases 2. I'd like to get feedback on it from users using it and then adjust the API  3️⃣  I don't want to include `Zod` by default just yet, it feels like a part of a bigger story and I think users who want to use Zod, can do so easily until we figure out the bigger idea of input/output validation. I would then use Zod for auth 100% (Wasp's internal validation + users' custom validation)  I hope all of the above makes sense, if not, let's continue the discussion here �

Description: when specifying a route in e.g. `onAuthFailedRedirectTo` it would be cool if we could put routes' identifier instead of a string (e.g. LoginRoute instead of "/login") -> that way we'd have type safety
Discuss Information: The question though is what if route is `/sign`, where login and signup are both happening and you choose them via `?action=login` or `?action=signup` or smth like that? In that case they want to have string, or some typed mechanism of providing those arguments. I don't think this is a very likely case, but it is possible.  One option is to provide both, via a union type once we have them, so they can provide either a string, or an identifier.

Description: Two big parts: 1. CLI now has offers additional option when you type `wasp new` command, which if chosen, generates wasp app on the disk using ChatGPT. It is marked as experimental. 2. CLI also has a new command now, `wasp new-ai-machine`, which is to be used by Wasp AI web app and is not listed in the CLI usage/help. It prints everything to stdout (logs and files it creates) in the format that machine can parse. 3. We have `wasp-ai/` web app (in Wasp) that calls wasp CLI and that way generates wasp app via ChatGPT. It is a UI for this new feature.  Give it a look, play with it, test a bit!  There is a bunch of TODOs in there that we yet have to take care of, but the core logic is here and it works!  TODO: - [x] Merge with `main` branch. - [ ] Docs? - [ ] Changelog. - [x] Repeat chatGPT request if timeout happened.       I wonder why timeout happens though. Is it possible that it is enforced by our library that we use to invoke an http request? If so, should we prolong its timeout maybe?   <details>     <summary> example error response </summary>       wasp-cli: HttpExceptionRequest Request {         host                 = "api.openai.com"         port                 = 443         secure               = True         requestHeaders       = [("Accept","application/json"),("Content-Type","application/json; charset=utf-8"),("Authorization","<REDACTED>")]         path                 = "/v1/chat/completions"         queryString          = ""         method               = "POST"         proxy                = Nothing         rawBody              = False         redirectCount        = 10         responseTimeout      = ResponseTimeoutDefault         requestVersion       = HTTP/1.1         proxySecureMode      = ProxySecureWithConnect       }      ResponseTimeout   </details> - [x] Repeat chatGPT request if invalid JSON was returned.       IDEA: We could send just JSON as a single, standalone chat message and tell it to fix it. Or, we could send whole conversation that was used to generate JSON + add new message to it, from the Assistant, where it returns JSON, and then one more message from us saying "hey this JSON is invalid, pls fix it". - [ ] Send files for repair (ideally with attached (compile) errors). - [x] When generating fails it puts status as `success`?       Fixed on `main` and merged back, this was general bug. - [x] Improve error message when OpenAI API key is missing.  Things to fix that chatGPT currently often gets wrong while generating Wasp app (mostly by prompt engineering) + ideas: - [ ] Sometimes it still omits `User` entity, especially if user is not mentioned in the app description. - [ ] Sometimes it omits to create any actions / queries and just goes for a very simple app, usually if description is too simple. - [ ] It often forgets to put `,` after `fn` field in query/action, and before `entities` field. - [ ] It relatively often, while generating JS implementations of queries/actions, doesn't put actual implementation, but puts a comment "// implementation goes here" or smth like that. - [ ] Every so, it tries to import prisma client in the operation file and use that directly instead of context. I believe that is because I mentioned that `context.<Entity>` is really a prisma thing? But I do want it know that hm.  - [ ] We had occurence of it leaving escaped "\n"'s in User entity psl definition.       This might however be a mistake on our side, of incorrectly using the response from chatGPT and doing one `show` too much or smth like that, so let's look into that first. - [ ] It generated ext import in action with just file path with no quotes even:       `fn: @server/actions.js` - [ ] Sometimes messes up relationship between User and some other entity, implementing only one half of that relationship. - [ ] Once it generated action as just `action deleteNote { ... }`. - [ ] In general, it loves putting `...` in places. That is probably because it saw that in our examples. We should not put `...` in our example then. - [ ] Should we try using multiple messages instead of one big message? Would that somehow help to emphasize certain things? For example, general context in first message, more precise instructions in another message? - [ ] Our queries currently consume max 2k tokens, even with the response included and JSON repairs. We have 16k tokens available. We should try enriching our chat messages with more examples and instructions. - [ ] We could have Wasp AI use ChatGPT4 to generate Plan, if ChatGPT4 is available as a model via the API.  Web app: - [x] progress bar with current action being performed - [ ] show a couple of last messages - [ ] find more good ways to indicate progress / what is happening (files could flash on change, progress bar also next to files, maybe grey files for the files that are yet coming, ...) - [x] download zip should become -> "run app" with two fold instructions: install wasp + download zip - [ ] Deploy the app! Will need to add custom Dockerfile that installs Wasp.

We implemented CRUD operations that automatically generate queries and actions based of an entity that user defined.  The default operations are pretty basic: - check if user is authenticated if the operation is not public - execute some DB query (get all, get one, create from data, update by id or delete by id)  ### Downsides of the current default implementations  1. The data is not validated in any way 2. There is no authorization mechanism to limit access to certain resources  Users have to override the default implementation to get those behaviours. This makes the CRUD operations less useful and appealing. The goal is to add certain features to Wasp to make the CRUD operations by default powerful enough for most common use cases.  ## Some thing we can do   ### Authorization   It would enable the user to define which users are allowed to perform which operations and on which resources.  ### Input validation  Save only valid data instead of saving everything that comes from the frontend.  ### Customization through Wasp DSL  We want to give users some knobs to turn in the Wasp DSL to customize the CRUD operations without writing JS code. Things like: - default order when return a list of entities - automatic connect with currently logged in user when creating an entity   ### Customization through pre/post hooks  Check things before operation is executed or perform some action after the operation is executed.
Discuss Information: Ideas for validation: 1. We introduce a simple language on top of PSL, via custom attributes, so you can define for each entity which fields are modifiable by user, and which are not. Also, which ones are visible by user, and which ones are not. This could be pretty useful. It is a good question how this scales with having roles and more complex authZ mechanisms though, would be great if we can make it work with that somehow, or at least not be in the way. If we pull this off, it can also be used in other parts of Wasp. 2. We define directly in CRUD which entity fields are allowed, both in input and output. We could do this declaratively, or we could provide hooks for validation / sanitizing the output, ... . This is more straight forward but it not usable outside of CRUD. | Hi @infomiho @Martinsos , I just stumbled upon this issue when checking out Wasp (very cool!).  I'm the creator of [ZenStack](https://github.com/zenstackhq/zenstack), which is a full-stack toolkit based on Prisma. One of the main things it does is to extend PSL and add a flexible access control layer there - both at the schema level and at runtime. The Prisma's query API is fully preserved and authorization works transparently under the hood.  Do you think it's an interesting idea to integrate ZenStack with Wasp? It'll probably look like:  ```wasp entity Task {=psl     id          Int     @id @default(autoincrement())     description String     isDone      Boolean @default(false)     user        User?    @relation(fields: [userId], references: [id])     userId      Int?      @@allow('all', auth() == user)   // full access to owner     @@allow('read', auth() != null)  // readable to all login users psl=} ```  Not sure if you guys already started to work on an authz solution yet. | @Case-E had some interesting insights into extending automatic CRUD!  Discord convo: https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/867713251479519232/1231994987156340736 and https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/867713251479519232/1234893790708240414 .  Important bits:  > I am eager to see when you implement  Automatic CRUD with where conditions. For example, getAll Tasks created by authenticated user or some other where condition like getAll Tasks Where authenticated user is a collaborator or any other where condition. Any insight on when this might be coming?  > Mine (use case) is more of a RBAC where I can specify which user can access which resources and features based on their org level role. It'd be great if there were automatic CRUD ops where I could specify the conditions/user roles. | supporting ZenStack would be amazing

Description: @Zeko369 (what a surprise, since he is known for practicing patching/hacking Wasp templates :D) suggested that we might consider adding a mechanism for patching the generated Wasp code / templates.  So right now, if you want to change how Wasp does some things internally, you can: 1. Modify the generated code. Doing it manually is not fun because each time something changes in Wasp project, those changes will get lost. So you would probably need to write some script, and run it either manually of automatically (which is also not an easy feat, automating that to happen at the right moment). 2. Modifying Wasp templates. Wasp uses those to define big parts of the code it generates. To modify these, you need to find them on disk and modify them. This is not documented, as a technique, nor path to them. Modifications to them are permanent, but only per Wasp version -> so new version of Wasp will not carry over the changes you did to the previous version, because it has its own template files. Also, changes are global, so for all Wasp projects you are working on.  Patching mechanism could work in two ways: 1. Patch the generated code. User would provide some kind of script that could modify generated files. It might be called for each file and given its contents to modify, or maybe it would manually read them from disk and modify them, or maybe it would just be a directory with files to use to overwrite Wasp generated file -> we would have to figure out the best interface. What is important is that Wasp should call it each time generated files change. Maybe it would know which files exactly script is interested in and therefore notify it only when those files change. Just having this hind of hook that would call user's script when specified generated files change could already be useful. 2. Path Wasp templates. They could provide template files that should replace our template files (some of them). We would then use those instead of ours. They would probably list them in a special directory. This would be per-project, which is big improvement compared to when done manually. It could also work even with version change. Although we might want to warn them if template file they are replacing has changed, so they can check if their template file still makes sense (compare checksums of old and new template).
Discuss Information: This could also be generalized via "Wasp lifecycle hooks". If we has such hooks, we could define in "pre-start" hook that we want to overwrite certain templates with other templates. Although it would then also be important to somehow reset them hm. Ok, maybe doing it via Wasp lifecycle hooks is not the best solution due to the need to reset them, so the remains don't remain for another project. Or, if we had a hook which is able to modify template files specifically, than that is a straightforward solution. | User needed to modify the client output to be able to use blob URLs with their Railway deployment https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1423076157280223374/1425770554866143325

Description: Fixes #1171  ### What has been implemented   ```c  crud tasks {    entity: Task,    operations: {      get: {},      getAll: {        isPublic: true      },      create: {        overrideFn: import { createTask } from '@server/tasks.js'      },      update: {},      delete: {},     },  }  ```  - Wasp language now has the `crud` declaration   - It accepts:     - `entity` for which the CRUD is built     - `operations` dict which has keys for each operations     - under each key the user can declare `isPublic` and a custom implementation with `overrideFn`  - checks for    - entity must have an `@id` field    - at least one operations must be defined (otherwise, what's the point)  - server routes  - client API via importing `import { tasks } from "@wasp/crud/tasks.ts"` and using it like:    - `tasks.getAll.useQuery()`    - or `tasks.create.useAction()`   ### What's left to figure out - a lot of our users connect the entity with the current user e.g. `Task` belongs to `User`, can we make this work out of the box? - ~types for the client API~ - ~do we need to do something for full-stack type safety (import the types for the client from the backend?)~ - do we want to validate the input received from the client? (other than delegating to Prisma for required fields check) - do we want to include all relations (`user User @relation(...)` -> we include `user` instead of just `userId`)
Discuss Information: Took a quick look, I quite like it!!!  I think this is the right direction, as we discussed. Even if this first version will end up a bit lacking in some areas, or a bit conflicting with some parts of Wasp, I think it will open a new area for us and allow us to explore and experiment more, and that is very important and a great next step. Others and also us will be able to play with it, reason about it, complain about it, and that will enable us to learn and more forward in this direction.  That said, I do like the current solution already quite a bit.  I wrote above about override ideas: I think we should do full override, but also hooks. I explained more above in another comment.  As for Wasp DSL, I have a different suggestion for syntax, which is a bit of a spin on the current one:  ``` crud Tasks {   entity: Task,   operations: {     create: {},     getAll: { public: true }   } } ```  So main difference is that use has to explicitly enumerate the operations they want to get. Why I think this is an improvement: 1. It is easy to throw a look here and see what is supported - Wasp style, less conventions/magic, more explicit/readable stuff. 2. We can change stuff in the future without breaking much -> e.g. we can add a new method, and it will not magically get added to their code without them aggreing on it. 3. They can't accidentally expose an operation, for example Delete because they forgot it is there. 4. It gives us a good place in DSL to provide additional configuration for each method/operation. I am not sure where we would do this otherwise.  Btw, there is a couple ways we can play where to make it a bit shorter:  ``` crud tasks (Task, {   operations: {     create: {},     getAll: { public: true }   } }) ```  or even  ``` crud tasks (Task, {   create: {},   getAll: { public: true } }) ```  This is all supported by current Wasp syntax.  As for the overrides, this is what overriding the whole method to use existing operation could look like with this syntax (and also attaching custom operations/methods):  ``` crud tasks (Task, {   operations: {     create: { use: createTask }, // override     getAll: { public: true },     custom: [       cloneTask,       toggleTask     ]   }, })  action createTask ...  action cloneTask ...  action toggleTask ... ```    | Ok, I took a look at the RFC where Wasp DSL syntax was discovered, very nice job there, you covered all of this!  I still think user should explicitly define which methods they want to use, due to arguments above. It is Wasp style + they can't expose method by accident. If I am correct, both @matijaSos and @sodic share this opinion with me.  Another thing is if operations should be defined as ```   operations: {     getAll: {},     create: { public: true }   } ```  or  ```   operations: [GetAll, Create],   config: [     (Create, { public: true })   ] ```  I kind of prefer the first approach, but I could also be ok with the second one if others prefer it! | > @infomiho this is looking pretty good, seems to me like we are close to being done! >  > I left some comments, check them out. >  > Some things to think about: >  > 1. Would be great if you can go through older comments that are still open and resolve them or answer them, so we clear those up. > 2. You asked a good question of validating the data that is being sent, for create and update methods. Well, I guess even for get and getAll hm. Seems to me like that would be pretty important for real usage to make sense. Even if we don't do it now, in this PR, I would love if that by the end of this PR we have a clear picture of what we would need to do there / what are our options -> so have a plan for it. > 3. You mentioned relations, especially the one to User -> for this also, same as for validation, it would be great if we at least make a plan in this PR. Look at some use cases, look at some options, propose some solutions. Even if we don't it right now, we would leave with some kind of a plan for the future. >  > Without (2) and (3), I think this feature will likely have to remain experimental, more of a showcase. But with (2) and (3) it feels like it could be a full feature.  Regarding (2) and (3) -> before we finalize this PR, could you, as a part of it, describe a bit a short plan on how would you handle these two points, your current ideas? I am ok with not handling them in this PR as that would be too much, but I would love if behind us we leave GH issues with some initial thoughts on both of these points, that later we can continue from. We could do that later, sure, but now it is fresh! | @infomiho looking good to me! I think we mostly solved everything.  I will approve the PR as I think all the major stuff is in order, although there are two things I think are still needed to call it done: 1. Gh issue(s) for next steps in CRUD: validation, auth, ... . I think it is important to create those now while we are still fresh. We can also include them in docs (link to them). 2. Docs  I am not sure if some of this already got done or not, if yes great. | I've added the docs in my last couple of commits �   I'll be creating the issues before I merge this PR | I've created an issue for further improvements that can benefit the CRUD operations. https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/1253

Description: ## Deploy stuff to Railway  ### Take into account things  1. `railway login` does not work in non-interactive mode. Current implementation checks if user logged in, otherwise throw out of the process with a friendly hint what to do next 2. Global options `--wasp-exe <path>` and `--wasp-project-dir <dir>` are being added to each subcommand in the global scope 3. This implementation expects a specific file for railway deployments to be present in the main root directory of Wasp - `wasp.deployrc.railway.json`    It's expected signature is:  ``` 	"environment": "production", 	"projectId": "bef41c2c-b1a9-42ef-adb9-e3afa84fbbea", 	"clientService": { 		"name": "client", 		"url": "client-production-6ea1.up.railway.app" 	}, 	"serverService": { 		"name": "server", 		"url": "server-production-dfaf.up.railway.app" 	} ```  Environment - required by Railway as there can exist multiple envs ProjectID - build folder has to be linked with a respective project Client / Server Service - info about the client service (ID and its URL). Railways projects consist of services, that you can spin up pretty easily. Each service has its own env variables etc.  Whenever "setup" feature is implemented, we can ensure that this file is create automatically, but I am not entirely sure if that's a good idea to do. Need peoplez thoughts on this. Alternative, we force users to provide those values as flags / arguments.. ?  ### Refactor suggestions things  4. Suggesting to keep all types in `.d.ts` files to clearly indicate what they are 5. Even though haven't done it properly myself, but I'd probably also remove Capital casing on type files, as I see no benefit in this, if we mark the file with `.d.ts` extension, it should be clear enough what file is for. 6. Stick to naming convention of types to indicate where they belong to - FlyDeployOptions, Railway(Rw)DeployOptions. Eases search of specific options across the codebase instead of doing a global search and having 10+ same names 7. Installed `fs-extra` package, hoping it's allowed :) otherwise, can be reimplemented (mainly used for `ensureDir`, but it provides other convenient stuffs)  8. `await $railway up --service ${clientService.name}` - is a stream of logs for deploying. Atm this implementation just fires & forgets, giving a URL of logs as an output, so maybe for v1 it's ok, but later do a re-stream? No idea how to re-stream output logs tho  9. Fn `HACK_PACKAGES_JSON` has to die, but it allows to continue with implementation, as types are not used in Docker and `tsc` is run, that leads to bunch of types missing. This is because Server is not built inside the Docker container, so I'd like opinions on what could be / should be done. It might come across as pretty obvious one - just build the server - but do we then cherry pick specific folders from server, aiming just specific types folders etc? I am not fan of this option because if we add a library tomorrow to the server side, and forget to cherry pick its types inside this deployment Docker, we will have a failing build. 10. Added prettier config for a consist styling, feedback / suggestions on its content is appreciated! For now, I just tried to mirror what has already been put together in terms of style by Shayne :)  A lot has been moved around from `/fly` provider, as a lot of nice conveniences were living in it, so had to move it up a scope to make them shareable.  **It is a very breaking change as I moved a lot of things around, but it was building fine for me... (as always). As I am not a `Fly.io` user and I have very limited time, I'd appreciate someone's help with testing this to ensure no regression.**  ### Select what type of change this PR introduces:  1. [ ] **Just code/docs improvement** (no functional change).  2. [ ] **Bug fix** (non-breaking change which fixes an issue). 3. [ ] **New feature** (non-breaking change which adds functionality). 4. [x] **Breaking change** (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected).  ### Update Waspc ChangeLog and version if needed If you did a bug fix, new feature, or breaking change, that affects waspc, make sure you satisfy the following: 1. [ ] I updated [ChangeLog.md](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/main/waspc/ChangeLog.md) with description of the change this PR introduces. 2. [ ] I bumped waspc version in [waspc.cabal](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/main/waspc/waspc.cabal) to reflect changes I introduced, with regards to the version of the latest wasp release, if the bump was needed.
Discuss Information: Very nice, @OlegGulevskyy �. This seems like a really great start. This is the first time I've actually looked at the deploy code as this isn't really my domain. Learning new things myself here :)  The one thing I could comment on here concerns the `wasp.deployrc.railway.json` file. From what I know about Railway, the user would have to set up a "service" and "generate a domain" for both the client and server first, before defining it in the json file. Currently, it seems that its not possible to create a service via the CLI (https://docs.railway.app/reference/cli-api#service), which would make the script a lot friendlier, although it does seem like you can generate a domain via CLI with `railway domain`. Do you know if there is a possible way to add a service programatically, without the user having to define it first within the Railway dashboard?  Also, would be nice at some point to use `railway add` to add the Postgres database and seed it with `wasp db seed` (https://wasp-lang.dev/docs/language/features#seeding).   But, yeah, great work. Looking forward to following the progress here � | > This is the first time I've actually looked at the deploy code as this isn't really my domain. Learning new things myself here :)  Yeah, deploy code is just a node cli with TS, so we all can contribute :)  > Do you know if there is a possible way to add a service programatically  Not that I know of, no. It seems their CLI is very limited when it comes to setting things up. That's also one of the reasons why I solely focused on deploying as a first feature. At least you can configure your stuff in their UI and just do one line command re-deployments. But I do agree that comparing to `fly` provider, this is much more humble functionality (even if we just compare `deploy` part and not entirely the provider).  > Also, would be nice at some point to use railway add to add the Postgres database  This seems to be a viable option, but needs to be played around with (maybe next item on the agenda), however I do have my doubts: - output results (if we don't know the details of the created DB, it's kind of pointless, as we need to link server and DB together, so it's easier to do it through UI potentially) - setting env variables on services. without those env variables on each service, they are pretty useless in terms of one click deployments.
