Description: Inspired by this thread: https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1098143517936734319/1098143517936734319 .  On each `wasp build` you have to wait for node_modules to get reinstalled, completely from scratch, which is a PITA. Here is the "offending" line: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/main/waspc/cli/src/Wasp/Cli/Command/Build.hs#L47 -> we delete the whole .wasp/build dir when creating new build. The idea behind this is to make sure we have clean start, that nothing from the previous build is by accident left over.  But this cleans up node_modules, so they have to be installed from scratch every time!  Potential solution is to remove everything but node_modules. But what if some redundant stuff is left in them from before? Well I think it doesn't matter, they are anyway not getting deployed, it is instead expected that when you deploy you do npm build (it is what we do in our Docker container for deploying server).  There is another thing I wonder here though -> why do we even need node_modules for `wasp build`? We are just producing code that is to be deployed -> Docker container is anyway building node_modules on its own from what I see, and doing prisma generation and stuff. I think we let `wasp build` install node_modules because we normally do that after generating code and we just reused logic for that, but it might actually be redundant in this case, and we could possibly just remove it!  We also run migration checks here, which are not really needed? Check the related issue linked in the next comment.  Proposal: - try skipping the installation of node_modules in the case when doing `wasp build`.   Place to do this is in `Wasp.Generator.writeWebAppCode` -> we call `runSetup` there after code generation. Well, we should not call it when doing `wasp build`! What is the purpose of `runSetup` anyway, what is it setting up? We should also note that `writeWebbAppCode` doesn't mention in its name or its header comment that it will do any setup, which is misleading. So I think we should figure out exactly what this setup is, maybe it is dev setup really, rename `runSetup` function accordingly, and then also make sure we have two versions of `writeWebAppCode` function -> one that doesn't call setup, and one that calls setup (but then the name should reflect it). Or, we have just one that doesn't call setup, and then the caller of it will call the setup after it if it wants. - If it turns out that we do need setup for `wasp build`, well then we can just try to not delete the node_modules/ directory when clearing up the .wasp/build/ dir, that will already make re-builds much faster.
Discuss Information: Same but older issue about this: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/968 , check it out for some more info.  Solution is the same: skip doing `runSetup` in `writeWebAppCode` when doing `wasp build`!

We can reopen if the need is great and then consider the solution again, but at this moment I think it is not worth the effort. | I would maybe consider doing the most basic check possible (without parsing the error that Docker produces and doing some smart error reporting). Just some simple output modification if `docker info` errors. ### What we have now 1. We say "The db is starting" 2. We say "Here's the connection string" 3. But then a Docker error shows up ### What I propose If we detect an exit code 1 from `docker info` or similar we alter the output to be: 1. Hey something is wrong with Docker, run `docker info` to see (or we just show the output) and that's it. I feel it improves the experience for our users since Wasp exits earlier and doesn't give mixed signals (here's the connection string but also an error). Custom error message gives the opportunity to educate our users as well: - `Some thing is off, make sure that Docker is installed AND running` - `You can install Docker like this ...` | Ok so the main purpose of this is to run it first, so they see that first, and don't have a feeling that db started already. Ok makes sense! I am voting for forwarding the error output to them, but also telling them to investigate on their own. Description: Wasp introduced support for full-stack type safety with #1090: 1. Operation input and output types are automatically propagated to the client 2. Superjson ensures proper serialization/deserialization of payloads  All that's left to do is ensure that users don't send payloads that Superjson can't stringify.  ### Example 1. A user-defined query returns a value of type `{ square: (x: number) => number, num: number }`. 2. Superjson and Express serialize it into a JSON-compatible value. Since JSON doesn't support functions, the field `square` is dropped. 4. The payload is sent over the network. 5. Axios and Superjson deserialize the payload into an object of type `{ num: number }`, but the type checker thinks you have an object of type `{ square: (x: number) => number, num: number }`.
Double check that if you create a migration, compile, then delete it in your project migrations dir, that it won't be left behind in your generated project on the next compile/copy process.

Fixes #976.  To keep TypeScript from lying to the user, we had to add a data serialization/transformation layer. I've decided to use `superjson` for the time being (thanks @Zeko369!).  In the future, I hope we can make this transformer configurable (as tRPC does).  ### Motivating example To understand why we had to use Superjson, let's see what would happen if we implemented #976 without it.  Imagine you had the following query: ```typescript export const getPerson: GetPerson<{ id: number }, { name: string, dob: Date }> = ({ id }, context) => {   // You do some processing and return a value of type { name: string, dob: Date }.   // All is well with the type checker. } ``` Then, you do this on the client: ```typescript import getPerson from '@wasp/queries/getPerson'  const person = getPerson({ id: 12 }) ``` TypeScript will tell you that `person` has type `{ name: string, dob: Date }`. This is probably the type you wanted, but not the one you actually have: 1. Your query returns a value of type `{ name: string, dob: Date }`, so far so good 2. Experss middleware serializes it into a JSON, but JSON serializes `Date`s into strings 3. The payload is sent over the network. 4. Axios deserializes the payload back into an object of type `{ name: string, dob: string }`, but the type checker things you have an object of type `{ name: string, dob: Date }`. 5. Chaos  ### Todo list:   - [x] Ensure proper serialization/deserialization to ensure types match runtime types   - [x] Clean up the Haskell code and reorganize it   - [x] Implement full-stack type safety for actions   - [x] Discuss whether server imports are a problem or not (could be creating technical debt)   - [ ] Require operation `Input` and `Output` types to extend superjson's serializable type
Discuss Information: I'm merging to get RC out asap, but will take a look at what else to do tomorrow.

Description: We want to provide a helper for storing data locally on the clients.  ### Abstract away the storage implementation We want to wrap `localStorage` or `sessionStorage` (something like https://www.npmjs.com/package/use-browser-storage) and expose it to the user.  ```js import { storage } from '@wasp/storage';  // Sets 'value' at 'key' storage.set('key', 'value')  // Gets value at 'key' storage.get('key')  // Removes 'key' storage.remove('key')  // Clears only things that were set by `storage.set` storage.clear()  ```  ### Help with security We could provide an API for storing user sensitive data which we could then automatically clear on log out. Something like ```js storage.setPrivate('key', 'userSpecificData') ```
Discuss Information: This might be useful, but I feel like we should do a bit more convincing ourselves before we go into this! What is the benefit of this, why should Wasp do it?

Description: After a detailed look into tRPC, we concluded it does not make sense to include it into Wasp (at least not at this point).   Still, we should study the tRPC docs to see how many of their features can we support (or how well).  Examples include: - Middleware - Transparent auth via `publicProcedure` and `protectedProcedure` - Automatically chosen HTTP methods - Full-stack type safety and Zod (main issue: #910)  ## Previous research and conclusions  Benefits: - The entire router is an object with autocomplete (you never need to manually specify routes) - No need to think about using the correct HTTP method - No need to think about whether you’re sending and receiving the correct payload - Instead of creating an error response, just throw errors   Downsides: - Forces you to use TypeScript on both the server and the client - It’s an RPC library - using it as an API is cumbersome and pretty ugly - The same goes for URLs - they’re not made with human-readability in mind  - A limited supports of HTTP methods - Exposing RPCs as external APIs requires extra steps and libraries ([docs](https://create.t3.gg/en/usage/trpc#expose-a-single-procedure-externally))  tRPC in Wasp: - **Using tRPC under the hood** - not much benefit here, tRPC is at its best when it’s user facing. - **Using tRPC directly** - Requires a lot of rethinking and makes most of the DSL definitions redundant
Instead of clearing everything, clear only the keys we set.  Fixes #1042
Discuss Information: @infomiho actually I think this is a bit more complicated than we thought!  So what to we want to do? As you stated now, we want to `removeLocalUserData`: remove local user data. Yes, we are logging out user, so we want to remove all their data.  So what is wrong with cleaning up the whole local storage? Well, we concluded that we might delete a bit too much stuff that way. What stuff? I am not sure actually -> I guess stuff that is not specific to the user, but that is specific to the global app stuff? Maybe some public data, that is not private to the user? @infomiho is this correct, could you give me an example?  The problem is, I don't see how we can know what belongs to user and what not. Yes, we can prefix stuff that Wasp puts in with `wasp` and delete that, that we know belongs to user, but what if user put some extra stuff into local storage? What if developer using Wasp put some extra stuff into local storage, some private user info, but they put it manually, and it doesn't get deleted with logout? Maybe some other token they obtained from somewhere? That sounds like we have an issue then.  What can we do about this then? 1. Be aggressive as we have been so far and delete all local data. We might delete too much, but ok at least nothing sensitive can remain. I wonder now if we should also clear other stuff, like cookies or something (can that even be cleaned like that?). 2. Explicitly have user define what gets deleted -> they have to provide their own function, which forces them to think about it and be explicit about it. 3. Something in between -> we could by default be aggressive, but let them override it and define their own handle for it.  I think first step is better understanding the use cases we have here, especially the one you encountered, which made you open this issue at the end, and then also taking into account that we want to make sure not sensitive stuff remains, be it Wasp that put it or developer using Wasp, and then work with that.  I guess that here I am questioning the very idea of the issue this is fixing: is it ok to delete only some user data on logout? How do we know what we need to delete on logout? What if we don't delete enough, and sensitive data remains? I do think that philosophy of "delete all by default, but you can override" might be the best, as it ensures that developer doesn't by accident leave any sensitive data behind? | ### Example  > So what is wrong with cleaning up the whole local storage? Well, we concluded that we might delete a bit too much stuff that way. What stuff? I am not sure actually -> I guess stuff that is not specific to the user, but that is specific to the global app stuff? Maybe some public data, that is not private to the user? @infomiho is this correct, could you give me an example?  I'll provide you with an example I encountered from building Phrase Tutor. When users land on the page for the first time, I display an intro banner. I don't want to display it each time they visit. I use `localStorage` to set a flag `isDisplayed=true` to avoid showing it again.  With the current Wasp implementation we get this:  https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2223680/225578723-028abbf3-e9e0-456c-bc0a-b021d882e22b.mov  (after logging out, I get the banner again!)  ### Data ownership  > I guess that here I am questioning the very idea of the issue this is fixing: is it ok to delete only some user data on logout? How do we know what we need to delete on logout? What if we don't delete enough, and sensitive data remains?  I would think about the problem like this: localStorage is a useful mechanism that developers like to use. Wasp uses it to achieve a part of its functionality i.e. storing the auth token. Developers using `localStorage` for their needs usually have full control over it.   Wasp interfering with that (clearing it) makes Wasp a no-go for some developers, since they have no way of overriding the logout behaviour (other than forking Wasp or writing their own custom `logout` action).   If we think about it in terms of data ownership, I think the proposed solution makes sense. Wasp owns the auth, therefore it owns the data and it is responsible to clear it on log out. On the other hand, if the developer puts something else in `localStorage`, they are responsible for it now. If it's something private, they need to take care of it on logout.  ### Outro In the future, if we want to offer a managed storage abstraction which the developer can use to mark some data as sensitive and then we can clear it as well. That would make sense to me. But I think it's outside of the scope of this bug fix (for me it's a bug currently since it prevented me from using `localStorage` reliably)

Description: When we figure out that migrations in the migrations/ dir, prisma schema, and database are not all together in sync, we let the user know that they should run `wasp db migrate-dev`.  That is already working pretty good right now, but one thing we don't detect is if user manually modifies database schema -> for example if they add a table, or create a fresh database. One such situation is when they switch from their custom database to our managed dev database -> not else changes, but they just changed the database, and we will not pick it up.  Right now we do the check on every Wasp (re)compilation, and we make sure compilation happens before any command that has anything to do with the DB. We do it by checking our local checksum file, which represents state of prisma schema at the moment of last sync (`migrate-dev`). If that checksum is not same as the current checksum, we do additional check: run `prisma migrate diff` which actually compares all of these (migrations dir, schema, db), and then if that says that there is difference, we warn the user.  This is the reason why we don't detect external db changes -> checksum file didn't change. Why don't we run `prisma migrate diff` every time? Well, it felt expensive and wasteful to run it on every recompile! Specifically during `wasp start` when we are often recompiling.  ## Solution?  What we could do is ensure that we run `prisma migrate diff` at the start of every wasp command. If we ran it at the start of every command, but during `wasp start` and its recompilations still used checksum, that could be best of the both wordls -> we don't run `migrate diff` on every recompile, but we still often enough (at start of every command) properly check stuff / do a deeper check.  One way to do this could be to just delete that checksum file when wasp command that uses a db starts, and that is it. That means we want to forget what we know, and deep check `prisma migrate diff` will happen.

## What we want  Right now, when Wasp runs a managed dev db (e.g. via `wasp start db`), it will run it on port 5432. If that port is already occupied by some other process, or by another Wasp app, starting the db will fail.  ## Potential solutions  To solve this, we can do one of two (or both) things: 1. Offer the user ability to specify on which port should managed dev db start. They could specify it via env var, or maybe via some kind of local settings file (we don't have such file yet though). 2. Wasp could automatically try different ports until one of them is free. It could start with 5432 and then add +1 to it until port is free, then use that port.  ## Solution implementation details  There are two parts of Wasp that need to know the port: the part where db is started, and later when Wasp project is generated. The only way to transfer that knowledge is to write it down to disk somehow, temporarily. So when managed dev db starts, Wasp should write down the port that it used to start it, to some local, hidden, non-versioned file, that then other wasp processes (for example if you run `wasp start` or `wasp db migrate-dev`) can read and obtain info about the port from it.  So far this was not needed because port was hardcoded to 5432, but for this change we will need this sharing of knowledge.  One thing to keep an eye on is that while we can write that file with info about port once that mangaed dev db stars running, we can't guarantee it will get deleted once managed dev db stops running, so we shouldn't even try to delete it we can just give up on that. That means that when we do read that file, we should not assume that means that managed dev db is running -> it just means that if it is running, it is running with this port. But ok, that makes sense!  As for which file could that be, the one into which we write port into -> hm, not sure, we don't have that kind of file yet actually, it would probably have to be a new file. We could store it into .wasp/ dir.

Description: We want to make a uniform interface for all of our auth providers: local, Github and Google.  We are introducing a concept of `AuthProvider` and treating our username and password the same as our oAuth providers. For each provider we define a config file which should contain a slug, optional init function and a `setupRouter` function.  The idea is to refactor it a bit, and move it in the right direction. The abstraction is very thin: just `setupRouter` and `init` but it gets the job done for now. Maybe later on we can do something more specific like `getLoginRoute`, `getRegisterRoute` and `getCodeAuth`.

I've tightened the logic for auth templates. It should be more resilient now. | @shayneczyzewski @Martinsos   Another thing I had to do: add a white-list for which auth config files we load.   If you recall, we used a new technique where we load all JS files from a dir (we used it to load provider configs) https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/998/files#diff-2045810b9aee7a4ac73a6ad743d4c94324d4bb969a13b2e43837de932c88863fR30  There is an issue with that since the config files are written in Typescript and then compiled to Javascript.  If you do the following steps: 1. Enable Google auth 2. Compile Wasp project once 3. Disable Google auth 4. Compile the project again  The Google auth config file `google.js` will stay. Why? Because we generate the `google.ts` file and `tsc` compiles it to JS. But `tsc` doesn't clean up previously generated JS files, and all previously generated files stay. This leads us to our generated code having some JS leftovers.  And to be clear, this is only an issue in development while the Wasp file is being actively changed.   In the future, we could maybe invest in FileDrafts that are aware of `tsc` compile artifacts and do a proper cleanup of our `tsc` compile artifacts �  For now, I've added a white-list of files that are allowed to be loaded, thus avoiding the problem. | > For now, I've added a white-list of files that are allowed to be loaded, thus avoiding the problem. Thanks | Thank you for the through review :)  We jumped on a call and went through the points:  Point 1. is related to my poor whitelist implementation, sorry about that, it's fixed in https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/998/commits/600ae0a08a7f7ed3d44c9917d3e9fe11265af77b  Point 2. seems related to `npm install` running after the deps change, but `nodemon` reruns before it finishes. Maybe we should consider restarting the server process after deps update.  Note: It was happening before, but it would be evaluated later when running the server, now it fails at the `tsc` step which then produces the error.  ``` Server: src/auth/providers/oauth/createRouter.ts(3,22): error TS2307: Cannot find module 'passport' or its corresponding type declarations. Server: src/auth/providers/oauth/init.ts(1,22): error TS2307: Cannot find module 'passport' or its corresponding type declarations. ```  Point 3. right you are, removed in https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/998/commits/eaa78b2b6e769b45e2f436dada637c074a218383  Point 4. Description: #969 introduced type support to operations (for the context object and optionally the return value). However, we still don't have type support for `args`: ```typescript import { Task } from '@wasp/entities' import { GetTasks} from '@wasp/queries'  const getTasks: GetTasks<Task[]> = (args, context) => {    // The function knows the type of 'context'    // It also knows it must return a value of type Task[]   // But it doesn't know the type of 'args', it treats it as 'any' } ``` We should implement support for `args`. We want something like this: ```typescript import { Task } from '@wasp/entities' import { GetTasks} from '@wasp/queries'  type Payload = Pick<Task, 'isDone'>;  const getTasks: GetTasks<Payload, Task[]> = (args, context) => {    // The function knows args has type 'Payload'   // The function also knows everything it knew before :) } ```
Discuss Information: ```ts export const getTasks: GetTasks<Payload, Task[]> = async function ( ```  We can also omit the second type variable, giving us `GetTasks<Payload>`, right? If we have the user point us to the argument type in their .wasp, we could also omit `Payload` inside the `getTasks` definition, because we could bake it into `GetTasks` during generation. In order to add the `Payload` type variable, do we extend `Query` -> `Operation`, as we can type `args` to be `Payload`? If so, will the default type of `Payload` be `unknown`, like with `Result`?  | @basdirks I know we went through it on Discord, but good thinking posting it here for visibility purposes :smiley:   Anyway, here goes:  > We can also omit the second type variable, giving us GetTasks<Payload>, right?  That's correct. We should be able to omit both type arguments (they default to `unknown`).  > If we have the user point us to the argument type in their .wasp, we could also omit Payload inside the getTasks definition, because we could bake it into GetTasks during generation.  Also correct, but let's not do that just yet (i.e., let's stick to TS-only, no changes to the syntax and semantics of Wasp).   The proposed additions only come into play once we get into full-stack type safety (#976).  Implementing it now would be much more work with no apparent benefits. Once we start working on #976, we'll see whether we want to move these types into the `.wasp` file  >  In order to add the Payload type variable, do we extend Query -> Operation, as we can type args to be Payload?  That is _exactly_ what we do :)  > If so, will the default type of Payload be unknown, like with Result?  Yes!

Description: This seems to "industry-standard" web app that encompasses all known security issues: https://owasp.org/www-project-juice-shop/. Might be useful down the road for us as we develop Wasp.

![Screenshot 2023-01-23 at 12 10 04](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1536649/214026383-d990dda7-94ea-4ad4-a248-95cb69a60091.png)  ![Screenshot 2023-01-23 at 12 09 17](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/1536649/214026400-ddfb0022-d9c7-4ac8-93e1-4d74c3af6a33.png)  Now it looks the same both with and without tailwind. Tailwind still affects e.g. button fonts via its global settings, so that's something we'll need to decide what to do about in the future.

Description: Our existing lookup process just checks if we already associated a user to the incoming social auth method/id and creates a new user if there is no association: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/main/waspc/data/Generator/templates/server/src/routes/auth/passport/generic/provider.js#L55  It would be better to allow an optional user-defined function to do this check. They could, for example, see that a GitHub user with email "shayne@wasp-lang.dev" is already associated with User 123, so when a Google user with the same email logs in for the first time we can associate them with the same user.
Discuss Information: It would also be nice if this method allowed users to link a social auth method to _their currently logged-in account_. | One thing to possibly keep an eye out for here is associating new social user with a email & password user that doesn't have a verified email -> that can result in a security issue where somebody created account with somebody's email and then gains access if that actual email owner logins with social account.  | We have new issue for this, to actually re-implement it. | @Martinsos I don't think we have a new issue for this? I used this one in our planning �  | Ah whoops I thought you created a new one, something like "Implement account merging logic"! OK, leaving this one then! | User asking for this: https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1271172858281852948/1271172858281852948 | > User asking for this: https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1271172858281852948/1271172858281852948  Since you mention it: I also recently started using Wasp and this feature would be my nº1 priority. | This is a pretty important one for me too  | Also an important feature for me! | I'd also love seeing this. It would help Wasp set itself apart from other frameworks. | @JakeLo123 confirmed this is his team's biggest blocker for enabling social auth. | +1 here ... Can't use social login without this.  | +1 here | I faced this issue while ago, I tried to handle it on onBeforeSignup but it's not even receive `providerProfile` to get user `email` and `email_verified` | +1 Bumping this issue, as I just came across this issue - I find this missing feature a major disadvantage for this framework. | +1 from me :) | In the meantime, I'm wondering if there's a way to customize the messaging? "Save failed: user with the same identity already exists" might not be the most obvious to users saying "maybe you logged in using a different method", but I'm guessing saying something like that would be a security concern. | > In the meantime, I'm wondering if there's a way to customize the messaging? "Save failed: user with the same identity already exists" might not be the most obvious to users saying "maybe you logged in using a different method", but I'm guessing saying something like that would be a security concern.  We could probably do some improvements there without reducing security: I added an issue for that here! https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/3002 | Came here to see if there was any progress here. I thought I added my vote on this one but I didn't. This is a blocker for us to enable social auth. We currently use email auth and once this is available in Wasp would like to add Google auth.  | Thanks for the info @Case-E. We've been doing internal stuff during the last quarter - improving our processes, ci, and all that.  We'll soon get back to implementing user-facing features, and this one is likely the top priority.  | Would it be worth switching to an auth library like `better-auth` to handle this kind of thing? It has [built in account linking](https://www.better-auth.com/docs/concepts/users-accounts#account-linking), provides a plugin ecosystem for more extensibility, and has 25+ oauth methods along with plugins for custom oauth and Anonymous, One-tap, and loads more sign-in options. I know a lot of work has gone in to building Wasp auth, but this could be better in the long run as it would be more flexible and have less code to maintain. | > Would it be worth switching to an auth library like `better-auth` to handle this kind of thing? It has [built in account linking](https://www.better-auth.com/docs/concepts/users-accounts#account-linking), provides a plugin ecosystem for more extensibility, and has 25+ oauth methods along with plugins for custom oauth and Anonymous, One-tap, and loads more sign-in options. I know a lot of work has gone in to building Wasp auth, but this could be better in the long run as it would be more flexible and have less code to maintain.  Yup we will be taking a look at it -> so one of big epics in the roadmap is to get Auth to netx level, and we will be als evaluation better-auth while doing it. Our hunch at the moment though is that it migth be too high-level for us, in the sense that we will have issues due t lack of control we can have over it. But we will know better once we evaluate it properly. In any case, good idea yes! less code to maintain is always great :D. | Here's an example app that implements account merging using merge codes: https://github.com/wasp-lang/account-merging-example/ | +1 to switching to an auth library like Better Auth or possibly Stack Auth. Once you consider modern table stakes features like more providers, 2FA, passkeys, account merging, teams/orgs/projects, etc. the effort to integrate may be far less than the effort to maintain Wasp's own auth. | Yep +1 for 2fa, account-merging etc.

Description: Similar to Django, have Wasp offer a UI for managing users!  First step might be just exposing Prisma Studio in production -> that already brings a lot of control, not only over users but other stuff. There is a lot to think about security and access control here though.  Another approach is building a custom solution for just managing users (or maybe using smth from out there?).  In any case, access control and security are quite important here.

Description: Similar like Codeship did some time ago -> you could have secrets in your source code, committed to vcs (git), encrypted with a master key that is not committed but is kept local. So instead of defining them all via env vars which are local, you would keep only the master key local, which makes it easier to distribute and track secrets. Also, you could organize secrets per environments.  This is still just an idea, it needs to be evaluated in more details, especially from the security side.  Here is an exchange @breadchris and I had about this:  ``` breadchris — 09/12/2022 05:06 it would be cool, in wasp, if you could define a map of secrets in your wasp file so that secret storage was dead simple  martinsos — 09/12/2022 12:04 Ok @breadchris this sounds super interesting, pls help me understand better!  So how would that work, would it be like declaring which secrets Wasp needs, so that it checks for those and errors if they are missing?  Or wait you said you would actually score secrets in git repo? That sounds like anti-practice right? I do understand that is encrypted, that file, so it isn't really a concern, but it does sound a tiny bit scary!  Although now that I think about it, I think we had the same setup with Codeship (now CloudBees) some long time ago, where it was also storing encrypted secrets in our git repo (or was it just on disk?).  Ok let's say you stored a bunch of encrypted secrets in the git repo. I guess those could be some API keys and similar.  How would that work with different environments (staging, production, development) -> you would have different files for different environments?  Who has the key for reading or updating the secrets, how is that distributed? That one is just distributed manually? 10 December 2022  breadchris — 10/12/2022 02:19 we should just hop on a call so I can mind dump on you guys lol [02:20] my neurons have been firing on this space for a while so I have a lot of information to share lol [02:23] So how would that work, would it be like declaring which secrets Wasp needs, so that it checks for those and errors if they are missing?  $ cat main.wasp ... secrets {   API_TOKEN: ./secrets/api-token.enc } ...  $ cat ./secrets/api-token.enc <gibberish>  $ cat .dev.secrets MASTER_KEY=...  $ wasp start  # runs app, reads master key from `.dev.secrets`, decrypts encrypted API keys [02:26] Or wait you said you would actually score secrets in git repo? That sounds like anti-practice right? I do understand that is encrypted, that file, so it isn't really a concern, but it does sound a tiny bit scary! I agree, but the thing is, every development team every where starts out with secrets in code, and they dont know about Kubernetes secrets, or aws secrets manager until a security person, or team, tells them. This is a stop gap solution that "stops the bleeding" https://medium.com/@slimm609/securely-storing-secrets-in-git-542771d3ed8c It is something that lets dev teams dev fast, and not be slowed down by things they have to learn. Medium Securely storing secrets in Git Secrets management and secure storage of secrets as code is always an interesting topic. There are various tools that exist for storing… [02:30] How would that work with different environments (staging, production, development) -> you would have different files for different environments? for simplicity, I would just have one master key stored in a git ignored file, that is shared in both local dev and production. Again, not ideal, but the security for the code goes WAY up when you do this. Now an attacker has to compromise the production system and read env vars AND get access to the code. Simply having access to the code is not sufficient. If you have a SASS, you could have a "setup project" flow that downloads the master key to the git ignored file automatically. In code, you would provide an sdk that lets you retrieve the secrets: // use master key env var to decrypt the encrypted API_TOKEN const secret = wasp.getSecret('API_TOKEN'); [02:31] I have not implemented this personally, but it sure gets my security stamp of approval for a dev team that is developing fast. In your docs you would have a warning of "consider using X, Y, Z to store secrets as they are one of the most common ways that attackers compromise systems" 15 December 2022  martinsos — 15/12/2022 14:32 Ok got it! Thanks for the detailed explanation. Let me try to summarize it so you can confirm if I got it right, and I might ask some extra questions.  1. Secrets (e.g. API keys/tokens) would be commited to git repo, but encrypted. They can be each separate file. I guess we could also just put them all in a single file, or even put them directly into main.wasp. Although it could be a bit boring to read that encrypted data there so maybe better a separate file. 2. Master key, they key used to decrypt and encrypt all of the secrets, would be present in a special file, and Wasp would use it to work with the secrets. This key would not be in git, it would be git ignored, and developer would have somebody share it with them when they are setting up their Wasp project.  In development, this means that you as a dev just obtain this master key once when setting up the project on your machine and you are good to go, from there on all updating of secrets is done via VCS (git) and you don't have to think about it really. Wasp CLI would provide some helpers for managing those, like adding a secret, updating it, deleting, ... .  In production, this could also be used. The only thing we need to provide is master key, which could be provided via whatever is the mechanism of that cloud provider (ASW secrets manager, ...), and the rest would be obtained from the code. However, there might be secrets that we don't share between dev and prod -> for example maybe in prod we use different API TOKEN for Stripe, because in dev we use one for testing, while in prod we use the real one. While some secrets maybe we want to have same both in prod and dev. So that means we need to specify for which env is which secret, right? I guess we would want to have some secrets that are used in all envs, some that are used only in dev, some only in prod, ... .  It does sound compelling all together, although it would require us to build all the utils for this in Wasp CLI. But certainly doable!  1 [14:38] One thing that also plays into this are ENV vars. Right now we specify those via .env files. And I think when looking at usage, they are somewhat connected to secrets, in the sense that some of them you want to share between devs, some of them should be local, and some of them you want to share even between different environments. Although maybe then they shouldn't be env vars hm. They are always tricky a bit but I wonder if those should also be included somehow in this. Just to be clear, I am talking about non-secret env vars here. Or maybe better way would be to say non-secret configuration.  breadchris — 15/12/2022 19:54 2. Master key, they key used to decrypt and encrypt all of the secrets, would be present in a special file, and Wasp would use it to work with the secrets. This key would not be in git, it would be git ignored, and developer would have somebody share it with them when they are setting up their Wasp project. my dream is that this is generated in lastpass, 1password, or another password manager so you encourage people to generate on master key once and no one has to think about it again (or actually copy the value around, that is how breaches happen) [19:56] ideally the master key never ends up in a file, it is just too easy to accidentally commit it. that is where my security smell meter on this idea starts to go off a little bit. Keeping it as a value you read ephemerally (like from a password manager) is much safer. [19:58] yeah what you said back to me sounds right. I have never rolled this out personally for a project, but from what I have heard/read it is definitely feasible, but needs some iteration. 20 December 2022  martinsos — Today at 16:26 Cool sounds good! Although if we are not keeping it in a file, that would be an issue hm. I do understand why it is a bit scary though. But it needs to be available on machine so wasp can keep encrypting/decrypting them as it uses them. But it could be for example defined in .bashrc of a user, as ENV var, and then that way wasp would see it but it would not be easy to commit it. ```

Discuss Information: Thanks for reporting this!  What happened is that in the new node-alpine Docker image, which we started using with Beta release since we upgraded the version of node we use, they by default ship openssl 3, instead of the openssl 1.1 that was shipped with it before. This by itself shouldn't be an issue, but, Prisma doesn't support openssl 3 when running it on alpine, for now! So while waiting for that, we need to use openssl 1.1  Quick fix for you is to add `openssl1.1-compat` at the end of `RUN apk add --no-cache ...` line in the .wasp/build/Dockerfile. You will have to add it again if Wasp regenerates the project, so that is not great, but it will fix the issue.  We will make sure to fix this soon and release a Wasp version that has it fixed! | Related Prisma issue here: https://github.com/prisma/prisma/issues/16553 . | New release is out, that fixes this! https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/releases/tag/v0.7.2 .

I wonder how we could potentially support that in the future -> some kind of hooking up into Wasp's generation process. Of course first-level support for Swagger would be even better. Btw I don't know a lot about Swagger -> why are these comments needed, what kind of comments exactly is it? The idea is that you define a comment per each route in Express and Swagger picks that up from the code, or how does that work?  As for hooking into express lifecycle, that Wasp doens't expose -> if you let us know which part specifically, we might look into exposing that! | > why are these comments needed, what kind of comments exactly is it? The idea is that you define a comment per each route in Express and Swagger picks that up from the code, or how does that work?  Yes, the typical implementation for JS/TS projects is via JSDoc comments on the route definitions, e.g.:  ```ts /**  * @swagger  * /api/job-listings/by-url:  *   get:  *     summary: Retrieves a user job listing by URL  *     description: Fetches a user job listing by the job listing URL for the authenticated user  *     tags:  *       - Job Listings  *     security:  *       - bearerAuth: []  *     parameters:  *       - in: query  *         name: url  *         required: true  *         schema:  *           type: string  *         description: The URL of the job listing to retrieve  *     responses:  *       200:  *         description: User job listing retrieved successfully  *         content:  *           application/json:  *             schema:  *               type: object  *               properties:  *                 id:  *                   type: string  *                 userId:  *                   type: string  *                 jobListingId:  *                   type: string  *                 status:  *                   type: string  *                 documents:  *                   type: array  *                   items:  *                     type: object  *                 jobListing:  *                   type: object  *                 match:  *                   type: object  *                 user:  *                   type: object  *       400:  *         description: URL parameter is missing  *         content:  *           application/json:  *             schema:  *               type: object  *               properties:  *                 error:  *                   type: string  *       401:  *         description: Unauthorized - User is not authenticated  *       404:  *         description: Job listing or user job listing not found  *       500:  *         description: Server error  */ router.get(   '/api/job-listings/by-url',   [auth, ...getUserJobListingByUrlApiMiddleware],   handleRejection(     (       req: Parameters<typeof _waspgetUserJobListingByUrlApifn>[0] & { user: AuthUserData | null },       res: Parameters<typeof _waspgetUserJobListingByUrlApifn>[1],     ) => {       // <snip>     }   ) ) ```  which then generates output like this:  ![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/0baa5806-88da-4f0f-b39f-2d3a7a3a3096)  > As for hooking into express lifecycle, that Wasp doens't expose -> if you let us know which part specifically, we might look into exposing that!  For a tool like `express-oas-generator`, it wants its functions to be added as [the first and last middleware](https://www.npmjs.com/package/express-oas-generator#advanced-usage-recommended). So having some ability to control the ordering/priority of middleware functions might suffice here. | @Genyus I think you should be able to modify global middleware at the moment, check out https://wasp.sh/docs/advanced/middleware-config#default-global-middleware- , it explains a bit, and then there is a section about customizing global middleware. You can take the existing one and add to it at the start and at the end, I believe.  Btw I am guessing alternative solution might be something like https://www.npmjs.com/package/swagger-routes-express or using https://www.npmjs.com/package/swagger-ui-express directly, where routes are described in a standalone yaml file? But that doesn't sound great hm, I would much rather prefer the descriptions stand next to the route code, as you went for.  But for https://github.com/Surnet/swagger-jsdoc that you used -> How we generate the project is a bit complicated, we link to your user code from the generated server code, so that might make it tricky, but I wonder if the problem might come down to not correctly setting that path in `options.apis` in  ``` const swaggerJsdoc = require('swagger-jsdoc');  const options = {   failOnErrors: true, // Whether or not to throw when parsing errors. Defaults to false.   definition: {     openapi: '3.0.0',     info: {       title: 'Hello World',       version: '1.0.0',     },   },   apis: ['./src/routes*.js'], };  const openapiSpecification = swaggerJsdoc(options); ```  I am not sure off the bat what the path should there be, but what have you tried? Are you sure you can't set that path to something that works?   | > I am not sure off the bat what the path should there be, but what have you tried? Are you sure you can't set that path to something that works?  @Martinsos Thanks for this! � I always thought the Swagger comments worked like Java annotations and had to be applied directly to the routes they describe, but I had a look at the package and realised it's just a simple regex-based parser, so they can live anywhere. I moved them to my *.wasp.ts files (which seemed like the most logical place as that's where the routes are defined) and updated the apis path as you suggested. Works perfectly now! The JSDoc comments can be maintained in the source files and get picked up at runtime.  I've [created a Gist](https://gist.github.com/Genyus/2642f94fe97a3a0f9f12a9d90b6863fb) and will share my updated findings in the original Discord thread | Awesome @Genyus, thanks for all the effort you put into this! This will be a nice starting point for when we decide to implement this as a feature! | I wonder if https://typespec.io project wuold be interesting for Wasp Operations - it is worth chekcking it out a bit.

Description: **Describe the bug** I've already spoken with @shayneczyzewski about something I've been noticing with Prisma Entities. It seems some fields aren't being recognized/compiled by Wasp depending on where they are within the entity object: ![image](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/70215737/200512108-f6a9337d-0666-4646-b9d9-acefc5bd7963.png) For example, I didn't notice these two properties in Prisma Studio until I bumped them up to the top of the Entity object (see screenshot). I'm also quite sure I created these properties when I initially created the Entity. After bumping them up to the top of the entity object, I finally get the migrate DB message: `� ---- Your Prisma schema has changed, please run `wasp db migrate-dev` when ready.` If I migrate the DB and then move the fields back down again, I get a `drop tables` warning. Maybe fields after a field with the "?" are affected? �‍♂️
Discuss Information: ``` app HiveMind {   title: "HiveMind",   db: {     system: PostgreSQL   },   dependencies: [     ("nodemailer", "^6.8.0")   ] }  job emailSender {   executor: PgBoss,    perform: {     fn: import { workerFunction } from "@ext/workers/emailSender.js"   },   schedule: {     cron: "* * * * *" // every minute   },   entities: [Task] }  entity Task {=psl     id          Int       @id @default(autoincrement())     createdAt   DateTime  @default(now())     updatedAt   DateTime? @updatedAt       name        String    @unique     status      String    @default("pending")     message     String     email       String     deadline    String     recurring   Int[]? psl=}  route RootRoute { path: "/", to: MainPage } page MainPage {   component: import Main from "@ext/MainPage.js" } ``` | I was able to reproduce this bug @vincanger, nice find! Will look into if this is a regression or something we never actually supported next. ��  | Ok, so it turns out this is not actually a Wasp or Prisma bug per se. Wasp does support optional modifiers (they are used in some sample apps, and even in this example for `updatedAt`). However, neither Wasp nor Prisma supports both `[]` and `?` simultaneously: https://www.prisma.io/docs/concepts/components/prisma-schema/data-model#type-modifiers  | Note: You cannot combine type modifiers - optional lists are not supported.  So I guess the correct behavior would be for us to show an error to the user, ideally, instead of just skipping whatever follows it. I will leave this open so we can improve this behavior in the future. ��  | @vincanger noted VS Code will give a warning if it was just a Prisma schema file. ![unknown](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/523636/200578125-c8780018-b842-4ea1-bf7c-ef5ee24c0ac4.png)  This is a good use case for extending our `psql` support in some way. | Done in #2035

Description: Our Realworld example app sometimes throws the following error after the user logs out: ``` react-dom.development.js:67 Warning: Can't perform a React state update on an unmounted component. This is a no-op, but it indicates a memory leak in your application. To fix, cancel all subscriptions and asynchronous tasks in a useEffect cleanup function.     at Navbar (http://localhost:3000/static/js/bundle.js:1414:19)     at div     at Container (http://localhost:3000/static/js/bundle.js:7948:23)     at WithStyles (http://localhost:3000/static/js/bundle.js:20180:31)     at UserSettingsPage (http://localhost:3000/static/js/bundle.js:3721:5)     at AddHeader     at http://localhost:3000/static/js/bundle.js:776:69 ```  We should fix it :)  NOTE: We've recently solved a [similar error](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/720#issuecomment-1246828596), looking at [the fix](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/727) might help you.
Discuss Information: Hey � @sodic . Great Project. Can I take up this one ? | Strange but I was not able to reproduce the error. Can you guide me with some steps to reproduce? | Hey, @anup-a, sorry for the late response? If you're still interested, I can give you the instructions on reproducing it in a couple of hours. | What I got so far: - it only triggers if you are on a component that's wrapped with `createAuthRequiredPage` when logging out - it seems that this warning was removed from React 18 completely: https://github.com/reactwg/react-18/discussions/82  I'll investigate a bit more to see if there is anything actionable or if we should move to upgrade to React 18 as a means of "fixing" this issue. | Finally they're removing this warning. Half of the code review fixes in my last company were either telling someone to use the `isMounted` check (when I'm the reviewer), or adding it (when I'm the author).  Anyway, if it's not a memory leak, we can just wait for React 18. | Since realworld no longer exists, I'm closing this.

Description:
Bumps [ansi-regex](https://github.com/chalk/ansi-regex) and [ansi-regex](https://github.com/chalk/ansi-regex). These dependencies needed to be updated together. Updates `ansi-regex` from 4.1.0 to 5.0.1
Release notes

Sourced from ansi-regex's releases.

v5.0.1

Fixes (backport of 6.0.1 to v5)

This is a backport of the minor ReDos vulnerability in ansi-regex@<6.0.1, as requested in #38.

  • Fix ReDoS in certain cases (#37) You are only really affected if you run the regex on untrusted user input in a server context, which it's very unlikely anyone is doing, since this regex is mainly used in command-line tools.

CVE-2021-3807

https://github.com/chalk/ansi-regex/compare/v5.0.0..v5.0.1

Thank you @​yetingli for the patch and reproduction case!

Breaking

Description: # Description  - `prismaErrorToHttpError` function refactorized - Added a custom error response for each prisma error in the current prisma documentation ([link](https://www.prisma.io/docs/reference/api-reference/error-reference#error-codes)) - Update utils tests  Fixes # ([issue](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/384))  ## Type of change  Please select the option(s) that is more relevant.  - [x] Code cleanup - [ ] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue) - [ ] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality) - [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected) - [ ] This change requires a documentation update
Discuss Information: Hey there @makinox! Thanks for your efforts here! � Apologies for our delay- we recently had a team retreat last week. I will take a look at this PR this week though. Thanks and be in touch soon! | > Hi @makinox, thanks for taking on this issue! I can see a lot of time went into this, and I appreciate the thoroughness of all the error codes to messages. :) I had a couple of questions/comments: >  > 1. What strategy did you use to map the Prisma error type to an HTTP response code? > 2. I do worry that returning many of these messages to the client would result in a possible security vulnerability, and even of not exploitable, at the very least provide too much info either way. >     >    * Ref: https://owasp.org/www-community/Improper_Error_Handling >    * What if instead, we logged most of this information, but only returned to the user a detailed error message for a subset of them (only the ones in which the frontend actually had to tell the user something about)? I think from a security perspective it should all probably be opaque, and we just return a standard response for everything, but perhaps in some cases we can provide more? >  > Curious to hear your thoughts. Thanks again!  Hello @shayneczyzewski, I hope you guys doing well.  1. I did not follow a particular strategy, I just did what I think it was the best, that is letting the user know what is happening instead of only responding `general error`, reading each prisma error and translating that to the wasp context; usually big frameworks always informs you what is happening, and personally I would like to add more details for some errors, but I think that is out of scope for now. 2. Yes I see your point, maybe for that case we could handle   - Common error (P1000 to P1017 errors)   - Prisma Client error (P2000 to P2034 errors)   - Prisma Migrate error (P3000 to P3022 errors)   - Prisma pull error (P3000 to P3002 errors) I think the problem with this general sectioned errors is that are less helpful, and it could save less time to the developer, from my personal experience I appreciate more detailed errors, but you have the last words guys | >   Thanks for the reply, @makinox! I hope you are well too! :)  Yeah, how to handle how much info to provide back to the client is tricky. I will think on it a bit more/research it and we can also see if @Martinsos has any immediate thoughts on this too. My gut tells me less is more here (in terms of what info goes down the wire to the client), but certainly, we can make use of all your work so far with additional server-side logging. �  Do we have any examples of how other frameworks handle this? I think I remember in Rails we get a ton of error info when in `dev` mode, but when in `prod` it just all becomes opaque. Maybe we can handle the errors differently depending on the mode? | > >  >  > Thanks for the reply, @makinox! I hope you are well too! :) >  > Yeah, how to handle how much info to provide back to the client is tricky. I will think on it a bit more/research it and we can also see if @Martinsos has any immediate thoughts on this too. My gut tells me less is more here (in terms of what info goes down the wire to the client), but certainly, we can make use of all your work so far with additional server-side logging. � >  > Do we have any examples of how other frameworks handle this? I think I remember in Rails we get a ton of error info when in `dev` mode, but when in `prod` it just all becomes opaque. Maybe we can handle the errors differently depending on the mode?  Yeah, that solution sound very good, meaningful messages for developers and more generic responses for production users, in fact we could extend this implementation for other errors in the app. | Hey again, @makinox! Hope things are good! @Martinsos and I chatted a bit yesterday about this PR and we were wondering what you think about the following.  What if instead of returning a unique `HttpErrror` per Prisma error code, we instead bucketed them into two broad categories: "validation" errors (to include DB constraints, invalid types, value too large, etc.) and everything else. I say validation in quotes since it may include a few that are not strictly validation, but this bucket would include anything that the frontend may want to notify the user about or act upon in some smart way.  Some examples of validation errors may include Prisma error codes like P2000, P2006, P2007, and many more. You can probably spot them quickly based on all your great work so far. :D For these, we could perhaps return 422 for most, if not all? Then, everything else that is _not_ one of those whitelisted Prisma error codes is just a 500.  Additionally, we were wondering what information we could pass along from the Prisma error object as part of the response. Right now you have a String literal message (perhaps this can be pulled off the error object instead? maybe not if a security issue, but quote possibly � ), and maybe the response should also include other useful JSON metadata for the frontend to consume, like the column name(s) that the validation failed for, perhaps (e.g. `meta.target` from here: https://www.prisma.io/docs/reference/api-reference/error-reference#prismaclientknownrequesterror)?  Lastly, one idea that would give the caller more control was that we could perhaps pass a list of codes into the function to modify the whitelist somehow (like add or remove Prisma codes to include for special processing). So maybe in some context they always want code Pxxxx to be a 500, or maybe one code we left out of special handling they want included, etc.  Anyways, this may not make 100% sense (apologies), because I think we too have only a foggy idea of what it could look like, but maybe this is helpful and a decent direction to refactor towards in your mind? Curious to hear your thoughts. Thanks! | Hello @shayneczyzewski, yes jeje this is turning a little big confusing, maybe I can help you to simplify the problem.
