Description: # Description  This change improves the algorithmic complexity of writing `FileDraft`s by replacing nested list operations (n^2) with `HashMap` based lookup versions (n*logn).  It also fixes a bug that was discovered as part of this process where a `FileDraft` was written twice. It does not add a check to prevent that from reoccurring. That is for another issue: #517   Fixes #503  ## Type of change  Please select the option(s) that is more relevant.  - [x] Code cleanup - [ ] Bug fix (non-breaking change which fixes an issue) - [ ] New feature (non-breaking change which adds functionality) - [ ] Breaking change (fix or feature that would cause existing functionality to not work as expected) - [ ] This change requires a documentation update
Discuss Information: > One sneaky comment coming in :D ![giphy](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/523636/158482482-3ebe713d-5245-4247-a2d2-414a0642d6cd.gif) � there will be time for a review lol While I have you though, I feel like I hit some weird snag where we now get a failure for a single e2e test in CI (server template file does not get processed properly), and locally, but it seems conditional on `stack clean`... if you build and run on this branch after `stack clean` it fails. Then swap to `main` and run it fails. Then `stack clean` on main again and it passes. Possibly stack related, but I wonder if some race condition in general for Tasty tests occurs once we swap lists to another data structure that may not be threadsafe? Puzzling, but we can use some time tomorrow to chat on if room. | Strange behavior summary (1 of 2): 1) starting on `shayne-martijn-use-maps-and-sets` - `stack clean` - `stack test :e2e-test`=> 1 failing test - `git checkout main` - `stack test :e2e-test`=> 1 failing test - `stack clean` - `stack test :e2e-test`=> ZERO failing tests | Strange behavior summary (2 of 2): 2. starting on `main` - `stack clean` - `stack test :e2e-test` => ZERO failing tests - `git checkout shayne-martijn-use-maps-and-sets` - `stack test :e2e-test` => ZERO failing tests - `stack clean` - `stack test :e2e-test` => 1 failing test | The failing test for reference: Seems like a template is going unprocessed for some reason. | File: `/Users/shayne/dev/wasp/waspc/e2e-test/test-outputs/waspMigrate-current/waspMigrate/.wasp/out/server/src/server.js` | @shayneczyzewski now I am wondering if it might be the same thing as @sodic had -> if you run `stack test :e2e-test`, but you don't do `stack build` first, you are really running tests for an old build, not for the current version of the code. That is why `stack clean` probably helps, maybe it forces rebuilding? Making this up hm, might not be connected but maybe worth investigating. | @shayneczyzewski so the whole thing with `stack clean`, I think it just forces project to rebuild, so it uses the current version of the code when running tests. If so, then the problem comes down to: for some reason, on this branch that uses maps, the server.js gets copied uncompiled! And that happens only for `waspMigrate` e2e test. That is super weird hm. | This is rather fascinating. It is good that we can replicate it consistently, across machines. On the other hand I have no idea where to start debugging this from. One thing that changes in this commit is version of strong-path. That is the only thing that is "global", all the other changes seem to be local. They are however affecting writing of file drafts, so some of those local changes could also be the cause. Probalby best to revert piece by piece of code until we detect which one is causing this. One thing I can think about right now, is that our code for writing template file drafts currently says that if data received is Nothing, it will just copy the template file. And it seems like exactly that is happening. So in case of waspMigrate e2e test, for some reason, no data (`Nothing`) is provided to the template file draft for server.js. Ok, so let's see what data is supplied to template file draft for server.js . I just searched for `server.js` in our code, and I see it is generated in ServerGenerator.hs . And looking at it, I see suspicious part: it is first mentioned in `genSrcDir`, where it is generated with no data supplied, so it is supposed to be just copied, but then it is mentioned again in `genServerJs`! Ha, weird. And `genServerJs` is called in `genSrcDir`, but somewhat later, after the generation of `server.js` without any data. So, this must be the problem, because we have 2 file drafts for generating server.js -> first without any data (and this one is wrong), and then with data (which is good). I am guessing the first one, with no data, is old one, when server.js didn't use any templating mechanisms. And then we added mustache stuff into it, and added `genServerJs`, but forgot to remove the old file draft. And that worked fine because the second file draft would overwrite the first one, until for some reason now, that order of writing changed! I guess because we sorted them differently. Ok great! So this is an issue. We need to remove the old `server.js` draft, so basically remove the ``` return $ C.mkSrcTmplFd $ C.asTmplSrcFile [relfile|server.js|], ``` line from `genSrcDir`. This is an old error that was now exposed. Now, an interesting question is, how can we avoid this in the future? Well, first thing is that e2e tests captured this, which is awesome :)! I think this is their biggest feat so far! Without them, we wouldn't capture this, and even if we did figure it out, it would be harder to debug. So they are awesome. Besides major refactoring of templating system where we get more stuff on the type level, what I think we can do right now is introduce a check that checks if we are attempting to write same file draft twice. That could be present in WriteFileDrafts.hs -> we check if there are two file drafts that have the same destination path. If so, we can throw an error -> it is a bug, it shouldn't be happening, because we don't guarantee the order of writing and it makes everything a mess! How can we even organize them into a map with path being key if we have two things that go under the same key. So yeah, our code there needs to assume there is only one filedraft for each dst path, and it needs to enforce that assumption. | > This is rather fascinating. Ha, weird. And `genServerJs` is called in `genSrcDir`, but somewhat later, after the generation of `server.js` without any data. I am guessing the first one, with no data, is old one, when server.js didn't use any templating mechanisms. And then we added mustache stuff into it, and added `genServerJs`, but forgot to remove the old file draft. And that worked fine because the second file draft would overwrite the first one, until for some reason now, that order of writing changed! I guess because we sorted them differently. > > Ok great! So this is an issue. We need to remove the old `server.js` draft, so basically remove the > > ``` > return $ C.mkSrcTmplFd $ C.asTmplSrcFile [relfile|server.js|], > ``` > > line from `genSrcDir`. > > This is an old error that was now exposed. > > Now, an interesting question is, how can we avoid this in the future? Well, first thing is that e2e tests captured this, which is awesome :)! I think this is their biggest feat so far! Without them, we wouldn't capture this, and even if we did figure it out, it would be harder to debug. So they are awesome. Besides major refactoring of templating system where we get more stuff on the type level, what I think we can do right now is introduce a check that checks if we are attempting to write same file draft twice. That could be present in WriteFileDrafts.hs -> we check if there are two file drafts that have the same destination path. If so, we can throw an error -> it is a bug, it shouldn't be happening, because we don't guarantee the order of writing and it makes everything a mess! How can we even organize them into a map with path being key if we have two things that go under the same key. So yeah, our code there needs to assume there is only one filedraft for each dst path, and it needs to enforce that assumption. Amazing debugging @Martinsos that seems to have fixed it! � (and I love the thinking out loud part!) :D Thanks so much. Agree there is value for e2e here in the first real bug, and we can also add extra safeguards to check for duplicate FD paths in this update as well. Thanks again!!!

Description: ## Description  JS code exists in the form of templates that are rendered to JS by Wasp during generation time.  Currently the only way to test the JS code generated by Wasp is to run a full generation and do a by-hand test against the generated application. (and a golden test of the generated code)  But this makes it impossible to use common JS tools like syntax checkers and linters, and it makes an evolution towards TypeScript more difficult too. For these purposes it would be better if we could maintain the JavaScript code as JavaScript (and eventually TypeScript). We could then run automated tests against it, and change it with the safety net of our tooling. For this purpose it would be useful to pull as much code into a Wasp runtime and refer to that from the templates.  At the same time, we still need to generate code and retain the flexibility of using templates. How do we factor the JS code that currently exists in templates so that it's easier to manage?  ## Acceptance criteria  * Do analysis of the templates  * Come up with a plan (in the form of more issues) with steps to take to make the JS code management situation easier
Ideas: - On Page/Route level (for UX purposes really, not security) - On Operation level - On Entity level - On Entity field level
Discuss Information: Related to #263  | Bumping this because it's a popular request.  Related: #2396 and #1133  | Related https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/584 | More users requesting this: https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1353781474456768714/1353781474456768714 | > More users requesting this: https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1353781474456768714/1353781474456768714  Some quotes:  > would it be possible to create staff accounts that aren't admin but can view pages that noraml users can't?  > would love to have "team" admins for my users & role based access within teams.  > I'm super reluctant to be adding a separate db query for every page just to get organisation data for a user. Are we not simply able to change the context.user to join our "organisation" entity so we have all the information here. Basically I want an organisation to hold the subscription details not the user. And only certain actions or pages can be performed based on your subscription. Also you can only load data that is linked to your organisation.  > In terms of restricting data I can do this pretty easily, just using queries, views, filters etc, but the crux is getting access within context.user or context.Org so that you don't have to do a db query everytime a page loads or a query/action is required

In short, we have it defined like so (in `ShellCommands.hs`): ```haskell data OutputDir = DevOutputDir | BuildOutputDir  -- Uses python2's json.tool to sort keys as `npm install` overwrites -- package.json when writing package-lock.json and different versions produce different ouput. -- NOTE: python3 won't sort them, but we want to, so force python2. -- Ref: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/482 reformatPackageJson :: OutputDir -> ShellCommandBuilder ShellCommand reformatPackageJson outputDir =   return $ combineShellCommands $ concatMap (reformatJsonCommands . componentPackageJsonFp) ["server", "web-app"]   where     componentPackageJsonFp :: String -> FilePath     componentPackageJsonFp component = case outputDir of       DevOutputDir -> ".wasp/out/" ++ component ++ "/package.json"       BuildOutputDir -> ".wasp/build/" ++ component ++ "/package.json"      reformatJsonCommands :: FilePath -> [ShellCommand]     reformatJsonCommands fp =       let tmpFp = fp ++ ".tmp"        in [ "python2 -m json.tool " ++ fp ++ " > " ++ tmpFp,             "mv " ++ tmpFp ++ " " ++ fp           ] ```  And use it in the test files like so: `reformatPackageJson DevOutputDir`. I wanted to keep it an explicit part of our commands so we remember we are doing it, and the param supports the standard `out` dir and `build` dir, depending on usage.  If that looks sane, I can go ahead and merge Part 1, and then retarget Part 2 for `main` and after any last minute feedback get the golden test dirs actually in there. Thanks!

Description: Right now, we rely on a migrate checksum to know if the user has migrated locally. Should we use the actual database (via `prisma migrate status`) in some way? Let's think about it.  Some pros are we can determine if users have manipulated the database from under us, and we can also reduce unnecessary warnings on initial checkout/clean.  However, a con is our checksum approach lets us know if they have changed their schema.prisma in a way that needs a migration to take effect, and `prisma migrate status` does not check for this.
Discuss Information: We have:  **schema.prisma <-> migrations <-> db**  and challenge is recognizing when any of these are our of sync, so that we can warn developer, so they may take appropriate actions based on that.  What `prisma migrate dev` does is sync _schema_ with _migrations_ , and then also apply _migrations_ to the _db_. So it takes care of everything.  Right now, we use our manual "checksum" approach to track if _schema_ is out of sync with the _migrations_. That works good -> we might produce false negative sometimes (false warning), but never a false positive, which is great.  However, we don't have a mechanism to track if _migrations_ got out of sync with the _db_. This could happen if developer manipulates database manually (modifies db schema) or if they provide a different database, that doesn't have correct schema. We could track this with `migrate status` -> it might be enough in practice to do it only at the beginning of `wasps start`, since it is a relatively expensive operation, and since we can assume developer is not messing up the database manually while running `wasp start`. So, besides checking the checksum on every compile to ensure that _schema_ is in sync with the _migrations_, we could also run `migrate status` on `wasp start` to ensure _migrations_ are in sync with the _db_.  There is also another interesting option, and that is `prisma db pull`. It creates schema.prisma based on the database schema. So the opposite operation of `migrate` or `db push`! We could in theory use this to check if _schema_ is in sync with the _db_ (skipping the check for migrations). Assuming `prisma db pull` is exact reverse of `migrate`, which might not be the case though. If `db pull` would be interesting, we would also probably do it only at the start of the `wasp start`.  | Your recap above was super helpful to remind me of what we were trying to do here @Martinsos, so thanks! :D   Did some initial research today, and it seems `prisma db pull` will overwrite our schema. We could move it somewhere as a backup, then diff and revert, etc. but I think I found an easier way to see if DB matches schema.  If we are in a situation where we do not know the state of the database, we should actually do two checks: - DB vs migrations: `prisma migrate status --schema ../db/schema.prisma`   - Look for "Database schema is up to date!" in the output (sadly no exit code check) - DB vs schema: `prisma migrate diff --from-schema-datamodel ../db/schema.prisma --to-url "$DATABASE_URL" --exit-code`   - Look at exit code  NOTE: There is also a `--from-migrations` option for `prisma migrate diff`, but that requires us to supply a `--shadow-database-url` where it will temporarily apply the migrations to diff the databases. If we just generate some random DB, it will error out with `Database 'todoapp-shadow' does not exist on the database server at 'localhost:5432'.` I figure better to not go down this route of creating and removing shadow DBs like `prisma db migrate` does.  Armed with the above two checks, the idea would be to check _both_ of these whenever we do not see our `db/.schema.prisma.wasp-migrate-checksum` file. If they both come back in sync, we could in theory just generate that file. The reason we want to check both and not just `prisma migrate status` for example is they may have done a `wasp clean` after editing their Entity, but they did not create and apply a new migration for it yet (by running `wasp db migrate-dev`). So in essence, if both of those say they are up to date with the DB, we can mark it as ok and then let Wasp just detect schema drift normally thereafter. Sound like a plan? | Sounds good to me @shayneczyzewski , and I also appreciated my old comment, it got me up to speed quickly :D!  Just to check -> what are our use cases, when do we care about syncing stuff and which stuff? So, let's say they pull some changes into the project -> they now have some migrations in their wasp project, but database might not be reflecting those. Also there might be no migrations in the generated project. Also, their schema might not be reflecting migrations. So we want to detect any of those I think. I guess we don't care about migrations in the generated project though, those will be created when needed? So what we do is maintain that checksum to compare migrations dir and schema file. So the only thing we need to check is if db is up to date with migrations, right? That we can do by using any of those two commands you listed, I think, right? As long as we generate needed stuff (migrations, schema). So it sounds to me like one of those should be enough? We could do two, but it sounds a bit redundant? But maybe I am missing something here? Maybe we additionally ensure migrations and schema are in sync although we actually already have a mechanism for that? | Hey @Martinsos good questions. We do have to care about both sadly, I think.  "Are all _existing_ migrations applied?" That can be answered by `prisma migrate status`. However, migrations are only _created_ from a schema update when they `migrate`. So, in what case will this be insufficient?  Imagine you make an update to your entity/model, and then run `wasp clean` (or equivalently, on a fresh checkout). We have no record of anything in the generated project now. However, you did not yet run `wasp db migrate-dev` and as such, there is no new migration reflecting this change. The only way to detect that difference is: "Is my schema different than my migrations and/or the DB?" We can answer if different than DB with `prisma migrate diff`.  You mentioned we have a mechanism for checking schema vs migrations, but I don't think so in this case, do we? The current mechanism just takes the schema checksum when `migrate` last occurred, but it is when we lack this file we run into problems. | Ah got it @shayneczyzewski ! Ok that makes sense -> the only way would be to run something yes. Makes sense!

Description: Here I am using a relatively primitive approach of validation, with no assurances done at the type system level. However, it is easy to implement and update, so I conclude it is the best solution to start with and we can upgrade to approach with more type safety as the time goes, if it will prove important.
Discuss Information: Great job @Martinsos Not much to add here. It felt familiar, and then I remembered why � I think this approach is a better first step than the type enforced one ��  | LGTM!

Description: The motivation for this behaviour was validating certain properties of AppSpec before it goes into Generator, so that Generator can have easier time due to not having to worry about certain properties being enforced or not.  Validations that we currently care about are: - AppSpec should have exactly one `app` declaration. - If a Page has `authRequired` set, then `app.auth` also needs to be defined. - Entity provided to `app.auth.userEntity` needs to have field `email` of type `string` and field `password` with type `string`.  Especially important is the first one, because many parts of the Generator use `app` and them having to check it every time for existence would be impractical.  In Haskell, we normally want to capture as much info as we can in the type system, so compiler helps us. Therefore, the best validation is the one that is supported by the type system! So, the ideal validation in Haskell is when the output of the validation logic ensures via the type system that it can't be invalid.  For example, for AppSpec and having exactly one `app`, that could be smth like:  - `validateAppSpec :: AppSpec -> Either [ValidationError] ValidAppSpec`  - `data AppSpec = { ... , app :: Maybe App, ... }`  - `data ValidAppSpec = { ... , app :: App, ... }`  So, output type guarantees that AppSpec has exactly one type, and don't have to worry about it ever again! There is no way we forget to validate AppSpec -> if we didn't validate it, we wouldn't be able to obtain ValidAppSpec at all. So this means validation was done successfully, if we have ValidAppSpec, and those properties that were validated are now encoded in the structure itself. There is a nice blog post by Alexis King about this: https://lexi-lambda.github.io/blog/2019/11/05/parse-don-t-validate/ .  So while that is ideal solution, I gave up on it relatively quickly, for the following reasons: 1. AppSpec is relatively complex and is also built from other structures (App, Page, Entity, ...). Duplicating all of these, or even a subset of them, would be a lot of work and would reduce our capability to make quick changes. Basically, we would be introducing a new intermediate representation (IR), and I felt that the cost of implementing and maintaining it is relatively high at this moment of time, compared to what we get.  2. I wasn't assured that we will be able to catch all of the properties in the structure, at least not in the practical way. For example, the first one (1 `app`) is easy to catch in the structure. However, the second one (authRequired requiring app.auth) is already harder -> I think we could store the info about Pages that have authRequired into app.auth, and that way would ensure that property, but that would make it a bit impractical to deal with specific pages, since we would have to do lookup in the app.auth. Finally, the third one, with userEntity, I don't even have an idea how to encode that into the structure, at least not in any remotely practical fashion. And I am sure there will be more validation checks in the future.  Also, we need to note that this output structure (from validation) is still "mutable" -> not really mutable of course, we are in Haskell, but one function could make a copy of it with some part somewhat different, invalidate some the properties that way, but still propagate it to other functions, and again we don't have a guarantee that validated properties hold!  So just encoding stuff in the structure is not enough -> we also need to ensure that copies of structure that are invalid can't be created on the fly. We could ensure this by hiding field accessors for its records and exposing only getters (validation function would be the only one with access to actual accessors so it can perform construction).  So taking those two into account, that is really a lot of work.  Therefore, I concluded I could avoid replicating the structure and introducing new IR, if I would instead somehow "mark" it as validated. It would not be as elegant as encoding properties in the structure, but since anyway we can't encode all of them, who cares? At least we "color" the validated structure, and even if we use only parts of it, they would also be "colored" and we would know they passed validation -> because only the validation function can "color" stuff. Therefore I created this PR, with `data Valid` that serves as this marker/color. The problem however, that I wasn't aware at that point, is that I still have this problem where structure is "mutable", in the sense that invalid copies of it can be made on the fly -> this is because `data Valid` is Functor, and while I needed that to allow accessing parts of it while still keeping them colored, I forgot that if something is Functor you can basically put anything in it (`(\_ -> "Foo") <$> someFunctor` will put "Foo" into functor regardless of what is already in it). Which means you could easily "color"/"mark" anything if you had even one instance of "colored" value. And that kind of ruins the whole thing! Again, solution is allowing only predefined getters, so we are sure it is a piece of existing structure that is being returned, but that would again require quite some boilerplate. And as you can see in the code, dealing with `Valid` and moving stuff out of it or manipulating them while inside of it is already complicated enough, so I at the very end concluded this is also just too complex at the time being.  I will leave this PR often for a bit still, just so you can see what it took to implement this, as I used some fun things for the first time (`pattern synnonims` language extension).   I wonder also if Lenses could somehow play into the whole thing, since they have the notion of Getter, but I haven't investigated that enough.  Conclusion: Real solution would need to do coloring OR use new IR, AND also make sure that only getters are available. In both cases, that is a lot of work and duplication, and I concluded it is best to skip it for now and go with the simplest solution, which you can see in another PR: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/459
Discuss Information: Great writeup and strongly-typed attempt, @Martinsos! I have not used pattern synonyms before, so it was neat to see and learn a bit more about here: https://ghc.gitlab.haskell.org/ghc/doc/users_guide/exts/pattern_synonyms.html  I think you had this approach, with the pros and cons very well thought out. I agree it would be great to leverage Haskell as much as we can, either via patterns or new type wrappers, and limiting getters. The injecting or recreating "valid" invalid ones, and vice-versa, is def a problem to consider. And if you went Lenses I think my mind may melt lol.  I think for now the second approach makes the most sense to me, as it is easily extendable with more checks, and uses a now familiar error/warning mechanism. When in doubt, I prefer the simpler approach :D But agree it would be nice if in the future could have the type system enforce it, but the other PR should be good enough to get us started in my eyes. ��   Lastly, in the TS-enforced vs convention/trust-required approach dichotomy, I think there are some aspects we want to protect against (like _accidentally_ making a valid become invalid, etc.) vs protecting against intentionally sneaky devs trying to usurp invariants (how the functor example partially came across). The TS part helps both for sure, but is quite costly for the reasons you outlined- I think in our case we can probably be pragmatic for now and just try our best to add enough safeguards to prevent the former (plus early/easy detection via tests/etc.), even if we don't have the 100% compiler guarantees to prevent the latter. :) Although the line between the two is sometimes thin haha  Great job!! | I just wanted to drop in a note that "Domain Modeling Made Functional" makes much of using types to help ensuring valid data, so it's neat to see you use the pattern of moving from an invalid app spec to a ValidAppSpec here too. AppSpec is, after all, a domain model of sorts. And validation at the boundary is indeed very important; you can get rid of a lot of defensive code that checks whether things are there if you do so.  | > If a Page has authRequired set, then app.auth also needs to be defined.  > I think we could store the info about Pages that have authRequired into app.auth, and that way would ensure that property, but  > that would make it a bit impractical to deal with specific pages, since we would have to do lookup in the app.auth.  Thinking about this, I guess there are two aspects of this:  * We don't want to see a page that says auth required if the app doesn't define auth  * We don't want to see an app without auth if there is any page that requires auth  I think your answer focuses on the second approach. The first would refuse to create a AuthRequiredPage unless the app defines the auth details. If any such page cannot be created, the whole process fails anyway. The problem remains that the App then has a maybe auth and the AuthRequiredPage still needs to check on it. I wonder whether the answer here isn't a form of normalization instead of validation - if an auth is missing we construct auth that always fails to authenticate. That way we can rely on the auth to always be there in the ValidatedApp. If we combine that with refusing to build a ValidatedAuthPage without there being an auth in the original app spec, we'd gain at least some of the benefits of the type system.   | quoting Shayne (it took me a while to read 'TS' not as typescript but type system):  > Lastly, in the TS-enforced vs convention/trust-required approach dichotomy, I think there are some aspects we want to protect > against (like accidentally making a valid become invalid, etc.) vs protecting against intentionally sneaky devs trying to usurp > invariants (how the functor example partially came across).  I agree with that: the type system is there to help developers avoid accidental mistakes. Deliberate corruptions of the system aren't a thing we shouldn't worry about with the type system. Avoiding accidental mistakes is good enough. The debate about 'private/protected/public' in OO languages is similar: is this to help developers to protect invariants in the code or is it a security measure? In C++ it can't really be a security measure as I could do all kinds of low level stuff to get around it. It can still be useful.  | Thanks for comments!  I have to admit I wasn't thinking much about protecting from user input, I was merely focusing on avoiding developer mistakes. But as you both said, both can be covered with parsing/validation.  Quote by Martijn:  > validation at the boundary is indeed very important; you can get rid of a lot of defensive code that checks whether things are there if you do so  This quote very well describes my intentions! And comes down to what is described here https://lexi-lambda.github.io/blog/2019/11/05/parse-don-t-validate/ .  Hmmmm, so your idea @faassen is to provide "default auth" when there is no auth, in the sense that "default auth" is auth that does not work really. Interesting direction but that seems to me like it pulls other problems with it -> how are we dealing with this down the line? But I think to reason about this concretely, you really need to have an idea of how it is used and what "auth" actually means, stands for. With my current knowledge I can hardly imagine this direction ending up practical, but it might be also that I am not fully in grasp of this solution hm.  Let's see what we come up with in the future! | Just to chime so you can see I also read the discussion! :D All makes sense to me, especially liked how @shayneczyzewski   and @faassen explicitly delineated between "accident" vs "sneaky action attempt" by developers -> I agree that the first one is the priority, if we manage to do that it would already be great :). I think it's great we are going with the simpler solution for now but are aware of the cost/shortcomings -> then when we become smarter in the future we'll be able to improve it in the right way :). In short, it's better to have a well-understood problem than the not-really-sure-about solution :D. | Thanks for the wise words @matijaSos :D! Closing this one then.

Description: It would be nice if we had some testing facility that made it easy/scalable to test the Wasp CLI via end-to-end blackbox style testing. This means we can create a new project and do some validation of the files on disk. We can modify the files in some way, run commands, and observe the outputs (in browser, stdout, and disk). We can test different permutations of commands, etc. All very fuzzy, but this is a research ideation Issue for brainstorming. :D  This would help us with a few things: 1) Ensure we do no introduce any regressions during active dev. For example, some PR may inadvertently change the contents of the `package.json` file, or write some file to the wrong place on disk, or output a new or empty file accidentally, or clean up things incorrectly, etc. 2) Ensure non-breaking changes between releases. 3) Ensure things still work as our underlying dependencies change (e.g. Prisma).  Ideally, this would be integrated into GitHub for CI/CD, but as a first step perhaps it is just something we can run locally. Maybe there are two styles of test to start with: 1) Run a series of commands using two different git commits of the CLI and ensure certain outputs/behavior are identical. 2) Run a series of commands, and specify changes on disk you expect to see at each step.  In an ideal world, it would be nice if this also allowed for some browser based testing as well. But that may be too big for Phase 1.  This would be a great way to rely less heavily on manual testing and shore up the lack of large scale IO testing we have in Haskell atm, as well as ensure we have a good, reliable experience for users.
Discuss Information: This sounds great!  As for comparing two git commits and ensuring certain outputs are identical -> I liked how they do it in Jest (JS test framework), where they have a concept of "snapshots" -> basically they do this diff and inform you which files / data changed, and you have to accept those changes / agree to them. In that context they are usually used for data files, CSS files, generated HTML and similar, since those are harder to unit test. In our case we could run that on the generated project, and that way if we change certain part of it we will expect changes only in that part and that is great, if only those happens we are happy, if not we fix the error.  I believe more generally such type of test is called "golden test". | If we use a tool like https://github.com/bats-core/bats-core we can do some pretty interesting things.  If you clone that repo, along with these others, locally: - https://github.com/bats-core/bats-file - https://github.com/bats-core/bats-support - https://github.com/bats-core/bats-assert  You can then run tests like ` ./bats-core/bin/bats test/test.bats` here: https://gist.github.com/shayneczyzewski/2ca926ca37dfcc16147ce426d388f691  The pro here is it is a pretty simple around bash, and you can just use normal commands to do things like modify and read files, as well as use BATS helpers for things like file existence, success/failure, and std output checks. | Interesting @shayneczyzewski ! I wonder if we could instead use Haskell for defining our tests, so that we get type safety and nicer language than using bash, as I am thinking it might become quite complex to write all the test checks in bash, but that really depends on how complex will this logic turn out at the end I guess. Good to know about this as an option. | Yep, I agree it depends on what the goal here is and how complex we envision it getting @Martinsos. If we want basic filesystem and stdout checks, something like BATS may be sufficient given how quickly we can get things running. If we need something more complex, it could make sense to invest into a more robust Haskell setup. | One other thing we want to keep in mind if we go Haskell is the standard stdin redirect/piping approaches to provide input to the Prisma migrate command did not work. It always ended in a CTRL-D after inputting the migration name string, but before the migration ran, ending in "cancelled by user". I had to use `Expect` to actually get it to work in that POC. Probably something with interactivity to further debug if we went Haskell. | Another approach I know about is https://www.texttest.org/  This compares log files generated with expected log files, and offer diffs as a failure. I don't know whether it could also use the same technique to compare our build artifacts. The framework has been around for a while and the website present is definitely not the most up to date, but it appears to be a relevant approach.   | Thanks for another possibility, @faassen! I will look into TextTest.  The two main things I was looking for during research was: 1) diffing of logs/stdout, and 2) filesystem based checks. I think many of these do (1) very well, but most omit (2). Perhaps doing FS based testing on the first round is overkill though, and it is enough to just run commands and inspect stdout/exit codes, but I think in the long run FS checks being supported would be nice to have (for example, to make sure certain files exist/do not exist, or that their contents change appropriately as you do more things). | Yeah, I don't know whether filesystem based comparison is a part of TextTest but it may fit the bill as it's not that different from diffing a log file.  I think in general we could get quite far if we could record the output of the logs to the filesystem, and then diff that along with the expected generated output. A visual diff tool like meld will indicate "test failures". :) | True, that would be nice! I think we may need to beef up our logging in general though if we went a pure log approach, perhaps by adding additional, more verbose, log levels, as well as quieting other things (like node output) that may make it too noisy to compare. Interesting space that will be helpful once we get it rolling. ��  | > Yeah, I don't know whether filesystem based comparison is a part of TextTest but it may fit the bill as it's not that different from diffing a log file. >  > I think in general we could get quite far if we could record the output of the logs to the filesystem, and then diff that along with the expected generated output. A visual diff tool like meld will indicate "test failures". :)  Yeah, just having snapshots (like Jest has snapshots) would probably get us pretty far already!  Haskell -> yeah let's not force it if it doesn't fit the problem, but if it does it might be cool to use it (whenever I start doing something in bash and it grows significantly I tell myself I will not use bash next time :P). Important: Haskell has this famous library called Turtle: https://hackage.haskell.org/package/turtle , that is specialized for doing shell stuff in Haskell, so that might be a good solution to use Haskell while having a nice experience. | There is also this: https://hackage.haskell.org/package/tasty-golden -> maybe this way we can utilize our existing testing infrastructure for these tests also? We are using Tasty testing framework at the moment, in Haskell. | Thanks for the discussion! A few more thoughts here: https://docs.google.com/document/d/1fIXMi2UHf9bOxBawKo2oE0GoBgqIKjwpvYJwX1hq3Kg/edit

Description: In AppSpec, declarations (Decl) don't contain their name inside them, instead when you fetch them from AppSpec you get them as `(String, <somedecltype>)` where `String` is the name of the declaration. e.g. `(String, App)` or `(String, Page)`.  Now, we often want to send them around together with their name, and seeing `(String, App)` in the type signature is not very non-descriptive.  So, I am thinking that we should introduce some additional type to better describe this.  Some ideas: 1. `newtype Named a = Named (String, a)` 2. `type Named a = (String, a)`  I am not sure what is easier to use in practice -> we don't want to make it too complicated to destructure this stuff, but it would also be nice to have some type level safety.

Description: *Below are a list of things that we wanted to keep an eye on that were discovered during the Waspleau example app creation. They may become their own issues or removed, but mainly wanted to note them here for future reconsideration.*  ### Docker  - Do we want to allow the user to customize the Dockerfile we generate during `wasp build`? - Do we want to support Docker Compose in the future to allow multiple services? - Do we need to change our Dockerfile usage of `python` to `python3`, or is that M1 specific? - Do we want to include any M1 specific Heroku/Docker deployment commands in our docs?  ### Deployments  - Would be nice if the CLI warned users when switching from SQLite to PostgreSQL that they need to remove migrations dir (this is captured in docs though). - Verify Heroku behavior of deploying first with SQLite, and then with PostreSQL that the database will no longer work and must be blown away. - We may want to change the default server response text from "Hello world" to "I am api-server" to avoid confusion that they still need to deploy the front-end.  ### Ignore files  Right now, we create minimal `.gitignore` and `.waspignore` files. It could be nice to enhance them with even more files/dirs/patterns. Things like `.DS_Store` for Mac users, and perhaps some popular IDE dirs.  A good place to look for inspiration would be: https://github.com/github/gitignore  Ref: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/master/waspc/cli/Wasp/Cli/Command/CreateNewProject.hs#L113-L130  ### Hidden files - It may be good to add language to `.wasproot` that says something like `Please never touch/delete this manually`

Description: https://wasp-lang.dev currently gives the following command for installing Wasp:  ``` curl -sSL http://get.wasp-lang.dev | sh ```  The lack of https makes this incredibly unsafe. You're asking people to run untrusted code that may not even have come from you. Please lead by example about concern for your users' safety. Switch get.wasp-lang.dev to https, and in the meantime point directly to https://raw.githubusercontent.com/wasp-lang/wasp/master/waspc/tools/install.sh on the frontpage. Another alternative is to use a url shortener that supports https.
Discuss Information: Thanks for pointing this out @akkartik !  I just updated how installer script is being downloaded so that it uses https, here are the new instructions (you will see them on updated webpage and docs): ``` curl -sSL https://get.wasp-lang.dev/installer.sh | sh ```  I believe this should be it! I will close the issue but please feel free to comment further if needed.

Description: If we have following Haskell types  ```hs App { defaultPage :: Page } Page { content :: String } ```  that are both instances of `IsDeclType`, and we have following Wasp source code as input to Analyzer:  ``` page HomePage { content: "Hi" } app MyApp { defaultPage: HomePage }  ```  then the output of Analyzer will be: ``` [ ("HomePage", Decl (Page { content = "Hi"})), ("MyApp", Decl (App { defaultPage = Page { content = "Hi"}})) ```  `Decl` is actually a heterogenous type so that is why we can have a list like this, which I described in a bit of pseudocodish manner above.  As is visible in the output of Analyzer, there is no way for us to figure out that `defaultPage` from `App` is the one named "HomePage"! And this is a problem, because Generator cares about that name, it considers it to be the very name of the page and needs it to be able to correctly work with it. But, it can't because it doesn't know the name of the page that `defaultPage` is describing.  In the old Wasp AST this is solved by all references being just names (Strings), so defaultPage would be just a String that is a name of the page. But that is in itself pretty unelegant and unsafe approach.  So what could be the solution to this? Some ideas: 1. Package the declaration name together with the Page somehow -> maybe say that if Page has name field, then we will always set it to the declaration name (while defining the IsDeclType with TH), or smth like that? 2. Introduce Ref type, as permanent construct of Wasp AST, and instead of having App { defaultPage :: Page }, we would have App { defaultPage :: Ref Page }, and Ref contains the name and is properly handed by the Analyzer. Ref is really `type Ref a = (String, a)` in its core, or maybe even just `type Ref a = String`, not sure yet.  It is up to us to decide how we want this to work, what makes the most sense, what does the "name of the declaration" mean in Wasp and how it should behave. Maybe going with Ref would also easily enable referencing declarations that have not yet been declared, which is currently not supported?
Discuss Information: Why does Generator care about the name of the page? I tried looking around the code for where it's used, but I don't know generator that well. Maybe it's possible to refactor Generator instead of adding this `Ref` type. No idea how possible this is, just throwing around an idea. | Well imagine having two pages with exactly the same content but different names -> somewhat silly, but it is something we should allow -> in that case, we do want to be able to distinguish them (ok this is debatable hm, but I think our life is easier if we can do this, check below as to how Generator works).  And in Generator right now -> so generator will create a React component for the HomePage and when choosing a name for that component it will actually name it HomePage..., and then when we have `defaultPage` set to HomePage as in example above, it will generate a route with path "/" that points to that page -> but for that it will need to know which React component to import and that is HomePage..., so the name is important! Otherwise how would it know what to import and to which component to point to?  I imagine there are other situations where stuff like this is important, although I haven't searched for concrete examples at the moment -> I can try to think more about it if you think it will help.  If we assume names are important -> does `Ref` sounds reasonable to you, or would you do it in some other way? What do you dislike about `Ref`?  Btw won't using `Ref` maybe help us with allowing referencing a declaration that is below in the source code? | I see, that makes sense. `Ref` sounds like a good option then, because solves this problem and out-of-order references. I think `newtype Ref a = Ref { unref :: String }` should be used, since having `(String, a)` brings back the ordering problem.  The implementation will require changing evaluator code a bit: - `decl` TypedExprEvaluation should have type `TypedExprEvaluation (Ref a)` (and implementation just returns a ref with the name of the variable). - `makeDeclType` code generator should no longer allow arbitrary `IsDeclType a => a` being treated as `KDecl :: WaspKind`. Should instead consider only `IsDeclType a => Ref a` as `KDecl`.  These changes means that (from your first example in issue) `data App = App { defaultPage :: Ref Page }` instead. | Awesome @craigmc08 -> yes, I think this is going in the right direction! Thanks for these notes, let's leave it at this for now then, it should be enough information for whoever picks up this issue to get this done.

Description: There are two commits -> first separates context from input, second one removes duplication between Evaluator and DictEvaluator. Check it out, we can merge only specific commits if that will be needed.  Hm I just realized that with second commit we loose some of the type safety that newtype gives us. Not sure if that is a problem or not. In theory, somebody could combine the combinators wrong I guess. Not right now, but in the future if some combinators work with two different evaluators that have same shape but are really different, then that could be an issue. Although that could also be solved by using newtype on the type parameter of the Evaluator -> hm yes maybe that is the way to go, if somebody wants to make sure stuff is different. EDIT: I added newtype to represent TypedDictEntries, that solves the problem from above.

Right now, when new release tag is created (e.g. v0.1.2.3), ci.yaml builds compiler in waspc/ and creates draft release with binaries attached to it.  It would be probably cool if web/docs in `web/` was also built by Github Actions at the same time and deployed to Github Pages. Right now we do it manually, but there is always a risk of us forgetting to do it so that is not great.  Although, this is slightly questionable, because release is only draft until we manually enable it, and if we publish docs immediately on creation of draft release, they will be earlier than the release. It would be ideal if they would be published once actual new release is created! Well, I am guessing Github Actions do have an option to trigger a workflow on new release? If so, that would be the way to go. I just checked and here it is: https://docs.github.com/en/actions/reference/events-that-trigger-workflows#release -> so that is it, we need to trigger publishing of `web/` on new release! Which version of the code do we publish though -> one on the master? Or commit that is connected to release? What is some changes were made after that commit and `web/` was published manually with those -> those will be overriden then! Probably that is a weird situation and we should not worry?
Discuss Information: Not doing this for now as it is not a big enough problem at the moment, there is anyway some manual steps involved with the versioned docs. We can reopen this if we decide it is important enough again. | Just changing the reasoning behind the close | We should probably take care of https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/2824 before we automate this since the current manual procedure helps us not forget about restarting the crawler.

Description: Right now dev can define operations (actions, queries) which are then consumed from client via RPC that works via http. However, they can't define custom http API roues at the moment! They might need this, so we should offer them support for this.  This basically means they should be able to drop one level of abstraction below the operations and directly define http routes (in Express right now).
Discuss Information: There are couple approaches to consider here: 1. We could somehow extend Operations in a way to provide access to headers in them -> basically give more control in them. Although there is probably a limit to what we can do here, we can't go completely custom, but maybe access to headers is already a big thing? 2. We can give them access to wasp express app / routes in app.server.setupFn, so they can define their own routes there. 3. We can provide a mechanism for injecting express middleware into server app in general. 4. We can have special mechanism directly for defining custom HTTP routes.  4 is probalby the strongest solution, 2 is a quick way out, while 1 and 2 are probably useful in their own regard but don't address the problem directly / completely. | Regarding middleware -> we talked a lot about it in some other capacities, e.g. Permissions, so I will also link to that https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/584 . @shayneczyzewski was big part of those previous conversations so also might have something to say here. | Here is request from a Waspeteer that is related to this: > Trying to implement uploading files for my app. Has anyone tried doing this with wasp? Is there a good way to do this within wasp? Any good libraries? > > Everything seems to go through multer, however I don’t see how we can add express middleware through wasp file or pass through headers to actions. Unless I am missing something? | I think we all agree our main choices are escape hatch into Express (super easy, but lose lots of info), or we provide Wasp syntax.  The Wasp syntax could take many forms, but one quick one that may be nice is if we just provided them a way to say something like: ```c post { path: "/foo/<id:integer>", to: import { sayHi } from "@server/actions.js" } ```  So we would let them specify the HTTP verb as a keyword, and in the path we can also encode some type info. This may also dovetail into the top level data schema issue https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/642  | One more idea: What if we would allow them to define Operations with custom API?  Think about it as a hybrid between custom API routes and normal Operations -> Custom Operation.  So what Custom Operation let's you do is define its behaviour in more details, including what its REST API looks like. I guess you would implement a piece of logic on backend that handles a request and then returns `args` - that could be custom part. And then you would also have to implement a piece of logic on frontend that knows how to take `args` and produce an HTTP request. But then once we define those two pieces, the rest can again work automatically and have all the benefits of normal Operations, which is stuff like injecting entities via context, and certainly some other stuff in the future.  Oooo this sounds exciting to me! Not only do we get custom API but we get custom Operations! Maybe this turns out to be silly if we dig a bit deeper, or unfeasible, but it sounds like it could be potentially doable and pretty cool to me! | > One more idea: What if we would allow them to define Operations with custom API? >  > Think about it as a hybrid between custom API routes and normal Operations -> Custom Operation. >  > So what Custom Operation let's you do is define its behaviour in more details, including what its REST API looks like. Not only do we get custom API but we get custom Operations! Maybe this turns out to be silly if we dig a bit deeper, or unfeasible, but it sounds like it could be potentially doable and pretty cool to me!  Interesting idea. I wonder how much impact this would have on the TS stuff @sodic is doing, but this is definitely one way to go about it and it does feel a bit more aligned with our conceptual framework. One thing I wonder if this could support though is websockets? Maybe that is just another verb though, so users can define `GET /blah/:id` and also `WS /socket`? exe: https://www.npmjs.com/package/express-ws | > > One more idea: What if we would allow them to define Operations with custom API? > > Think about it as a hybrid between custom API routes and normal Operations -> Custom Operation. > > So what Custom Operation let's you do is define its behaviour in more details, including what its REST API looks like. I was always imagining we will have a special abstraction for sockets, due to their "live" nature. I am having a bit hard time now imagining how this would work in the context of Operations, but I am sure it can in some way I just don't have enough knowledge at the moment. Interesting! | Related issue: #983 -> exposing `app` to `setupFn`. This is a very quick escape hatch that enables writing custom routes and that is also generally useful! | I gave it a bit of thought @Martinsos, and tried to play off your idea about adding a route notion to Operations. What if it looked like something as simple as:  ``` query getTask {   fn: import { getTask } from "@server/queries.js",   entities: [Task],   route: GET "/task/:id(number)?show=:show(string)"  � } ```  The `:name(type)` segments in the route and query params implicitly become args to the `query` or `action` (so you don't even need a mapping function). You will also get `req`/`res` in the context when you use `route`.  You still invoke it in the same way: ```js const { data: task, isFetching, error } = useQuery(getTask, { id: taskId, show: "everything" }) ```  In fact, we can now do a better job of warning the user if they do _not_ supply everything. This now just becomes a URL like `GET http://localhost:3001/task/123?show=everything` instead of our default `POST http://localhost:3001/operations/get-task`.  You now get some extra things like this in your query, as mentioned: ```js export const getTask: GetTask<Task> = async ({ id, show }, context) => {     // can use context.req or context.res } ```  Users can then do things like `context.res.send()` as we can check for: https://expressjs.com/en/api.html#res.headersSent If they were sent, we do not do `res.json(result)` like we always do now.  Possible types: - string - number - bool  Maybe we can also define some notion of optionality with a `?` prefix? Could be overkill though.  Possible verbs: - GET - POST - PUT - DELETE - ALL  Then, anything else more complex requires using `startupFn(app)` (e.g., web sockets)? | Looks pretty cool @shayneczyzewski !  Ok, so basically we allow them to define custom HTTP route, with mapping from it to the `args` of the Operation. We also expose `req` and `res` in `context`.  Are these two things orthogonal? We could expose `req` and `res` in `context` without introducing custom HTTP route, I think? And we could introduce custom HTTP route without exposing `req` and `res` in context, right?  If so, should we always give them `req` and `res`? Or should we allow them to get access to them without specifying custom route?  By giving them control over `req` and `res`, can they mess something up? I guess they could mess up `req` somehow by changing its values, but ok, that is really on them then. Regarding `res`, right now we take returns value and return it as JSON. They can now return it in some other way, that is not a JSON -> that could be an issue? Or is it good? I am not sure how we can then deal with it if we want to use it on frontend and they don't return JSON. But ok that is their problem then I guess?  How much flexibility can they achieve with this approach you suggested? I guess they have max flexibility? They can declare any kind of route really, and then they can just parse `req` and do anything with `res`. Can they not specify any names/args in route and then manually extract them from `req`, form query params?  Instead of us being smart and implementing this small language/DSL for routes that maps from route to args and vice versa, we could just let them provide two functions: one that receives route as a string and produces JS object, and vice versa. So basically they could define those mappings on their own, via two functions. But I do like the idea that we do this for them, that is quite nice indeed, just wanted to think through this.  We could add `json` to types.  Yup, we should also have `startupFn(app)` as final escape hatch.  | Had a quick chat with @shayneczyzewski , we concluded that we could think about this in following steps:  1. We can implement escape hatch via `startupFn(app)` as very first step. This should already enable any kind of custom api routes, but is a bit manual / ugly. 2. We can make Operations more customizable by providing users a way to customize URL/route with stuff like `route: GET "/task/:id(number)?show=:show(string)"`. Also, offering them access to `req`, maybe -> not sure if that is needed. We concluded that we don't want to give them access to `res`, because they can just mess things up (what kind of Operation is it if it doesn't return JSON? What would be weird). So maybe `req`, not `res`.   But the thing is, this is not really custom routes then, it doesn't solve that use case, it is instead customizing Operations. How needed is this customization of Operations though? Not sure. 3. As a continuation on step 1, we might watn to provide nicer way to customize routes than just that `setupFn` escape hatch. We could do this with interface similar to Operations -> smth like `api myTwitterCallback { fn, entities, httpRoute }` -> actually what @shayneczyzewski described here https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/268#issuecomment-1382367877 . But it would not work as an Operation, you couldn't call it on frontend with useQuery, and it would be less restricted -> it would give you both `req` and `res`, you can return what you want, ... . So it would have different guarantees / expectations.  It is good to remind ourselves that main use case for custom routes is for callbacks and some custom stuff, not for RPC between client and frontend -> for RPC we have operations. Operations = RPC, custom routes = anything.  I think we certainly need to do (1) and I do think we should also go for (3) after it, it is much nicer than (1).  I think (2) is also attractive, but we should evaluate a bit how useful it is, and we should check with @sodic how it plays with adding types to Operations, because there will be some interplay there almost certainly.  | @Martinsos @shayneczyzewski I'll reply to the conclusions outlined in the last comment.  > 1. We can implement escape hatch via startupFn(app) as very first step. This should already enable any kind of custom api routes, but is a bit manual / ugly.  I agree this makes sense as the first step.  > 2. We can make Operations more customizable by providing users a way to customize URL/route with stuff like `route: GET "/task/:id(number)?show=:show(string)"`. Also, offering them access to `req`, maybe -> not sure if that is needed. We concluded that we don't want to give them access to `res`, because they can just mess things up (what kind of Operation is it if it doesn't return JSON? What would be weird). So maybe `req`, not `res`.  Can't say I agree with this one. I think we should either: - Replicate Express's usual interface (i.e., go low level and expose everything they expose, both `res` and `req`). - Come up with our own higher-level API.  Exposing `req` without `res` feels weird to me.  >    But the thing is, this is not really custom routes then, it doesn't solve that use case, it is instead customizing Operations. How needed is this customization of Operations though? Not sure.  Me neither :smile:. One more reason to stick with the "dumb" `serverSetup` approach for now.   > 3. As a continuation on step 1, we might want to provide nicer way to customize routes than just that `setupFn` escape hatch. We could do this with interface similar to Operations -> smth like `api myTwitterCallback { fn, entities, httpRoute }` -> actually what @shayneczyzewski described here [Allow defining custom API routes (http) #268 (comment)](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/268#issuecomment-1382367877) . But it would not work as an Operation, you couldn't call it on frontend with useQuery, and it would be less restricted -> it would give you both `req` and `res`, you can return what you want, ... . So it would have different guarantees / expectations.  This makes a lot of sense to me. I even like that you can't use it with `useQuery`. That should hopefully prevent users from abusing the api for everything  > It is good to remind ourselves that main use case for custom routes is for callbacks and some custom stuff, not for RPC between client and frontend -> for RPC we have operations. Operations = RPC, custom routes = anything. > > I think we certainly need to do (1) and I do think we should also go for (3) after it, it is much nicer than (1).  Yes, Exactly!  > I think (2) is also attractive, but we should evaluate a bit how useful it is, and we should check with @sodic how it plays with adding types to Operations, because there will be some interplay there almost certainly.  Types shouldn't be an issue here, but I'd definitely punt on (2) for now.   | Thanks @Martinsos and @sodic. I appreciate the discussion and thoughts. I agree with the next steps. So the plan will be: - Implement `startupFn(app)` first; I will do this next - Do not do any route customization of Operations at this time - Start on `api myTwitterCallback { fn, entities, httpRoute }` when needed (possibly after step 1, but possibly after more important items this release, we can decide)  Thanks again! �  | @shayneczyzewski sounds like a plan!  @sodic although we agree about punting on (2) for now, I wanted to follow up on couple things, for the sake of further discussion we might do on this topic: 1. Why would it be weird giving only `req` and not `res`? You said you have weird feeling about it, which is valid, usually feeling is coming from somewhere, but I don't have weird feeling in this case, so I would love to understand some arguments if you can unpack it a bit. Btw term I saw being used for stuff like this is that it "smells" :) -> e.g. "this looks smelly to me". Also term "code smell" is often used. 2. We could maybe wrap `req` a bit into something, if we want to provide our own higher API I guess, although I am not sure if we want to guess right now what they need. 3. You said types shouldn't be an issue. What I was interested in is if we do the `route` thing where we describe the types in it "(int), (bool)", how would that play with what you are working on, which is typing the Operation payloads? Aren't we then repeating the type information?

Description: Tags are used in `ThoughtsPage.js`, `MainPage.js` and `TagsSidebar.js`.  They all have differing implementations.  `ThoughtsPage.js` tags are the most basic implementation. They are not clickable and are simply used to categorise Thoughts in the list view.  `MainPage.js` tags are a little more complex. They are clickable, when the user clicks on one it is removed from the list. It's styles are very similar to those in `ThoughtsPage.js`, except when it is hovered, it's styles change to indicate that clicking on it will delete it with `text-decoration: line-through;` and `cursor: pointer;`  `TagsSidebar.js` has a slightly different implementation. This renders a `Link` rather than a `div`. The styles are also a bit different for this one. The tag itself has padding instead of a margin and applies `text-decoration: none;` for some reason (not really sure why it's there, might be able to pull it out without causing any issues). On hover it becomes bold, and it can have a class of active to underline it.  The re-usable component we build will need to take into account all of the above in order to be used in all three cases. We can define a new CSS file for the Tag component to handle it's different configurations, this should include classes to handle: - Universal layout (margin/padding, try to figure something out that works for all three) - Universal `::before` in order to prefix `#` on `content` (might not work with `Link`s, will need to trial this) - Hover for deletion - Hover for bold - Active (underline it)  We can then pass props to the component to decide which of these classes should be applied.  We can define an `href` prop. If this is defined, it should be rendered as a `Link`, else, it should be rendered as a `div`.
Discuss Information: Great, sounds good, love the analysis! I commented in the PR, I believe that should address most of the things from here also.

Description: I discussed this a lot here: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/173#issuecomment-815238435https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/173#issuecomment-815238435 -> the main idea is that we could have Wasp manage the development database completely, so users don't have to think about it at all. This is all about the ease of usage and DX.
Discuss Information: This doesn't mean we have to manage it completely and hide details for them -> instead, it could mean that we give them docker-compose.yaml file that will run it for them, so they don't have to remember that docker command!  This is how @Fecony wrote a docker-compose for his Wasp app, for running postgre database: ```yaml version: '3.8' services:   db:     image: postgres:15.1-alpine     restart: always     environment:       - POSTGRES_USER=postgres       - POSTGRES_PASSWORD=postgres     ports:       - '5432:5432'     volumes:       - db:/var/lib/postgresql/data volumes:   db:     driver: local ```  It could be something else also, like .sh script, or a command by Wasp, or smth. But, ideally, we would make it simple to just run that database without them having to think about how to run it and if ENV vars are set correctly and so on. Notice I am talking about development now, not about production. It might also be interesting to offer something for production, like a complete docker-compose that spins up frontend, backend and database for you, but that is a bit different thing. Here, focus is on having the dev experience be as smooth with postgre database as it is with sqlite db. For me personally this would be a big thing, because the main "pain" when I need to maintain a side project that I wrote in Wasp is figuring out how to run the postgre database. Especially when I have multiple of wasp projects on disk. Part of it is that my env vars that I have locally might get lost because they are not in repo so I also have to figure those out. | So what I do for env vars for my projects is sticking them in `.env.*` files and git ignoring them if they are secret. You can include them in a docker compose with `env_file` https://github.com/cookwherever/cookwherever/blob/master/docker-compose.dev.yaml#L18. | An additional idea: I could take a "snapshot" of the database.
