Severity: bug, enhancement, no-haskell, f: database, f: deployment, prisma
Description: As stated [in Prisma docs](https://www.prisma.io/docs/concepts/components/prisma-migrate#production-and-testing-environments):   > `migrate deploy` should generally be part of an automated CI/CD pipeline, and we do not recommend running this command locally to deploy changes to a production database (for example, by temporarily changing the `DATABASE_URL` environment variable). It is not generally considered good practice to store the production database URL locally.  Right now we run `migrate deploy` inside the deployed docker container whenever the app is started, as a part of command that launches the app, which is good in the sense that it is not run locally, but it is bad because it runs too often (every time app starts instead of once when it is deployed) and also if `migrate deploy` fails we are left with the crashed app that doesn't run.  So Prisma recommends to make it a part of CI. Unless we are generating the CI files ourselves, the only way we can do this is by offering users a command that they can put into their CI. They can then also run this command locally if they want.  So, what we really need to do is let users run `migrate deploy` on their own, probably by having a command like `wasp db migrate-prod` or smth like that. They can then use it locally or in CI if they have it, as a step before pushing the docker container. They will however need to provide DATABASE_URL for a prod database. We don't want this in .env file since it is stored on their machine, that is dangerous. But, we can ask them to input it, or for example they can get it from Heroku with `heroku config:get DATABASE_URL -a <app_name>`, or they can provide it via env var directly in their command line. Once we do this, we should remove the `migrate deploy` from docker container and describe in docs they have to run it on their own.  One thing that I am worried about here is: what happens in the time between successful `migrate deploy` and successful deployment of actual server? What if deployment of server doesn't work or takes long time? We have app in the wrong state for some time. Can we roll-back easily? I am guessing there is no easy solution for this and we need a mechanism on the hosting provider side to handle this. Actually, looking at Heroku, they talk about this here https://devcenter.heroku.com/articles/release-phase -> it is specifically  meant for these purposes (db migrations). From reading this, it seems we should do `migrate deploy` between `heroku container:push` and `heroku container:release` -> we can just tell users to do it this way. They also have special mechanism on Heroku to do this completely with containers: you push a container that you want to use in `release' phase, and then you call upon it when you are calling `container:release` for whatever you are releasing. So in our case, we would prepare container (by adding target to dockerfile) that runs `prisma migrate deploy` and then users would have extra step between server container push and release, so all together it would be: ``` heroku container:push --app <myapp> web heroku container:push --app <myapp> release # 'release' can also be some other name? Not sure. heroku container:release --app <myapp> web release # This tells 'container:release' to use previously pushed 'release' container for its release step. ```  So, two obvious approaches seem to be a command to do it and container that does it, let's see what makes more sense.
Discuss Information: Two commands they added in Prisma 3.13.0 (https://github.com/prisma/prisma/releases/tag/3.13.0) are also interesting: - `migrate diff` - `db execute`  They are worth checking out while we are figuring out how to best deal with migrations! | I have a coworker that has drilled into me the value of forward-only migrations (no renaming, no dropped columns). By doing this, you can migrate before deployment, and you dont worry about the application going down. Once you deploy, your two apps can exist simultaneously without any problems. Over time, when you are confident the column is "dead", you can drop it, also without any problems.   This is a developer practice that is rarely seen, but it is kind of the only way to really do this safely.  | Oh that sounds great! Aha so basically you ensure that you have a database that supports both old/currentl release and new release, so you can publish new release without modifying the database at the same time, instead you first prepare the DB, all works, then you do the new release. Yup, that is perfect, I remember at last job we were also trying to do it that way! I am not sure if we can somehow enforce that in Wasp though hm, maybe it could be an advice instead.

Description: Right now, if you make changes that modify schema.prisma (right now that means modify or create new `Entity`, or modify or create `db` block), changes will not take effect until you run `wasp db migrate-dev`. This means that client that Prisma generates might be stale, or that migration might be missing. It can be easy to forget about this during development, and then you are working while you have no idea that Prisma client is not reflecting your changes or that database schema is not up to date!  While figuring out when to automatically run `wasp db migrate-dev` might be really hard / impossible (we can't really know when user is done making changes), what we could do is produce a warning that informs user that database and prisma client are out of the sync with the current source code, and that they can sync them with running `wasp db migrate-dev`.  The only source of information for Prisma is schema.prisma file, which Wasp generates as a part of the generated code. If generated schema.prisma changes, we can warn user that DB might be out of sync and that they can run `wasp db migrate-dev` to take care of this. To do this, we can track what was the checksum of schema.prisma the last time that `wasp db migrate-dev` was run -> we can persist this in a wasp specific helper file somewhere in the generated code, maybe in .waspinfo or smth similar. Each time code is compiled, we compare that checksum with current checksum. When `wasp db migrate-dev` is run, we update the persisted checksum to the current checksum.  The edge-case to take care of is when new app is just created and there are no entities yet. In that case we are generating schema.prisma but it has no models. I think we can say: if there are no entities, and there is no checksum persisted, don't produce warning (but do persist the current checksum). Otherwise, we produce warning as normal (e.g. if there are no entities but checksum is already persisted and it is different than current checksum, produce warning.).  In the codebase, since generator is pure, it might make sense to implement this as a IO action that runs after the code is generated -> a "check" step, that checks the generated code and reports warnings like this (and reads and writes .waspinfo and similar files).
Discuss Information: @turbodami and I are using this as a task to solve during pair programming.  In this first, relatively short session, we were investigating at which depth to locate the logic that will be dealing with schema.prisma file. We started at cli/Main.hs and ended up in src/Lib.hs and stopped there for now. Plan is to go one step deeper, into the Generator, to get the full picture, and then decide where the logic should go, and we will continue from there in the next session. | During the second pair programming session @Martinsos and I defined two key operations for this:  1. generating checksum: when "wasp db migrate-dev" is called, read schema.prisma and generate checksum and write to disk; 2. comparing checksums: when recompile function is called, read last checksum from disk and compare it with the new one.  For 1 we could write a function writePrismaSchemaChecksumToFile inside DbGenerator to be called either by Migrate.hs or DbGenerator/Operations.migrateDev.  For now we decided to go for the second one.  For 2 we could write a function comparePrismaSchemaChecksums inside DbGenerator to be called either by Command/Watch.hs or Generator.writeWebAppCode.  We decided to go for the second one, which implies to write a new generic datatype for describing generator errors/warnings (which we currently don't have in Wasp) and modify bottom up the types returned from IO to new datatype to reflect that. | We stopped at implementation of function that calculates schema checkshum, in DbGenerator.hs . | Implemented calcFileChecksum. | Implemented checkIfPrismaSchemaIsSyncedWithTheDb inside Generator.hs. Next step is implementing readPrismaSchemaChecksumFromFile in DbGenerator.hs. | @Martinsos what are your thoughts on a quicker solution here that simply copies/backs up the `schema.prisma` file during a `wasp db migrate` (as, say, `schema.prisma-last-migrate` under the same .wasp/out/db dir) and the check to see if we need to run `wasp db migrate` on `wasp start` is to simply diff the current `schema.prisma` file against the `schema.prisma-last-migrate` (if it exists)? I'm on the fence if it is elegant or ugly given how minimally invasive it is lol  The future enhancement could be to then make use of the checksum file from https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/209  We could also wait until that is done for this one, but if we went checksum here now, I think it would largely be thrown out after that merge. | Well I think it is very similar actually -> you will either store the checksum of schema.prisma in some schema.prisma.checksum file, or you will store the whole schema.prisma in schema.prisma-last-migrate . The difference is merely having a short function that calculates checksum.  Performance/elegance wise, I like the checksum idea better, but that is also not crucial at the moment.  Reusing the checksum file -> I actually think you won't be able to reuse that file, the one where we store the checksums of generated files. Checksum for schema.prisma is captured when we call `wasp migrate dev`, which is not the same moment as when compilation happens -> so checksum of schema.prisma in "checksums" file is not what we need here. Let me know if I got this wrong, but it makes sense to me at the moment. So the only thing you will be able to reuse is checksum function, which you can just copy right now from other PR and probably won't have any or just little conflicts. | @shayneczyzewski I forgot to mention your username so doing it now -> check above reply! | Thanks for the reply, @Martinsos! Yeah, you are correct in that they are structurally very similar processes, and only the contents of the prisma backup/checksum file would differ for that first part. I was thinking (maybe incorrectly, hoping :D) I could _still_ leverage the future checksums file for the comparison part, however, since anything that uses this is going to be downstream of a recent compile (like `wasp start`). So in that world we just recently did a genPrismaSchema, wrote the (potentially updated) updated checksum, and can theoretically diff the checksum file value with the existing schema.prisma.checksum file (that was updated on the last migrate). But perhaps it is best to not even assume that compile precondition and any freshness checks are made on demand by computing the checksum of the schema.prisma as it stands at the moment it is needed and not within any checksum file.  I'm good pulling down the checksum function and any other helpful tidbits early to go a checksum route vs file diff route now �� will be a fairly quick update to my current POC, thanks for sanity checking me! | > Thanks for the reply, @Martinsos! Aha, I see what you mean! Instead of calculating checksum for prisma file separately, you would read it from the checksum file.  But yeah, maybe it is better not to mix those two hm, to avoid having that dependency. Although, this would save us from recalculating prisma.schema checksum. One additional question hm -> let's say that I run `wasp migrate dev`, migration gets, prisma.schema checksum is stored on disk in prisma.schema.checksum in generated project dir. Now, you check out the project on your machine by pulling my git branch. Or, maybe I delete the generated project. In any case, that checksum file gets lost. On compile, it won't get created, since it gets created only on `wasp db migrate`. So what will happen? And what should happen, hm? I believe we will, with current approach, inform user they need to run `wasp migrate dev` -> they will run it, which will result in no op from the prisma side since migrations are already up to date, and prisma.schema.checksum will be created. Description: Right now we print some messages like "Wasp generated code" or "Db operations succeeded" and similar, and there is also a lot of messages coming from client, server, db.  Sometimes, when a lot of output is produced, it can be really hard to figure out if everything together succeeded or not! That seems to be especially true for the `wasp db` commands, where output from Prisma can be confusing.  We should look into making it more obvious when operation is success or not.  Ideas; - Use progress bars / numbers / percentages. - Use emojis, specifically green checkmark! It is very visible and gives clear feedback. Also, red Xs for failure. - Clearly delineate subprocesses / steps. E.g. if we have "wasp is compiling ..." and then some output and then "wasp compiled successfully", it would be great if he can somehow connect the two messages, either by indentation, number, color, or smth else. Somehow mark it as [start] ... [end], as a whole.

Description: Right now, if you delete `migrations/` dir from the Wasp project root dir and run `wasp start` or `wasp build`, any previously generated `migrations/` dir will remain in the generated code -> it will not get removed! Instead, we want it to get removed. We should do it the same way we did this for .env file.

The whole "IO shell, pure core" approach. However, you will inevitably have some part of the code left in the IO, and then the questions comes how to test that. In javascript, you would just monkey-patch and therefore mock the functions that are doing IO, and that way do the tests. In Haskell you can't do that (at least not in a nice way), so what people do instead is abstract away the IO / side effects. I believe there are different ways to do that, and you can see us using one approach to mock and test the Writeable typeclass (that is the reason why `write` doesn't have IO in it, but instead has `WriteableMonad`, which then in real code we replace with IO but in tests we replace it with the state monad, so we can simulate the side effects and count function calls and similar). I believe in the mean time there have been some libraries created that help with this, basically they automatically generate this boilerplate for you -> but I have to admit I haven't looked into that enough. Description: Wasp compiles source and generates code in outDir. But, it does not clear the ourDir first -> instead it just generates the code over whatever is already there, meaning there might be some left-over files that shouldn't exist any more. This wasn't a problem so far, since redundant files were just not used by the rest of the code. However, it starting being a problem with .env file, because if you remove .env from Wasp project, the old one remains in the generated project, and env vars from it area loaded while they shouldn't be.  For now we did a quick fix -> we just delete .env file from the generated directory before we generate new code.  Advanced approach would be to, before actually writing file drafts to disk, generate a list of files that are to be created together with their checksums. Then, we can use that list to compare it to the previous list (that we generated the last time we generated code and we persisted it in helper file next to the generated code) and we can do fancy stuff like: - Delete all the files that were on the previous list but are not any more. - Not write files that have not changed (that have the same checksum as before), to avoid unnecessary writes to disk.  This means that we need a way to generate this list before actually writing the file drafts to the disk. I think the best way to do this is to add additional method to Writeable, that is somewhat of a "dry run" method -> it does not write anything to disk, instead it tells us path of where it wants to write + the checksum of the file that it would write. It would probably need to do some IO operations like reading for this, but it will not write anything. This can then be used to construct the list, and then actual `write` method can be used to finally execute the drafts that need to be executed (many of them will not be needed to be executed because they will not change).  Once we do this, we can probably remove `preCleanup` method from the ServerGenerator.hs.

Just a bit more info on error we got, possibly reiterating some what you wrote Shayne but shouldn't hurt:  So with lazy IO, if you do content <- openFile, it basically opens files on the disk and then leaves it open until Haskell actually consumes the whole content  String. So for example, if you do head content after that, that will consume only a bit of file, just one character, and file remains open on disk. And this is cool, but for example if in the meantime you try to open it again, then you get problems because it is already open! One solution is to just use strict IO -> there are strict versions of these functions, so we can be sure in that case that content <- openFile actually opens, reads and closes the file as part of that one IO action. But, then we use the possible cool effects of automatic "streaming". So, we could instead try to think deeper into why is laziness causing us the problems (is it because we try to open same files multiple times? once with write and once with dryRun?) -> maybe we can think through it and figure out how to make it work in lazy way. But, if that proves too complicated we can always go the strict route. | In this Holiday Hippo � �  version of pair programming with @Martinsos :D we successfully completed phase 1! �  This is the step of calculating the checksums for all `FileDraft`s to be written and persisting this information as JSON file on disk stored in the file: `.wasp/out/.waspchecksums`  Coming into today, we briefly glanced at the error thinking we hit a lazy IO issue due to the reference to `hContents`, but thankfully Martin suggested we check the error one more time and he rightfully identified the actual issue: we were reading the input file incorrectly! We updated this and added a `checksumFromByteString` helper function. The trick to put it in an ASCII friendly hex version of the string was a StackOverflow win:  ```haskell bytestringToHex :: B.ByteString -> Hex bytestringToHex = Hex . concatMap (printf "%02x") . B.unpack ```  This just uses `printf` to format the raw bytes as 0-padded, 2-digit hex. We also added a test. NOOOOO  ![giphy (1)](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/523636/151414808-8fe70d93-f0fd-40e9-87c5-5875b2847012.gif)  To be fair, we did know ahead of time we would have to treat Files and Directories differently at some point, but confirmed we are ok keeping a directory notion included as a `FileDraft` and handle the differences here (in a more limited scope).  > Aside: The reason we added `CopyDirFileDraft` to begin with was so the Generator could manage the migrations directory. The idea of copying directories will be used in other instances in the future I'm sure, and we took the if you squint hard enough like Linux does, "everything is a file" approach.  So we had to start by handling both cases in `Writeable` somehow. To encode it in the type system , we added a new type called:  ```haskell type FileOrDirPathRelativeTo a = Either (Path' (Rel a) File') (Path' (Rel a) Dir') ```  and used `FileOrDirPathRelativeTo ProjectRootDir` in the return type for both `dryRun` and `getDstPath` instead of `File'`. From there, most of the updates to the other `FileDraft` types were easy (just adding `Left $`), but the updates to Generator took the rest of our time.  In that file, we now had to care if we were dealing with Files or Directories, as well as add more info when writing the checksum file so we knew what we were reading back in. Instead of just a `FilePath` (serialized as a `String` by Aeson), we now write a tuple which tells us if the FP is a `"file"` or a `"dir"`. Sample:  ``` [     [         "aa1bd54732b015158d24c55e03bc79b2f211109bc2b0478d6f2ad6b52d15847f",         [             "file",             "web-app/README.md"         ]     ],     [         "957e98c79e0d021dd3a21f2bce313bc58c164723d78172918577a032f8764bf5",         [             "file",             "web-app/package.json"         ]     ], ... ```  We are now in a position to do next week what we wanted to do today, implement `dryRun` for `CopyDirFileDraft` :D c'est la vie, but still good progress and we are compiling again! | Today, we spent about half our allotted time talking about a PR. However, we did have time to finalize `dryRun` for `CopyDirFileDraft` as planned. The approach we took is to get all the files in the directory (recursively), calculate their individual file checksums, and then calculate the checksum of those checksums in aggregate.  We then spent a bit of time refactoring that definition from: ```haskell dryRun :: w -> IO (Checksum, FileOrDirPathRelativeTo ProjectRootDir) ```  to: ```haskell getChecksum :: w -> IO Checksum ```  since we now have `getDstPath` available to us. The plan for next week is to fix all the places we just broke. � However, we are getting closer, and it is getting better each session. �  | FYI squashed and rebased master, so you will need to reset hard to origin before next time ��  | Today we got things compiling again by replacing uses of `dryRun` with `getChecksum`. All of this happened in Generator.hs. We also did some refactoring to simply a few functions based on this, and some explanations in comments (after we re-remembered the rationale from the `TODO: better explain rationale` � )  One important milestone is we can remove all `preCleanup` since they were trying to remove stale artifacts from FileDrafts that we now will automatically remove when the FileDrafts to write and last compile checksum don't align.  Another neat things is, it appears to work on some early testing I just did! � After a full compile to create `.waspchecksums`, here is what `fswatch` says are files and directories that are modified during re-compile now (with no changes):  ``` /Users/shayne/dev/wasp/waspc/examples/todoApp/.wasp/out/.waspchecksums /Users/shayne/dev/wasp/waspc/examples/todoApp/.wasp/out/server/src/.copyFile85627-0.tmp /Users/shayne/dev/wasp/waspc/examples/todoApp/.wasp/out/server/src/server.js /Users/shayne/dev/wasp/waspc/examples/todoApp/.wasp/out/server/src/server.js /Users/shayne/dev/wasp/waspc/examples/todoApp/.wasp/out/.waspinfo /Users/shayne/dev/wasp/waspc/examples/todoApp/.wasp/out/.waspchecksums ```  Ok, cool. And what if I change an Entity's column type? I get all the above plus this  one extra change on re-compile:  ``` /Users/shayne/dev/wasp/waspc/examples/todoApp/.wasp/out/db/schema.prisma ```  Changing the route `to:` has the original lines plus this on re-compile: ``` /Users/shayne/dev/wasp/waspc/examples/todoApp/.wasp/out/web-app/src/router.js ```  If I remove the ext/Main.css file and recompile, it has the original lines plus: ``` /Users/shayne/dev/wasp/waspc/examples/todoApp/.wasp/out/web-app/src/ext-src/Main.css /Users/shayne/dev/wasp/waspc/examples/todoApp/.wasp/out/server/src/ext-src/Main.css ```  Showing they are removed. And so on. �  | Nice @shayneczyzewski and using `fswatch` sounds fun!  So when you change the route `to:`, only one file is generated/changed, right? | > Nice @shayneczyzewski and using `fswatch` sounds fun! >  > So when you change the route `to:`, only one file is generated/changed, right?  Thanks!! It was fun to try it :D  Sorta... I elided the others that change during _every_ compilation though, and only highlighted the deltas in the subsequent examples. So here is the full output:  ``` /Users/shayne/dev/wasp/waspc/examples/todoApp/.wasp/out/.waspchecksums /Users/shayne/dev/wasp/waspc/examples/todoApp/.wasp/out/web-app/src/router.js � /Users/shayne/dev/wasp/waspc/examples/todoApp/.wasp/out/server/src/.copyFile16894-0.tmp /Users/shayne/dev/wasp/waspc/examples/todoApp/.wasp/out/server/src/server.js /Users/shayne/dev/wasp/waspc/examples/todoApp/.wasp/out/server/src/server.js /Users/shayne/dev/wasp/waspc/examples/todoApp/.wasp/out/.waspinfo /Users/shayne/dev/wasp/waspc/examples/todoApp/.wasp/out/.waspchecksums ```  And I changed it to a`Page` that already existed. Seems like checksum file shows twice for deletion and addition, waspinfo is updated, and server.js is probably removed/added. | Today we pulled out all the checksum logic (incl the writing/deleting of files) into its own module (`Wasp.Generator.WriteFileDrafts`) and refactored some nested pure logic to make it more testable. We started implementing some tests. �  �  | Spent today writing more tests for `Wasp.Generator.WriteFileDrafts`! Got to use infinite lists and some core vanilla Haskell/FP �  | > Spent today writing more tests for `Wasp.Generator.WriteFileDrafts`! Description: It seems that in certain situations when Wasp crashes, nodejs server remains running on port 3000. The next time wasp is run, that causes a problem since port 3000 is already taken and it can't start a new nodejs server on that same port.  Ideal solution would be to never allow nodejs process to remain running once wasp process ends. I am not sure if we even ensure that though, due to Wasp CLI possibly not having the time to clean up if it is being killed immediately. Maybe there is a way to spawn nodejs process in such way that it dies automatically once Wasp process is dead? Is that supported by the Linux/OSX? Or maybe we can do this via Docker (I think they have an option to kill instance once parent process is dead or smth like that), but that means we switch to docker in development.  Another option might be to check for any left-over wasp processes once wasp is started again and clean them up first. This however means we have to be careful not to delete something that is not a Wasp process -> so we can't just delete the process that is occupying port 3000, we need to check that it is actually a wasp process. Also, this means the "runaway" process will be deleted only once you run wasp again -> if you don't, it might remain running and consuming resources, which is not cool.  One spin on that might be that we don't let Wasp kill that processes, instead we print useful error message telling user to kill them + exact commands do that.  So, while first suggestion is certainly the best, I am not sure if it is possible - we should investigate what can be done and what not and then pick an approach.
Discuss Information: Found a workaround for this while hacking on Thoughts on Ubuntu, simply run the following and then run Wasp again.  ``` fuser -k 3000/tcp fuser -k 3001/tcp ``` | Thanks for sharing @cursorial , that should help!  So you encountered the problem? We introduced some changes that we hoped will make it so that processes are always cleaned up, but if you encountered it then this is now always true and we still have this problem.  Do you maybe know which error brought you into this situation? On which OS? | I can confirm this is unfortunately still happening. Very rarely, but I had it happen again after couple of month of not happening. I believe there is a rare case when certain factors come together and cause this. | I found a way to replicate it. When global authentication is not specified (meaning there is no `auth` field in `app`, specify `auth` field for any of the operations. Set it to true or false, doesn't matter. Run `wasp start` and you will get an error that `auth` can't be specified for specific operation if it is not enabled for the whole app. This is expected, and `wasp start` will crash, which is also expected. However, if you remove that `auth` and run `wasp start` again, you will get `already running on port 3000` error -> this is not expected and is a problem. | Good way to go about this would be to check if something is running on port 3000 or 3001, and if so, ask user, interactively, if it is ok to kill those processes. Even better would be that we don't leave any processes running when `wasp start` crashes but we haven't managed to do that yet hm. | While chasing down a different issue, I noticed this problem was likely caused by how we terminate the spawned Node jobs. It was addressed here: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/524 by terminating the _entire process group_, as the Node process spawned a child and grandchild process. No reoccurrences so far �� ��  | This seems to have happened again: https://discord.com/channels/686873244791210014/1250905353256960084/1250926580813791232 .  > When I stop the wasp process developing locally(opensaas), using ctrl-c in iterm, the process keeps running in the background.  > Mac OS 13, iterm, wasp 0.13.2, it it killed the process, but released the console before it had maybe?  but then previously I had several spun up that had been running in the background that I had to manually kill.

Description: Right now, if JWT token verification fails, error 500 is sent. Verification normally fails because either JWT_SECRET changed or token has expired. This is not right -> it should sent 401. Also, client should respond to this by deleting/forgetting the JWT token, therefore forcing client to perform login.  So, to fix this, we should: 1. Throw 401 when JWT token verification fails. Right now this happens at waspc/data/Generator/templates/server/core/auth.js, line 31 -> `const userIdFromToken = (await verify(token)).id`. `verify` function here throws an error. We should catch it and return 401. 2. On the client, we should delete token if error was 401. I am not sure what is the best way to handle that -> probably by adding an axios response interceptor in waspc/data/Generator/templates/react-app/src/api.js .
Discuss Information: Hi @Martinsos I would like to work on this issue! | Hi @jpcarroll , sounds great :)! I see that your background is mostly web development which is great, and probably no Haskell, which should be also fine since this issue should not require any Haskell code.  To help you get started, I suggest you go through the https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/tree/master/waspc#first-time-contributor-checklist, most important being https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/tree/master/waspc#basics -> you should make it as far as running an example TodoApp app, which is in `waspc/examples/todoApp`.  Hardest part might be correctly installing stack, especially if you are on Windows -> let me know if you get stuck somewhere and I can try to help.  Once you are able to run TodoApp example app, I suggest developing this PR in a following manner: `wasp compile` while in TodoApp will compile Wasp code into TodoApp/.wasp/out. Then go to .wasp/out/server and run `npm start` to start running the server. Go into .wasp/out/web-app and run `npm start` to start running the web app. Then you can go into .wasp/out and directly modify the code there, until you get it behaving as it should. So for this PR, you will probably want to modify .wasp/out/server/core/auth.js and .wasp/out/react-app/src/api.js . You can also add new files, whatever is needed. Once you are done, we will want to take those changes back into Wasp code, which most likely means into Wasp templates -> those are under waspc/data/Generator/templates, and based on them is created generated code that you were modifying above. For this PR, I think it should be enough to just modify templates and not write any Haskell code, but if there will be some Haskell code to write, we can help with that. Finally, once you feel like you updated the templates correctly, run `wasp compile` again and it will overwrite code in .wasp/out with the freshly generated code -> you will possibly want to store the changes you did manually so they are not lost at this moment in case templates are not completely correct and you want to look into your manual changes for guidance. Now, run `wasp start` and if you succeeded, app will behave as it should!  Now that I am writing this it seems relatively complicated, which is because it is hehe :D, but once you get into it you will realize it is nothing super smart. Let me know if anything is confusing and I will be happy to help, either here or on our Discord: https://discord.com/invite/rzdnErX . | @Martinsos I have pushed up changes and created a PR for this issue [here](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/177).  I ran through the steps above which all succeeded and the app behaved as it should! I also ran the testing suite using `stack test` which passed all tests. Would there be tests I need to write/update for my change - I couldn't find any tests pertaining to the bug I was fixing? | Awesome! We unfortunately don't yet have tests for generated code (shame shame!!) -> I wrote more about it in the PR :). | Fixed by @jpcarroll !

Description: Right now, telemetry in Wasp sends usage information and that is it. Modeling by what Prisma.io does, we want to do more -> we want telemetry in Wasp to also check for any updates/messages. Since telemetry is sending POST HTTPS request, this can be easily done by returning any messages as the response to that request. Those messages could be notification about new version of Wasp, or a security notice. Prisma.io calls this sytem "checkpoint", and they actually took the idea from Hashicorp.
Discuss Information: This can be useful when we know there is an issue with current version of Wasp (e.g. when react-query released a broken release, or if there is a security incident), as we can then notify the users about this, so they don't spend time on trying to fix it on their own (or not knowing about it at all).

Severity: documentation, good first issue, refactoring, hacktoberfest
Description: Right now, if you call signup(), you will not be logged in, you still have to call login(). This has to be made clear in the documentation, or we should make it automatic to be logged in after doing signup().
Discuss Information: Hello, I'd like to work on this issue.  ### My Plan - [x] understand how `signup` and `login` currently work - [x] determine (w/ the help of wasp devs) if we want to update documentation or refactor - [x] implement  - [ ] review/iterate | Hello @Martinsos, :wave: I wanted to check with you if I should update the documentation for `signup()` to resolve this issue or make it automatic to be logged in after `signup()`.  Thanks!  | Hey @ussgarci I can help you brainstorm and answer questions here. The updating of docs is a pretty safe fallback, but if you wanted to first automatically try to log in the user after signup first that could be fun too. :D I'd recommend trying the latter first if you are up for it, but no worries if you'd prefer just to do the docs!  If you have questions about how they currently work after playing with an example Wasp app and reviewing the code let me know! ��  | Hi @shayneczyzewski , sounds good.  I'll start working on making it automatic to be logged in after `signup()`.  Thanks! | Hey @ussgarci! Thanks for making the PR on this one. I think you found the correct spot to add that `login` function call in `signup`. Nice!  However, after thinking about this issue a bit more, I was starting to wonder if perhaps overloading `signup` in this way is perhaps _not_ desirable (or at least, should be configurable). I can imagine in the future a dev may want to add some sort of email confirmation gate to the signup process, etc. and that may make this a non-starter for them. Also, the name `signup` to me does not really imply "and also login". :) So I have a proposal (curious about your thoughts @Martinsos too, since you created this one): - we either just update the docs to state signup does not also login, or - we make it configurable  In practice, we already log them in after signup in the form component anyways: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/main/waspc/data/Generator/templates/react-app/src/auth/forms/Signup.js#L19  I surveyed a few other auth APIs and didn't get the idea that doing both automatically was the most common, but could be wrong. Thoughts by all on the above? Thanks! | You make a good point about this behavior potentially excluding some users who might have particular use cases for `signup` and `login` behavior.  I do like the idea of making it configurable with a default parameter,  something like `signup(userFields, autoLogin=false)` that way the side effect of also logging in the user would have to be explicitly stated.  That said, part of me thinks that the responsibilities of signing up a user and logging in a user should stay separated.  I have no problem with just updating the docs if that is what you guys decide.  Thank you for the feedback!    | Hi guys, discussing whether this behavior should be configurable is definitely interesting (I also think it should).  But as far as I can tell, we already support login after signup by default, but on the [client](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/4020295740989b3a3b717fac9ebfc807ce07d47a/waspc/data/Generator/templates/react-app/src/auth/forms/Signup.js#L19).  We can discuss whether we should move the support to the endpoint (i.e., have `/signup` log you in, as @ussgarci did in their PR). If yes, we can merge #750, but not without first removing the login API call from the client. Merging the current changes would make us call login twice (once from the signup action, the other time as a separate API call). | I think at this point I'd probably just vote for a doc update saying `signup` does not `login` and keep them totally separate. I'm not sure that adding a config arg gives us much more help beyond just making the additional separate call if a user needed to. Sounds like @ussgarci is in the same camp. That sound ok to you @sodic and @Martinsos? Thanks all! | > I think at this point I'd probably just vote for a doc update saying `signup` does not `login` and keep them totally separate. I'm not sure that adding a config arg gives us much more help beyond just making the additional separate call if a user needed to. Sounds like @ussgarci is in the same camp. That sound ok to you @sodic and @Martinsos? Thanks all!  Yup, I think you all together analyzed it really well, and agree with the latest conclusions, which is let's keep them separate then, and just add the docs that clarify the situation! | Sure, and just to emphasize one more time: We are already logging users in after they sign up, but this is done [on the client](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/4020295740989b3a3b717fac9ebfc807ce07d47a/waspc/data/Generator/templates/react-app/src/auth/forms/Signup.js#L19). | > Sure, and just to emphasize one more time: We are already logging users in after they sign up, but this is done [on the client](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/blob/4020295740989b3a3b717fac9ebfc807ce07d47a/waspc/data/Generator/templates/react-app/src/auth/forms/Signup.js#L19).  Yep! ��  | Hey everyone :wave: as per our discussion I've opened a new PR to update the documentation to clarify the behavior of `signup`.  I also closed my previous PR.  Thanks for all the input!  :+1:

Description: Right now, it is not possible to change user's password. Wasp developer does not access to any API that would support it. We need to add support for this, e.g. by creating a special function for that. We should also make sure dev cannot easily overwrite the existing password.
Discuss Information: Done by @matijaSos !

Description: Right now we are generating codebase in Javascript. It might make more sense to generate it in TypeScript instead, to get extra type safety. On the other hand, Javascript might be easier to work with and make adoption of Wasp simpler.  To make introduction of TypeScript closer to becoming a reality, I think best first step is figuring out exactly how much work is this and what does it mean for the end user.  Some questions I have right now (my experience with TypeScript is very basic): - Let's say we generate codebase in Typescript. Would users still be able to write React components and Queries/Actions in Javascript? I guess they would, since JS is subset of TS from what I remember reading some time ago? But could they do this without even knowing that the generated code is TS, or would they need to care about that? TLDR: If we switch to TS from JS, and there is user who is using Wasp via JS, will the change affect them? - How much work would it be to do this change? From what I remember, migration to TS can be done gradually, right? So we can introduce TS compiler, all will work with existing JS, and then we can just modify part by part when we have time? - Could this affect people to not want to use Wasp? Might somebody say -> hey, I don't want it to eject TS code, that is not for me, I use JS? I am sure that could be a problem for some, same as for some others problem will be that ejects code is JS and not TS. But what is better then, JS or TS? Hard to say I think. Maybe TS is better. It certainly is for larger projects.
Discuss Information: - You can totally have a codebase with mixed JS/TS, it doesn't prevent you from doing anything in TS or JS. Users that would want to stick with JS or integrate generated TS files in their manual JS files could do it. The really interesting thing here is even if they decide to stick with JS, they would benefit from IDE autocompletion thanks to TS, even in their JS files. - You would have multiple solutions :    - Rewrite everything now to generate only TS, since the project is very early stage, it's kind of still realistic.   - Migrate core features one-by-one to TS, could be a good solution, also it would allow to rethink the structure gradually, not all at once.   - Just generate .d.ts files on top of your JS generated code. Some libraries do that, but more because they were started more than 4 years ago and TS wasn't a clear pick at the time. I won't recommend it for a project starting in 2020 since plain TS will really help you throughout the development process of this framework. - TS, hands down, it's better in anyway since one could use the generated TS functions and classes in their JS code, the opposite is not true. Also think of a CTO or an intermediate/experienced dev thinking about using your framework, nowadays, not having TS, at least typings, in a framework or lib is a red flag, I could name a plethora of framework I use everyday, from small to massive : Luxon, Amplify SDK, Apollo, Redux, Recoil, React... All major companies now provide nice typing for theirs frameworks. I can also see that every "cool" new lib/framework is rarely in pure JS now. I'm not a huge fan of the hype argument, but this time the hype meets a very very very good pattern (static typing), so it would be a shame not to follow it.  Also consider that the generated code could be better with TS types, because it would be more structured. Just look at what graphql-codegen generates from GraphQL queries to TS React hooks, it's very readable for generated code. https://graphql-code-generator.com/ | Migration from JS to TS isn't going to take too much time at any scale. The sooner it's done, the better.  Any larger company using JS instead of TS has a lot of problems due to lack of good maintainability.  | Thanks @lucasbordeau and @bashovski , this sounds convincing, I am in :D! But seriously, that makes complete sense and I do agree we should do the complete migration relatively soon. I think it is not the top priority, since right now it is more important for us to make the current version of Wasp more full/rounded/complete, but after that (in a month or two?) it would be good to take a little bit of time to do a rewrite to TS.  I will leave this issue open and we can continue from here when the time comes! | I would use this as an opportunity to build a flexible system from the start. Don't get me wrong, I use TypeScript for everything but having the option to generate a codebase that is vanilla has its merits, especially when working with smaller web apps with very limited data types. In those cases you basically use no perks of Typescript at all. To pick the output type would be something quite powerful to behold.  Then perhaps one day the system could be accessible and flexible enough so that it could be compiling out nextjs, rails, laravel, kotlin, go or whatever other language or framework the community could imagine. | @krazyjakee that is exactly our "big vision" :)! What we really want to do is make Wasp in big part agnostic of the underlying technology, so that you can use React, or Vue, or Nodejs, or go, or any of the supported languages, and it will integrate with those and generate codebase in them. We are already keeping this in mind while designing and implementing Wasp. However, there is so much work even for just one stack, that we decided to focus on a specific one for some time, and once we get that stable and running, we will be looking into expanding into other stacks. For now that is React/Node/Prisma, all on JS. Main question for now is: if it had to be on just one stack, because it will be for some time, what is the best one, to drive adoption and help Wasp grow? We thing React/Node/Prisma is a good combo, but JS vs TS -> it is hard to say. We thought JS is better start for the simplicity, but static typing that TS gives is really nice and we heard multiple people asking if we support TS. Plus, people can still write JS even if we generate project as TS. So decision is slightly tricky here. I think we will be smarter when more people start using Wasp, we should have more information then. | not sure if it belongs here, but trpc's types are insane. typescript's types being turing complete (and the lack of principled design patterns for writing types) have put so much work on the IDE to communicate the contract of a type. The problem is that the types _are not in code_, they are generated dynamically which requires too much thought for someone to reason about.   Also, a note on graphql. I am still in the process of figuring out if graphql should be a technology that is exposed to the frontend, but my gut is currently tell me no. I have built a number of sites with the frontend querying graphql and there are a lot of things that it does really well, but I think it fails for a similar problem as with trpc; the types are too complicated.   I haven't built with RPC in a while, but I think the frontend and backend communicating with an RPC (with simple types) is correct. The backend can do whatever crazy data retrieval it needs to, but the frontend should only ever see the data that it was intended to access.   I believe that intentionally hiding data you can access on the frontend (and exposing simpler, more "agreed upon" types) is the best way to create a more future-proof application. This is why I am such a big fan of grpc. They have absolutely nailed the simplicity of a type system you interface with. Where grpc currently fails is a sane build toolchain, and hence why it is not used outside of big tech.   So, 1) I don't like trpc, the types are insanely complicated, and partially defeat the purpose of having types in code in the first place 2)  graphql is really useful, but not always. it will get you going faster than an RPC, but in the long term might lead to confusing code since you can do _everything_ and people will do _everything_ 3) grpc has an ok type system, simple, but expressive types.  | @breadchris oh interesting, thanks for this! I have to try them in more details, but this certainly motivates me to check grpc in more details.  So just to conclude, you think the grpc is the best solution, but it is hard to set up. That means that it might be a good choice for Wasp then, since Wasp can nicely set it up for people and they don't have to worry about that? | I'm closing this since most of our codebase is already written in TypeScript, and we're using the TypeScript compiler for all parts of Wasp (server, web app, SDK).  We still have parts of our framework written in JavaScript (our `tsconfig.json` files have `"allowJs": true`), but that' covered by different issues. For example:  - #2010   - #1938

Description: We need to make sure that web apps we are generating are holding to the highest security standards.  For example, right now we are using https://www.npmjs.com/package/secure-password, which while it does seem legit, is not well documented. Do we trust the maintainer enough to maintain the integrity of the package? Are we sure what are the guarantees of this package and that it will hold to them? Are enough people using it / is it getting enough attention to somewhat reduce the risks mentioned before? Do we understand how secure is the method that this package is using? We should go through all these questions and if we can't find a satisfying answer, look for another solution.  There are also many other things we should care about -> we should make a list at some point.  This is "epic" issue, meaning it is an umbrella issue over a bunch of smaller related issues.
Discuss Information: An alternative might be the widely used [bcrypt](https://www.npmjs.com/package/bcryptjs)

Description: Big part of Wasp is generating a web app that follows best practices, e.g regarding security, performance, code quality, design, ... -> and one part of this is also accessibility.  We should identify all the places in Wasp where we can ensure high standards of accessibility. What immediately comes to my mind are forms (which Wasp does not generate yet probably will soon) -> we would want to ensure those are implemented with care for accessibility. Then comes general website metadata, then semantically correct usage of html, ... .  We should make a checklist of all the stuff that we need to care about, and also occassionaly do an "inspection" to ensure we are not missing anything.  We could check in Wasp, while compiling, if there are any known accessibility issues (e.g. app title is missing) and throw warnings to the developer, warning them if something is missing and similar -> this is somewhat advanced, but it would be cool.  This is an "epic" issue, meaning it serves as an umbrella issue for smaller issues related to this topic.

Right now, Wasp is installed via installer script. There is no special support for upgrading Wasp, you just run installer script and it installs the latest version.  If something is already installed on the locations where Wasp wants to install, with the same name as Wasp, or if there is previous installation of Wasp, Wasp will warn user to delete whatever is there to continue. Those warning are currently coming one after another, meaning that installation fails, user deletes stuff, and then it fails again with another "delete that" message, and user has to delete stuff again -> it would be nicer if instead he was told at once: these locations needs to be deleted. It can also be confusing because locations have simlar paths so he could think it is same message again which means he did smth wrong but he didn't.  Further, we should probably look into upgrading being a part of the Wasp itself, done with smth like `wasp upgrade`.  TLDR: Instead of reporting "delete this location because there is smth already there" errors one by one, installer script should report all of them (2 for now) at once.
Discuss Information: Done (reporting all errors at once).

Description: Right now, if we are doing sign up (via createNewUser) and email already exists, 500 is returned, which is internal server error. Instead, we should return more descriptive message, and it certainly shouldn't be 500. I am not sure what is the best practice, is it ok security-wise to say that email is already used up, so we should figure that out.  We should check out the generated code for createNewUser and make sure to handle Prisma errors there and throw appropriate HttpErrors.

Severity: bug, help wanted, hmm, no-haskell, security
Description: Not really a bug, but a deficiency that we are aware of and want to improve.  In the node server that Wasp is generating, we currently just set CORS with `app.use(cors())`, which allows all CORS requests. This is not restrictive enough, it should allow only requests from the domain of the client I believe. Should we also configure something else? I am not actually what it takes to say that CORS is well configured, that is the first thing that we should investigate, and then we can make a plan for implementing that.  If you are reading this and have a good idea of how CORS should be configured, do share the thoughts!
Discuss Information: @Martinsos I've thought about the best possible implementation for CORS. So I came up with a couple of ideas: - Adding direct support for other environment variables (not just PORT, DATABASE_URL, JWT_SECRET), maybe I am wrong, but I didn't find an option to add my own environment variables that are accessible by the app. - Adding support for AWS SSM/Hashicorp's Vault  Now, you might ask yourself, why?  - The best way to store allowed origins is to specify them in a config file that could be easily changed (so maybe a .env file at very least) - They could be stored in JSON or YAML files as well: could be easily parsed, acceptable format: ```json {   "allowed_origins": [     "https://test-origin.com"   ] } ```  App could access those environment variables and configure CORS options according to the allowed origins array. Wasp could additionally allow the setting of other CORS options if needed. | @bashovski Awesome, that's a good analysis!  1. So right now, in generated Wasp code, we look for those ENV vars that you listed. We could add more as needed, for example we can add CORS_ORIGINS, or CORS_OPTIONS or smth like that. That is sometihng we would define in Wasp, since CORS part is also managed by Wasp. 2. AWS SSM / Hashicorp's Vault -> ha interesting! That makes sense, I believe AWS SSM basically just exposes the variables as env vars if I remember it correctly, so maybe we don't need explicit support for it? I haven't used Hashicorp's Vault but I am sure we can figure it out. I guess we will want to add support for both of these at some point, but probably later when we refine the API that Wasp exposes for CORS.  - Ok, it makes sense to me to store CORS origins in the env var since they depend on the environment. In previous projects I have actually often mixed all the origins (localhost, staging, production) and had them hardcoded directly in the code, but I guess security-wise it would be better to shrink that list when possible (e.g. based on the environment).  For a moment, let's look at the whole thing from the "Wasp" perspective. If I am defining my app in Wasp, right now it is a SPA + API server, both generated by Wasp. API is currently ready to be consumed by the external client, it is here just to be consumed by the SPA. So, is there anything user might want to configure regarding CORS or not? I think we should be able to configure it all for them on our own right now. In time, if things change and we realize they need more control, we can figure out how to give them more control (expose CORS options, ...). Right now, the only thing we don't know is what is the domain to which SPA is deployed (we don't have staging right now, so only production matters). Ideally , we would specify that in Wasp code, in `deploy` block, where the rest of the deployment would also be described. However, we don't have that right now. We could implement it thought, if needed, for the purpose of this. Then, when developing locally, we could generate code that has only localhost as an origin, and when building for production, we could hardcode the production origin.  Or, we could just implement it so that origins are pulled from CORS_ORIGINS env var, when running in dev mode localhost is also added, and we tell users when deploying to set CORS_ORIGINS to the correct value on the hosting provider. Maybe that is the easiest for now? In the future, we could have more advanced approach as I described above, where we ask them about the domain of the SPA and take care of these details for them. | Hey, I think SSM and Vault could be implemented later, as they are nice to have. When it comes to the most basic implementation, I'd suggest on adding a global property inside an App element: ``` app testApp {     title: "test app"     domain: "test-app.com" } ``` We'd automatically allow localhost, as well as the above-mentioned domain "test-app.com".  What else could be done is to allow more domains simultaneously by allowing users to specify domains in a certain array (perhaps in an environment variable):  ``` app testApp {     title: "test app"     domain: "test-app.com",     allowed_origins: ["test-application.com", "test-app.org", "test-app.dev", "test-app.io"] } ```  or for more advanced users: ``` app testApp {     title: "test app"     domain: "test-app.com",     allowed_origins: parseArrayFromEnv("env_var_name_here") } ```  ```parseArrayFromEnv``` should be a wasp-specific fn, which would read a string, and then deserialize it into an array or a similar, adequate structure. | Makes sense, I like the direction!  Obviously there are different levels of complexity here we could go with, and I agree with you that specifying domain in the .wasp code might be the easiest way to start. I think we will want to do that at some point anyway, so why not add it right now.  `allowed_origins` also sounds good (both with array directly in .wasp code and with reading it from the env var) as a next step, but it is something we can postpone for later and figure out better at that point.  One thing I am also thinking about in the meantime is "hardcoding" the env var that can be used to set cors origins directly -> same like PORT and JWT_TOKEN. So it would be CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS and if you set it on Heroku or AWS or wherever, it would be read by the deployed Wasp app and used. How this plays with `app.allowed_origins`? Well, the main difference is that with CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS you can set the value based on the environment, directly from the environment. We could also do that with `app.allowed_origins` if we do the `parseArrayFromEnv` approach, assuming that `parseArrayFromEnv` means it will be read from environment once deployed, and not while building a Wasp app.  Anyway, I am in for `app.domain` approach for now! One thing here: the idea is to at some moment introduce `deployment` block, looking smth like: ``` deployment production {   domain: "test-app.com",   provider: "heroku",   ... } ``` and in that case, as shown above, it would make sense to put the domain in this block. So I was thinking, we could take a step in that direction right now and instead of having a `domain` in `app`, we could have: ``` deployment {   domain: "test-app.com" } ``` But, we can also just go with `app.domain` and move `domain` to `deployment` once we make `deployment` more prominent.  Sorry for a lot of text, I am thinking through about different approaches as I type this.  Soooo, what now? Adding `domain` to `app` would require modifying Haskell code (mostly copying), so if you are not ready to play with Haskell this might not be the best fit. What you could do right now is configure CORS to use localhost + whatever is written in env CORS_ALLOWED_ORIGINS, so we have that working for us now, and later we can add `app.domain`. | I like the suggestions, I might as well take a look at the Haskell part of the code and see how it's been implemented for the title, maybe it could be implemented similarly for the app domain. Nonetheless, I'll add a basic CORS configuration as soon as I grab some free time tomorrow. | Awesome :)! Ping me if you need any help (maybe best to do it on Discord), we can maybe even throw some pair programming if you need help with Haskell. | We did decent progress here https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/pull/206 but never finalized it -> let's continue from there once we are ready to continue with this issue! | Currently we have the following logic implemented: ```ts //...   ['cors', cors({ origin: config.allowedCORSOrigins })], // ...  const allowedCORSOrigins = allowedCORSOriginsPerEnv[env.NODE_ENV] const allowedCORSOriginsPerEnv: Record<NodeEnv, string | string[]> = {   development: '*',   production: [frontendUrl] } ```  Does this close the issue? @Martinsos  I think it does. | Yes, I think it does, closing!

Description: Currently we don't do any checks, but we should - length, special characters and everything else that determines the strength of the password.
Discuss Information: Hii @matijaSos i can work on this | Hi @utkarsh4321 thanks a lot for your interest and reaching out! I suggest you write here a short proposal on how would you implement this feature so we can review it together before you start.  Also, have you already checked our [guide to contributing](https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp#contributing)? Here you will find the instructions on how to compile Wasp locally and get started with adding new features!  Let us know if any questions! Also, I'd recommend joining our group on Discord for more conversational-style discussions: https://discord.com/invite/rzdnErX | I noticed that password hashes of the same input result in the same value in the backend. Should add salt | @tom-f-hall thanks for letting us know about this!  We are using https://www.npmjs.com/package/secure-password for hashing the passwords, and from what I read and know, it does use salt when hashing the password. On my machine, when I save two same passwords, hash is different, not the same. However, I see that the start and end of each password is the same (some kind of padding I guess), so maybe that is what made you think they are always the same? If you are indeed getting completely same hashes, could you please let me know on which system are you running this so we can try to replicate it.  Btw we also do have a plan to revisit the security once again when we got closer to production, as per this issue: https://github.com/wasp-lang/wasp/issues/127 . | You are correct, it's just the padding ��‍♂️ | > You are correct, it's just the padding ��‍♂️  It confused me at first also, I needed to expand the content to figure it was more than padding! Atlhough I do wonder what is the purpose of all that padding hm. | I decided to add an additional middleware to do the validation, just before our hashing middleware. While technically a monkey patch of Prisma's create function just for User, it feels like the cleanest approach to ensure: - sane defaults always run - users can enable/disable - users can add their own custom validations  Something like this: ```js   prismaClient.$use(async (params, next) => {     // Ensure strong plaintext password. Must come before hashing middleware.     // Throws an EntityValidationError on the first validation that fails.     if (params.model === '{= userEntityUpper =}' && params.action === 'create') {         const data = params.args.data || {}          const defaultValidations = [           { name: 'email must be present', fn: data => !!data.email },           { name: 'password must be present', fn: data => !!data.password },           { name: 'password must be at least 8 characters', fn: data => data.password.length >= 8 },           { name: 'password must contain a number', fn: data => /\d/.test(data.password) },         ]          let validations = params.args._waspSkipDefaultValidations ? [] : defaultValidations         if (Array.isArray(params.args._waspCustomValidations)) {           validations = validations.concat(params.args._waspCustomValidations)         }          for (const validation of validations) {           if (!validation.fn(data)) {             throw new EntityValidationError('{= userEntityUpper =}', validation.name)           }         }          // Remove from downstream Prisma processing to avoid "Unknown arg" error         delete params.args._waspSkipDefaultValidations         delete params.args._waspCustomValidations     }      const result = next(params)      return result   }) ```  Then, users can customize in the following ways: ```js // Runs just our default validations const newUser = context.entities.User.create({         data: { email: 'some@email.com', password: 'this will be hashed!' }     }) ```  ```js // Runs no validations (previous behavior) const newUser = context.entities.User.create({         data: { email: 'some@email.com', password: 'this will be hashed!' },         _waspSkipDefaultValidations: true, // defaults to false     }) ```  ```js // Runs just their validations const newUser = context.entities.User.create({         data: { email: 'some@email.com', password: 'this will be hashed!' },         _waspSkipDefaultValidations: true,         _waspCustomValidations: [           { name: 'password should not be "password"', fn: data => data.password !== 'password' },           // More can be added below (note: it stops on first to return false)         ]     }) ```  You could also run them all by omitting `_waspSkipDefaultValidations` in the final example, or setting it to `false`. | :warning: These code snippets are now out of date. Please see the PR for the latest versions.

Description: When node server, running with `npm start` (nodemon) crashes, it says ``` Server: [nodemon] app crashed - waiting for file changes before starting... ``` but it does not end/exit, waiting for something to change to try again.  Problem is, while nodemon does print the error message to terminal, we don't forward it for some reason (it should come just after the message above). This means user will see something is wrong but will not know what, making it very hard / impossible to recover from problem.  How to reproduce it: run `wasp start` but delete .wasp/out/server/node_modules in the middle of it running, node server will say that app crashed. To see the error, don't run `wasp start` but instead run `npm start` from .wasp/out/server.  Question is, is node server sending the error message, and if yes, why are we not showing it? Maybe nodemon/node is not printing the error message because it knows it is not in terminal? Or it is but it is not neither stdout nor stderr but it is writing directly to /dev/tty (I read that is usually how output gets by stdout/stderr).
Discuss Information: Solved with a06410dad8543547af2bc7fe820475b64a9542d5 -> turns out, node was sending error message to stderr, but I was incorrectly capturing stderr and actually not capturing it at all -> I was really capturing only stdout, and capturing of stderr would start only once stdout would stop (which means process is over). I now made sure to run capturing of stdout and sterr in parallel, and that works now.

Description: https://www.npmjs.com/package/helmet -> sets some reasonable default HTTPS response headers.
Discuss Information: Draft: 1. Add `helmet` to the list of npm dependencies in waspc/src/Generator/ServerGenerator.hs. 2. Add `helmet` as top-lvl Express middleware (via app.use) in waspc/data/Generator/templates/server/src/app.js, and configure it if needed.  This might be enough! Although I don't know enough about `helmet` to know for sure at the moment. | Hi, is this still needed? If so, then would the default configuration options be good enough for now? | Hey @caz-gh , yes, this is still on! Well, the idea was just to make the app more secure, and it seemed like using helmet might be a good step in that direction. That said, I never used helmet nor have I explored its options, so I am not sure what is the right config -> default will probably work! What is your experience with helmet? Ideally, whoever implements this issue, would explore the options to some extent, to make sure they are making sense + to understand the effects of it. Let me know if you want to work on this and I will help you out with Wasp/Haskell stuff if needed! | @Martinsos, here are the HTTP header behaviors set when using the default helmet top-level wrapper (`app.use(helmet())`) containing all helmet's middleware:  - Sets [Content-Security-Policy](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Content-Security-Policy) header which helps protect against [cross site scripting](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Glossary/Cross-site_scripting) attacks. - Sets [X-DNS-Prefetch-Control](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/X-DNS-Prefetch-Control) header to `false`, which improves user privacy but with a performance cost.  - Sets [Expect-CT](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Expect-Ct) header to help prevent the use of mississued SSL certificates. - Removes [X-Powered-By](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/List_of_HTTP_header_fields#Powered-By) header to avoid revealing to potential attackers that Express is being used. Some discussion to whether this is actually beneficial can found [here](https://github.com/expressjs/express/pull/2813#issuecomment-159270428). - Sets [Origin-Agent-Cluster](https://whatpr.org/html/6214/origin.html#origin-keyed-agent-clusters) header. (I'm still looking into this one) - Sets [Strict-Transport-Security](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Strict-Transport-Security) header tells the browser to not load a a site using HTTP, and that it should convert attempts to access the site to HTTPS requests instead of HTTP. - Sets [X-Download-Options](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/archive/blogs/ie/ie8-security-part-v-comprehensive-protection) header to `noopen`. (This is Internet Explorer specific) - Sets [X-Frame-Options](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/X-Frame-Options) header to `SAMEORIGIN`. This header helps defend against [clickjacking](https://owasp.org/www-community/attacks/Clickjacking) attacks. - Sets [X-Content-Type-Options](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/previous-versions/windows/internet-explorer/ie-developer/compatibility/gg622941(v=vs.85)?redirectedfrom=MSDN) header to `nosniff` to mitigate [MIME type sniffing](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Basics_of_HTTP/MIME_types#mime_sniffing). - Sets [X-Permitted-Cross-Domain-Policies](https://owasp.org/www-project-secure-headers/#x-permitted-cross-domain-policies) to `none`. - Sets [Referrer-Policy](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/Referrer-Policy) header to `no-referrer`. - Sets [X-XSS-Protection](https://developer.mozilla.org/en-US/docs/Web/HTTP/Headers/X-XSS-Protection) header to `0` to disable it. This header offers some protection against basic cross site scripting attacks. [Here](https://github.com/helmetjs/helmet/issues/230) is some discussion on why it is disabled by default.  I don't have much experience with using anything other than the default helmet settings, but I can definitely look into this further! So far, I've added helmet to my fork of Wasp and the defaults seem to be working when I look at the response headers I get with a newly created Wasp app.  | @caz-gh sounds good, and thanks for putting in the work! Please go for it and make a pull request -> if you get stuck or just slowed down at any point at all, let me know and I will help out. | Hey @caz-gh , how is it going, are you still interested in taking care of this one? | Hi @Martinsos , sorry I was a little busy recently but I'll be making the pull request in a bit!  | No prob, just checking in, looking forward to it :)!
